Recognizing the unauthenticated state, the Service Provider intercepts the request. It constructs a formal XML AuthnRequest payload (OASIS SAML V2.0 Core) and issues an HTTP 302 Redirect, instructing the Browser to transition to the trusted Identity Provider (IdP).
Artifact Produced: SAML AuthnRequest Object
3. Browser ferries request vector to identity node
Operating as an HTTP relay, the User Agent executes the redirect and delivers the deflated AuthnRequest URL parameter alongside standard SAML relay state elements to the IdP.
4. Identity Provider requests baseline credentials

Parsing the inbound XML, the central IdP explicitly confirms the identity of the requesting downstream SP via registered metadata. If the SP is unregistered, the IdP terminates the connection and immediately logs an Unknown_SP_Origin_Alert to the SIEM.
5. User computes and transmits primary password
Satisfying the initial UI hook, the user inputs their fundamental baseline credentials (username and explicit password) and submits them back upstream toward the IdP via an encrypted POST tunnel.
6. Identity Provider affirms baseline matrix

The IdP parses the inbound credentials, systematically validating the password hash against its local directory (e.g., Active Directory). Repeated failures trigger a Brute_Force_Threshold_Monitor alert directly to the SIEM stream to deter credential stuffing logic.
Artifact Produced: Primary Context Matrix
7. Identity Provider demands step-up TOTP verification

Having evaluated baseline identity, the IdP consults the specific SP access policies. Recognizing the mandate for high-assurance Multi-Factor Authentication, it pauses the SAML execution to serve a secondary HTML challenge.
8. User extracts and computes secondary token protocol

Operating a separate hardware boundary (e.g., a smartphone authenticator app, conforming to RFC 6238), the user manually acquires the current ephemeral 6-digit TOTP variable and submits it into the IdP capture prompt.
9. Identity Provider validates ephemeral execution timeline

Receiving the transmission, the IdP runs the localized TOTP HMAC computation algorithm. It synchronously compares the input against its stored encrypted seed value. A validation failure logs a TOTP_Rejection_Alert, and excessive clock drift pushes a Time_Sync_Skew_Alert strictly to telemetry.
10. Identity Provider cryptographically mints assertion payload

Clearing all layered requirements, the IdP synthesizes a standard XML SAML Assertion. Uniquely, it formally injects the TimeSyncToken specification natively into the AuthnContextClassRef property (OASIS SAML V2.0 Core, §2.7.2.2), proving TOTP was executed.
Artifact Produced: Signed SAML 2.0 Assertion (TOTP Verified)
11. IdP dispatches finalized cryptographic envelope

Finalizing architectural construction, the IdP securely pushes the Base64-encoded encrypted XML chunk down toward the User Agent, packaged mathematically inside an auto-submitting invisible HTTP POST form.
12. Browser completes explicit delivery loop
Strictly operating as a courier, the client Browser triggers the hidden HTML Javascript, launching the SAML Base64 architecture securely toward the mandated Service Provider ACS URI.
13. Service Provider assesses mathematical envelope boundary

Safely intercepting the inbound SAMLResponse, the SP structurally evaluates the complex XML Digital Signature (DSig). Any validation failure triggers an XML_Signature_Spoofing_Alert indicating severe active Man-in-the-Middle tampering or misconfiguration.
14. Service Provider trusts federated authority mechanism

Locating the crucial AuthnContextClassRef node, the SP observes the TimeSyncToken. If step-up MFA was somehow bypassed (e.g., only PasswordProtectedTransport is present), the SP blocks access and fires an MFA_Bypass_Attempt critical security event to the SOC dashboard.
15. Platform officially orchestrates validated execution context
Concluding all architectural verification securely, the Service Provider executes its local session-creation sequence, definitively unlocking the requested enterprise infrastructure.
Artifact Produced: Local Application Session Cookie
AttributeStatement — carries attributes about the subject:
Name — the attribute identifier; can use OID-based URIs (as in eduPerson/SCHAC for academic federations), URN-based names, or simple strings
NameFormat — specifies how to interpret the Name value: uri (OID/URN), basic (simple strings), or unspecified
Multi-valued attributes are represented by multiple <AttributeValue> child elements within the same <Attribute>
The SP must validate that attribute names match what was agreed in federation metadata or bilateral configuration — arbitrary attributes from the IdP should be ignored
AuthzDecisionStatement — conveys an authorisation decision for a specific resource (SAMLCore §2.7.4):
Decision — one of Permit, Deny, or Indeterminate

Rarely used in practice — most SAML deployments rely on AuthnStatement for authentication and derive authorisation decisions locally at the SP from attribute values rather than delegating authorisation decisions to the IdP. The XACML (eXtensible Access Control Markup Language) standard is the preferred approach for externalised authorisation decisions

SAML bindings define how SAML protocol messages (AuthnRequests, Responses, LogoutRequests) are transported between entities over underlying communication protocols. SAMLBind defines four primary bindings:
2.2.1 HTTP-Redirect Binding: The HTTP-Redirect binding transmits SAML messages as URL query parameters using the HTTP 302 redirect mechanism. The message processing pipeline:
Deflate — the XML message is compressed using the DEFLATE algorithm (RFC 1951)
Base64-encode — the compressed bytes are base64-encoded
URL-encode — the base64 string is URL-encoded and placed in the SAMLRequest or SAMLResponse query parameter

Signature — if the message is signed, the signature is carried in separate query parameters (SigAlg, Signature) rather than embedded in the XML — this is because the message is deflated before signing

Message size limit — URLs are practically limited to ~2,048 characters (browser and server defaults), though RFC 2616 does not define a maximum. After deflate + base64 encoding, this limits the uncompressed XML to roughly 4–8 KB. Suitable for AuthnRequest messages (typically 500–1,500 bytes) but not for Response messages carrying assertions with attributes, which routinely exceed this limit
Signature location — signatures are query-string parameters, not embedded XML DSig. The signed content is the query string itself (SAMLRequest=...&RelayState=...&SigAlg=...), not the XML document

RelayState — an opaque value (≤ 80 bytes per SAMLBind §3.4.3) that the SP sends with the AuthnRequest; the IdP must return it unchanged with the Response. The SP uses it to redirect the user to the originally-requested resource after SSO
No encryption support — the binding carries the message in the URL, which appears in browser history, server logs, and proxy logs. Not suitable for sensitive assertion content

2.2.2 HTTP-POST Binding: The HTTP-POST binding transmits SAML messages as base64-encoded values within an HTML form submitted via HTTP POST. The IdP (or SP, for AuthnRequests) renders an auto-submitting HTML form:

No practical message size limit — form POST bodies are not subject to URL length restrictions. The only limits are HTTP server buffer sizes (typically configurable; defaults range from 64 KB to several MB). Suitable for large Response messages with multiple attribute statements and encrypted assertions
Signature location — signatures are embedded in the XML as enveloped XML Digital Signatures (<ds:Signature> within the <saml:Assertion> or <samlp:Response>)
Browser-mediated — the message passes through the user's browser as a front-channel POST. The base64-encoded content is visible in the page source but not in the URL bar or browser history
JavaScript dependency — the auto-submit form requires JavaScript; a <noscript> fallback button is standard practice
2.2.3 HTTP Artifact Binding: The Artifact binding separates message delivery into two phases:

Front-channel artifact delivery — a small, opaque reference (the artifact) is transmitted through the browser via redirect or POST. The artifact is a 44-byte value (SAMLBind §3.6.4) containing the source ID (SHA-1 hash of the issuer's entityID) and a random message handle

Back-channel artifact resolution — the receiving entity (SP or IdP) uses the artifact to retrieve the actual SAML message directly from the issuing entity via a SOAP-based ArtifactResolveRequest over a mutually-authenticated TLS channel
Message never transits the browser — the actual SAML assertion is exchanged server-to-server, providing confidentiality without requiring XML encryption. The browser only sees the artifact reference
Requires back-channel connectivity — direct HTTPS connectivity between IdP and SP is mandatory. This is problematic in environments with strict firewall rules or where entities are behind NAT
Additional round-trip — the artifact resolution adds latency (one additional HTTPS round-trip)
Artifact lifetime — artifacts are one-use and short-lived (typically valid for seconds to minutes). The IdP must maintain state to map artifacts to messages
The 44-byte artifact value (SAMLBind §3.6.4) has the following internal structure:
Identifies the artifact type (0x0004 for SAML 2.0)
SHA-1 hash of the issuer's entityID (20 bytes)
Random 20-byte value identifying the specific message
1. User Agent sends GET request to Service Provider for protected resource
An unauthenticated user attempts to access a protected URL at the Service Provider (e.g., https://sp.example.com/dashboard). The browser issues a standard HTTP GET request without any session cookie:

The SP's authentication filter intercepts the request, finds no valid session cookie, and initiates the SAML Artifact binding flow. The SP preserves the originally-requested URL internally (or as a short token in a server-side store) so the user can be redirected back to it after successful authentication — the RelayState parameter carries this reference throughout the flow.
2. Service Provider generates AuthnRequest and redirects User Agent to Identity Provider

The SP generates a SAML AuthnRequest declaring ProtocolBinding="urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:bindings:HTTP-Artifact" to signal that it expects the Response to be delivered via the Artifact binding rather than directly through the browser. The SP applies the HTTP-Redirect encoding pipeline (DEFLATE → Base64 → URL-encode), optionally appends a query-string signature, and issues an HTTP 302:

Choosing the Artifact binding here is deliberate: the actual SAML assertion will only ever travel over the back-channel between SP and IdP (mTLS), providing confidentiality without XML encryption. The RelayState carries an opaque server-side token (≤ 80 bytes, per SAMLBind §3.4.3) that maps to the originally-requested URL stored server-side.
3. User Agent delivers SAMLRequest to Identity Provider SSO endpoint
The browser follows the redirect automatically, issuing an HTTP GET to the IdP's Single Sign-On service endpoint with the full query string:

The IdP receives the deflate-compressed, base64-encoded AuthnRequest and begins parsing the SAML protocol message. If the user already has a valid IdP session, the IdP may bypass the login UI entirely and proceed directly to artifact generation.
4. Identity Provider authenticates User and stores SAML Response in artifact map
Before presenting any login UI, the IdP validates the AuthnRequest:
URL-decode → base64-decode → inflate the message
Verify XML schema conformance (SAMLCore §3.2.1)
Confirm the Issuer matches a known SP entityID in the IdP's federation metadata
Confirm the Destination attribute matches the IdP's own SSO endpoint URL (prevents message-forwarding attacks)
Validate the query-string signature using the SP's signing certificate from metadata (if signed)
Verify the AssertionConsumerServiceURL matches an ACS endpoint registered in SP metadata — rejecting any unrecognised ACS URL prevents open-redirect assertion injection attacks
Evaluate ForceAuthn and RequestedAuthnContext

If validation fails on any of these criteria, the IdP returns an AuthnFailed or RequestDenied status response and emits a SIEM event: SAML_AUTHN_REQUEST_REJECTED recording the SP entityID, failure reason, and source IP.
Upon successful user authentication (via login form, MFA, passkey, etc.), the IdP:
Builds a fully-signed Response containing a Bearer-method Assertion with AuthnStatement and AttributeStatement
Generates a cryptographically random 44-byte artifact (SAMLBind §3.6.4): TypeCode (2 bytes) || SourceID (SHA-1 of IdP entityID, 20 bytes) || MessageHandle (random 20 bytes) || Reserved (2 bytes)
Stores the artifact → Response mapping in an in-memory or distributed artifact map with a short TTL (typically 60–120 seconds)
Artifact Produced: 44-byte SAML artifact stored in IdP artifact map (one-time use, TTL ≤ 120 s)
5. Identity Provider redirects User Agent to Service Provider ACS with artifact

The IdP issues an HTTP 302 redirect directing the browser to the SP's Assertion Consumer Service (ACS) URL. The response carries the 44-byte artifact in the SAMLart query parameter and echoes the original RelayState unchanged:

The 44-byte artifact (AAQAALm5... when base64-encoded) contains only the TypeCode, SourceID, and MessageHandle — no assertion data whatsoever. An attacker intercepting this URL from browser history gains nothing of value: the artifact is one-time use, short-lived, and resolvable only by an entity that can establish a mutually-authenticated TLS connection to the IdP's artifact resolution endpoint.
6. User Agent delivers artifact to Service Provider ACS endpoint

The browser follows the IdP's redirect and issues an HTTP GET to the SP's Assertion Consumer Service URL, passing the 44-byte artifact as the SAMLart query parameter together with the echoed RelayState:

The SP receives the request and extracts the artifact. It does not attempt to decode the artifact locally — the artifact is opaque to anyone but the IdP's artifact resolution service. The SP's next action is to resolve the artifact to the actual SAML Response via the back-channel SOAP call in Step 7.
7. Service Provider requests artifact resolution via SOAP

The SP constructs a SOAP ArtifactResolveRequest containing the 44-byte artifact and sends it directly to the IdP's Artifact Resolution Service endpoint over a mutually authenticated (mTLS) back-channel HTTPS connection.
8. Identity Provider validates ArtifactResolveRequest and retrieves stored Response
The IdP's Artifact Resolution Service receives the SOAP request over the back-channel and performs the following validation sequence before returning the stored Response:

mTLS client certificate validation — the SP's TLS client certificate must match the certificate registered in the SP's SAML federation metadata. If the certificate is absent, expired, or untrusted, the IdP closes the connection immediately and emits a SIEM event: SAML_ARTIFACT_RESOLVE_MTLS_FAILURE (SP entityID, certificate subject, rejection reason).
SOAP message schema validation — the ArtifactResolve element must conform to the SAMLCore schema.
Issuer validation — the Issuer in the ArtifactResolve must match the SP entityID associated with the mTLS certificate.
ArtifactResolve signature validation (if signed) — verify the XML Digital Signature using the SP's signing certificate from metadata.

Artifact lookup — query the in-memory artifact map using the 44-byte artifact value as the key. If the artifact is unknown or has expired (TTL ≤ 120 s), return an ArtifactResponse containing a StatusCode of urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:status:Requester and emit: SAML_ARTIFACT_NOT_FOUND (artifact value, SP entityID, expiry status).
Single-use invalidation — immediately delete the artifact from the map upon first retrieval. Any subsequent resolution attempt for the same artifact returns a not-found error, preventing replay.
9. Identity Provider returns SAML Response via SOAP
Upon successful validation and artifact resolution, the IdP encapsulates the full SAML Response inside a SOAP ArtifactResponse envelope and returns it synchronously over the back-channel.

Because the message transits exclusively over the mutually-authenticated back-channel, the assertion is protected from network interception without requiring explicit XML encryption (though encryption may still be applied to protect against SP-internal logging exposure).
10. Service Provider validates Response and creates session

The SP extracts the samlp:Response from the SOAP envelope and executes the mandatory SAML validation sequence. Failure at any point results in immediate rejection and generation of a SAML_RESPONSE_REJECTED SIEM event recording the failure reason:

XML signature verification — validate the <ds:Signature> element using the IdP's signing certificate from federation metadata, checking that the Reference URI matches the active element ID exactly (preventing XML Signature Wrapping).
Issuer verification — verify the Issuer matches the expected IdP entityID.
Status code check — ensure <samlp:StatusCode> is set to Success.
Conditions validation — NotBefore ≤ current_time ≤ NotOnOrAfter (with ~30–120 seconds of clock skew tolerance).
AudienceRestriction — verify the SP's own entityID is listed in <saml:Audience>.
SubjectConfirmation — verify InResponseTo matches the original AuthnRequest ID, Recipient matches the SP ACS URL, and the timeframe is valid.
Replay detection — confirm the assertion ID is not present in the replay cache, then add it.

Upon successful validation, the SP maps the subject's NameID and attributes to a local user principal, stores the SessionIndex (for future Single Logout requests), and mints a local application session.
Artifact Produced: Cryptographically secure tracking session for the authenticated user
11. Service Provider redirects User Agent to original resource

The SP completes the SSO flow by issuing a final HTTP 302 redirect. The browser is sent back to the exact URL preserved in the RelayState from Step 1, accompanied by a Set-Cookie header establishing the new authenticated session state:
The user is now authenticated and their browser accesses the protected resource seamlessly, possessing the required session cookie.
2.2.4 SOAP Binding: The SOAP binding transmits SAML messages within SOAP 1.1 envelopes over HTTP. It is a back-channel only binding — no browser involvement. Used primarily for:
Artifact resolution (ArtifactResolveRequest/Response)
Back-channel Single Logout (LogoutRequest/Response)
The SOAP binding requires direct network connectivity between the communicating entities and mutual TLS authentication.
2.2.5 Binding Comparison Matrix
Front (artifact) + Back (resolve)
Practically unlimited (HTTP body)
Either (front-channel artifact unsigned; resolved message has XML DSig)
Response/Assertion (IdP → SP)
Response (when confidentiality required without XML encryption)
Artifact resolve, attribute query, back-channel SLO
✅ (EncryptedAssertion in POST body)
✅ (inherent — message never in browser)
1 redirect/POST + 1 back-channel resolve

The most common production binding combination is HTTP-Redirect for AuthnRequest (SP → IdP, because requests are small) and HTTP-POST for Response (IdP → SP, because responses carry assertions that exceed URL limits).

SAML profiles combine assertions, protocols, and bindings to achieve specific use cases. The three most operationally significant profiles are Web Browser SSO, Single Logout, and Enhanced Client or Proxy.
2.3.1 Web Browser SSO Profile: The Web Browser SSO Profile (SAMLProf §4.1) is the most widely deployed SAML profile. It supports two initiation modes:
SP-Initiated SSO — the user navigates to a protected resource at the SP; the SP generates an AuthnRequest and redirects the user to the IdP for authentication:
1. User Agent requests protected resource at Service Provider

The SSO flow begins when a user attempts to access a protected resource at the Service Provider (e.g., https://app.example.com/dashboard). The browser sends a standard HTTP GET request. The SP's authentication filter intercepts the request and determines that the user does not have a valid local session — no session cookie is present, or the existing session has expired.

The SP must preserve the originally-requested URL (the RelayState) so the user can be redirected back to it after authentication completes. The RelayState is limited to 80 bytes (SAMLBind §3.4.3), so SPs typically store the full URL server-side and use a short reference token as the RelayState value.
2. Service Provider generates AuthnRequest and applies HTTP-Redirect encoding
The SP constructs an AuthnRequest XML message (SAMLCore §3.4.1) containing:
Destination — the IdP's SSO endpoint (from metadata)
AssertionConsumerServiceURL — where the IdP should POST the Response (must match value in SP metadata)
ProtocolBinding — the binding the SP wants the IdP to use for the Response (typically HTTP-POST)
ForceAuthn — if true, the IdP must re-authenticate the user even if an existing session exists. Used for step-up authentication scenarios (§24.5)
IsPassive — if true, the IdP must not visibly interact with the user; if the user has no session, the IdP returns an error rather than presenting a login form
RequestedAuthnContext — requests a minimum authentication strength. The Comparison attribute can be exact, minimum, maximum, or better
NameIDPolicy — specifies the desired NameID format (§2.5) and whether the IdP may create a new identifier
The SP then applies the HTTP-Redirect encoding pipeline: DEFLATE compress → base64-encode → URL-encode. If the SP signs the request, the signature parameters are appended to the query string.
3. Service Provider redirects User Agent to Identity Provider

The SP responds with an HTTP 302 redirect. The payload instructs the browser to issue a GET request to the IdP's SSO endpoint, carrying the SAMLRequest and the RelayState. If the request is signed, SigAlg and Signature parameters are identically appended:
4. User Agent delivers AuthnRequest to Identity Provider
The browser transparently follows the Location header, generating an HTTP GET to the IdP's single sign-on endpoint.
The IdP receives the URL-encoded query string and processes the deflated, base64-encoded AuthnRequest parameter.
5. Identity Provider parses and validates the AuthnRequest
The IdP performs the following validation steps on the received AuthnRequest:
URL-decode → base64-decode → inflate — reverse the encoding pipeline to obtain the original XML
Schema validation — verify the XML conforms to the SAML 2.0 schema
Issuer verification — check that the Issuer element matches a known SP entityID from the IdP's metadata store
Destination check — verify that the Destination attribute matches the IdP's own SSO endpoint URL (prevents message forwarding attacks)
Signature validation (if signed) — verify the query-string signature using the SP's signing certificate from metadata

ACS URL validation — verify that AssertionConsumerServiceURL matches an AssertionConsumerService endpoint listed in the SP's metadata. This is critical — an attacker who can inject an arbitrary ACS URL could redirect assertions to a malicious endpoint
RequestedAuthnContext evaluation — determine whether the requested authentication context can be satisfied
6. Identity Provider presents login interface to User Agent

If the user does not have an existing IdP session (or ForceAuthn="true" was requested), the IdP renders its authentication HTML interface. The specific authentication method depends on the IdP configuration and the RequestedAuthnContext — it may be a password form, an MFA challenge, a passkey prompt, or a certificate-based authentication flow.

If the user already has a valid IdP session and ForceAuthn is not set, the IdP may skip the login UI entirely — this is the mechanism that enables SSO across multiple SPs. The user is immediately transitioned to Step 8 to build a new assertion referencing the existing authentication event.
7. User Agent submits credentials to Identity Provider

The user provides authentication credentials to the IdP. This interaction is entirely between the user and the IdP — the SP has no visibility into the authentication method or credentials used. This separation is a core architectural principle of federated authentication: the SP trusts the IdP's assertion about authentication without needing to see or validate the credentials directly.
8. Identity Provider authenticates user and builds SAML Response
Upon successful authentication, the IdP constructs the SAML Response message containing:
A Status element indicating success or failure
One or more Assertion elements, each containing AuthnStatement, AttributeStatement, and the Subject with the NameID
The AuthnContextClassRef reflecting the actual authentication method used (which may be stronger than what the SP requested)
The assertion is signed using the IdP's private signing key, and optionally encrypted using the SP's public encryption certificate from metadata
9. Identity Provider delivers SAML Response via HTTP-POST to User Agent

The IdP renders an HTML page containing a form with the base64-encoded SAML Response as a hidden field. JavaScript auto-submits the form to the SP's Assertion Consumer Service (ACS) URL. This is the HTTP-POST binding in action — the Response travels through the browser but is delivered as a POST body, not in the URL.
10. User Agent POSTs SAML Response to Service Provider ACS endpoint
The browser executes the form submission, sending the payload to the SP's ACS validation endpoint.
The POST body delivers the base64-encoded Response XML (SAMLResponse) alongside the opaque state reference (RelayState) echoed unchanged by the IdP.
11. Service Provider validates the SAML Response

Response validation is the most security-critical step. The SP must perform the following checks, all of which are mandatory — failure to implement any single check creates an exploitable vulnerability:

XML signature verification — validate the <ds:Signature> element using the IdP's signing certificate retrieved from federation metadata. The signature must cover the <saml:Assertion> element (assertion-level signature) or the <samlp:Response> element (response-level signature), or both. The SP must verify that the Reference URI in the signature points to the exact assertion being processed — this prevents XML Signature Wrapping attacks (§2.7.1)
Issuer verification — the Issuer in both the Response and the Assertion must match the expected IdP entityID
Status code check — <samlp:StatusCode Value="urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:status:Success"/> must be present
Conditions validation — NotBefore ≤ current_time ≤ NotOnOrAfter (with configurable clock skew tolerance, typically 30–120 seconds)
AudienceRestriction — the SP's entityID must be listed in the <saml:Audience> element
SubjectConfirmation — for bearer method: InResponseTo must match the original AuthnRequest ID (if SP-initiated), Recipient must match the ACS URL, NotOnOrAfter must not have passed
Replay detection — the assertion ID must not have been seen before (stored in a time-limited replay cache)
Decryption — if the assertion is encrypted (<saml:EncryptedAssertion>), decrypt using the SP's private decryption key
12. Service Provider creates local session and maps identity
After successful validation, the SP creates a local application session:

Identity mapping — the NameID from the assertion is mapped to a local user account. Mapping strategies include: direct username match, email address match, JIT (Just-In-Time) provisioning of new accounts, or lookup in a mapping table for opaque persistent NameIDs
Attribute extraction — attribute values from the AttributeStatement are extracted and stored in the session (e.g., roles, groups, email, display name)

SessionIndex storage — the SessionIndex from the AuthnStatement is stored alongside the session. This is required for the SP to participate in Single Logout — the IdP will reference this SessionIndex when sending a LogoutRequest
Session cookie issuance — a secure session cookie is set (HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Lax or Strict)
13. Service Provider redirects User Agent to the originally-requested resource

With the SAML response validated and an application session initialized, the SP issues a final redirect instructing the browser to load the URL originally requested in Step 1 (recovered using the RelayState reference token).
The user is now authenticated and their browser accesses the protected resource seamlessly, possessing the required session cookie.

IdP-Initiated SSO — the user starts at the IdP (e.g., an enterprise portal) and clicks a link to an SP application. The IdP generates an unsolicited Response (no corresponding AuthnRequest) and POSTs it directly to the SP's ACS URL. The SP processes the response identically to SP-initiated SSO, with one critical difference: the InResponseTo attribute is absent (there was no request to respond to), so the SP cannot perform InResponseTo validation. This makes IdP-initiated SSO inherently less secure — it is vulnerable to assertion injection attacks where an attacker replays a captured assertion (§2.7.2). SP-initiated SSO is always preferred when both parties support it.
Security property comparison — SP-initiated vs IdP-initiated SSO:
✅ Verifiable — SP stores the AuthnRequest ID and matches it
❌ No InResponseTo — no request-response binding
✅ — SP checks that response targets the ACS URL specified in the AuthnRequest
⚠️ — SP must validate against metadata, but has no request to compare against
✅ — SP-provided opaque value returned by IdP
⚠️ — IdP controls RelayState; SP must sanitise or ignore
✅ InResponseTo + assertion ID cache
⚠️ Assertion ID cache only — shorter protection window

The key weakness of IdP-initiated SSO is the absence of InResponseTo binding. An attacker who captures a legitimate SAML Response can replay it to the SP — the SP cannot distinguish the replay from a genuine IdP-initiated response because there is no request ID to validate against. The only defence is the assertion ID replay cache and short assertion validity windows. Organisations with high security requirements should disable IdP-initiated SSO entirely.

2.3.2 Single Logout Profile: The Single Logout (SLO) Profile (SAMLProf §4.4) enables near-simultaneous termination of all sessions associated with a principal across multiple SPs. When a user logs out at one SP (or at the IdP), the IdP propagates LogoutRequest messages to all SPs where the user has active sessions.

Front-Channel Logout — the IdP sequentially redirects the user's browser to each SP's SLO endpoint. Each SP terminates its local session and redirects back to the IdP. This approach is visible to the user (browser redirects through each SP) and suffers from reliability issues — if any SP is slow or unreachable, the logout chain breaks. The user may navigate away before all SPs have been reached.

Back-Channel Logout — the IdP sends LogoutRequest messages directly to each SP's SLO endpoint via SOAP over HTTPS (back-channel). This is server-to-server communication with no browser involvement. Each SP locates the session using the SessionIndex and NameID from the LogoutRequest, terminates the session, and returns a LogoutResponse. This approach is more reliable than front-channel because it does not depend on browser cooperation, but it requires back-channel connectivity between the IdP and all SPs.

In practice, SLO in SAML is notoriously unreliable across large federations due to timing issues, network failures, and SP implementation inconsistencies. Many organisations treat SAML SLO as best-effort and enforce session timeouts as the primary session termination mechanism.

2.3.2.1 Front-Channel SLO Flow: Front-channel SLO chains browser redirects through every SP that holds a session for the principal. The IdP generates the first redirect; each SP terminates its session and redirects back to the IdP; the IdP then redirects to the next SP.
1. User Agent initiates logout
The user either clicks a native "Logout" button inside an SP application that forwards them, or they navigate directly to the Identity Provider's global central logout portal endpoint.
2. Identity Provider identifies active sessions
The IdP looks up its session index table to determine all SPs that hold active federated sessions bound to this specific principal. It finds SP1 and SP2.
3. Identity Provider issues redirect to SP1 SLO endpoint

The IdP generates a SAML LogoutRequest, encodes it for the HTTP-Redirect binding, and instructs the browser to visit the first SP in the sequence (SP1). The RelayState is populated with the IdP's own "continue" URL so the browser inherently returns to the IdP after SP1 is done.
4. User Agent requests SP1 SLO endpoint
The browser inherently follows the 302 redirect and hits the SP1 SingleLogoutService endpoint, passing the DEFLATE-encoded SAMLRequest alongside the user's active SP1 session cookie.
5. Service Provider 1 validates and terminates session
SP1 extracts the SAMLRequest and performs mandatory logout validation:
Signature verification — validate the query string signature using the IdP's signing certificate from federation metadata.
Issuer check — verify Issuer matches the expected IdP entityID.
Session identification — use the embedded NameID and SessionIndex to deterministically locate the active local tracking session.
Session termination — destroy the server-side session, clear any associated internal caches, and prepare to invalidate the user's application cookie.
Artifact Destroyed: SP1 local application session
6. Service Provider 1 issues redirect to RelayState

The SP produces an empty HTTP 302 response targeted at the URL it extracted from the RelayState (which is the IdP's orchestration endpoint), appending a success indicator. Crucially, it attaches a Set-Cookie directive to explicitly expire the user's session cookie in the browser.
7. User Agent requests IdP continue endpoint
The browser follows the redirect, returning to the IdP and informing the orchestration engine that the SP1 operation concluded. The browser automatically attaches the global IdP session cookie.
8. Identity Provider records SP1 logged out and determines next SP
The IdP updates its session matrix, marking SP1 as successfully logged out. It peeks at the index and prepares to trigger SP2.
9. Identity Provider issues redirect to SP2 SLO endpoint

The IdP generates a fresh LogoutRequest aimed precisely at SP2's registered logout endpoint. It packages it up as another HTTP-Redirect binding containing the same IdP continue endpoint as the RelayState.
10. User Agent requests SP2 SLO endpoint
The browser executes the next bounce in the chain, presenting the LogoutRequest to SP2.
11. Service Provider 2 validates and terminates session
SP2 intercepts the request, verifies the signature, and destroys its local instance of the application session for this principal.
12. Service Provider 2 issues redirect to RelayState
SP2 complies with the protocol, ejecting the browser back out to the IdP's RelayState continuation callback URL.
13. User Agent requests IdP continue endpoint
The browser returns to the IdP, registering that SP2 has completed its operation.
14. Identity Provider terminates own session
The IdP sees that its dependency list of dependent Service Providers is finally empty. Because all peripheral sessions are dead, it drops its own central master session cookie.
15. Identity Provider issues redirect to final destination
The IdP breaks the bouncing loop and issues a concluding HTTP 302 redirect sending the browser to a static logout confirmation page indicating the widespread process was successful.

2.3.2.2 Back-Channel SLO Flow: Back-channel SLO uses SOAP over HTTPS to send LogoutRequest messages directly from the IdP to each SP's SLO endpoint — the user's browser is not involved in the logout propagation.
1. User Agent initiates logout at Identity Provider

The user clicks a "Log out" button within a Service Provider application or navigates directly to the Identity Provider's central logout portal. The browser issues an HTTP GET or POST request to the Identity Provider's Single Logout (SLO) endpoint, requesting the termination of the global session (SAMLProf §4.4.3).

Security Audit: The Identity Provider logs a LOGOUT_INITIATED telemetry event, recording the originating IP address and browser fingerprint to monitor for automated mass-logout denial-of-service attempts.
2. Identity Provider identifies active Service Provider sessions

The Identity Provider accesses its internal session index table to determine all Service Providers that currently maintain active, federated sessions bound to this specific principal. It creates an explicit execution plan to systematically instruct each identified Service Provider (SP1, SP2) to terminate their respective local sessions.
3. Identity Provider sends SOAP LogoutRequest to SP1

Instead of relying on fragile browser redirects, the Identity Provider constructs a samlp:LogoutRequest message and wraps it in a SOAP envelope (SAMLBind §3.2). It transmits this payload directly to SP1's SingleLogoutService endpoint via a synchronous, server-to-server HTTPS connection. This back-channel mechanism entirely bypasses the user's browser, eliminating reliance on client-side state execution.
4. Service Provider 1 validates request and terminates session

Service Provider 1 receives the SOAP envelope, extracts the LogoutRequest, and performs strict cryptographic validation. It verifies the XML Digital Signature using the Identity Provider's public key from the federation metadata, mitigating spoofed logout injection. It then matches the NameID and SessionIndex against its internal active session cache and permanently destroys the local application session state.
Rejection Scenario: If the signature is invalid or the session cannot be found, the Service Provider aborts the termination and returns an error status in the LogoutResponse.
Security Audit: The Service Provider emits a BACKCHANNEL_LOGOUT_SUCCESS (or FAILURE) SIEM event, tying the termination to the central orchestration request.
Artifact Produced: Terminated local application session context
5. Service Provider 1 returns SOAP LogoutResponse

Service Provider 1 confirms the successful internal session termination by generating a samlp:LogoutResponse message with a Success status. It returns this XML structure within the synchronous HTTP response back over the existing TLS connection to the Identity Provider orchestration engine.
6. Identity Provider sends SOAP LogoutRequest to SP2

The Identity Provider processes the acknowledgment from SP1 and proceeds down its session index list. It dispatches a distinct LogoutRequest to Service Provider 2 over a new server-to-server HTTPS connection. In highly scaled enterprise federations, Identity Providers often dispatch these outbound SOAP requests concurrently across worker threads to prevent cascading latency or hanging due to unresponsive downstream services.
7. Service Provider 2 validates and terminates session

Service Provider 2 intercepts the incoming LogoutRequest and independently executes the identical cryptographic validation and state termination logic performed by SP1. It purges the local session records associated with the specified principal.
Rejection Scenario: If SP2 is offline or fails to validate the signature, the local session unfortunately remains orphaned until its natural timeout expiration.
8. Service Provider 2 returns SOAP LogoutResponse
Service Provider 2 completes its termination transaction by returning a synchronous SOAP LogoutResponse indicating a Success status back to the Identity Provider.
9. Identity Provider terminates own session

Having collected successful acknowledgment vectors from all reliant Service Providers (or having reached a configurable timeout threshold for unresponsive ones), the Identity Provider conclusively destroys its own central master authentication session state.
Security Audit: The Identity Provider emits a GLOBAL_LOGOUT_COMPLETE SIEM event, recording the exact suite of Service Providers that were successfully detached.
10. Identity Provider redirects User Agent to final state

The Identity Provider responds to the user's initial front-channel logout request (from Step 1) by issuing an HTTP 302 redirect. This steers the browser to a public landing page or a final confirmation screen, concluding the global logout operation.
Browser redirect hangs → user navigates away; remaining SPs never logged out
SOAP request times out; IdP can retry or continue
Configure per-SP timeouts (typically 5–10 s); retry failed back-channel requests
LogoutResponse with non-Success status; IdP may skip or abort chain
IdP logs error; may continue to next SP
Accept partial logout; enforce session timeout as safety net
Chain breaks immediately; remaining SPs retain sessions
No impact — back-channel is independent of browser
Back-channel SLO is the primary defence against this
User perceives delay; may navigate away
IdP waits (configurable timeout)
Set aggressive timeouts; skip non-responsive SPs
Inconsistent state — some SPs logged out, others not
IdP should persist logout state and resume on recovery
SP may redirect to IdP which has no pending session
SP returns Success (idempotent per spec)
Idempotent handling is mandatory per (SAMLCore, §4.1.2)

Practical recommendation: Deploy back-channel SLO wherever possible. Use front-channel SLO only as a fallback for SPs that cannot establish back-channel connectivity to the IdP. In large federations, treat SLO as best-effort and rely on short session timeouts (15–30 minutes for high-security applications) as the definitive session termination mechanism.

HTTP-Redirect binding for SLO responses. In front-channel SLO, the SP returns a LogoutResponse to the IdP via HTTP-Redirect binding (Base64-encoded, DEFLATE-compressed). The response confirms session termination:
The IdP uses the InResponseTo attribute to correlate the response with the original LogoutRequest, and the RelayState parameter preserves the IdP's SLO chain state across redirects.

SLO fragility in practice. SAML SLO is structurally the most fragile component of SAML deployments. The redirect chain model (HTTP-Redirect binding) is particularly brittle in multi-SP environments — if any SP is unreachable, the chain breaks and the user's browser is left in a blank state. Even the SOAP binding requires every SP to maintain a publicly accessible SLO endpoint that is consistently available. Many enterprises adopt a pragmatic alternative: short session max-ages combined with Conditional Access enforcement. Rather than implementing fragile SLO, they configure an IdP session lifetime of ~8 hours, SP session lifetimes of 1–4 hours, and Conditional Access that re-evaluates on every token acquisition — sessions expire naturally within 4 hours even without SLO.

2.3.3 Enhanced Client or Proxy (ECP) Profile: The ECP Profile (SAMLProf §4.2) supports authentication for non-browser clients — native applications, command-line tools, web services, and other software that cannot participate in browser-based redirects. ECP uses the SOAP binding and the PAOS (Reverse SOAP) binding:
The client sends an HTTP request to the SP with a PAOS header indicating ECP capability
The SP responds with a SOAP envelope containing the AuthnRequest
The client forwards the AuthnRequest to the IdP via SOAP (the client may need IdP discovery logic)
The IdP authenticates the client (typically via HTTP Basic Auth or client certificates) and returns a SOAP envelope containing the Response
The client forwards the Response to the SP's ACS endpoint via PAOS
The SP validates the assertion and returns the protected resource
ECP is used primarily in academic/research federation contexts (e.g., eduroam, InCommon) where researchers need programmatic access to federated resources.
1. ECP Client sends HTTP request with PAOS header

The ECP Client (a native application, CLI tool, or programmatic API consumer) sends a standard HTTP request to the Service Provider's protected resource. To differentiate itself from a standard browser, the client injects a PAOS header (SAMLProf §4.2.3.1) signaling its ECP profile support and capability to process SOAP responses.
2. Service Provider generates AuthnRequest
The Service Provider detects the PAOS header, determines that the client lacks a valid session cookie, and programmatically compiles a SAML AuthnRequest intended for the downstream Identity Provider.
3. Service Provider returns AuthnRequest in SOAP envelope

The Service Provider responds with an HTTP 200 payload—rather than a 302 redirect—delivering the AuthnRequest wrapped securely in a SOAP envelope (SAMLProf §4.2.3.2). This envelope includes an ECP:Request header element dictating the ultimate Assertion Consumer Service (ACS) endpoint where the client must eventually drop off the resolved token.
4. ECP Client extracts AuthnRequest from SOAP body

The ECP Client acts as an intelligent intermediary. It parses the inbound HTTP 200 payload, strips the SOAP envelope, extracts the raw AuthnRequest, and resolves the endpoint of its configured organizational Identity Provider.
Artifact Produced: Raw samlp:AuthnRequest XML structure.
5. ECP Client forwards AuthnRequest to Identity Provider

The ECP Client establishes a direct HTTP POST connection to the Identity Provider's SOAP-based SSO endpoint. It drops the AuthnRequest into the request body and simultaneously provides its own authentication credentials (e.g., HTTP Basic Auth or an mTLS client certificate) directly in the HTTP headers, entirely bypassing human web forms.
6. Identity Provider validates client credentials

The Identity Provider processes the inbound POST, skips all web-based credential collection logic, and natively validates the provided HTTP Basic Auth headers or mTLS certificate properties against its centralized backend directory.
Rejection Scenario: If the credentials mismatch or the mTLS chain is untrusted, the Identity Provider rejects the request with a SOAP Fault and an HTTP 401 Unauthorized code.
Security Audit: The Identity Provider emits an ECP_AUTHN_ATTEMPT telemetry event, flagging anomalous authentication volumes from headless clients.
7. Identity Provider builds SAML Response

Having cryptographically confirmed the client's identity, the Identity Provider generates the corresponding SAML Response carrying the signed AuthnStatement and relevant profile attributes mapped to the authenticated principal.
8. Identity Provider returns SAML Response to ECP Client
The Identity Provider wraps the generated Response back inside a SOAP envelope and returns it to the calling ECP Client in a standard HTTP 200 OK wrapper.
Artifact Produced: Wrapped samlp:Response containing signed Assertion.
9. ECP Client delivers Response to Service Provider

The ECP Client fulfills its final intermediary duty: it encapsulates the Identity Provider's SAML Response within a final SOAP envelope—injected with the required ECP:Response header—and POSTs it directly to the Service Provider's PAOS endpoint designated back in Step 2.
10. Service Provider validates Response and creates session
The Service Provider strips the envelope and performs standard SAML mandatory security checks.
Signature Verification: Validates the Identity Provider's digital signature over the assertion.
Audience Restriction: Confirms the assertion targets this specific Service Provider.
Replay Detection: Validates the assertion ID against its replay cache.
Upon successful validation, the Service Provider initializes a secure application session for the verified principal.
Artifact Produced: Local application session context
11. Service Provider returns protected resource

The Service Provider finally honors the original HTTP GET request from Step 1, returning an HTTP 200 payload containing the requested application data alongside the newly minted session cookie or token for subsequent API calls.
SAML's security model relies fundamentally on XML Digital Signatures (XML DSig, W3C) and XML Encryption (XML Enc, W3C) for integrity, authenticity, and confidentiality of assertions.
2.4.1 Signature Types: SAML uses two signature scopes:

Response-level signature — signs the entire <samlp:Response> element, covering all contained assertions. This provides integrity for the response structure but does not independently protect individual assertions if they are extracted from the response.

Assertion-level signature — signs each <saml:Assertion> element individually. The assertion remains verifiable even if extracted from its enclosing response. This is the recommended and more common approach — it ensures assertions are self-contained, independently verifiable security tokens.
Both use enveloped signatures — the <ds:Signature> element is placed as a child of the signed element:

Canonicalisation (C14N) — a critical step in XML signature processing. Because XML permits semantically equivalent documents to differ in byte representation (attribute ordering, namespace declarations, whitespace), the signed content must be canonicalised to a deterministic byte-level representation before hashing. SAML uses Exclusive XML Canonicalization (C14N, xml-exc-c14n#) which processes only explicitly-used namespaces, preventing namespace injection attacks across document boundaries.

2.4.4 XML Canonicalisation: Internals: The canonicalisation problem arises because XML permits multiple byte-level representations of the same logical document. Consider these semantically equivalent fragments:

All three are logically identical per the XML Namespaces and XML Information Set specifications. However, a cryptographic hash operates on bytes, not logical structure. If the IdP signs Variant A and the SP processes Variant B (because an intermediary normalised or re-serialised the document), the signature verification fails even though the logical content is identical. Canonicalisation solves this by defining a deterministic byte-level normalisation that maps any semantically equivalent XML document to a single canonical form.

Exclusive C14N Algorithm — SAML uses Exclusive XML Canonicalization 1.0 (http://www.w3.org/2001/10/xml-exc-c14n#), defined in W3C Exclusive XML Canonicalization (Boyer, 2002). This algorithm was designed to address limitations of the original Canonical XML 1.0 in the context of XML fragments and signed subsets.

Canonical XML 1.0 (c14n) inherits all namespace declarations from ancestor elements, including those not visibly utilised by the element being canonicalised. In SAML, an assertion is often extracted from a Response for independent verification — if the Response contained namespaces not used by the Assertion itself, c14n would include them in the serialisation, potentially changing the hash if the Assertion were extracted and re-signed in a different document context. Exclusive C14N includes only visibly utilised namespaces — those explicitly referenced by the element or its attributes.
The Exclusive C14N processing model:
Let EEE be the XML element being canonicalised. The algorithm processes EEE as follows:

Namespace render list — compute the set of namespace declarations to render with EEE. For exclusive C14N, this set {N}E\mathcal\{N\}_E{N}E​ includes all namespace prefixes visibly utilised by EEE and its descendants (in attribute values or element names), plus the default namespace if visibly utilised

Attribute ordering — sort all attributes of EEE into a deterministic order: namespace declarations first (sorted by prefix), then non-namespace attributes second (sorted by namespace URI, then local name)
Child element processing — recursively process child elements in document order
Whitespace normalisation — xml:space="default" normalises whitespace in text content; xml:space="preserve" preserves whitespace exactly; all whitespace in attribute values is normalised regardless
Character encoding — output uses UTF-8 exclusively. Characters are represented as-is (no entity references for ASCII-range characters). Line endings are normalised to #xA (LF)
C14N in SAML signature processing — the verification pipeline applies canonicalisation at two distinct points:

{SignedInfo}{∗}={C14N}({SignedInfo}{{raw}},{Algorithm}{{C14N}})\text\{SignedInfo\}^\{*\} = \text\{C14N\}(\text\{SignedInfo\}_\{\text\{raw\}\}, \text\{Algorithm\}_\{\text\{C14N\}\}){SignedInfo}{∗}={C14N}({SignedInfo}{​{raw}},{Algorithm}{​{C14N}})
h={SHA−256}({SignedInfo}{∗})h = \text\{SHA-256\}(\text\{SignedInfo\}^\{*\})h={SHA−256}({SignedInfo}{∗})

{Verify}({SignatureValue},h,{IdP}{{pubkey}},{Algorithm}{{sig}})\text\{Verify\}(\text\{SignatureValue\}, h, \text\{IdP\}_\{\text\{pubkey\}\}, \text\{Algorithm\}_\{\text\{sig\}\}){Verify}({SignatureValue},h,{IdP}{​{pubkey}},{Algorithm}{​{sig}})

Step 1 — canonicalise the ds:SignedInfo element using the algorithm specified in ds:CanonicalizationMethod (typically xml-exc-c14n#). Step 2 — hash the canonical bytes using the digest algorithm specified in ds:SignatureMethod (typically SHA-256). Step 3 — verify the cryptographic signature against hhh using the IdP's public key. Step 4 — for each ds:Reference within SignedInfo, apply the ordered transforms (typically enveloped-signature then xml-exc-c14n#), hash the result, and compare to the stored ds:DigestValue.

The enveloped-signature transform removes the ds:Signature element itself from the subtree before hashing — because the signature is a child of the signed element and should not be included in its own digest.
2.4.2 EncryptedAssertion: XML Encryption provides confidentiality for assertion content. An EncryptedAssertion is the standard mechanism:
Why encrypt when TLS provides transport security? Encryption addresses a different threat model than TLS:

Multi-party flows — in the HTTP-POST binding, the assertion transits the user's browser. While the TLS channel protects the data in transit between browser and server, the user (or malware on the user's device) can inspect the form field content. Encryption ensures only the intended SP can read the assertion

Intermediary trust — in proxy deployments, SAML responses may traverse intermediate proxies, load balancers, or WAFs that terminate TLS. Encryption provides end-to-end confidentiality between the IdP and the ultimate SP
Attribute sensitivity — when assertions carry sensitive attributes (national ID numbers, healthcare identifiers, financial data), encryption provides defence-in-depth beyond transport security
Regulatory compliance — regulations like HIPAA, PSD2, and GDPR-derived standards may mandate application-layer encryption for sensitive PII in transit, regardless of transport encryption

The IdP encrypts using the SP's public encryption certificate published in the SP's metadata (<KeyDescriptor use="encryption">). The SP decrypts using its corresponding private key. Separate signing and encryption certificates are recommended (§2.6.2).

2.4.3 Certificate Rollover Patterns: SAML certificate rollover is a critical operational concern in production federations. Certificates expire periodically (typically 1–3 years), and the rollover must occur without service disruption.
Dual-Certificate Rollover — the standard zero-downtime approach:
Generate new certificate — create a new key pair and certificate; keep it inactive

Publish both certificates in metadata — update the entity's metadata to include both old and new certificates in <KeyDescriptor> elements. The new certificate can be published with use="signing" alongside the existing one
Distribute metadata — federated partners import the updated metadata containing both certificates. Partners must be configured to accept signatures from either certificate
Activate new certificate — switch the signing (or encryption) operation to the new key pair
Grace period — maintain both certificates in metadata for a sufficient period (weeks to months, depending on federation governance)
Remove old certificate — after confirming all partners have successfully processed assertions signed with the new certificate, remove the old certificate from metadata

Metadata-Driven Automation — federations that use metadata distribution services (e.g., SAML metadata aggregators in academic federations) can automate certificate propagation. Partners poll the metadata URL periodically and automatically update their certificate stores. This reduces the coordination burden but introduces a propagation delay — the metadata refresh interval must be shorter than the certificate rollover grace period.
The NameID element identifies the subject of an assertion — the authenticated user. SAML defines several standard formats (SAMLCore §11.3), each with distinct properties:
urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:nameid-format:persistent
Opaque, stable per IdP-SP pair; pseudo-random; non-correlatable across SPs
Privacy-preserving federations (academic, government); account linking without sharing real identifiers
urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:nameid-format:transient
Random, one-time per session; different on every authentication; cannot be used for account linking
Attribute-only access (authorisation based on attributes, no persistent account at SP); privacy-maximising flows
urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:1.1:nameid-format:emailAddress
User's email address; must conform to RFC 2822 addr-spec format
Legacy SPs that use email as primary key; simple deployments where privacy is not a concern
urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:nameid-format:unspecified
IdP chooses the format; SP must not make assumptions about syntax or stability
Default when no specific format is required
urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:nameid-format:kerberos
Kerberos principal name (user@REALM)
Windows/AD-integrated environments
urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:1.1:nameid-format:X509SubjectName
Distinguished name from X.509 certificate
Certificate-based authentication environments

Privacy implications of persistent NameIDs — the persistent format was specifically designed to enable privacy-preserving federation. The IdP generates a unique opaque identifier for each SP-user pair — the same user receives different persistent NameIDs at different SPs, preventing cross-SP correlation. The SAML Core specification (§11.3.7) mandates that persistent NameIDs "MUST NOT be shared in clear text with providers other than the providers that have established the shared identifier." However, persistent NameIDs are still linkable across sessions at the same SP — if unlinkability across sessions is required, the transient format must be used.

The Subject Identifier Attributes Profile (OASIS, 2019) addresses deficiencies in the NameID mechanism by defining subject identifier SAML attributes (subject-id and pairwise-id) as alternatives to NameID. These attributes provide a cleaner separation between transport-level identifiers and application-level identifiers, and are the recommended approach for new deployments in the U.S. federal (InCommon) and European (eduGAIN) academic federation ecosystems.
2.6.1 Why SAML Persists Despite OIDC: SAML endures in production environments for reasons that are primarily structural rather than technical:

Regulated sectors — government agencies (U.S. federal, EU eGov), healthcare institutions (HIPAA-governed systems), and financial institutions often mandate SAML-based federation through policy or compliance requirements. Migration to OIDC requires re-certification and policy updates that take years

Existing infrastructure investment — large enterprises with hundreds of SP integrations built on SAML cannot justify the cost and risk of migrating all integrations simultaneously. The long-tail of legacy SPs that support only SAML ensures coexistence

XML-native enterprise stacks — organisations built on Java EE, .NET WCF, and SOA architectures have deep XML processing capabilities. SAML integrates naturally with these stacks (SOAP, XML Schema validation, XSLT transformations), while OIDC's JSON/JWT model requires introducing new processing infrastructure

Formal federation governance — academic federations (InCommon, eduGAIN, GÉANT), government identity federations (eHerkenning in NL, FranceConnect), and healthcare federations have established governance models, trust frameworks, and metadata exchange agreements built on SAML. These governance structures cannot be trivially replicated in the OIDC ecosystem

Assertion richness — SAML's XML-based assertion model supports complex attribute schemas (eduPerson, SCHAC, LDAP-backed OID attributes) with natively structured, multi-valued attributes. OIDC claims in JSON are simpler but less expressive for deeply hierarchical attribute structures

2.6.2 Federation Metadata: SAML metadata (SAMLMeta) is the mechanism by which entities in a federation discover and validate each other's capabilities, endpoints, and certificates. Metadata is exchanged out-of-band (typically via secure download or metadata aggregator) and cached locally.
An EntityDescriptor defines a single SAML entity:
entityID — globally unique identifier for the entity (typically a URL)
IDPSSODescriptor — describes Identity Provider capabilities (SSO/SLO endpoints, supported bindings, signing certificates, NameID formats)
SPSSODescriptor — describes Service Provider capabilities (ACS endpoints, requested attributes, encryption certificates, AuthnRequestsSigned preference)

KeyDescriptor — declares X.509 certificates separately for signing and encryption uses. Using separate certificates for these functions is a best practice — it allows independent lifecycle management and limits the blast radius of key compromise
SingleSignOnService — declares SSO endpoint locations per binding type
SingleLogoutService — declares SLO endpoint locations per binding type

In large federations, metadata for hundreds or thousands of entities is aggregated into an EntitiesDescriptor document, signed by the federation operator, and distributed via a metadata URL. Entities poll this URL periodically (e.g., hourly) and validate the aggregator's signature before processing updates.

Signed Metadata Trust Model — in production federations, metadata is not exchanged as plain XML files — it is digitally signed by the federation operator (or by the entity itself for bilaterally-deployed metadata). The trust chain model:

Trust bootstrapping — each entity must pre-configure the federation operator's signing certificate (or its root CA). This is the sole out-of-band trust anchor. All subsequent trust derives from verifying the operator's signature on the metadata document. The verification chain: (1) entity downloads EntitiesDescriptor from the federation metadata URL, (2) validates the <ds:Signature> using the pre-configured federation operator certificate, (3) if valid, trusts all EntityDescriptor elements within the aggregate, (4) extracts endpoint URLs, certificates, and attribute requirements, and (5) uses the IdP's signing certificate from metadata to verify SAML response signatures — no separate certificate exchange with each IdP is needed.
Absolute expiration timestamp for the metadata document
6–12 months from publication
Advisory maximum caching interval before re-fetch
PT1H (1 hour) to PT24H (24 hours)
Entities must not use metadata past the validUntil timestamp. Production deployments should monitor metadata expiration and alert when validUntil is within a configurable threshold (e.g., 30 days).
Real-world federation examples:
InCommon (U.S. higher education)
Centralised aggregate signed by Internet2
Hourly refresh; SHA-256 signatures
PKIX — federation operator certificate in bundle
~4,000+ entities across 50+ national federations
Hierarchical — each national federation signs its own aggregate; eduGAIN aggregates the aggregates
Daily refresh per federation
Chain of trust — each national federation's signing key signed by eduGAIN root
UK Access Management Federation (UKAMF)
~500 entities in UK education/research
Centralised aggregate signed by Jisc
FranceConnect (French government)
~800 public service providers + 8 certified IdPs
Centralised metadata agency (AIFE)
Government PKI — ANSSI-certified certificates
~300 service providers, 10+ identity providers
Broker-managed metadata (Logius)
Real-time via broker protocol
Government PKI — PKIoverheid
SWITCH operates central metadata

2.6.3 SAML Proxying (IdP-as-SP Pattern): In hub-and-spoke federation architectures, a SAML proxy (also called an IdP proxy or federation hub) acts as an intermediary that is simultaneously an SP to upstream IdPs and an IdP to downstream SPs:

The proxy receives an AuthnRequest from an SP, determines the appropriate upstream IdP (via IdP discovery or policy), forwards a new AuthnRequest to that IdP, receives the assertion, transforms it (attribute mapping, NameID transcryption, claim enrichment), and issues a new assertion to the original SP.

Metadata simplification — each SP only configures metadata for the proxy; each IdP only configures metadata for the proxy. Without proxying, a federation of N SPs and M IdPs requires N × M bilateral metadata exchanges; with a hub, it requires N + M

Protocol translation — the proxy can translate between SAML and OIDC, enabling OIDC-native SPs to authenticate against SAML-native IdPs (and vice versa) without requiring each endpoint to support both protocols

Attribute transformation — centralised attribute mapping and enrichment. Different SPs may require different attribute schemas; the proxy can transform the IdP's native attributes into each SP's expected format
Policy enforcement — centralised enforcement of authentication policies (minimum assurance levels, MFA requirements, attribute release consent) at the proxy layer

Privacy enhancement — the proxy can re-encrypt NameIDs (NameID transcryption), ensuring that the upstream IdP does not learn which SP the user is accessing, and the SP does not learn the user's identity at the IdP
Single point of failure — the proxy must be highly available; its outage disconnects all SPs from all IdPs

Assertion freshness — the proxy must issue new assertions with fresh timestamps and its own signature, not simply forward the upstream assertion (which would expose the upstream IdP's identity and violate audience restrictions)

Double signing — the SP receives an assertion signed by the proxy, not the original IdP. The SP's trust relationship is with the proxy, not the upstream IdP — this is an explicit design choice in hub-and-spoke federations
SAML's XML-based design and browser-mediated message flow create a distinct attack surface. The following threat categories are specific to SAML and must be addressed in every production deployment.

2.7.1 XML Signature Wrapping (XSW) Attacks: XML Signature Wrapping is the most dangerous class of SAML-specific vulnerabilities. It exploits a fundamental architectural separation in XML security processing: the signature validation component and the business logic component may resolve element references differently.
The attacker intercepts a legitimate, signed SAML response (e.g., from a previous session)
The attacker moves the original signed <saml:Assertion> to a different location in the XML document (e.g., inside an <Extensions> element or a new wrapper element)
The attacker inserts a forged, unsigned assertion in the location where the SP's business logic expects to find the assertion — typically as the first child of <samlp:Response>

The signature validation library verifies the signature and finds it valid — because the original signed assertion still exists in the document (just relocated), and the signature's Reference URI still resolves to it
The business logic extracts the assertion from the expected location — which now contains the forged assertion with attacker-controlled attributes (e.g., admin role, different user identity)
The SP grants access based on the forged assertion content

Historical impact: XSW attacks have been demonstrated against major SAML implementations repeatedly. Notable CVEs include CVE-2024-45409 (Ruby SAML library, CVSS 9.8 — arbitrary user login via forged SAML response), CVE-2024-8698 (Keycloak XMLSignatureUtil scope determination flaw), CVE-2024-6800 (GitHub Enterprise Server SAML SSO bypass), and CVE-2023-34923 (TOPdesk user impersonation).

Validate that the signature's Reference URI points to the specific assertion being processed. After signature verification, the SP must extract the assertion only from the element referenced by the signature's ds:Reference/@URI — never by document position or XPath traversal
Accept exactly one assertion. Reject responses containing multiple <saml:Assertion> elements unless the application explicitly expects them
Schema validation — validate the response against a strict local copy of the SAML schema. Reject documents with unexpected elements, extra assertions, or non-standard nesting

Use a SAML library that is explicitly hardened against XSW. The canonical research paper — Somorovsky et al., "On Breaking SAML" (USENIX Security 2012) — catalogued 8 distinct XSW variants (XSW1–XSW8). Modern libraries like OneLogin's python-saml, Spring Security SAML, and node-saml implement specific defences against all known variants

2.7.2 Assertion Replay and InResponseTo Validation: Replay attacks — an attacker captures a valid SAML response (from network interception, browser history, or server logs) and replays it to the SP's ACS endpoint.

InResponseTo validation — for SP-initiated SSO, the SP must verify that the response's InResponseTo attribute matches the ID of the original AuthnRequest. The SP stores pending request IDs and removes them after successful response processing. This prevents replay of responses from previous sessions
Assertion ID uniqueness — maintain a time-windowed cache of processed assertion IDs. Reject any assertion whose ID has been seen before within the NotOnOrAfter validity window
Strict time validation — enforce NotBefore and NotOnOrAfter conditions with minimal clock skew tolerance (30–120 seconds). Longer windows increase the replay vulnerability window

⚠️ IdP-initiated SSO has no InResponseTo — because there is no preceding AuthnRequest, there is no request ID to bind the response to. This is an inherent design weakness of IdP-initiated SSO — the SP's only replay protection is assertion ID uniqueness checking and time-window enforcement. Organisations with high security requirements should disable IdP-initiated SSO entirely.
SAML CVE vulnerability timeline:
On Breaking SAML (Somorovsky et al., USENIX Security 2012)
Catalogued 8 XSW variants against major SAML libraries
Improper validation of SAML response signatures — attacker can forge assertions
OpenSAML XXE + signature validation flaw in SAML broker
SpEL injection via SAML authentication binding
User impersonation via crafted SAML response
Microsoft Entra ID SAML SSO bypass via crafted assertion
Arbitrary user login — signature validation accepts unsigned assertions
Crafted SAML response bypasses authentication
XMLSignatureUtil scope determination flaw — assertion from wrong element accepted
MessageReplayChecker allows multiple uses of assertions within the validity window
SAML SSO bypass via crafted SAML response
Certificate validation allows attacker-signed SAML assertions

Pattern analysis: The dominant vulnerability class remains XML Signature Wrapping (XSW) and its variants, accounting for ~60% of high-severity SAML CVEs. The second most common class is assertion replay — insufficient replay detection or overly permissive replay windows. These result in complete authentication bypass in production systems.
Defence-in-depth recommendations:

Patch aggressively — SAML library vulnerabilities have a median exploit availability time of ~30 days from disclosure. Subscribe to security advisories for all SAML libraries in the stack (OpenSAML, python-saml, node-saml, Spring Security SAML)
Validate signatures strictly — use the specific assertion element referenced by the ds:Reference/@URI, not by XPath or document position
Reject unsigned assertions — enforce WantAssertionsSigned="true" in SP metadata and AuthnRequestsSigned="true" in IdP metadata
Limit replay window — configure the assertion replay cache to the minimum practical window (matching NotOnOrAfter, not longer)
Schema-validate all SAML messages — reject documents with unexpected elements or non-standard nesting before signature processing
Run a SAML-specific security scanner — tools like SAML Raider (Burp Suite extension) and SAMLtrap can test for XSW, replay, and audience restriction bypass

2.7.3 Confused Deputy and Audience Restriction: The confused deputy attack occurs when an SP accepts an assertion that was intended for a different SP. If SP-A and SP-B are both registered with the same IdP, an attacker authenticated at SP-A could capture the SAML assertion and present it to SP-B.

Mitigation: AudienceRestriction enforcement — the SP must verify that its own entityID appears in the assertion's <saml:AudienceRestriction> element. This is not optional — assertions without audience restrictions or with incorrect audiences must be rejected unconditionally.
1. Attacker initiates access to low-value Service Provider A

A malicious insider (the Attacker) navigates to a low-privilege Service Provider A — an application they are legitimately authorized to use. The intent is not to use SPA itself, but to obtain a validly signed SAML assertion for later replay.
2. Service Provider A issues standard AuthnRequest to Identity Provider
Following the normal SAML 2.0 Web Browser SSO Profile (OASIS SAML V2.0 Core, §3.4), Service Provider A constructs an AuthnRequest and redirects the Attacker to the central Identity Provider.
Artifact Produced: SAML AuthnRequest (Issuer: SPA)
3. Identity Provider presents authentication challenge to Attacker
The IdP receives the AuthnRequest, validates the SP metadata, and serves a standard login form. From the IdP's perspective, this is an entirely routine authentication request.
4. Attacker submits valid credentials to Identity Provider

The Attacker provides legitimate enterprise credentials. Since they are a genuine employee, the IdP's directory backend (e.g., Active Directory) validates the password hash against the stored record without issue.
5. Identity Provider mints SAML assertion scoped to Service Provider A

The IdP constructs a signed SAMLResponse containing a properly scoped AudienceRestriction element (OASIS SAML V2.0 Core, §2.5.1.4) that explicitly designates SPA as the sole permitted consumer of this assertion.
Artifact Produced: Signed SAML 2.0 Assertion (Audience: SPA entityID)
6. Attacker intercepts SAML Response before delivery to SPA

Instead of allowing the browser's auto-submitting POST form to deliver the assertion to SPA, the Attacker uses an interception proxy (e.g., Burp Suite) to capture the raw Base64-encoded SAMLResponse parameter from the HTTP POST body before it reaches SPA's Assertion Consumer Service.
7. Attacker replays captured assertion against high-value Service Provider B
The Attacker crafts a new HTTP POST request, injecting the intercepted SAMLResponse directly at Service Provider B's ACS endpoint — a higher-privilege target they are not authorized to access.
8. Service Provider B validates XML Digital Signature successfully

Service Provider B parses the inbound SAMLResponse and verifies the XML Digital Signature against the IdP's public key. Because the assertion was genuinely signed by the trusted IdP, this check passes — DSig validation is necessary but not sufficient for security.
9. Service Provider B confirms temporal validity of assertion

Continuing its validation pipeline, SPB evaluates the NotBefore and NotOnOrAfter conditions. Since the Attacker replays the assertion within the validity window (typically 2–5 minutes), the time-bounds check also passes.
10. Service Provider B fails to enforce AudienceRestriction

Due to a misconfigured or incomplete SAML library, SPB skips validation of the AudienceRestriction element (OASIS SAML V2.0 Core, §2.5.1.4). A compliant implementation would compare <saml:Audience> against its own entityID and reject the assertion with a 403 Forbidden. Instead, SPB accepts the assertion silently. A properly instrumented SP would emit an Audience_Mismatch_Rejection event to the SIEM; the absence of this check means the attack produces no telemetry at all — a critical monitoring blind spot.
11. Service Provider B grants unauthorized access to Attacker

With all executed checks passing, SPB creates a local session for the Attacker, granting full access to high-privilege resources. The Attacker has achieved lateral movement across trust boundaries without ever authenticating to SPB directly. Post-incident forensics must correlate the IdP's authentication log (showing SPA as the intended audience) with SPB's access log to reconstruct the attack path.
Artifact Produced: Unauthorized SPB Session Cookie (Confused Deputy)

2.7.4 Clock Skew Handling: SAML assertions carry two time-based conditions: NotBefore and NotOnOrAfter on the <saml:Conditions> element, and NotOnOrAfter on <saml:SubjectConfirmationData>. These values are set by the IdP based on its system clock.

If the IdP and SP clocks are not synchronised, valid assertions may be rejected (if the SP's clock is ahead of the IdP's) or expired assertions may be accepted (if the SP's clock is behind). The standard mitigation is:
NTP synchronisation — both IdP and SP must synchronise to a reliable NTP source
Configurable skew tolerance — implement a configurable allowed clock skew (typically 30–120 seconds) that is added to the validity window in both directions
Log and monitor — log time-based rejections with both the assertion timestamps and the SP's local time to diagnose clock drift issues
The skew window represents a security tradeoff: larger windows increase resilience to clock drift but expand the replay attack window proportionally.
Production SAML deployments should validate conformance using established test suites and tools:
SAML Conformance Test Service
OASIS-endorsed interoperability testing
Metadata, SSO, SLO, Attribute Query, Artifact
Security-focused SAML testing
XSW attacks, assertion manipulation, signature bypass, replay
SP integration testing with reference IdP
SSO, attribute consumption, metadata
XML signature, encryption, canonicalisation
Keycloak-specific SAML validation
Browser extension for SAML debugging
Inspect AuthnRequest/Response, decode Base64, validate signatures
Pre-production validation checklist:
Positive path SSO — verify SP-initiated SSO with all supported bindings (Redirect + POST)
Negative path SSO — verify error handling for invalid credentials, expired assertions, wrong audience, malformed requests
IdP-initiated SSO — verify if supported; test RelayState handling and default landing pages
SLO (front-channel) — verify session termination at all SPs
SLO (back-channel) — verify SOAP-based logout if configured
Signature validation — verify signatures with correct certificate, wrong certificate, expired certificate, self-signed certificate
Encryption — verify EncryptedAssertion with correct key, wrong key, expired key
Metadata rotation — verify certificate rollover with dual-certificate metadata
Clock skew — verify assertion acceptance with +120s and -120s clock skew
XSW resistance — test all 8 XSW variants using SAML Raider
Replay protection — verify duplicate assertion ID rejection within validity window
