Attribute handling — verify all expected attributes are present, correctly formatted, and properly mapped
NameID formats — verify persistent, transient, and email NameID handling
Large assertions — test with assertions containing 20+ attributes to verify no message size issues
Concurrent sessions — verify multiple simultaneous SSO sessions for the same user across different SPs

This checklist is non-exhaustive but covers the most common failure modes in production SAML deployments. Organisations in regulated sectors (government, healthcare, finance) should additionally perform penetration testing with a SAML-specialised security assessor.

Federation protocols — SAML, OIDC, WS-Federation, and the newer OpenID Federation 1.0 — introduce a unique class of authentication attacks that exploit the trust relationships between domains rather than targeting individual credentials. An attacker who can compromise or exploit a federation trust chain can bypass MFA, impersonate entire organizations, or intercept credentials across multiple relying parties simultaneously.

DR-0003's §22 (Attack Taxonomy) and §23 (Defensive Engineering) cover authentication attacks comprehensively at the credential and session level. This section extends that coverage to federation-specific attack vectors: the Evil Twin IdP, SAML assertion injection, OIDC token substitution, cross-origin vulnerabilities, trust chain manipulation, and confused deputy patterns. Each subsection includes real-world examples, detection techniques, and mitigations.

The Evil Twin IdP (also called "Coercion Attack" or "IdP Impersonation") is one of the most dangerous federation attacks in the wild. An adversary registers a domain that closely resembles a legitimate IdP (e.g., login-contoso.com vs. login-contoso.com, using Unicode homoglyphs or subtle typos) and tricks a user into authenticating against it.
The attacker registers https://login-contoso.example.com (the real IdP is https://login.contoso.example.com — note the hyphen placement).
The attacker sends a phishing email containing a link to an SP (or a fake SP) that is configured to trust the attacker's IdP.
The user clicks the link, sees what appears to be a legitimate login page, and enters their corporate credentials.

The attacker's IdP captures the credentials, then forwards the authentication request to the real IdP — the user sees a second login prompt (which the attacker can pre-fill with the captured credentials, or the attacker proxies the entire flow in real-time).

The user authenticates successfully (because the attacker relayed the credentials to the real IdP), the SP receives a valid SAML assertion or OIDC token, and the attacker now has both the user's credentials and a valid session.

Why it defeats MFA. The Evil Twin doesn't bypass MFA directly — the user completes MFA on the real IdP (through the attacker's proxy). The attacker captures the MFA code or session cookie that results from the legitimate authentication. The victim's MFA device displays a legitimate push notification or OTP prompt, so there's no anomaly to detect from the user's perspective.

Domain monitoring. Continuously register and monitor domains that are similar to the organization's IdP domain (including homoglyphs, typosquatting, and newly registered similar domains). Tools like CERT-EU's passive DNS monitoring and commercial brand protection services automate this.

SP metadata restriction. RPs should configure their federation metadata to accept assertions only from the legitimate IdP's entity ID and endpoint URLs. A strict allowlist prevents a fake IdP from injecting assertions.
Certificate transparency monitoring. Monitor Certificate Transparency logs for certificates issued to domains similar to the IdP domain.
User reporting. Educate users to inspect the URL bar during authentication and report suspicious login pages.

SAML assertions are XML documents signed with XML Signature (XMLSig). XMLSig is notoriously complex and has been the source of numerous attack variants, collectively known as XML Signature Wrapping (XSW) attacks. In an XSW attack, the adversary modifies the signed XML document without invalidating the signature, causing the SP to accept a fraudulent assertion.
passages.headingText: 2.9.2 SAML Assertion Injection and XML Signature Wrapping

passages.text: How XML Signature Wrapping works:
passages.headingText: 2.9.2 SAML Assertion Injection and XML Signature Wrapping

passages.text: The SP validates the signature by locating the <ds:Reference> URI within the signed document and verifying the digest over the referenced element. An XSW attack exploits the fact that XML allows multiple elements with the same ID or the same namespace, and the SP's XML parser may select a different element than the one the signer intended.
passages.headingText: 2.9.2 SAML Assertion Injection and XML Signature Wrapping

passages.text: XSW attack variants (Somorovsky, 2014):
passages.headingText: 2.9.2 SAML Assertion Injection and XML Signature Wrapping

passages.text: Move signed assertion into new wrapper, add unsigned assertion
passages.headingText: 2.9.2 SAML Assertion Injection and XML Signature Wrapping

passages.text: SP processes unsigned assertion
passages.headingText: 2.9.2 SAML Assertion Injection and XML Signature Wrapping

passages.text: Duplicate assertion, modify unsigned copy
passages.headingText: 2.9.2 SAML Assertion Injection and XML Signature Wrapping

passages.text: Add new SAML response wrapping original signed response
passages.headingText: 2.9.2 SAML Assertion Injection and XML Signature Wrapping

passages.text: SP validates inner signature but processes outer response
passages.headingText: 2.9.2 SAML Assertion Injection and XML Signature Wrapping

passages.text: Replace signed element's parent with attacker-controlled element
passages.headingText: 2.9.2 SAML Assertion Injection and XML Signature Wrapping

passages.text: SP follows attacker's reference
passages.headingText: 2.9.2 SAML Assertion Injection and XML Signature Wrapping

passages.text: Add comments or processing instructions to shift element positions
passages.headingText: 2.9.2 SAML Assertion Injection and XML Signature Wrapping

passages.text: SP parser misidentifies the signed element
passages.headingText: 2.9.2 SAML Assertion Injection and XML Signature Wrapping

passages.text: Use XML namespaces to create shadow elements
passages.headingText: 2.9.2 SAML Assertion Injection and XML Signature Wrapping

passages.text: Namespace confusion causes SP to select wrong element
passages.headingText: 2.9.2 SAML Assertion Injection and XML Signature Wrapping

passages.text: Embed signed assertion inside attacker-controlled XML structure
passages.headingText: 2.9.2 SAML Assertion Injection and XML Signature Wrapping

passages.text: SP extracts and processes attacker's structure
passages.headingText: 2.9.2 SAML Assertion Injection and XML Signature Wrapping

passages.text: Use XPath injection to redirect reference resolution
passages.headingText: 2.9.2 SAML Assertion Injection and XML Signature Wrapping

passages.text: Reference points to attacker-controlled element
passages.headingText: 2.9.2 SAML Assertion Injection and XML Signature Wrapping

passages.text: Real-world impact. XSW attacks have been demonstrated against major SAML implementations including Shibboleth, SimpleSAMLphp, and commercial SaaS platforms ( Dropbox's SAML implementation was found vulnerable in 2022). The CVE database lists dozens of XSW-related vulnerabilities across SAML libraries.
passages.headingText: 2.9.2 SAML Assertion Injection and XML Signature Wrapping

passages.text: Strict XML parsing. Disable external entity processing (XXE), DTD processing, and XML comments. Use a strict, validating XML parser that rejects malformed documents.
passages.headingText: 2.9.2 SAML Assertion Injection and XML Signature Wrapping

passages.text: Signature verification with reference validation. Verify not only the cryptographic signature but also that the signed element is the one the SP actually processes. The SP should check that the <ds:Reference> URI points to the assertion element that the SP extracts for attribute consumption — not a sibling or wrapper element.
passages.headingText: 2.9.2 SAML Assertion Injection and XML Signature Wrapping

passages.text: Canonicalization hardening. Use the same canonicalization algorithm for both signing and verification (typically http://www.w3.org/2001/10/xml-exc-c14n#). Verify the canonical form of the signed element matches the expected content.
passages.headingText: 2.9.2 SAML Assertion Injection and XML Signature Wrapping

passages.text: Short-lived assertions. Set assertion NotOnOrAfter to a short window (minutes, not hours) to limit the window for replay and injection attacks.
passages.headingText: 2.9.2 SAML Assertion Injection and XML Signature Wrapping

passages.text: OIDC's dynamic client registration (RFC 7591) and the redirect_uri parameter create opportunities for token substitution attacks, where an attacker causes one RP to accept a token that was intended for a different RP or a different IdP.
passages.headingText: 2.9.3 OIDC Token Substitution and IdP Mix-Up

passages.text: Token substitution via redirect_uri manipulation:
passages.headingText: 2.9.3 OIDC Token Substitution and IdP Mix-Up

passages.text: Attacker registers as an RP on the IdP with a redirect_uri pointing to the victim RP's token endpoint.
passages.headingText: 2.9.3 OIDC Token Substitution and IdP Mix-Up

passages.text: Attacker lures a user to authenticate with the IdP (for the attacker's client).
passages.headingText: 2.9.3 OIDC Token Substitution and IdP Mix-Up

passages.text: The IdP issues an authorization code and redirects to the attacker's redirect_uri — which is actually the victim RP's token endpoint.
passages.headingText: 2.9.3 OIDC Token Substitution and IdP Mix-Up

passages.text: The victim RP receives the authorization code and exchanges it for tokens at the IdP's token endpoint — but the tokens were issued for the attacker's client, not the victim RP.
passages.headingText: 2.9.3 OIDC Token Substitution and IdP Mix-Up

passages.text: The victim RP may incorrectly trust the tokens if it does not validate the token's aud (audience) claim against its own client_id.
passages.headingText: 2.9.3 OIDC Token Substitution and IdP Mix-Up

passages.text: Mitigation: RPs must always validate the aud claim in ID tokens and access tokens. The aud must contain the RP's own client_id. This is mandated by OpenID Connect Core §3.1.3.7 but has been incorrectly implemented in several commercial OIDC libraries.
passages.headingText: 2.9.3 OIDC Token Substitution and IdP Mix-Up

passages.text: IdP mix-up attack (Schwarz, 2024):
passages.headingText: 2.9.3 OIDC Token Substitution and IdP Mix-Up

passages.text: The IdP mix-up attack exploits the fact that many RPs support multiple IdPs simultaneously (e.g., "Log in with Google" and "Log in with Microsoft"). An attacker can:
passages.headingText: 2.9.3 OIDC Token Substitution and IdP Mix-Up

passages.text: Register a malicious IdP that mimics the legitimate IdP's metadata.
passages.headingText: 2.9.3 OIDC Token Substitution and IdP Mix-Up

passages.text: Trick the RP into sending the authentication request to the malicious IdP.
passages.headingText: 2.9.3 OIDC Token Substitution and IdP Mix-Up

passages.text: The malicious IdP returns an ID token containing the victim's email address (which the attacker controls).
passages.headingText: 2.9.3 OIDC Token Substitution and IdP Mix-Up

passages.text: The RP accepts the token because the email matches a legitimate user.
passages.headingText: 2.9.3 OIDC Token Substitution and IdP Mix-Up

passages.text: Mitigation: RPs must validate the iss (issuer) claim in ID tokens against the expected IdP issuer URL. They must also verify that the authorization endpoint used matches the iss claim (i.e., the iss matches the issuer URL in the discovery document at the /.well-known/openid-configuration URL derived from the authorization endpoint's host).
passages.headingText: 2.9.3 OIDC Token Substitution and IdP Mix-Up

passages.text: Federation endpoints — metadata URLs, SSO endpoints, discovery documents — are web endpoints that must be hardened against cross-origin attacks. Misconfigurations in CORS policies, cookie handling, and iframe embedding are common and exploitable.

CORS misconfiguration. Federation metadata endpoints and SSO endpoints often need to accept cross-origin requests (the RP's JavaScript calls the IdP's endpoint, or vice versa). If the CORS policy is too permissive (Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * or reflection of the Origin header without validation), an attacker's website can make authenticated requests to the federation endpoint on behalf of a logged-in user.

Impact: An attacker's page can fetch the user's ID token, profile information, or session state from the IdP's userinfo endpoint. This is particularly dangerous for SP-initiated SSO flows where the session cookie is sent automatically.
Restrict Access-Control-Allow-Origin to known RP origins (explicit allowlist, not wildcards).
Never reflect the Origin header in the Access-Control-Allow-Origin response header without strict validation.
Use Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true only with explicit origin allowlisting (never with *).

SameSite cookie issues in federation. SSO flows rely on cookies to maintain session state across the authentication redirect. If the IdP's session cookie lacks the SameSite=Strict or SameSite=Lax attribute, a cross-site request from an attacker's page will include the cookie, allowing the attacker to initiate SSO on behalf of the user.

Mitigation: Set SameSite=Lax (at minimum) or SameSite=Strict on all IdP session cookies. Use SameSite=None; Secure only when cross-site embedding is explicitly required (e.g., iframe-based SSO — but this pattern is deprecated and should be avoided).

WAYF/DS iframe embedding. The "Where Are You From" (WAYF) or Discovery Service (DS) is a web page that lets users select their home IdP from a list. If the WAYF is embedded in an iframe on an attacker's page, the attacker can intercept the user's IdP selection and redirect the SSO flow to a malicious IdP.

Mitigation: Set X-Frame-Options: DENY or Content-Security-Policy: frame-ancestors 'none' on the WAYF/DS page. Use SameSite=Strict on any cookies set during the discovery flow. Avoid iframe-based federation patterns entirely in favor of redirect-based flows.
OpenID Federation 1.0 (§7) introduces trust chains as a mechanism for automatic trust establishment. Trust chains are powerful but create new attack surfaces.

Forged entity statements. If an intermediate entity's signing key is compromised, the attacker can issue fraudulent entity statements for all entities beneath that intermediate. This is analogous to an intermediate CA compromise in PKI — the impact cascades through the hierarchy.

Mitigation: Short key lifetimes, key rotation with overlap periods, and monitoring of entity statement changes (a sudden change in an intermediate's entity statement for a subordinate should trigger an alert).

Metadata injection via policies. A compromised intermediate can attach malicious metadata policies to its subordinates. For example, it could inject a redirect_uris policy that includes the attacker's URL, enabling authorization code interception.

Mitigation: Trust anchors should restrict which metadata fields intermediates are allowed to override in their subordinates' entity statements. The OpenID Federation specification's policy operators (essential, subset_of) can be used to enforce such restrictions.

authority_hints abuse (DoS). The specification warns (§18.1) that an attacker can enumerate an entity's authority hints to discover its federation relationships. More critically, an entity that lists many authority hints forces the resolver to verify many chains, creating a denial-of-service vector.
Mitigation: Rate-limit federation resolution requests. Validate authority hints against a known trust anchor list before initiating chain resolution.

Federation proxy architectures — where an intermediary sits between the RP and the IdP to add value (e.g., protocol translation, attribute mapping, multi-factor step-up) — introduce confused deputy risks. The RP trusts the proxy, the proxy trusts the IdP, but the proxy may not correctly enforce the RP's security requirements.
passages.headingText: 2.9.6 Confused Deputy and Credential Relay Attacks

passages.text: Credential relay. An attacker convinces a user to authenticate to a legitimate IdP through a malicious proxy. The proxy obtains a valid SAML assertion or OIDC token and relays it to the attacker-controlled RP. The user sees a legitimate login flow; the attacker obtains a valid session on the target application.
passages.headingText: 2.9.6 Confused Deputy and Credential Relay Attacks

passages.text: Mitigation: Use the aud claim and client_id to bind tokens to specific RPs. Implement azp (authorized party) validation to detect relay attacks. Use DPoP (RFC 9449) or mTLS to bind tokens to the RP's key pair.
passages.headingText: 2.9.6 Confused Deputy and Credential Relay Attacks

passages.text: Session fixation in multi-hop federation. In a multi-hop federation (user → IdP A → IdP B → RP), an attacker can inject a session fixation token at any hop. If an intermediate IdP does not validate that the incoming session belongs to the authenticated user (not the attacker's pre-set session), the attacker can hijack the session.
passages.headingText: 2.9.6 Confused Deputy and Credential Relay Attacks

passages.text: Mitigation: Each hop in the federation chain must validate the sub (subject) claim against the authenticated user's session. Use nonce values that bind the request to a specific session. Implement in_response_to validation for SAML assertions.
passages.headingText: 2.9.6 Confused Deputy and Credential Relay Attacks

passages.text: Domain monitoring, CT log scanning
Strict entity ID allowlists, certificate pinning for IdP metadata
Revoke sessions from suspicious IdPs, notify affected users
Signature validation failures, assertion content anomaly detection
Strict XML parsing, canonicalization hardening, short assertion lifetime
Patch SAML library, rotate signing keys
aud mismatch in token validation logs
Validate aud, iss, azp claims on every token
Revoke compromised tokens, update client registrations
iss mismatch in authorization responses
Validate iss against discovery document for the auth endpoint used
Block misconfigured IdPs, update IdP allowlists
Security scanner reports, browser console errors
Explicit origin allowlists, never reflect Origin
Update CORS policy, deploy CSP headers
Session cookie sent in cross-site requests
SameSite=Lax or SameSite=Strict on all session cookies
Expire and re-issue cookies with correct attributes
X-Frame-Options bypass attempts
X-Frame-Options: DENY, CSP frame-ancestors: 'none'
Migrate to redirect-based discovery
Entity statement change monitoring
Short key lifetimes, policy restrictions on intermediates
Rotate intermediate keys, revoke forged statements
DPoP or mTLS token binding, nonce validation
Revoke relayed tokens, block malicious proxy
Session mismatch in intermediate assertions
Validate sub and in_response_to at every hop
Invalidate sessions across all federation hops

OpenID Connect (OIDC) and OAuth 2.0 form the dominant protocol stack for modern authentication and authorisation. Understanding their relationship requires recognising that they are layered specifications, each building on the one below — OAuth 2.0 provides the authorisation framework, OIDC adds an authentication identity layer on top, and FAPI 2.0 further constrains both for high-security environments.
passages.headingText: 3. OpenID Connect and OAuth 2.0: Protocol Foundations

passages.text: The three specifications form a strict layering hierarchy where each layer inherits all capabilities of the layer below and adds domain-specific constraints:
passages.headingText: 3.1 Protocol Stack: OAuth 2.0 to OIDC to FAPI

passages.text: Authorisation framework — delegated access to protected resources
passages.headingText: 3.1 Protocol Stack: OAuth 2.0 to OIDC to FAPI

passages.text: Grant types, access tokens, refresh tokens, scopes, client registration
passages.headingText: 3.1 Protocol Stack: OAuth 2.0 to OIDC to FAPI

passages.text: Authentication layer — verifiable user identity
passages.headingText: 3.1 Protocol Stack: OAuth 2.0 to OIDC to FAPI

passages.text: ID Token (JWT), UserInfo endpoint, openid scope, standard claims, session management
passages.headingText: 3.1 Protocol Stack: OAuth 2.0 to OIDC to FAPI

passages.text: Security-hardened profile for financial/high-value APIs
passages.headingText: 3.1 Protocol Stack: OAuth 2.0 to OIDC to FAPI

passages.text: Mandatory PAR, sender-constrained tokens (DPoP/mTLS), asymmetric client auth, PKCE S256
passages.headingText: 3.1 Protocol Stack: OAuth 2.0 to OIDC to FAPI

passages.text: OAuth 2.0 (RFC 6749) defines the authorisation framework — it enables a resource owner (user) to grant a third-party application (client) limited access to a protected resource (API) without sharing credentials. OAuth 2.0 intentionally does not define an authentication protocol — it provides access tokens that authorise API calls but does not standardise how the user's identity is communicated to the client. The access_token returned by OAuth 2.0 is opaque to the client; it carries no standardised identity claims.
passages.headingText: 3.1 Protocol Stack: OAuth 2.0 to OIDC to FAPI

passages.text: OpenID Connect Core 1.0 adds the authentication layer by introducing the ID Token — a signed JWT that contains claims about the authentication event and the authenticated user. When a client includes the openid scope in the authorisation request, the authorisation server returns an ID Token alongside the access token. The ID Token allows the client to verify who the user is (authentication), while the access token allows the client to access resources on behalf of the user (authorisation). OIDC also standardises the UserInfo endpoint for retrieving additional user claims, discovery metadata (/.well-known/openid-configuration), and dynamic client registration.
passages.headingText: 3.1 Protocol Stack: OAuth 2.0 to OIDC to FAPI

passages.text: FAPI 2.0 Security Profile constrains OIDC and OAuth 2.0 for environments where the consequences of token theft or authorisation bypass are severe — financial services, healthcare, government, and open banking. FAPI 2.0 mandates security mechanisms that are optional in baseline OIDC: Pushed Authorization Requests (PAR), sender-constrained access tokens (via DPoP or mTLS), asymmetric client authentication (private_key_jwt or tls_client_auth), and PKCE with the S256 challenge method. FAPI 2.0 is covered in detail in §4.1.
passages.headingText: 3.1 Protocol Stack: OAuth 2.0 to OIDC to FAPI

passages.text: OAuth 2.0 defines multiple grant types (also called flows) — each is a specific protocol sequence for obtaining an access token. The choice of grant type depends on the client type (confidential vs. public), the presence of a user (interactive vs. machine-to-machine), and the deployment context (browser, native app, limited-input device, server).

The Authorization Code Grant with Proof Key for Code Exchange (PKCE, RFC 7636) is the primary and recommended grant type for all interactive authentication scenarios — web applications, single-page applications, mobile apps, and desktop applications. OAuth 2.1 mandates PKCE for all authorization code flows, regardless of client type.
PKCE mechanism — PKCE prevents authorization code interception attacks by binding the authorization request to the token request via a cryptographic proof:
The client generates a random code_verifier — a high-entropy string (43–128 characters from the unreserved character set [A-Z] / [a-z] / [0-9] / "-" / "." / "_" / "~")
The client derives the code_challenge by computing BASE64URL(SHA256(code_verifier)) (the S256 method) or using the verifier directly (the plain method — discouraged)
The client sends the code_challenge and code_challenge_method in the authorization request
The authorization server stores the code_challenge alongside the issued authorization code
When exchanging the code for tokens, the client sends the original code_verifier
PKCE entropy — The security of PKCE depends on the code verifier entropy. For nnn bits of entropy in the verifier, the probability of an attacker guessing the correct verifier in kkk attempts is:

With the recommended minimum of 256 bits of entropy (32 bytes of random data, base64url-encoded to 43 characters), even with 2{40}2^\{40\}2{40} guesses per second, the expected time to guess the verifier exceeds the age of the universe. OAuth 2.1 (RFC 9700) mandates the S256 method and a minimum code verifier length of 43 characters — implementations should use a cryptographically secure random source and generate the full 43–128 character range. 6. The authorization server re-derives the challenge from the verifier and compares it to the stored value — if they match, the code was not intercepted by an attacker (who would not have the verifier)
1. Client Application generates PKCE code verifier and code challenge

The flow commences when the user clicks "Sign In". The Client Application programmatically generates a cryptographically random code_verifier—a high-entropy string spanning 43 to 128 characters (RFC 7636 §4.1). The Client Application computes the corresponding code_challenge by aggressively hashing the verifier using SHA-256 and applying base64url-encoding (the S256 method). The code_verifier is subsequently pinned to the Client Application's secure state session to prevent tampering.
2. Client Application redirects User Agent to Authorization Server

The Client Application builds the authorization request URL (RFC 6749 §4.1.1), enforcing response_type=code, its registered client_id, the strict redirect_uri, requested scope, state binding to neutralize CSRF vectors, and the derived code_challenge (code_challenge_method=S256). The Client Application responds to the User Agent with an HTTP 302 redirect pointed at the Authorization Server.
3. User Agent delivers authorization request to Authorization Server
The User Agent blindly follows the HTTP 302 redirect, carrying all query parameters exclusively through the front-channel directly to the Authorization Server's public-facing endpoint.
4. Authorization Server validates request and stores PKCE challenge
The Authorization Server ingests the raw request parameters and validates the context dynamically:
Client Registration Validation: Verifies the client_id is an active, known entity.
Redirect Validation: Exactly matches the redirect_uri against static whitelisted vectors, neutralizing Open Redirect injections.
Scope Entitlements: Confirms the client is legally permitted to request the target scope.
State Immobilization: Stores the code_challenge_method and code_challenge securely backend.

Rejection Scenario: If the redirect_uri is unverified or the client_id is unknown, the Authorization Server immediately terminates the flow, rendering an error page and emitting an AUTHORIZATION_REQUEST_REJECTED audit telemetry event.
5. Authorization Server presents login interface to User Agent

The Authorization Server evaluates the existing browser session and mandated acr_values. If no high-assurance session exists, it renders an authentication interface tailored to the user's domain (passwords, WebAuthn, MFA).
6. User Agent submits credentials to Authorization Server

The User Agent posts the raw credentials synchronously back to the Authorization Server. This absolute credential isolation ensures the Client Application categorically never witnesses the underlying secret material.
7. Authorization Server authenticates user and generates authorization code

The Authorization Server validates the provided credentials. Upon success, it securely mints a short-lived (30-60 second) authorization code and binds it explicitly to the authenticated user, the client_id, redirect_uri, scope, and the stored PKCE code_challenge.
8. Authorization Server redirects User Agent to Client Application with authorization code
The Authorization Server issues an HTTP 302 redirect bound for the strictly validated redirect_uri, appending the single-use authorization code and the echoed state parameter originally provided.
9. User Agent delivers authorization code to Client Application
The User Agent navigates to the callback URL, exposing the authorization code and state parameter directly to the Client Application via the query string.
Artifact Produced: Authorization code (extracted from query string)
10. Client Application verifies state parameter for CSRF protection

The Client Application evaluates the incoming state parameter against its local persistent session bounds. This verification explicitly blocks CSRF injection attacks that target the callback endpoint.
Rejection Scenario: State mismatch triggers an immediate abortion of the transaction and local session termination.
11. Client Application exchanges authorization code for tokens at token endpoint

The Client Application transmits a direct POST request over the secure back-channel to the Authorization Server's token endpoint. It includes grant_type=authorization_code, the retrieved code, matching redirect_uri, client_id, and critically: the raw, unhashed code_verifier (RFC 7636).
12. Authorization Server validates authorization code and PKCE verifier
The Authorization Server evaluates the full contextual bundle:
Code Validity: Verifies the authorization code is active and unused.
Binding Match: Verifies the client_id and redirect_uri match the phase 1 initialization.
PKCE Proof of Possession: The Authorization Server explicitly hashes the inbound code_verifier and enforces a strict string-match against the originally stored code_challenge.

Rejection Scenario: If the code_verifier is mangled or missing, or the code was previously utilized, the Authorization Server violently aborts the token issuance (returning invalid_grant) and triggers a TOKEN_EXCHANGE_FAILURE_PKCE SIEM alert spanning potential interception scenarios.
13. Authorization Server returns tokens to Client Application

If validation succeeds, the Authorization Server burns the used authorization code and responds with a JSON payload containing the access_token, the optional refresh_token, and the OIDC id_token (if openid was scope-requested).
Artifact Produced: access_token, refresh_token, id_token
14. Client Application accesses protected resource with access token
The Client Application structurally injects the newly minted access_token into the HTTP Authorization: Bearer header, transmitting the resource request asynchronously to the backend Resource Server.
15. Resource Server validates the access token

The Resource Server evaluates the presented token autonomously (for JWTs via JWKS verification) or via an introspection endpoint (RFC 7662). Validation explicitly guarantees unexpired exp, correct audience bounds aud, and authoritative issuer provenance iss.
Rejection Scenario: Failed validation results in a strict 401 Unauthorized or 403 Forbidden bearing a WWW-Authenticate descriptive header.
16. Resource Server returns protected resource to Client Application
The Resource Server streams the requested endpoint JSON payload back to the originating Client Application over the TLS boundary.

Native app security: embedded WebViews are prohibited. The Authorization Code Grant with PKCE is the recommended flow for native mobile and desktop applications. However, the choice of user agent for the authorization redirect is critical. OAuth 2.0 for Native Apps (RFC 8252, §11.12) and OAuth 2.0 Security Best Current Practice (RFC 9700) explicitly prohibit embedded WebViews (e.g., WKWebView on iOS, WebView on Android) for OAuth flows. The risks include credential leakage (the native app can inspect and capture user credentials entered in the WebView), session cookie theft, no shared session state with the system browser, no password manager integration, and custom URI scheme interception. The native application hosting the WebView is both the relying party (which needs the authentication result) and the user agent (which handles the credential) — a confused-deputy scenario that violates the principle of least privilege.
Recommended native app redirect mechanisms:
iOS: ASWebAuthenticationSession — system-provided browser session with process isolation, shares cookies with Safari
Android: Chrome Custom Tabs or Auth Tab — system-managed browser component with URL bar verification
Desktop: Loopback redirect (http://127.0.0.1:{port}/callback, RFC 8252 §10.3) — client starts a local HTTP server on a random port for strong isolation
For a comprehensive comparison of same-device authentication approaches (redirect, popup, embedded WebView), see §29.

3.2.1.1 Refresh Token Rotation Patterns: Refresh tokens are long-lived credentials that allow a client to obtain new access tokens without requiring the user to re-authenticate. They are the most sensitive credential in the OAuth ecosystem — long lifetimes (days, weeks, or months) combined with the ability to silently generate new access tokens make them a high-value target for attackers. Several patterns have evolved to protect them (see §34.4 for refresh token rotation in session management context):
Refresh tokens have a maximum lifetime (e.g., 90 days) regardless of use
Limits the window of vulnerability
Each use extends the refresh token's lifetime (e.g., 7 days from last use)
Balances security with user experience
Refresh token expires if not used within a threshold (e.g., 14 days of inactivity)
Detects and expires abandoned sessions
Each use invalidates the previous refresh token and issues a new one
Detects token theft via reuse
If a previously-used refresh token is presented again, all tokens in the family are revoked
Refresh token is bound to the client's key (DPoP or mTLS)
Prevents use by non-intended clients

Refresh Token Family — the set of all refresh tokens descended from a single authorization grant through rotation. If any member of the family is reused after rotation, the authorization server MUST revoke the entire family (all refresh tokens and all access tokens issued from any member of the family).

Non-rotating refresh tokens are the simplest pattern: the token is issued once and remains valid until it expires or is explicitly revoked. If stolen, the attacker has unlimited access for the refresh token's entire lifetime, and the legitimate client and attacker operate indistinguishably.

Rotating refresh tokens invalidate the old token on each use and issue a new one. If a stolen token is used before the legitimate client, the legitimate client's next use fails — creating a detection signal — but the attacker already has the new token.
Rotating with reuse detection is the strongest pattern. If a previously-used refresh token is presented again, the authorization server detects reuse and revokes the entire token family:
1. Client Application requests token refresh normally

At the end of an access token's operational lifespan, the legitimate Client Application securely POSTs its current refresh token (RT-001) targeting the Authorization Server's /token endpoint utilizing the grant_type=refresh_token profile over the TLS back-channel.
2. Authorization Server validates token and generates new set

The Authorization Server parses the payload, validates RT-001's digital signature or central database validity, and rigidly verifies the token has not expired and holds an unused state. It officially transitions RT-001 to a consumed state. It synchronously mints a completely new access token (AT-002) and a rotating refresh token (RT-002).
3. Authorization Server returns new tokens to Client Application
The Authorization Server concludes the exchange by transferring the minted token bundle to the Client Application via an HTTP 200 OK wrapper.
4. Client Application stores new token and discards old
The Client Application extracts the json, writes the new RT-002 into its Secure Enclave or encrypted session storage partition, and aggressively erases all trace references to the burned RT-001.
Artifact Produced: access_token (AT-002), refresh_token (RT-002)
5. Attacker replays stolen refresh token

Sometime later, a Threat Actor who previously exfiltrated RT-001 (such as via an XSS side-channel or device breach) attempts to mint a rogue access token sequence by blindly POSTing the stolen RT-001 directly to the Authorization Server token endpoint.
6. Authorization Server detects token reuse

The Authorization Server processes RT-001 and deterministically identities its status flag as already consumed. Because legitimate rotational clients permanently discard consumed tokens, the Authorization Server decisively classifies this secondary transmission as a malicious replay attack.
7. Authorization Server aggressively revokes the entire token family

The Authorization Server initiates immediate threat containment: it traces the relational lineage (token family) tied to the compromised grant and issues a database-wide blackout, systematically revoking RT-001, the currently active RT-002, and all associated active access tokens (AT-002).
Security Audit: The Authorization Server fires a SEV-1 TOKEN_FAMILY_COMPROMISE SIEM anomaly event isolating the originating attacker IP infrastructure and the compromised client identifiers.
8. Authorization Server returns error to Attacker
The Authorization Server actively halts the HTTP request, yielding an HTTP 400 Bad Request alongside an invalid_grant error string. The Threat Actor is structurally denied a usable credential.
9. Legitimate Client requests token refresh later

When the legitimate Client Application's AT-002 naturally expires, the client blindly attempts to execute another silent rotation utilizing its seemingly undisturbed RT-002 token over the standard back-channel.
10. Authorization Server detects revoked family status
The Authorization Server interrogates its database and validates that RT-002 was actively blacklisted during the automated anomaly mitigation event triggered in Step 7.
11. Authorization Server returns error to Legitimate Client
The Authorization Server blocks the flow, replying with an HTTP 400 invalid_grant exception to the Client Application.
12. Client Application drops session and prompts re-authentication

The Client Application traps the exception, destroys any localized user state data, and enacts a hard system logout. It forcibly steers the user through a front-channel redirection mandate to establish an entirely clean authentication baseline.

The Client Credentials Grant (RFC 6749 §4.4) is the machine-to-machine (M2M) grant type — it issues an access token to the client itself, not on behalf of a user. There is no user interaction, no browser redirect, and no authorization code. The client authenticates directly to the token endpoint and receives an access token scoped to the client's own permissions.
The authorization server authenticates the client (using any of the methods described in §3.4), verifies the requested scope is permitted for this client, and returns an access token:

No refresh token is issued — the client can request a new access token at any time by re-authenticating. No id_token is issued — there is no authenticated user. The sub claim in the access token (if it is a JWT) typically identifies the client application, not a user. Machine-to-machine authentication patterns are covered in depth in §21.

The Device Authorization Grant supports authentication on input-constrained devices — smart TVs, gaming consoles, IoT devices, CLI tools, and other devices that lack a full browser or convenient text-input capability. The device displays a short user code and a verification URL; the user completes authentication on a separate device (phone, laptop) with a full browser.
The protocol operates in two phases:
Phase 1 — Device requests authorisation:
The authorization server responds with:

The device displays the user_code and verification_uri (or renders a QR code from verification_uri_complete). The user navigates to the verification URL on their phone or laptop, enters the user code, authenticates, and grants consent.
Phase 2 — Device polls for token:
While the user authenticates on the secondary device, the primary device polls the token endpoint:
The authorization server responds with authorization_pending until the user completes authentication, slow_down if the device is polling too frequently, or the final token response upon success.
CLI tools and developer utilities — Command-line tools (e.g., aws, gcloud, kubectl) run in a terminal without a graphical browser. Two OAuth approaches are available:
Local browser redirect (loopback)
CLI opens a local HTTP server on 127.0.0.1:{port} and launches the system browser
Low friction (seamless for users with a browser)
High (full OAuth redirect with PKCE)
CLI polls token endpoint; user authorizes in browser on a separate device
Medium friction (user code entry)
Medium (user code provides some phishing resistance)

Use local browser redirect (loopback) when a system browser is available on the same machine (most desktop environments). Fall back to Device Authorization Grant for headless environments or remote SSH sessions where no local browser is accessible. Avoid manual token paste approaches — they expose tokens in the clipboard and provide no cryptographic binding.
Cross-device authentication patterns are covered comprehensively in §28.

Token Exchange enables a client to exchange one security token for another at the authorization server's token endpoint. It supports three distinct semantic patterns — delegation, impersonation, and downscoping — using the subject_token and actor_token parameters. Token exchange is covered in full detail in §3.6.

CIBA (OpenID Connect Client-Initiated Backchannel Authentication Core) enables decoupled authentication — the client initiates authentication on one device, and the user authenticates on a different device (typically their phone) without any browser redirect. CIBA defines three response modes: Poll, Ping, and Push. Full protocol coverage is provided in §31.
Two grant types from OAuth 2.0 (RFC 6749) have been formally removed in OAuth 2.1:

Implicit Grant (response_type=token) — returned the access token directly in the URL fragment of the redirect URI. This was designed for single-page applications that could not make back-channel requests to the token endpoint. The implicit grant was removed because:

Token exposure — the access token appears in the URL fragment, which is accessible to JavaScript on the page (including third-party scripts), visible in browser history, and potentially leaked via the Referer header
No PKCE support — the implicit grant does not use authorization codes, so PKCE cannot be applied. There is no mechanism to bind the authorization request to the token delivery
No refresh tokens — the implicit grant does not support refresh tokens; the client must perform a full redirect-based flow to obtain a new access token when the current one expires
Front-channel only — the token is delivered exclusively through the front-channel (browser redirect), providing no opportunity for back-channel client authentication
SPAs should use the Authorization Code Grant with PKCE instead, optionally combined with a Backend-for-Frontend (BFF) pattern (§32) to keep tokens out of the browser entirely.

Resource Owner Password Credentials (ROPC) Grant (grant_type=password) — allowed the client to collect the user's username and password directly and exchange them for tokens at the token endpoint. This was an anti-pattern because:
Credential exposure — the client handles the user's raw credentials, violating the fundamental OAuth 2.0 principle of credential isolation
Phishing enablement — training users to enter their credentials into third-party applications normalises the behaviour that phishing attacks exploit
No MFA support — the ROPC grant provides no standardised mechanism for multi-factor authentication; it assumes single-factor password authentication
No SSO — each client independently collects and validates credentials; there is no centralised session at the authorization server

OAuth 2.1 (draft-ietf-oauth-v2-1) consolidates OAuth 2.0 (RFC 6749), PKCE (RFC 7636), OAuth 2.0 for Browser-Based Apps, OAuth 2.0 Security Best Current Practice, and Bearer Token Usage (RFC 6750) into a single specification. It does not introduce new features — it codifies a decade of security best practices:
Optional (recommended for public clients)
✅ Mandatory for all authorization code flows
Defined (response_type=token)
Defined (grant_type=password)
Allows pattern matching (implementation-dependent)
✅ Exact string matching required
Bearer tokens in query string
✅ Recommended (sender-constrained or rotation)
Web apps, SPAs, mobile apps, desktop apps
M2M, microservice-to-microservice, batch jobs
Delegation, impersonation, downscoping, cross-domain
✅ Yes (on authentication device)
Decoupled auth, call centres, POS, AI agent approval
Grant Type Security Properties Comparison:
User credentials exposed to client
Authorization code interception protection
Token delivered front-channel
Token delivered back-channel
Sender-constrained token support

The ID Token is the core artifact that distinguishes OpenID Connect from OAuth 2.0. It is a JSON Web Token (JWT) — a base64url-encoded, signed (and optionally encrypted) JSON payload — that contains claims about the authentication event and the authenticated user.
passages.headingText: 3.3 ID Token, Access Token, and UserInfo Endpoint

passages.text: A JWT consists of three base64url-encoded parts separated by dots: header.payload.signature.
Header — identifies the signing algorithm and key:
alg — the signature algorithm (RS256, ES256, PS256, EdDSA)
typ — token type; JWT for ID Tokens, at+jwt for JWT Access Tokens (RFC 9068)
kid — key identifier; used to select the correct public key from the authorization server's JWKS endpoint for signature verification
Signature — the digital signature over BASE64URL(header) + "." + BASE64URL(payload) using the algorithm specified in the header and the authorization server's private key.
The following claims are mandatory in every ID Token (OIDC Core §2):
Issuer — the authorization server's URL; must exactly match the issuer value in the discovery document
Subject — a locally unique, stable identifier for the authenticated user at the issuer; never reassigned to a different user
Audience — the client_id of the relying party; may be a string or array. The client MUST verify its own client_id is present
Expiration — Unix timestamp after which the ID Token must be rejected; typically 5–15 minutes after issuance
Issued At — Unix timestamp when the token was issued; used by clients to assess token freshness
These optional claims provide critical information about how the user authenticated — they are the OIDC equivalent of SAML's AuthnContextClassRef and AuthnStatement:
Unix timestamp of the actual authentication event; required when max_age is requested or require_auth_time is set
Step-up authentication — compare auth_time to determine if re-authentication is needed
Opaque value sent by the client in the authorization request; the client MUST verify it matches
Prevents ID Token replay — binds the token to a specific client session
Authentication Context Class Reference — a URI or string identifying the authentication assurance level achieved (e.g., AAL values from §1)
Maps to NIST AAL or eIDAS LoA; enables risk-based decisions based on authentication strength
Authentication Methods References — a JSON array of strings identifying the authentication methods used (e.g., ["pwd", "otp"], ["hwk"], ["face"])
Audit trail and policy enforcement — know exactly which methods were used, not just the assurance level

Step-up authentication via acr_values — The Relying Party can request a specific authentication level by passing the acr_values parameter in the authorization request (see §1.3 for the full ACR vocabulary). For example, requesting acr_values=urn:mace:incommon:iap:silver signals AAL2-equivalent authentication to the IdP. The IdP evaluates whether the user's current session satisfies the requested ACR; if not, it triggers step-up authentication — prompting the user for a second factor such as TOTP (RFC 6238). The IdP returns the actual ACR achieved in the ID token's acr claim, and the RP can verify that it meets the required level before granting access. This mechanism is protocol-agnostic at the IdP level — the step-up factor could be TOTP, WebAuthn, push notification, or any other authenticator — but the OIDC acr_values contract ensures the RP's assurance requirement is communicated and enforced regardless of which specific factor the IdP deploys. Full step-up authentication patterns and trigger-based elevation are covered in §24.5. | at_hash | Left half of the SHA-256 hash of the access token, base64url-encoded | Binds the ID Token to a specific access token; prevents token substitution when tokens travel through the front-channel | | c_hash | Left half of the SHA-256 hash of the authorization code, base64url-encoded | Binds the ID Token to a specific authorization code; used in hybrid flows | | azp | Authorized Party — the client_id of the party the token was issued to; required when the aud array contains multiple values | Clarifies which client is the intended direct consumer when multiple audiences are listed | | sid | Session ID — an identifier for the user's session at the authorization server | Used by OIDC Back-Channel Logout to identify which session to terminate |
Relying parties MUST validate the ID Token signature before trusting any claims. The validation process follows the JWKS (JSON Web Key Set) discovery chain:
Discover the JWKS URI — retrieve the authorization server's OpenID Connect discovery document from {issuer}/.well-known/openid-configuration. Extract the jwks_uri value
Retrieve the JWKS — fetch the JSON Web Key Set from the jwks_uri. The JWKS contains an array of public keys in JWK format, each identified by a kid (key ID)
Select the signing key — match the kid from the ID Token header to a key in the JWKS. Also verify the alg matches the key's intended algorithm
Verify the signature — use the selected public key to verify the signature over the header and payload
Validate claims — after signature verification, validate iss, aud, exp, iat, and nonce
Key discovery metadata fields:
Authorization server identifier (MUST match iss in tokens)
URL for authorization requests
https://auth.example.com/authorize
https://auth.example.com/token
URL for user claims retrieval
https://auth.example.com/userinfo
URL for JSON Web Key Set (public keys)
https://auth.example.com/.well-known/jwks.json
URL for token introspection (RFC 7662)
https://auth.example.com/introspect
URL for token revocation (RFC 7009)
https://auth.example.com/revoke
URL for Pushed Authorization Requests (RFC 9126)
https://auth.example.com/par
require_pushed_authorization_requests
dpop_signing_alg_values_supported
Supported DPoP signing algorithms
code_challenge_methods_supported
token_endpoint_auth_methods_supported
Supported client auth methods
["private_key_jwt", "tls_client_auth"]
["authorization_code", "client_credentials", "refresh_token"]
id_token_signing_alg_values_supported
Supported ID Token signing algorithms
Supported subject identifier types
["openid", "profile", "email", "address", "phone"]
backchannel_logout_supported
Whether back-channel logout is supported

Protected Resource Metadata (RFC 9728) extends the discovery pattern to resource servers, published at /.well-known/oauth-protected-resource. It allows clients to discover which authorization server to use for a specific resource server, the required scopes, and the supported token types. This is critical for dynamic federation scenarios like the Model Context Protocol (MCP), where clients discover authorization requirements programmatically rather than through manual configuration.
OAuth 2.0 does not mandate a specific access token format. Two patterns dominate:

JWT Access Tokens (RFC 9068) — self-contained tokens that carry claims about the authorisation grant. The resource server validates the token locally by verifying the signature and checking claims (iss, aud, exp, scope, client_id). Advantages: no network call to the authorization server for validation. Disadvantages: cannot be revoked before expiry without additional infrastructure (see §3.5 for introspection-based revocation patterns).

Opaque (Reference) Tokens — random, high-entropy strings with no embedded claims. The resource server must call the authorization server's token introspection endpoint (RFC 7662, see §3.5) to validate the token and retrieve the associated claims. Advantages: immediately revocable, no claim leakage to interceptors. Disadvantages: adds latency (one HTTP call per API request, mitigated by introspection response caching).
Opaque vs JWT Access Token — Decision Matrix:
⚠️ Requires short lifetime + introspection
❌ Every service must introspect
⚠️ Requires sender-constraint
✅ Session at backend, opaque to frontend
⚠️ If BFF validates, either works
❌ Resource server needs introspection credentials
✅ Public key verification only
Regulatory compliance (real-time revocation required)
✅ Immediate revocation via introspection
❌ Requires introspection on every request
JWT Access Token required claims (RFC 9068 §3.1):
Authorization server identifier (URL)
MUST equal the issuer value in the authorization server metadata
Subject — the resource owner or client the token represents
MUST be present; format is issuer-specific
Audience — the resource server(s) the token is intended for
MUST contain the resource server's identifier as a string value
Expiration time (Unix timestamp)
MUST reject tokens after this time
JWT ID — unique identifier for the token
SHOULD be present; enables revocation lists and replay detection
JWT Access Token recommended claims:
Space-separated list of granted scopes
Fine-grained authorization at the resource server
The client that obtained the token
Resource server can apply client-specific policies
Confirmation — proof-of-possession key binding
Sender-constrained tokens (DPoP: jkt; mTLS: x5t#S256)
Tokens with future activation (rare)

The cnf claim is critical for sender-constrained tokens. When DPoP is used, the cnf claim contains {"jkt": "SHA256(DPoP public key thumbprint)"} — the resource server verifies that the DPoP proof JWT presented with the request was signed by the same key, binding the access token to a specific client instance (see §36 for device-bound sessions).
Introspection caching TTL (for opaque token deployments):
High-security APIs, financial services
Internal low-sensitivity APIs
The OIDC UserInfo endpoint provides additional claims about the authenticated user beyond those included in the ID Token. The client calls it with a valid access token:
The response contains user claims based on the requested scopes:

The UserInfo response is controlled by scopes: profile returns name and demographic claims, email returns email claims, phone returns phone claims, address returns address claims. The sub claim in the UserInfo response MUST match the sub in the ID Token — a mismatch indicates a token substitution attack and the client must reject the response.

Client authentication determines how a client proves its identity to the authorization server's token endpoint. This is distinct from user authentication — client authentication establishes trust in the application, while user authentication establishes trust in the person. The choice of client authentication method has direct implications for security posture, operational complexity, and compliance with security profiles like FAPI 2.0.
OIDC Core §12 and RFC 8705 define seven standardised client authentication methods:

Public clients (SPAs, native mobile apps) cannot securely store a client secret — the application code runs in an environment the user controls (browser, mobile device). These clients authenticate with token_endpoint_auth_method=none and rely entirely on PKCE to bind authorization requests to token exchanges.
No client secret, no client assertion, no TLS certificate — the client_id is sent as a form parameter but is not a secret (it is visible in the authorization request URL). PKCE is mandatory.
The client sends its client_id and client_secret as HTTP Basic Authentication credentials — base64-encoded in the Authorization header:

The Authorization header value is Basic BASE64(client_id:client_secret). Both the client_id and client_secret must be URL-encoded before base64 encoding (RFC 6749 §2.3.1) — this is a common implementation error when client IDs or secrets contain special characters.
The client sends its credentials as form parameters in the POST body:

Functionally equivalent to client_secret_basic but with credentials in the body rather than the header. Some authorization servers prefer this method because it avoids issues with proxy servers that strip or modify Authorization headers.
The client creates a JWT assertion signed with the client secret using an HMAC algorithm (HS256, HS384, HS512). The shared secret serves as the symmetric key for both signing and verification:
The client assertion JWT payload:
iss and sub — both set to the client_id (the client is the issuer and the subject)
aud — the token endpoint URL of the authorization server
jti — a unique identifier for the assertion; the authorization server SHOULD reject JWTs with previously-seen jti values to prevent replay
exp — short expiry (typically 60–300 seconds)

The advantage over client_secret_basic/post is that the client secret itself is never transmitted over the wire — only a signed JWT derived from it. This prevents credential interception by network intermediaries or server-side logging.

The client creates a JWT assertion signed with its private key using an asymmetric algorithm (RS256, ES256, PS256, EdDSA). The authorization server verifies the assertion using the client's public key, which is pre-registered or published at a jwks_uri:
The JWT payload is structurally identical to client_secret_jwt — the critical difference is the signing mechanism:
No shared secret — the authorization server only holds the client's public key; even if the authorization server is compromised, the attacker cannot impersonate the client
Non-repudiation — the assertion can only have been created by the holder of the private key, providing a stronger audit trail than symmetric signatures
Key rotation — the client can rotate its key pair independently by updating the registered jwks_uri or JWKS; no secret exchange ceremony is required with the authorization server
FAPI 2.0 compliance — private_key_jwt is one of only two client authentication methods permitted by the FAPI 2.0 Security Profile (the other being tls_client_auth)

Mutual TLS (mTLS) client authentication (RFC 8705 §2.1) uses TLS client certificate authentication at the transport layer. The client presents an X.509 certificate issued by a CA trusted by the authorization server during the TLS handshake. The authorization server validates the certificate chain and maps the certificate's subject (DN, SAN, or specific attribute) to a registered client.
passages.headingText: 3.4.6 tls_client_auth: Mutual TLS with PKI Certificate

passages.text: No client_assertion or client_secret is sent — the authentication occurs at the TLS layer, before the HTTP request is processed:
passages.headingText: 3.4.6 tls_client_auth: Mutual TLS with PKI Certificate

passages.text: The authorization server matches the certificate subject to the registered client using one of: tls_client_auth_subject_dn (Distinguished Name), tls_client_auth_san_dns (SAN DNS name), tls_client_auth_san_uri (SAN URI), tls_client_auth_san_ip (SAN IP address), or tls_client_auth_san_email (SAN email).
passages.headingText: 3.4.6 tls_client_auth: Mutual TLS with PKI Certificate

passages.text: mTLS client authentication also enables certificate-bound access tokens (RFC 8705 §3) — the authorization server embeds a hash of the client's TLS certificate in the access token's cnf (confirmation) claim:
passages.headingText: 3.4.6 tls_client_auth: Mutual TLS with PKI Certificate

passages.text: The resource server then verifies that the client presenting the access token is using the same TLS certificate — this creates a sender-constrained access token that is useless if stolen (see §36 for device-bound sessions and §39.4.1 for the complete end-to-end RFC 8705 validation walkthrough).
passages.headingText: 3.4.6 tls_client_auth: Mutual TLS with PKI Certificate

passages.text: Identical to tls_client_auth in wire format, but the client's X.509 certificate is self-signed rather than issued by a trusted CA (RFC 8705 §2.2). The authorization server validates the certificate by comparing it to a pre-registered JWKS containing the client's public key (as an X.509 certificate in a JWK x5c parameter) rather than validating a CA chain.
passages.headingText: 3.4.7 self_signed_tls_client_auth: Mutual TLS with Self-Signed Certificate

passages.text: This method eliminates the dependency on PKI infrastructure — the client generates its own key pair and certificate, and registers the public key with the authorization server. It provides the same transport-layer authentication and certificate-binding benefits as tls_client_auth without requiring PKI certificate procurement (see §39.4.1 for the end-to-end token binding execution).
passages.headingText: 3.4.7 self_signed_tls_client_auth: Mutual TLS with Self-Signed Certificate

passages.text: Secret in every request (header)
Requires coordinated secret rotation
Secret in every request (body)
Requires coordinated secret rotation
Never transmitted (only signed JWT)
Requires coordinated secret rotation
Never transmitted (only signed JWT)
Independent — client updates public key
Certificate lifecycle via CA
Client generates and registers new cert

Dynamic Client Registration (DCR, RFC 7591) enables clients to programmatically register themselves with an authorization server without manual administrative intervention. This is essential for ecosystems with many clients — app stores, IoT platforms, and open banking — where manual registration is operationally infeasible.

The authorization server validates the request, creates a new client record, and returns the client_id and (for confidential clients) a client_secret. In production deployments, the registration endpoint is typically protected by an initial access token or a trusted software statement assertion.

FAPI 2.0 considerations — In FAPI 2.0 environments (e.g., Open Banking), DCR is typically not used as-is — clients are registered through a semi-manual onboarding process that includes financial regulatory checks, terms of service agreement, and risk assessment. However, some FAPI 2.0 implementations support DCR with additional security constraints:
SSA (Software Statement Assertion)
The registration request includes a signed JWT from a trusted authority (e.g., the Open Banking directory) that attests to the client's identity and compliance
Client must provide a URI containing a JSON array of all redirect URIs; enables pairwise subject identifier computation
Only authorization_code and refresh_token are permitted
Client registration records that the client will use PKCE with S256

Token introspection enables a resource server to query the authorization server about the current state of an access token or refresh token. This is the primary validation mechanism for opaque (reference) tokens that carry no embedded claims, but it is also used with JWT access tokens to check real-time revocation status.
token — the token to introspect
token_type_hint — optional hint to the authorization server about the token type (access_token or refresh_token); helps the server locate the token efficiently in storage

The calling resource server authenticates using its own credentials (typically client_secret_basic or private_key_jwt). The authorization server must verify that the caller is authorised to introspect tokens — not every registered client should have introspection access.
Response (inactive/expired/revoked/unknown token):

The active boolean is the single critical field — if false, the resource server must reject the token. No additional information is provided for inactive tokens to prevent information leakage about why the token was rejected. The cnf claim (if present) enables the resource server to verify sender-constrained token binding (§36).

Token revocation allows a client to notify the authorization server that a token (access token or refresh token) is no longer needed and should be invalidated. Common triggers: user logout, session termination, detected compromise, and application uninstall.

The revocation endpoint always returns HTTP 200, even if the token was already invalid or unknown — this prevents token probing (RFC 7009 §2.2). Revoking a refresh token SHOULD also revoke all access tokens issued from that refresh token.

The JWT revocation problem — revoking JWTs is fundamentally harder than revoking opaque tokens because JWTs are validated locally without contacting the authorization server. Once issued, a JWT access token remains valid until its exp claim expires. Revocation strategies for JWTs include:
Short-lived tokens — issue access tokens with very short lifetimes (5–15 minutes); the window of vulnerability is limited to the token's remaining lifetime
Token introspection on every request — the resource server calls the introspection endpoint for every request, negating the self-contained advantage of JWTs but enabling real-time revocation
Event-based revocation via CAEP — the authorization server pushes Security Event Tokens (SETs) to resource servers, notifying them of revoked sessions or tokens in near-real-time (§38)

Revocation lists — the authorization server publishes a list of revoked token identifiers (jti values); resource servers periodically poll this list. This is analogous to CRL/OCSP in PKI but not standardised for OAuth

Token lifetimes are a security-usability tradeoff: shorter lifetimes reduce the window of vulnerability but increase user friction (more re-authentication prompts) and system load (more token refresh requests). The following table provides evidence-based recommendations calibrated to different deployment contexts (RFC 9700 §2.2.1).
High-Security (Financial/Healthcare)

Autonomous agents and workloads. Token lifetime recommendations for autonomous agents (§22.2) diverge from human-centric patterns in several respects. Programmatic token refresh is frictionless — no user interaction is required — so shorter access token lifetimes (1–5 minutes) are practical even for internal services. Absolute expiry should align with task duration rather than human session patterns: a batch processing agent running a multi-hour job may justify 24–72 hours, while a real-time API agent should rotate every 1–8 hours. Idle timeout must be aggressive (1–3 hours) — unlike a human who may return to an idle session, a stalled agent workflow typically indicates a malfunction or deadlock, not a temporary absence. Refresh token rotation should enforce halt-on-failure: if a refresh request fails, the agent must cease processing and signal an error rather than retrying indefinitely with stale credentials (see §34.4 for the refresh token lifecycle model).

The access token lifetime is the most critical parameter. In FAPI 2.0 or high-security deployments, access tokens should expire within 5 minutes. Combined with DPoP sender-constraint and introspection for real-time revocation checks, this limits the window of exploitation for a stolen token to minutes. The authorization code lifetime should be as short as operationally feasible — RFC 6749 allows up to 10 minutes, but 30–60 seconds is recommended to reduce the window for code interception and code injection attacks (RFC 9700 §2.1.1).

Token exchange defines a protocol for exchanging one security token for another at the authorization server's token endpoint. It uses the grant type urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:token-exchange and supports three distinct security semantics.
passages.headingText: 3.6 Token Exchange (RFC 8693): Delegation, Impersonation, and Downscoping

passages.text: Token type URIs — the subject_token_type and actor_token_type parameters use standardised URIs to identify the token format:
passages.headingText: 3.6 Token Exchange (RFC 8693): Delegation, Impersonation, and Downscoping

passages.text: urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:access_token
passages.headingText: 3.6 Token Exchange (RFC 8693): Delegation, Impersonation, and Downscoping

passages.text: urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:refresh_token
passages.headingText: 3.6 Token Exchange (RFC 8693): Delegation, Impersonation, and Downscoping

passages.text: urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:id_token
passages.headingText: 3.6 Token Exchange (RFC 8693): Delegation, Impersonation, and Downscoping

passages.text: urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:jwt
passages.headingText: 3.6 Token Exchange (RFC 8693): Delegation, Impersonation, and Downscoping

passages.text: urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:saml2
passages.headingText: 3.6 Token Exchange (RFC 8693): Delegation, Impersonation, and Downscoping

passages.text: SAML 2.0 assertion (base64url-encoded)
passages.headingText: 3.6 Token Exchange (RFC 8693): Delegation, Impersonation, and Downscoping

passages.text: urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:saml1
passages.headingText: 3.6 Token Exchange (RFC 8693): Delegation, Impersonation, and Downscoping

passages.text: SAML 1.1 assertion (base64url-encoded)
passages.headingText: 3.6 Token Exchange (RFC 8693): Delegation, Impersonation, and Downscoping

passages.text: In delegation, the issued token identifies both the original subject and the acting party. The act (actor) claim in the resulting JWT records the delegation chain:

This token says: "the subject is user-456, but service-A is acting on their behalf, and service-A was itself called by service-B." The resource server can distinguish between the original user and the intermediary services — this is critical for audit trails and fine-grained authorization in microservice architectures. Use case: a user calls API Gateway → Service A → Service B; each hop exchanges the token with an additional act layer, preserving the full delegation chain.
In impersonation, the issued token identifies only the original subject — the acting party is invisible. There is no act claim:
The resource server cannot distinguish this from a token obtained directly by user-456. Use cases:
Legacy system integration — a modern API gateway impersonates the user when calling legacy backends that do not understand delegation semantics
Customer support — a support agent impersonates a customer to reproduce their experience
Migration — during protocol migration (e.g., SAML → OIDC), a proxy impersonates the user by exchanging a SAML assertion (subject_token_type=saml2) for an OAuth access token

⚠️ Impersonation has weaker audit properties than delegation — the act claim is absent, so the resource server cannot determine that a third party is acting on the user's behalf. Authorization servers should require explicit consent or administrative approval for impersonation grants.
Downscoping narrows the permissions of an existing token without introducing a new actor. The client exchanges its broad-scoped token for a narrower one:
The resulting token has the same sub as the original but with reduced scope and a specific resource audience. Use cases:
Least-privilege enforcement — a service with broad permissions creates narrow-scoped tokens for each downstream call
Temporary access — create a short-lived, read-only token for a specific resource to share with a less-trusted component
Cloud storage — exchange a service account token for a narrowly-scoped, time-limited token for a specific storage bucket

3.6.3.1 Token Exchange Sequence Diagram: The following diagram illustrates the delegation pattern in a microservice architecture — a user calls an API Gateway, which exchanges the user's token for a delegated token to call a downstream service:
1. User attempts API call to the API Gateway
The client application attaches the user's primary access token (AT-001) as an HTTP Bearer token and targets a protected endpoint hosted on the edge API Gateway.
2. API Gateway validates the initial token locally

The API Gateway intercepts the HTTP request, performs a localized cryptographic verification of the JWT signature, enforces that the exp timestamp is valid, and verifies the requested scope maps accurately to the target route endpoints.
Rejection Scenario: If the token signature is mangled, the API Gateway immediately drops the request with an HTTP 401 Unauthorized, returning no functional insights to the client.
3. API Gateway initiates Token Exchange request

Identifying a required down-stream dependency (Service A), the API Gateway utilizes the RFC 8693 urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:token-exchange assertion grant out-of-band to the Authorization Server's /token endpoint. It structurally encapsulates the user's initial access token as the subject_token and authenticates intrinsically via its own explicit actor_token credential.
4. Authorization Server evaluates delegation policies

The Authorization Server cross-validates the inbound subject_token and the actor_token. Based on strict, centralized internal policy matrices, it assesses whether the identified API Gateway maintains legal authorization to downscope or mint delegated cross-boundary tokens on behalf of the identified principle.
5. Authorization Server returns delegated Token to API Gateway

The Authorization Server structurally mints a fully customized delegation token (AT-002) and returns it over the TLS wrapper. The payload intrinsically embeds the delegation sequence utilizing the act claim, formally logging the API Gateway as the active proxying node in the act.sub structure.
Artifact Produced: access_token (delegated context)
6. API Gateway calls downstream Service A
The API Gateway binds the newly authorized, delegated AT-002 Bearer token into an outbound internal HTTPS request aimed across the private network mesh, targeting Service A directly.
7. Service A validates the delegated token

Service A independently retrieves the global JWKS keys directly from the Authorization Server. It applies exhaustive cryptographic validation against the inbound token payload, verifies its own unique identifier inside the aud (audience) claim array, and unpacks the act.sub matrix to validate the chain of provenance tracing back to the API Gateway.
8. Service A executes on behalf of User

With authorization formally secured across the upstream user and traversing API Gateway, the Service A backend processes the actual operational logic historically launched by the initiating principal.
Security Audit: Service A triggers an INTERNAL_RESOURCE_ACCESSED SIEM audit event specifying the precise principal from the sub claim and the proxy source via the act claim.
9. Service A returns internal result to API Gateway
Service A streams its securely processed internal response parameters over the private network directly back to the active API Gateway thread.
10. API Gateway returns final payload to User

The API Gateway unwraps the microservice-internal response object and routes the final, sanitized payload bound up within an HTTP 200 OK stream back to the originating user client over the public edge.
3.6.3.2 Token Exchange Real-World Use Cases
Delegated token with act chain
Delegation — full audit trail
Token for downstream service
Impersonation token (no act)
Impersonation — backward compatibility
Customer support impersonation
Impersonation token (sub = customer)
Impersonation — admin consent required
Narrowly-scoped token for specific bucket
Downscoping — least privilege
Delegated token with agent in act chain
Delegation — agent acting for user

Refresh tokens from token exchange. RFC 8693 §4.1 permits the authorization server to include a refresh_token in the token exchange response alongside the issued access token. This is policy-dependent — the AS evaluates whether to issue a refresh token based on the client's grant type, the subject token type, and the delegation semantics. Issuing a refresh token is critical for long-running delegated tasks: without one, the agent or gateway must re-perform the full token exchange (including re-authentication or re-authorization) when the access token expires, which may be impractical for tasks spanning hours. When issued, the refresh token is subject to the same rotation and expiry policies as standard refresh tokens (§34.4) — including absolute expiry, idle timeout, and rotation with token family-based fraud detection. Support varies across implementations: Microsoft Entra ID and Okta support refresh token issuance in token exchange responses; some open-source authorization servers do not.

RFC 8693 addresses single-hop token delegation — a resource server exchanges an incoming access token for a backend token via a single round-trip to the authorization server. However, modern microservice architectures execute transactions that span multiple synchronous hops: an API gateway calls a risk-evaluation service, which calls an approval service, which calls a settlement engine. Each hop needs to know who initiated the transaction, what the original request parameters were, and whether those parameters have been tampered with — without returning to the authorization server at every hop.

The OAuth Transaction Token specification (draft-ietf-oauth-transaction-tokens, v08, IETF OAuth Working Group Last Call) solves this by introducing a short-lived, cryptographically signed JWT that carries immutable authorization context through an entire call chain. The token is issued once by a Transaction Token Service (TTS) — itself an RFC 8693 profile — and then propagated unmodified through every downstream workload. Each workload validates the token's signature, audience, and expiry, then extracts the transaction context for its own authorization decision. The trade-off is deliberate: Transaction Tokens sacrifice the dynamic re-evaluation that per-hop AS round-trips would enable, in exchange for millisecond-latency propagation and cryptographic integrity guarantees.

JWT Structure and Wire Format: A Transaction Token is a JWS-signed JWT (RFC 7515, RFC 7519) with the media type application/txntoken+jwt, identified by the typ header parameter txntoken+jwt. The specification defines the following claims:
txn — Unique transaction identifier (string or UUID), correlated with RFC 8417 Security Event Tokens for cross-system traceability.
sub — Principal identity (user or workload) unique within the trust domain.
aud — Trust domain identifier; defines the validity boundary of the token.
iat, exp — Issued-at and expiration timestamps. Tokens are intentionally short-lived (typically 30–120 seconds).
scope — Transaction purpose or intent, narrowly scoped (e.g. trade.stocks).
req_wl — Requesting workload identifier(s); traces which workloads initiated the chain.
rctx — Requester context (JSON object): contains the original requester's IP address (req_ip) and authentication method (authn).
