✅ Excellent (JSON, PKCE, native SDKs)
✅ Via access tokens (JWT or opaque)
XML DSig (canonicalisation required)
JWS (base64url, simple JSON parsing)
Shibboleth, ADFS, Okta, PingFederate

Where WS-Federation is simpler: WS-Federation has only one browser-based binding (passive redirect + POST) compared to SAML's four bindings. Configuration is often simpler — there is no AuthnRequest signing requirement, no artifact resolution endpoint, and no complex XML canonicalisation in the request path. The protocol surface area is smaller.

Where WS-Federation lacks capability: No artifact binding (security tokens always travel through the browser), no standardised back-channel logout, no dynamic client registration, no native mobile support, no PKCE equivalent, and no JSON-based tooling. The XML-centric design makes WS-Federation a poor fit for modern API-first and mobile architectures.

Organisations with WS-Federation deployments face a clear technical trajectory toward OIDC. This section provides a comprehensive migration framework covering Microsoft's deprecation timeline, migration paths, step-by-step cloud migration procedures, claims mapping, a decision matrix, and common pitfalls.
Microsoft has progressively deprecated WS-Federation across its identity platform, signalling that OIDC is the strategic direction for authentication:
Entra ID (Azure AD) stopped recommending WS-Federation for new applications
New App Registrations default to OIDC; WS-Fed available but not recommended
WS-Federation support removed from ASP.NET Core
Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authentication.WsFederation package archived; no .NET Core support
Microsoft announced ADFS-to-Entra ID migration guidance
"Migrate from federation to cloud authentication" documentation published
WS-Federation flagged as deprecated in Entra ID application templates
New Enterprise Application templates default to SAML/OIDC; WS-Fed option hidden by default
WS-Federation continues to function but receives no new feature investment
No breaking removal announced, but deprecation warnings appear in Entra admin centre

WS-Federation is not being "turned off" — existing ADFS and Entra ID WS-Federation integrations continue to function. However, Microsoft no longer invests in the protocol, new applications should use OIDC, and existing applications should plan migration.
The recommended migration trajectory follows a two-stage path:
passages.headingText: 6.8.2 Migration Path: WS-Fed to SAML to OIDC

passages.text: Stage 1: WS-Federation → SAML 2.0 — an intermediate step useful when the target IdP supports SAML but the application's OIDC migration requires code changes. ADFS supports issuing SAML 2.0 tokens to WS-Federation Relying Party Trusts, so this stage may require only a configuration change (switching TokenType from SAML 1.1 to SAML 2.0) rather than a code change.
passages.headingText: 6.8.2 Migration Path: WS-Fed to SAML to OIDC

passages.text: Stage 2: SAML 2.0 → OIDC — the final target state. This stage typically requires code changes in the application (replacing XML parsing with JWT validation, changing middleware libraries). For .NET Framework applications, this means replacing WS-Federation OWIN middleware with OIDC OWIN middleware.
passages.headingText: 6.8.2 Migration Path: WS-Fed to SAML to OIDC

passages.text: Direct migration (WS-Fed → OIDC): some organisations can skip the SAML intermediate stage and migrate directly. This is possible when the IdP supports both protocols simultaneously (ADFS 2016+, Entra ID), the application can be modified to use OIDC middleware, and the organisation can absorb the change risk of a single migration step.
passages.headingText: 6.8.2 Migration Path: WS-Fed to SAML to OIDC

passages.text: Microsoft's recommended migration path moves from on-premises ADFS to cloud-native Entra ID authentication via two parallel workstreams: user authentication and application authentication.
passages.headingText: 6.8.3 ADFS to Entra ID Migration: Cloud-First Approach

passages.text: User authentication migration:
passages.headingText: 6.8.3 ADFS to Entra ID Migration: Cloud-First Approach

passages.text: Install Microsoft Entra Connect (or Entra Connect Sync) to synchronise user accounts from on-premises AD to Entra ID
passages.headingText: 6.8.3 ADFS to Entra ID Migration: Cloud-First Approach

passages.text: Enable Password Hash Sync (PHS)
passages.headingText: 6.8.3 ADFS to Entra ID Migration: Cloud-First Approach

passages.text: Begin syncing password hashes to Entra ID as a fallback authentication method
passages.headingText: 6.8.3 ADFS to Entra ID Migration: Cloud-First Approach

passages.text: Configure Pass-Through Authentication (optional)
passages.headingText: 6.8.3 ADFS to Entra ID Migration: Cloud-First Approach

passages.text: Deploy PTA agents for organisations requiring on-premises password validation
passages.headingText: 6.8.3 ADFS to Entra ID Migration: Cloud-First Approach

passages.text: Configure Entra ID Seamless SSO for domain-joined devices (Kerberos-based auto-sign-in)
passages.headingText: 6.8.3 ADFS to Entra ID Migration: Cloud-First Approach

passages.text: Create a security group for pilot users; convert their domain from federated to managed authentication using Set-MsolDomainAuthentication
passages.headingText: 6.8.3 ADFS to Entra ID Migration: Cloud-First Approach

passages.text: After pilot validation, convert all user domains from federated to managed authentication
passages.headingText: 6.8.3 ADFS to Entra ID Migration: Cloud-First Approach

passages.text: Remove ADFS federation trust from Entra ID; decommission ADFS servers
passages.headingText: 6.8.3 ADFS to Entra ID Migration: Cloud-First Approach

passages.text: Application authentication migration (per application):
passages.headingText: 6.8.3 ADFS to Entra ID Migration: Cloud-First Approach

passages.text: Create an App Registration in Entra ID (or Application Group in ADFS) for OIDC
passages.headingText: 6.8.3 ADFS to Entra ID Migration: Cloud-First Approach

passages.text: Replace WS-Fed middleware with OIDC middleware; update claim type references
passages.headingText: 6.8.3 ADFS to Entra ID Migration: Cloud-First Approach

passages.text: Verify SSO, claims mapping, logout behaviour, and error handling
passages.headingText: 6.8.3 ADFS to Entra ID Migration: Cloud-First Approach

passages.text: Update the application's authentication configuration to use the OIDC endpoint
passages.headingText: 6.8.3 ADFS to Entra ID Migration: Cloud-First Approach

passages.text: Delete the old Relying Party Trust from ADFS (if still used) or Enterprise Application from Entra ID
passages.headingText: 6.8.3 ADFS to Entra ID Migration: Cloud-First Approach

passages.text: One of the most challenging aspects of migration is mapping claim types between protocols. WS-Federation (via ADFS) uses long-form URIs, SAML 2.0 uses short-form attribute names with namespace prefixes, and OIDC uses compact JWT claim names:
passages.headingText: 6.8.4 Claims Mapping: ADFS to SAML to OIDC

passages.text: urn:oid:1.3.6.1.4.1.5923.1.1.1.6 (eduPersonPrincipalName) or custom
passages.headingText: 6.8.4 Claims Mapping: ADFS to SAML to OIDC

passages.text: upn (Entra ID) / preferred_username
passages.headingText: 6.8.4 Claims Mapping: ADFS to SAML to OIDC
6.8.4 Claims Mapping: ADFS to SAML to OIDC

passages.text: urn:oid:2.5.4.42 (givenName)
passages.headingText: 6.8.4 Claims Mapping: ADFS to SAML to OIDC

passages.text: Custom attribute or urn:oid:1.3.6.1.4.1.5923.1.1.1.1 (eduPersonAffiliation)
passages.headingText: 6.8.4 Claims Mapping: ADFS to SAML to OIDC

passages.text: Custom attribute (binary SID)
passages.headingText: 6.8.4 Claims Mapping: ADFS to SAML to OIDC

passages.text: groups (Entra ID — object IDs)
passages.headingText: 6.8.4 Claims Mapping: ADFS to SAML to OIDC

passages.text: .../claims/authenticationinstant
passages.headingText: 6.8.4 Claims Mapping: ADFS to SAML to OIDC

passages.text: Identity provider that authenticated the user
passages.headingText: 6.8.4 Claims Mapping: ADFS to SAML to OIDC

passages.text: Migration note for .NET applications: ADFS claims use the ClaimTypes class constants (e.g., ClaimTypes.Upn, ClaimTypes.Email, ClaimTypes.Name), which map to long-form URIs. OIDC claims in ASP.NET Core use the JwtClaimTypes class (e.g., JwtClaimTypes.Subject, JwtClaimTypes.Email, JwtClaimTypes.Name). A claim mapping layer must be added during migration to translate between the two vocabularies.
passages.headingText: 6.8.4 Claims Mapping: ADFS to SAML to OIDC

passages.text: Use this decision matrix to determine the optimal migration strategy for a WS-Federation deployment:
No legacy constraint; OIDC is the modern standard with better mobile/API support
Existing .NET Framework app, low risk tolerance
WS-Fed → OIDC (dual-protocol coexistence)
Lowest risk; both protocols run simultaneously on ADFS until migration is verified
Existing .NET Framework app, must move fast
WS-Fed → OIDC (direct, with gateway proxy)
Protocol translation proxy (e.g., Entra Application Proxy) allows OIDC client communication without modifying the app
WS-Fed → SAML (maintain ADFS)
SharePoint requires SAML or WS-Fed; migrate to SAML first, then evaluate SharePoint-to-cloud migration separately
Multiple applications, phased rollout
WS-Fed → OIDC (application-by-application)
Each app migrates independently; ADFS/Entra ID supports both protocols concurrently
ADFS → Entra ID (cloud migration)
No new on-premises ADFS; migrate users to PHS/PTA and apps to Entra ID OIDC
Regulatory requirement for on-premises IdP
Retain ADFS, migrate apps to OIDC
Keep ADFS as the on-premises STS but switch application protocol from WS-Fed to OIDC
Retain SAML 2.0 for federation, OIDC for internal apps
SAML remains the dominant B2B federation protocol; use OIDC only for internal applications
Users may experience re-authentication when navigating between migrated (OIDC) and non-migrated (WS-Fed) apps
Ensure the IdP maintains a unified SSO session across protocols (ADFS and Entra ID do this natively)
Applications expect ADFS claim URIs but receive OIDC JWT claim names
Implement a claim mapping layer; test thoroughly with all expected claim values
FedAuth cookie (WS-Fed) and session cookie (OIDC) have different formats
Clear all cookies during migration; expect one-time re-authentication per user
WS-Fed front-channel logout differs from OIDC back-channel logout
Test logout across all application combinations; implement RP-Initiated Logout as a consistent fallback
RPs that cached federation metadata may not pick up certificate changes after migration
Enforce periodic metadata refresh; use certificate thumbprint validation rather than static certificate storage
Third-party integration breakage
API consumers, SaaS integrations, or middleware that expects WS-Fed tokens
Inventory all token consumers before migration; provide advance notice and transition period

§6's existing coverage focuses on protocol architecture and implementation. This section provides a dedicated security analysis covering XML Signature Wrapping (XSW) attacks, token replay, SOAP message header spoofing, and notable CVEs.

WS-Federation tokens are XML documents secured with XML Digital Signatures (XML DSig). The same class of XML Signature Wrapping attacks that affect SAML 2.0 (detailed in DR-0003 §2, Ch.2) apply to WS-Federation tokens with equal severity. The attacker intercepts a legitimate signed SAML assertion, modifies it by adding or moving XML elements outside the signed portion, and replays the modified assertion — because XML DSig signs specific elements by ID reference (not the entire document), the added elements pass validation.
The eight canonical XSW attack variants (as categorised by Somorovsky et al., 2012) are all applicable to WS-Federation:
Move <Signature> to sibling of <Assertion>
Signature references assertion by ID but is now outside it
Wrap <Assertion> in new root element
New root is unsigned; validator parses the wrong assertion
Duplicate <Assertion>, place signature on original
Attacker modifies the unsigned duplicate
Remove <Assertion> from signed <SignedInfo> reference
<Reference> URI changed; parser may accept unsigned assertion
Add unsigned sibling elements to signed assertion
Added elements contain attacker-controlled claims
Extend namespace declarations to include unsigned content
Namespace confusion allows unsigned elements to be treated as signed
Use XPath transform to exclude attacker's modifications
XPath filter excludes verification of injected elements
Comment-based wrapping to confuse XML parser
XML comments used to bypass canonicalisation
Use a validated XML-DSig library (e.g., Apache Santuario, .NET's SignedXml with security patches) — do not implement XML signature validation from scratch
Enforce Exclusive XML Canonicalisation (http://www.w3.org/2001/10/xml-exc-c14n#) to prevent namespace-based wrapping attacks
Validate assertion structure after signature verification — correct namespace, correct element hierarchy, no unexpected sibling elements
Verify the signing certificate against the expected STS's token-signing certificate, not an attacker-controlled certificate
Enforce strict XML parsing — disable DTD processing and external entity expansion to prevent XXE attacks

Token replay — WS-Federation tokens are bearer tokens with no protocol-level nonce or replay detection mechanism. An attacker who intercepts a valid token (via network sniffing on HTTP, XSS, or log exposure) can replay it against the RP within the token's validity window (typically 1 hour).
passages.headingText: 6.9.2 Token Replay and Audience Restriction Bypass

passages.text: TLS everywhere — the wresult POST from the STS to the RP must use HTTPS
passages.headingText: 6.9.2 Token Replay and Audience Restriction Bypass

passages.text: Short token lifetimes — reduce the default 60-minute lifetime to 5–15 minutes for high-security applications
passages.headingText: 6.9.2 Token Replay and Audience Restriction Bypass

passages.text: Replay detection cache — the RP can maintain a cache of recently seen assertion IDs and reject duplicates. ADFS supports this via the detectReplayedTokens configuration
passages.headingText: 6.9.2 Token Replay and Audience Restriction Bypass

passages.text: SameSite cookie policy — set SameSite=Strict on the FedAuth cookie to prevent cross-site request forgery
passages.headingText: 6.9.2 Token Replay and Audience Restriction Bypass

passages.text: Audience restriction bypass — the <AudienceRestrictionCondition> limits the token to a specific RP. If an RP fails to validate the audience restriction (a common implementation bug), the token can be replayed against a different RP, granting access based on claims intended for another application.
passages.headingText: 6.9.2 Token Replay and Audience Restriction Bypass

passages.text: ✅ Vulnerable (all 8 XSW variants)
✅ Vulnerable (all 8 XSW variants)
❌ Not supported (wreq is unsigned)
✅ Supported (AuthnRequest signing)
SAML provides request integrity
✅ <InResponseTo> + AssertionID caching
SAML links response to specific request
WS-Fed requests can be tampered (mitigated by HTTPS)
✅ Token by reference (no token in browser)
Artifact reduces token exposure
Disable DTD processing in all XML parsers
Both rely on metadata for certificate distribution
ADFS token spoofing — attacker could forge a token by manipulating claim values if ADFS has misconfigured encryption
ADFS privilege escalation — race condition in the claims pipeline
ADFS security feature bypass — bypasses EAP protections
Exploits ADFS's lack of replay detection by default — tokens captured from one RP can be replayed against another
Adversary extracts the token-signing private key and forges arbitrary SAML tokens for any RP — total identity compromise

Golden SAML (McDermott et al., 2020) deserves special attention. If an attacker compromises the ADFS server, they can extract the token-signing certificate's private key. With this key, the attacker can forge valid SAML assertions for any Relying Party Trust, impersonating any user, with any claims — undetectable by the RP because the forged token has a valid signature from the legitimate STS certificate. Mitigations include: strict network segmentation and endpoint detection on ADFS servers, storing token-signing private keys in an HSM, monitoring ADFS audit logs for configuration changes and token issuance anomalies, and implementing claim-based anomaly detection at RPs (e.g., detecting impossible travel or unusual privilege escalation).
Despite Microsoft's deprecation signals and the industry's shift toward OIDC, WS-Federation remains in active production use:
SharePoint Server 2016/2019/SE requires WS-Federation or SAML for user authentication with ADFS. Microsoft does not support OIDC for on-premises SharePoint
High — thousands of farms globally
Exchange hybrid deployments (on-premises + Online) use ADFS with WS-Federation for Autodiscover and OAuth integration
Legacy applications using WIF or OWIN WS-Federation middleware cannot migrate to OIDC without code changes
Organisations using ADFS to accept tokens from partner IdPs and re-issue for local RPs have invested heavily in claims pipeline rules
WCF-based microservices using WS-Trust active profile for service-to-service authentication
Some regulated industries have approved architectures that reference WS-Federation and require change management approval for new protocols
A key strength of ADFS is its multi-protocol support — a single farm can simultaneously issue tokens for WS-Federation, SAML 2.0, and OIDC clients:
SAML 1.1 (default), SAML 2.0, JWT
Application Group (Native/Server App)
JWT (ID Token + Access Token)
Application Group (Server App)

ADFS maintains a unified SSO session for the user regardless of which protocol initiated authentication. If a user authenticates via an OIDC application, subsequent sign-in requests from a WS-Federation application are satisfied without re-authentication (and vice versa). Entra ID similarly supports WS-Federation, SAML 2.0, and OIDC for different Enterprise Applications with a unified SSO session.
✅ Access tokens (JWT/opaque)
Government/academic federation
✅ Standard (InCommon, eduGAIN)
WS-Federation will not be "turned off" — Microsoft has committed to maintaining backward compatibility in ADFS and Entra ID for the foreseeable future. However, the protocol's trajectory is clear:
No new feature investment — Microsoft is not adding new capabilities (no back-channel logout, no PKCE, no native mobile support, no JSON-based tooling)
No .NET Core / .NET 5+ support — the WS-Federation OWIN middleware has no ASP.NET Core equivalent; new .NET applications cannot use WS-Federation without third-party libraries
Ecosystem erosion — identity vendors beyond Microsoft (Okta, PingIdentity, Forgerock) have deprioritised or removed WS-Federation support; SAML-to-OIDC migration tools dominate vendor documentation
Security concern accumulation — as WS-Federation ages, it accumulates unpatched weaknesses (XSW, no request signing, no replay detection) that modern protocols address natively

Organisations should treat WS-Federation as a legacy protocol with a managed sunset: maintain existing deployments, plan migration for all applications where OIDC is viable, and accept that a small subset (SharePoint on-premises, WCF services) may require indefinite WS-Federation support.
WS-Federation is a legacy protocol — no new applications should be designed to use it. However, it remains actively deployed in enterprise environments for concrete technical reasons:

SharePoint Server — the largest single driver of WS-Federation persistence. Thousands of on-premises SharePoint farms rely on WS-Federation with ADFS for claims-based authentication. Migration to SharePoint Online or SharePoint SE with OIDC is underway but slow

Windows Identity Foundation (WIF) applications — .NET Framework 4.x applications built with WIF's FederatedAuthentication module are deeply coupled to WS-Federation. These applications are in maintenance-only mode — rewriting them to use OIDC is a non-trivial development effort

ADFS infrastructure investment — organisations that have invested in multi-farm ADFS deployments with complex claims rules pipelines, custom authentication methods, and device registration services. Migrating to Entra ID or another cloud IdP requires replicating this logic in the new platform

Hybrid identity topologies — environments where ADFS serves as a federation hub, accepting tokens from multiple upstream Claims Provider Trusts (partner IdPs) and re-issuing tokens for downstream RPs. The WS-Trust/WS-Federation STS chaining model is difficult to replicate in pure OIDC

Compliance inertia — authentication protocol changes in regulated environments require formal change management, security assessment, and often external audit. The protocol works, is well-understood, and introduces no new attack surface — the risk of maintaining it is lower than the risk of migrating it

The strategic direction is clear: OIDC replaces WS-Federation for all new deployments, with SAML 2.0 as the alternative for enterprise IdP integrations that do not yet support OIDC. WS-Federation enters end-of-life through natural attrition as SharePoint farms migrate to SharePoint Online, .NET Framework applications are replaced by .NET 6+ services, and ADFS deployments are decommissioned in favour of Entra ID.

OpenID Federation 1.0 (OpenID Foundation, February 2026) replaces the ad-hoc bilateral trust relationships of traditional OIDC deployments with a structured, cryptographically verifiable trust infrastructure. Where conventional OIDC requires every relying party (RP) to be pre-registered with every OpenID provider (OP) — a O(n2)O(n^2)O(n2) configuration problem — federation enables automatic trust establishment through hierarchical trust chains, metadata policies, and accreditation tokens called trust marks. The specification is already in production across education federations (eduGAIN, SWAMID, InCommon) and is a required component of several national eIDAS implementations and the EU digital identity infrastructure.

Bilateral OIDC trust does not scale. In a traditional deployment, each RP maintains a static client registration with every OP it trusts: redirect URIs, allowed grant types, signing algorithms, token lifetimes, and subject identifier policies are all configured out-of-band, typically by copying values from an OP's discovery document into an admin console. This pairwise model works for a handful of relationships but becomes operationally unmanageable at federated scale — an organisation participating in eduGAIN, for example, may need to trust hundreds of OPs across dozens of countries, each with slightly different metadata requirements.

SAML 2.0 addressed the same problem decades earlier through metadata aggregation (SAML Metadata Profile for Algorithm Support, RFC 7644; and the SAML Metadata Profile for Entity Categories). A SAML federation operator publishes a signed metadata aggregate containing every participant's EntityDescriptor, and SPs and IdPs download this aggregate to populate their trust stores. While this works, it suffers from well-known limitations:

Monolithic metadata files. A single XML document containing thousands of entities — eduGAIN's aggregate exceeds 100 MB — must be downloaded, parsed, and validated by every participant. Metadata refresh cycles (typically 6–24 hours) introduce lag: a revoked key or a deactivated entity persists in participant caches until the next refresh window.

No fine-grained policy enforcement. SAML metadata supports entity categories (e.g., REFEDS-MFA, REFEDS-COMPLETE) but lacks a mechanism for hierarchical policy constraints — a federation operator cannot express "all sub-entities MUST use http://macedir.org/entity-category-support AND restrict signing algorithms to RSA-PSS-SHA256."

No per-entity accreditation. Trust in SAML federations is binary: an entity is either in the metadata aggregate or it is not. There is no standardised mechanism for a third-party accreditation authority to attest that a specific entity conforms to a security profile (e.g., FAPI 2.0) independently of the federation operator's inclusion decision.

OpenID Federation 1.0 was designed to address these limitations while preserving the hierarchical trust model that makes federations workable. The key innovations over SAML metadata and bilateral OIDC are:
Out-of-band client registration
Per-entity, on-demand resolution
Policy operators with hierarchical resolution
Entity categories (operator-controlled)
Trust marks (third-party, delegable)
Immediate via entity statement update
O(n)O(n)O(n) (single aggregate)
O(n)O(n)O(n) (per-entity, on-demand)
Near-instant (next resolution)

The specification was published by the OpenID Foundation on 17 February 2026 as a Final specification, alongside two companion documents: OpenID Federation 1.1 (a protocol-independent refactoring) and OpenID Federation for OpenID Connect 1.1 (protocol-specific bindings for OIDC 1.1). The federation model draws on decades of PKI/X.509 experience — trust anchors, intermediate authorities, certificate chains — but applies these concepts to OAuth 2.0/OIDC metadata rather than to X.509 certificates directly.
OpenID Federation 1.0 organises entities into a hierarchical trust model that mirrors the X.509 PKI architecture. Three entity roles exist within a federation:

The root of a federation's trust hierarchy. Signs entity statements for its immediate subordinates and publishes its own entity configuration. Operated by a federation operator (e.g., GEANT for eduGAIN).

An entity that is both a subordinate of a higher authority and an authority for its own subordinates. Common in multi-level federations (e.g., a national federation operator that is itself subordinate to a cross-federation trust anchor).
An endpoint entity — an OP, RP, or resource server — that is a subordinate of an authority but does not sign entity statements for other entities.
Comparison with PKI/X.509 certificate chains. The federation model is intentionally analogous to X.509 PKI, but with important differences:
X.509 certificate (signed by issuer CA)
Entity statement JWT (signed by issuing authority)
Leaf certificate → intermediate CA → root CA
Entity statement → … → trust anchor entity statement
Entity statement expiry or removal from authority
Certificate Policies extension, Extended Key Usage
Metadata policies with operators (value, default, subset_of, etc.)
Qualified certificate, EV certificate
Trust marks (signed by accreditation authority)
Directory services, LDAP, HTTP
HTTPS federation endpoints (fetch, resolve)
RSA/ECDSA key pair in certificate
JWKS published in entity configuration

The critical distinction is that in X.509 PKI, the certificate itself is the trust artifact — it binds a public key to an identity and is signed by the issuing CA. In OpenID Federation, the entity statement is the trust artifact — it binds an entity's metadata (not just a public key, but its full operational configuration) to its position in the trust hierarchy. An entity statement is a signed JWT (JWS Compact Serialization) whose claims include the subordinate's entity identifier, its JWKS, its metadata, and any policy constraints imposed by the issuing authority.

Every entity in a federation publishes an entity configuration — a self-signed JWT that serves as the entity's cryptographic identity document. The entity configuration contains the entity's public keys (JWKS), its authority_hints (which entities are authorised to issue entity statements about it), its trust_anchor_hints (which trust anchors it expects to validate against), and its operational metadata. This is fetched from a well-known URL:

The trust chain for a leaf entity is constructed by resolving a sequence of entity statements from the leaf up to the trust anchor. Each statement in the chain is signed by the authority that issued it, and each signature is verified against the issuing authority's JWKS (obtained from that authority's own entity configuration). If every signature in the chain validates and the chain terminates at a trusted anchor, the leaf entity's metadata is considered trustworthy.

The entity configuration is the foundational artifact in OpenID Federation 1.0. It is a self-signed JWT (JWS Compact Serialization) published at the well-known URL /.well-known/openid-federation on each entity's web server. The entity configuration serves three purposes:
Cryptographic identity. The self-signed JWT proves possession of the private key corresponding to the entity's JWKS — analogous to a self-signed certificate.
Trust chain anchor. The authority_hints claim tells a resolver which entities are authorised to issue entity statements about this entity, enabling bottom-up chain construction.

Operational metadata. The metadata claim contains the entity's protocol-specific configuration — for an OP, this includes supported grant types, scopes, token endpoint authentication methods, and other OpenID Connect provider metadata.
Fetching an entity configuration:

The response body is a JWS Compact Serialization string — a signed JWT containing the entity's claims. The header's alg and kid identify the signing key, which must correspond to a key in the JWT's own jwks claim (self-referential integrity).
Entity configuration JWT structure (decoded payload):
Issuer — MUST equal the entity's identifier (self-signed). Matches the sub claim.
Subject — MUST equal the entity's identifier. Together with iss, identifies the entity whose configuration is being presented.
Audience — MUST equal the entity's identifier (self-referential). The configuration is addressed to itself.
Issued at — Unix timestamp of when the configuration was created.
Expiration — Unix timestamp after which the configuration is no longer valid. Short lifetimes (hours) limit the window for key compromise exploitation.
JSON Web Key Set — the entity's current signing keys. Must include the key used to sign this configuration (self-referential).
Authority hints — array of entity identifiers that are authorised to issue entity statements about this entity. Used for bottom-up trust chain construction.
Trust anchor hints — array of trust anchor identifiers that this entity expects to be validated against. Used for top-down discovery.

Protocol metadata — nested object keyed by metadata type. For OIDC: openid_provider (for OPs), openid_relying_party (for RPs), openid_resource_server (for RSs). Contains the standard OIDC Discovery metadata claims.
Self-imposed metadata policy — constraints that the entity imposes on its own metadata. Resolvers treat these as the entity's baseline policy.

The authority_hints claim is the mechanism that enables bottom-up chain construction. When a resolver wants to validate entity https://op.uni-a.edu, it fetches the entity's configuration, reads authority_hints to discover that https://fed-a.example.org is the issuing authority, and then fetches the entity statement issued by https://fed-a.example.org about https://op.uni-a.edu. This process repeats recursively until a trust anchor is reached.

The metadata_policy claim, when present in the entity configuration, allows an entity to declare constraints on its own metadata — for example, restricting the set of signing algorithms it will accept for incoming tokens. This self-imposed policy is the leaf node in the policy hierarchy: superior entities may further constrain the entity's metadata through their own entity statements, and the final effective metadata is the result of resolving all policies from the leaf up to the trust anchor.

Trust chain construction is the process by which a relying party verifies that an entity's metadata is trustworthy by tracing a sequence of cryptographically signed entity statements from the entity up to a shared trust anchor. The chain is analogous to an X.509 certificate chain but operates on metadata rather than on public key bindings.

Entity statements. An entity statement is a signed JWT issued by an authority about a subordinate entity. Its structure is identical to an entity configuration in format (JWS Compact Serialization, signed by the issuing authority's private key), but its claims differ:
The issuing authority's entity identifier
The subordinate entity's identifier (the entity being described)
The intended audience — typically the trust anchor or a specific verifier
Issued-at and expiration timestamps
The subordinate entity's public keys (as asserted by the authority)
The subordinate's authority hints (as asserted by the authority)
The subordinate's metadata (potentially constrained by the authority's policy)
Policy constraints imposed by the authority on the subordinate
Example entity statement — issued by the intermediate https://fed-a.example.org about the leaf https://op.uni-a.edu:

In this example, the intermediate authority imposes two policy constraints on the leaf OP: it restricts the accepted token endpoint authentication methods to a subset of private_key_jwt and tls_client_auth, and it mandates PKCE with S256 as the only challenge method.
Chain construction algorithm. The following pseudocode describes the process of constructing and validating a trust chain from a leaf entity to a trust anchor:

Signature verification at each hop. The critical security property of the trust chain is that every entity statement in the chain is signed by the authority that issued it, and that authority's public keys are verified through its own entity configuration (which is either self-signed, for the trust anchor, or verified through the next link in the chain). This creates a transitive trust relationship:
The verifier trusts the trust anchor (configured out-of-band, like a root CA certificate).
The trust anchor's entity configuration is self-signed → verified by the verifier using the pre-configured trust anchor public keys.
The intermediate's entity statement (about the leaf) is signed by the intermediate → verified using the intermediate's entity configuration keys.
The intermediate's entity configuration is self-signed → verified by the verifier using the keys from step 3.
The intermediate's entity statement (about itself) is signed by the trust anchor → verified using the trust anchor's keys from step 2.

Wait — step 5 reveals an important nuance. The intermediate is itself a subordinate of the trust anchor, so its own metadata is described by an entity statement issued by the trust anchor. The complete chain for a leaf entity through one intermediate is:

Each link in the chain is verified against the next entity's public keys, creating a chain of trust that terminates at the verifier's pre-configured trust anchor. If any signature in the chain fails verification, or if any entity statement has expired, the entire chain is rejected.

Max path length. Federation operators MAY configure a maximum trust chain length to prevent excessively deep hierarchies. The OpenID Federation 1.0 specification recommends a default maximum of 5 hops (trust anchor + 4 intermediates) but does not mandate it — individual federation policies may set stricter limits.

Metadata policies are the mechanism by which superior entities in the federation hierarchy enforce constraints on their subordinates' metadata. A federation operator, for example, may require that all OPs in its federation support private_key_jwt for token endpoint authentication, or that all RPs use pairwise subject identifiers. Rather than relying on each entity to configure these constraints correctly, the federation operator encodes them as policy operators in the entity statements it issues.

Policy operators. OpenID Federation 1.0 defines seven policy operators that control how metadata values are resolved when multiple policies in the chain specify conflicting or overlapping constraints:
Replace the metadata value entirely. This is the most restrictive operator — it overrides any value specified by the entity or lower-level authorities.
"id_token_signing_alg_values_supported": { "value": ["PS256"] }
Provide a fallback if the entity does not specify a value. If the entity already has a value for this metadata key, the default is ignored.
"token_endpoint_auth_method": { "default": "private_key_jwt" }
Constrain the entity's value to be a subset of the specified set. The entity may use any subset of the listed values, but no values outside the set.
"grant_types_supported": { "subset_of": ["authorization_code", "client_credentials"] }
Require the entity's value to include at least the specified values. The entity may support additional values beyond the listed ones.
"scopes_supported": { "superset_of": ["openid"] }
Restrict the entity's value to exactly one of the specified options. Used for single-valued metadata keys.
"token_endpoint_auth_method": { "one_of": ["private_key_jwt", "tls_client_auth"] }
Flag a metadata key as required. The entity MUST provide a value for this key; if combined with other operators, the essential flag makes the constraint mandatory.
"code_challenge_methods_supported": { "essential": true, "value": ["S256"] }
Add values to the entity's existing set. Used to inject additional values (e.g., required scopes) without removing the entity's own values.
"scopes_supported": { "add": ["profile", "email"] }

Policy hierarchy and resolution. Metadata policies can be specified at multiple levels in the trust chain — the entity's own configuration, and each authority's entity statement about its subordinates. Resolution proceeds from the top of the chain (trust anchor) down to the leaf (entity configuration), applying each policy operator in order:
Start with the entity's raw metadata (from its entity configuration).
Apply the trust anchor's policy (from the trust anchor's entity statement about the intermediate).
Apply the intermediate's policy (from the intermediate's entity statement about the leaf).
Apply the entity's own self-imposed policy (from its entity configuration's metadata_policy).
The result is the effective metadata — the metadata that the entity will actually use.
Resolution example. Consider a leaf OP with the following metadata and policy chain:
Start with leaf's raw metadata: token_endpoint_auth_methods_supported = ["private_key_jwt", "client_secret_jwt"]

Trust anchor applies subset_of: the intermediate's effective set must be ⊆ ["private_key_jwt", "tls_client_auth", "client_secret_jwt"] — this passes (the intermediate's own metadata already satisfies this).

Intermediate applies subset_of: the leaf's ["private_key_jwt", "client_secret_jwt"] must be ⊆ ["private_key_jwt", "tls_client_auth"] — this fails because client_secret_jwt is not in the allowed set. The effective value becomes ["private_key_jwt"] (the intersection).
Intermediate applies essential + value: code_challenge_methods_supported must be exactly ["S256"] — the leaf already specifies ["S256"], so this passes.
Leaf self-policy applies value: id_token_signing_alg_values_supported is overwritten to ["PS256"].

Trust marks are signed JWTs that attest to an entity's conformance to a specific security profile, accreditation standard, or operational requirement. They are the federation equivalent of X.509 extended key usage or certificate policies — but issued by independent accreditation authorities rather than the trust anchor itself.

trust_mark_id: A URI identifying the trust mark type. Standardized trust mark IDs include https://refeds.org/sirtfi (Sirtfi — Security Incident Response Trust Framework for Federated Identity), https://refeds.org/mfa, and organization-specific marks.

iss: The entity issuing the trust mark — typically an accreditation authority, not the trust anchor. This separation of concerns allows specialized bodies to certify conformance to specific profiles without the trust anchor needing to evaluate every entity individually.
sub: The entity that the trust mark applies to.
mark: Additional metadata about the trust mark (logo, specification reference, version).

Delegation model. A trust anchor may delegate the authority to issue trust marks to one or more accreditation authorities. This is expressed in the trust anchor's entity configuration or in a subordinate's entity statement. When an OP receives a trust chain for an RP, it can verify that the trust mark issuer is authorized by checking the delegation chain.
Issuance. The accreditation authority evaluates the entity (typically through an audit, self-attestation, or automated compliance check) and issues a signed trust mark JWT.
Inclusion in entity statement. The trust mark is included in the superior's entity statement for the subordinate (see the trust_marks array in §7.4).

Verification. When another entity resolves the trust chain, it verifies the trust mark's signature using the accreditation authority's keys, checks the delegation chain, and confirms the trust mark has not expired or been revoked.
Revocation. Trust marks can be revoked by the issuing authority via a trust mark status endpoint (similar to OCSP in PKI). The OP can check the trust mark's status before accepting it.

Sirtfi compliance. The Research and Education FEDerations (REFEDS) Sirtfi framework requires federated entities to have a security incident response process. A Sirtfi trust mark signals that the entity meets this requirement.
FAPI 2.0 conformance. A financial-grade API profile trust mark indicates that an OP or RP implements FAPI 2.0 security requirements (PAR, JARM, MTLS, DPoP).
MFA assurance. A trust mark can indicate that an OP supports phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2/WebAuthn), enabling RPs to make access control decisions based on the OP's authentication strength.

OpenID Federation defines a set of REST endpoints that entities expose to support trust chain resolution and metadata discovery. These endpoints are hosted under the entity's federation URL, typically at a well-known path.
Retrieve an entity statement
Signed JWT (entity statement)
trust_anchor_id (optional filter)
Signed JWT (array of entity IDs)
Fetch endpoint (GET /federation/fetch?sub=...):

Returns the entity statement that this entity has issued about the specified subordinate. If this entity has not issued any statement about the requested subordinate, the endpoint returns an error (typically a JWT-signed error response).
List endpoint (GET /federation/list):
Returns a list of all entity IDs for which this entity has issued entity statements. Optionally filtered by trust anchor ID. The response is a signed JWT containing a JSON array.
Resolve endpoint (GET /federation/resolve?sub=...&trust_anchor_id=...):

Performs trust chain resolution on behalf of the requester. This is a convenience endpoint — instead of the requester manually walking the chain, the entity resolves it and returns the complete chain as a signed JWT. Not all entities implement this endpoint; it is optional.
Trust mark status endpoint (GET /federation/trust_mark_status):

Returns the current status of a trust mark (active, revoked, suspended). Similar in concept to OCSP stapling — the requesting entity can check whether a trust mark is still valid without contacting the issuing authority directly.
Historical keys endpoint (GET /federation/historical_keys):

When an entity rotates its signing keys, the old keys must remain available for a transition period so that previously issued entity statements and trust marks can still be verified. This endpoint provides access to the entity's previous JWKS. The specification recommends retaining historical keys for at least the maximum lifetime of any trust chain that referenced them.
OpenID Federation supports two modes of client registration, both of which leverage the trust chain rather than manual configuration:

Automatic registration occurs during the OIDC authentication request itself. When an RP sends an authorization request to an OP, it includes its trust chain as a parameter (specifically, the RP's entity configuration and any intermediate entity statements). The OP verifies the chain, resolves metadata policies, and dynamically registers the RP based on the resolved metadata. No prior registration step is required.
RP constructs an authentication request with its entity configuration and trust chain.
RP sends the request to the OP's authorization endpoint.
OP verifies the RP's trust chain (entity config → intermediate statements → trust anchor).
OP resolves metadata policies through the chain.
OP validates the request against the resolved RP metadata (redirect URI, response type, etc.).
If valid, the OP treats the RP as a registered client and proceeds with authentication.

This is the "zero-registration" model: the RP never calls a registration endpoint. The trust chain is the registration. The main advantage is simplicity — the RP only needs to publish its entity configuration and ensure its superior has issued an entity statement for it.

Explicit registration uses the federation's registration endpoint. The RP sends its entity configuration and trust chain to the OP's federation registration endpoint (similar to OIDC Dynamic Client Registration, RFC 7591, but with federation metadata instead of manual client metadata). The OP verifies the chain, resolves policies, and returns a signed registration response.
The explicit registration flow:
RP sends POST /federation/registration with its entity configuration and trust chain.
OP verifies the trust chain and resolves metadata policies.
OP returns a signed registration response containing the resolved RP metadata, client_id, and any OP-specific parameters.
The RP uses the registered metadata (particularly the client_id) in subsequent authentication requests.
Explicit registration is used when:
The OP needs to assign a client_id that differs from the RP's entity identifier.
The RP and OP need to negotiate additional parameters not expressible in federation metadata.
The RP requires a registration access token for future metadata updates.
OpenID Federation supports multiple discovery patterns, depending on the deployment topology and the entities' prior knowledge of each other.

Bottom-up resolution (the most common pattern) starts from the leaf entity and walks up the chain to the trust anchor. This is the algorithm described in §7.4: fetch the leaf's entity configuration, read its authority_hints, fetch the superior's entity statement, and repeat until the trust anchor is reached. This pattern is used when the verifier knows the leaf entity's identifier but does not know which trust anchor(s) it belongs to.

Top-down discovery starts from the trust anchor and walks down to the leaf entity. The verifier calls the trust anchor's list endpoint to enumerate all subordinate entity IDs, then fetches individual entity statements for the entities of interest. This pattern is useful for federation browsers, monitoring tools, or OPs that want to discover all RPs in their federation.

Single-point-of-trust resolution is used when both entities share exactly one trust anchor. The verifier fetches the leaf's entity configuration, reads trust_anchor_hints to identify the common anchor, and resolves the chain directly. This is the simplest pattern and is appropriate for closed federations where all entities belong to a single trust anchor.

Cross-federation resolution (bridging) occurs when two entities belong to different federations that share a trust relationship. For example, SWAMID (Sweden) and DFN-AAI (Germany) might both trust a higher-level European anchor (GEANT). An RP in SWAMID can resolve its chain to the GEANT anchor, and an OP in DFN-AAI can resolve its chain to the same GEANT anchor. Both entities verify that their chains converge on a common trust anchor, establishing cross-federation trust. This is analogous to cross-certification in PKI.
Practical deployment patterns in eduGAIN:

The eduGAIN federation connects over 60 national research and education federations. Each national federation operates as a trust anchor for its member institutions. A student at a Swedish university (SWAMID) accessing a resource at a German university (DFN-AAI) triggers cross-federation resolution:
The RP (German university) resolves the student's OP (Swedish university) through SWAMID to the GEANT anchor.
The RP verifies that GEANT is a trust anchor it also belongs to (through DFN-AAI).
Trust is established without any bilateral configuration between the two universities.
OpenID Federation introduces new attack surfaces beyond those of traditional OIDC. The trust chain mechanism, while powerful, requires careful implementation to avoid introducing vulnerabilities.

Malicious intermediate injection. An attacker who can convince a leaf entity to list a malicious entity in its authority_hints can redirect the trust chain through the attacker-controlled intermediate, which can then inject arbitrary metadata policies (e.g., weakening algorithm requirements). Mitigation: trust anchors should only issue entity statements for entities they have verified, and RPs should validate the complete chain rather than trusting individual statements in isolation.

Key compromise at any level. If an intermediate entity's signing key is compromised, the attacker can issue fraudulent entity statements for all subordinates beneath that intermediate. This is analogous to an intermediate CA key compromise in PKI. Mitigation: short key lifetimes, key rotation with overlap periods, and the historical keys endpoint for verification during transitions.

Metadata injection. A compromised intermediate can add or modify metadata in its entity statements for subordinates. For example, it could inject a malicious redirect_uri into an RP's metadata, enabling authorization code interception. Mitigation: metadata policies at the trust anchor level should restrict which metadata fields intermediates are allowed to override. RPs should also validate that their resolved metadata matches their expectations (e.g., verify that redirect_uris contains only URIs the RP actually controls).

Forged trust marks. An attacker could issue a self-signed trust mark claiming FAPI 2.0 conformance. Mitigation: OPs must verify the trust mark's signature against the issuing authority's keys, and verify that the issuing authority is delegated by a trusted entity in the chain.

Expired trust marks. An entity that was FAPI-compliant at trust mark issuance may have since changed its configuration. Mitigation: OPs should check trust mark status (via the status endpoint) before relying on a mark, rather than caching the mark indefinitely.
Comparison with bilateral OIDC trust security:
Requires OP compromise or client secret theft
Requires chain compromise at any level
Requires RP misconfiguration (wrong issuer)
Requires chain compromise or metadata injection
Requires admin console access
Requires intermediate key compromise
Manual reconfiguration on both sides
Immediate via entity statement update
Automatic via entity statement expiry/removal

The key takeaway: OpenID Federation's security is bounded by the weakest link in the trust chain. A single compromised intermediate undermines trust for all entities beneath it — exactly as in PKI. Federation operators must therefore apply stringent key management and monitoring at every level of the hierarchy.

OpenID Federation 1.0 is being adopted across several major federation ecosystems, driven by the operational limitations of SAML metadata aggregates and the desire for automated, policy-driven trust establishment.

Education federations (eduGAIN, SWAMID, InCommon): These were the earliest adopters, motivated by the O(n²) scaling problem of bilateral OIDC registration and the refresh latency of SAML metadata aggregates. SWAMID (Sweden) has published an OpenID Federation trust anchor and is migrating its member institutions from SAML metadata to federation trust chains. InCommon (United States) is running a parallel OpenID Federation infrastructure alongside its existing SAML aggregate. The GEANT Association (which operates eduGAIN) is developing a cross-federation bridge that allows national federations to interoperate through shared trust anchors.

eIDAS 2.0 implementations: The EU's eIDAS 2.0 regulation requires member states to establish cross-border digital identity wallets that can authenticate citizens across borders. OpenID Federation is the trust infrastructure for these wallets: each national wallet provider is an OP in a European federation, and each relying service is an RP. Trust marks are used to signal wallet conformance to the eIDAS 2.0 assurance levels (low, substantial, high). The European Commission has published a reference architecture that mandates OpenID Federation for cross-border trust establishment.

Microsoft Entra External ID: Microsoft's cloud identity platform supports OpenID Federation for B2B (business-to-business) collaboration scenarios. An organization can publish its entity configuration in a federation, allowing partner organizations to discover and trust it without manual configuration. Entra External ID implements the explicit registration flow (§7.8) and supports metadata policies for enforcing organizational security requirements (e.g., requiring all partner RPs to use conditional access).

Interoperability with SAML: Many deployments run OpenID Federation alongside existing SAML metadata. The federation bridge pattern — where an OpenID Federation trust anchor issues entity statements for SAML entities (and vice versa) — allows gradual migration. An entity can participate in both SAML and OIDC federations simultaneously, with the bridge translating trust assertions between the two protocols. This is particularly important for eduGAIN, where most institutions still rely on SAML but are gradually adopting OIDC.

This example demonstrates how policies cascade through the hierarchy: the trust anchor sets a broad constraint, the intermediate tightens it, and the leaf's own configuration may further restrict it. The resolver computes the intersection (or applies the appropriate operator) at each level, producing the effective metadata that the relying party uses for its runtime configuration.

The System for Cross-domain Identity Management (SCIM) 2.0 is the IETF standard for automated identity provisioning — creating, updating, deactivating, and querying user and group resources across domains. Where SAML and OIDC authenticate users at runtime, SCIM manages the lifecycle of those identities behind the scenes: when an employee joins an organization, SCIM automatically creates their accounts in Google Workspace, Salesforce, and Slack; when they leave, SCIM deactivates every account in a single operation. SCIM is defined by two core specifications: RFC 7643 (schema) and RFC 7644 (protocol).
passages.headingText: 8. SCIM 2.0: System for Cross-Domain Identity Management

passages.text: DR-0003 covers authentication protocols extensively but does not treat provisioning as a first-class topic. This chapter fills that gap, covering SCIM's architecture, data model, protocol operations, integration patterns with OIDC/SAML, and the security considerations that arise when provisioning and authentication intersect.
passages.headingText: 8. SCIM 2.0: System for Cross-Domain Identity Management

passages.text: Authentication systems are only as reliable as the identity data they consume. A user who has been terminated in the HR system but whose accounts remain active in SaaS applications represents a serious security risk — a ghost account that no longer has a legitimate purpose but still holds valid credentials. Conversely, a new hire who must wait days for manual account provisioning is an unproductive user and a candidate for shadow IT workarounds (personal email, unsanctioned SaaS).
passages.headingText: 8.1 Motivation: Why Provisioning Matters for Authentication

passages.text: SCIM solves this by providing a standardized, API-based provisioning protocol that connects identity providers (IdPs) to service providers (SPs):
passages.headingText: 8.1 Motivation: Why Provisioning Matters for Authentication

passages.text: Automated lifecycle management. Joiner-mover-leaver events in the IdP (e.g., Azure AD, Okta) are automatically propagated to every connected SP via SCIM API calls. No manual account creation or deactivation.
passages.headingText: 8.1 Motivation: Why Provisioning Matters for Authentication

passages.text: Real-time synchronization. Profile changes (name, email, phone number) and group membership changes are pushed to SPs within minutes, keeping identity data consistent across the enterprise.
passages.headingText: 8.1 Motivation: Why Provisioning Matters for Authentication

passages.text: De-provisioning as a security control. When an employee is deactivated in the IdP, SCIM deactivates their account across all connected SPs simultaneously, closing the window of vulnerability that manual deprovisioning leaves open.
passages.headingText: 8.1 Motivation: Why Provisioning Matters for Authentication

passages.text: SCIM 2.0 is supported by every major IdP (Azure AD/Entra ID, Okta, Google Workspace, OneLogin) and thousands of SaaS applications. The protocol's simplicity — REST-based, JSON payloads, Bearer token authentication — has made it the de facto standard for enterprise identity provisioning.
passages.headingText: 8.1 Motivation: Why Provisioning Matters for Authentication
