passages.text: SCIM 2.0 defines a resource-oriented data model built on JSON. The two primary resource types are User and Group, with a core schema and optional extensions.
passages.headingText: 8.2 Data Model: Users, Groups, and Enterprise Extensions

passages.text: User resource (core attributes):
passages.headingText: 8.2 Data Model: Users, Groups, and Enterprise Extensions

passages.text: id: System-generated unique identifier (opaque, immutable). This is the primary key for SCIM operations.
passages.headingText: 8.2 Data Model: Users, Groups, and Enterprise Extensions

passages.text: externalId: An identifier from the provisioning client (typically the IdP) that maps to this user. Used for correlation between the IdP's user store and the SP's user store.
passages.headingText: 8.2 Data Model: Users, Groups, and Enterprise Extensions

passages.text: userName: A unique, human-readable identifier (typically email). Must be unique within the SP's SCIM tenant.
passages.headingText: 8.2 Data Model: Users, Groups, and Enterprise Extensions

passages.text: active: Boolean flag. When false, the user is "soft-deactivated" — their account exists but they cannot authenticate. This is the standard deprovisioning mechanism: the IdP sets active: false rather than deleting the user resource (preserving audit trail and allowing reactivation).
passages.headingText: 8.2 Data Model: Users, Groups, and Enterprise Extensions

passages.text: meta: System metadata including creation/modification timestamps, ETag version (for concurrency control), and the canonical URL of the resource.
passages.headingText: 8.2 Data Model: Users, Groups, and Enterprise Extensions

passages.text: Groups are used to manage role-based access control. An IdP can push group membership changes (e.g., adding a user to the "Admins" group) via SCIM PATCH operations, and the SP translates those groups into application-specific roles.
passages.headingText: 8.2 Data Model: Users, Groups, and Enterprise Extensions

passages.text: Enterprise User Extension (RFC 7643, §4.3):
passages.headingText: 8.2 Data Model: Users, Groups, and Enterprise Extensions

passages.text: The urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:enterprise:2.0:User extension adds attributes common in enterprise deployments:
passages.headingText: 8.2 Data Model: Users, Groups, and Enterprise Extensions

passages.text: These enterprise attributes are critical for provisioning decisions: a SP might auto-assign premium licenses based on costCenter, or restrict access based on department. The extension model is pluggable — vendors can define their own extensions for application-specific attributes (e.g., Salesforce custom fields, ServiceNow assignment groups).
passages.headingText: 8.2 Data Model: Users, Groups, and Enterprise Extensions

passages.text: SCIM 2.0 (RFC 7644) defines a RESTful protocol for CRUD operations on User and Group resources. All operations are authenticated with a Bearer token (OAuth 2.0) issued by the SP to the provisioning client (IdP).
Create (POST /Users or POST /Groups):
If the user already exists (matched by userName), the SP returns 409 Conflict. Some implementations support "upsert" semantics via PATCH instead.
Read (GET /Users/{id} or GET /Users?filter=userName eq "john.doe@example.com"):

SCIM supports both direct lookup by id and filtered queries. The filter language (RFC 7644, §3.4.2) supports operators like eq, ne, co (contains), sw (starts with), ew (ends with), pr (present), gt, ge, lt, le, and logical operators and, or, not.
Responses are paginated with startIndex and count parameters.
Update (PUT /Users/{id} — full replace):
Replaces the entire user resource. The client must send the complete resource (all attributes). Partial updates use PATCH instead.
Patch (PATCH /Users/{id} — partial update):
The most commonly used operation in production. PATCH supports two operations:
replace: Set an attribute to a new value.
add: Add a value to a multi-valued attribute (e.g., add an email address).
remove: Remove an attribute or a specific value from a multi-valued attribute.
The op: replace on active: false is the standard deprovisioning operation. When the IdP detects a terminated employee, it sends this PATCH to every connected SP.
Delete (DELETE /Users/{id}):

Permanently deletes the user resource. Most enterprise deployments avoid DELETE in favor of PATCH active: false (soft delete) to preserve audit trails. DELETE is typically reserved for GDPR data erasure requests.

SCIM does not operate in isolation — it is tightly coupled with the authentication protocols that DR-0003 covers extensively. The relationship is symbiotic: authentication verifies identity at runtime; provisioning manages identity lifecycle behind the scenes.
passages.headingText: 8.4 Integration with OIDC and SAML Federation

passages.text: SCIM + OIDC (SaaS applications):
passages.headingText: 8.4 Integration with OIDC and SAML Federation

passages.text: The typical pattern for a SaaS application that supports both OIDC SSO and SCIM provisioning:
passages.headingText: 8.4 Integration with OIDC and SAML Federation

passages.text: Administrator configures OIDC federation. The SaaS app registers as an RP with the enterprise IdP (Azure AD, Okta). Users authenticate via the IdP.
passages.headingText: 8.4 Integration with OIDC and SAML Federation

passages.text: Administrator enables SCIM provisioning. The IdP generates a Bearer token for the SaaS app's SCIM endpoint. The token is stored in the IdP's configuration for that application.
passages.headingText: 8.4 Integration with OIDC and SAML Federation

passages.text: User lifecycle events flow automatically. When a new user is created in the IdP, the IdP sends a POST /Users to the SaaS app. The SaaS app creates a local user linked to the IdP's externalId. When the user authenticates via OIDC, the SaaS app matches the OIDC sub claim to the provisioned user.
passages.headingText: 8.4 Integration with OIDC and SAML Federation

passages.text: Attribute sync. When a user's profile changes (e.g., name change, department transfer), the IdP sends a PATCH /Users to the SaaS app. The updated attributes are reflected on the user's next login.
passages.headingText: 8.4 Integration with OIDC and SAML Federation

passages.text: Deprovisioning. When the user is deactivated in the IdP, the IdP sends PATCH active: false to the SaaS app. The user's SaaS account is immediately disabled, preventing further authentication.
passages.headingText: 8.4 Integration with OIDC and SAML Federation

passages.text: The security benefit is significant: without SCIM, a terminated employee's OIDC session would remain valid until the SaaS app's own session timeout (which could be hours or days). With SCIM, deactivation is near-instantaneous.
passages.headingText: 8.4 Integration with OIDC and SAML Federation

passages.text: SCIM + SAML (enterprise applications):
passages.headingText: 8.4 Integration with OIDC and SAML Federation

passages.text: Legacy enterprise applications often use SAML for SSO and SCIM for provisioning. The pattern is identical: the IdP provisions users via SCIM and authenticates them via SAML assertion. The SAML NameID claim is matched to the SCIM externalId to correlate the two.
passages.headingText: 8.4 Integration with OIDC and SAML Federation

passages.text: Because SCIM Bearer tokens grant the ability to create, modify, and deactivate user accounts, they are high-value targets:
passages.headingText: 8.4 Integration with OIDC and SAML Federation

passages.text: Token theft. A stolen SCIM Bearer token allows an attacker to create arbitrary accounts, escalate privileges (by adding themselves to admin groups), or deactivate legitimate users (denial of service). Tokens must be stored securely (not in configuration files committed to Git) and rotated regularly.
passages.headingText: 8.4 Integration with OIDC and SAML Federation

passages.text: No authorization scoping. SCIM 2.0 defines authentication (Bearer tokens) but does not define a fine-grained authorization model. A token that can create users can typically create any user, including admins. Some implementations add authorization layers (e.g., Okta's SCIM provisioning scopes), but this is not standardized.
passages.headingText: 8.4 Integration with OIDC and SAML Federation

passages.text: Injection via attribute manipulation. A compromised IdP (or a malicious SCIM client) can push malicious data via user attributes — for example, injecting XSS payloads into displayName, or setting active: true for a previously deactivated account to regain access.
passages.headingText: 8.4 Integration with OIDC and SAML Federation

passages.text: Short-lived tokens, mutual TLS, IP allowlisting
passages.headingText: 8.4 Integration with OIDC and SAML Federation

passages.text: Application-layer role checks on SCIM operations
passages.headingText: 8.4 Integration with OIDC and SAML Federation

passages.text: Input validation, output encoding, attribute allowlists
passages.headingText: 8.4 Integration with OIDC and SAML Federation

passages.text: Rate limiting, approval workflows for bulk operations
passages.headingText: 8.4 Integration with OIDC and SAML Federation

passages.text: Privilege escalation via group PATCH
passages.headingText: 8.4 Integration with OIDC and SAML Federation

passages.text: Separate admin provisioning flow, no SCIM group write
passages.headingText: 8.4 Integration with OIDC and SAML Federation

passages.text: Full sync (initial provisioning). When an enterprise first enables SCIM for an application, the IdP performs a full synchronization: it queries all existing users in the application via GET /Users (with pagination), compares them against its own user directory, and sends PATCH operations for any differences. This "reconciliation" step ensures that the SP's user store matches the IdP's.

Incremental push (ongoing sync). After the initial sync, the IdP pushes incremental changes as they occur. Most IdPs (Azure AD, Okta) maintain a provisioning queue and process changes with a near-real-time cadence (typically 5–40 minutes).

Event-driven (real-time sync). Some IdPs support webhook-based provisioning events that push changes within seconds. This is closer to real-time but requires the SP to implement an event-driven provisioning model (receiving SCIM PATCH calls triggered by IdP events rather than being polled).

Multi-tenant SCIM. SaaS vendors that serve multiple enterprise customers implement SCIM multi-tenancy by partitioning users by tenant (organization) ID. Each tenant's SCIM Bearer token is scoped to that tenant's user store. The SCIM base URL typically includes the tenant identifier: https://scim.saaS.example.com/v2/{tenantId}/Users.

Provisioning error handling. Real-world SCIM deployments must handle: transient failures (retry with exponential backoff), permanent failures (quarantine the user and alert the administrator), conflicts (409 on duplicate userName), and rate limiting (429 Too Many Requests). Most IdPs provide a provisioning dashboard that shows success/failure counts and allows administrators to retry failed operations.

SCIM 2.0 (RFC 7643/7644) was published in 2015 and has remained remarkably stable. However, several limitations have driven work on SCIM 2.1 (as of 2026, still in draft within the IETF SCIM working group):
Filter performance. The SCIM filter language is expressive but computationally expensive on large directories. SCIM 2.1 may introduce indexed filter attributes and query optimization hints.

Bulk operations. Large enterprises need to provision or deprovision thousands of users at once. SCIM 2.0 defines a /Bulk endpoint (RFC 7644, §3.17), but implementations vary widely. SCIM 2.1 aims to standardize bulk operation semantics and error handling.

Schema versioning. SCIM 2.0 has no mechanism for schema evolution. Adding a new attribute requires a new extension URI, which breaks backward compatibility. SCIM 2.1 may introduce schema version negotiation.

Event subscription. SPs currently have no way to notify the IdP of changes initiated within the SP (e.g., a user changes their password in the SP, and the IdP needs to know). A bidirectional event model would close this gap.

Despite these limitations, SCIM 2.0 remains the dominant provisioning protocol. Organizations considering implementation should start with RFC 7643/7644 and monitor the SCIM 2.1 drafts for backward-compatible improvements.
Delete (DELETE /Users/{id}):

Permanently deletes the user resource. Most enterprise deployments avoid DELETE in favor of PATCH active: false (soft delete) to preserve audit trails. DELETE is typically reserved for GDPR data erasure requests.

This group examines the evolution and vulnerability of credentials based on user memory. It traces password authentication through three generations of increasingly sophisticated cryptographic storage and breach-detection mechanisms, before detailing the taxonomy of passwordless methodologies designed to eliminate shared secrets entirely.

Password authentication — verifying identity by comparing a remembered secret against a stored reference — remains the most widely deployed authentication mechanism despite decades of effort to replace it. The history of password storage is a history of escalating failures: each generation of protection emerged in response to catastrophic real-world breaches that exposed the inadequacy of the previous approach. This chapter traces the evolution from plaintext storage through modern breach-intelligence-driven verification, providing protocol-level detail on the cryptographic primitives, encoded formats, and operational considerations that define each generation.

Passwords alone satisfy only AAL1 (§1.1) — NIST SP 800-63B classifies a memorised secret as a single-factor authenticator. No amount of hashing sophistication changes this classification; the assurance level is an inherent property of the factor type, not the storage mechanism. The techniques covered here determine how well the verifier protects credentials after authentication — a concern orthogonal to, but equally critical as, the assurance level.

The earliest computer systems stored passwords exactly as users typed them — plaintext strings in a file readable by the operating system. The original Unix /etc/passwd file (1971) is the canonical example: user credentials were stored as cleartext entries, accessible to any process with file-read permissions. The security model relied entirely on operating system access controls, which proved brittle in practice.
passages.headingText: 9.1 First Generation: Plaintext and Unsalted Hashes

passages.text: Plaintext password storage has a single, catastrophic failure mode: any breach of the storage system — SQL injection, backup theft, insider access, misconfigured cloud storage — exposes every credential simultaneously and irrecoverably. There is no computational barrier between the attacker and the passwords. The attacker does not need to crack anything; they simply read the passwords.
Despite being universally condemned, plaintext storage persisted far longer than security professionals expected. Notable examples include:

RockYou (2009) — 32 million passwords stored in plaintext in a SQL database, exposed via SQL injection. The breach provided researchers with the first large-scale empirical dataset of real-world password choices, revealing that 123456, 12345, and 123456789 were the three most common passwords
Adobe (2013) — discussed below in §9.1.3 as a case study in encryption-as-storage failure

Facebook (2019) — disclosed that hundreds of millions of Facebook, Instagram, and Facebook Lite passwords were stored in plaintext in internal log files, accessible to thousands of employees. Passwords were logged during authentication processing and retained indefinitely

The first improvement over plaintext was to store a one-way hash of the password instead of the password itself. During authentication, the system hashes the user's input and compares it to the stored hash — if they match, the password is correct, and the original password never needs to be stored.

The fundamental error of the first generation was using fast, general-purpose cryptographic hash functions — MD5 (128-bit, RFC 1321), SHA-1 (160-bit, RFC 3174), or SHA-256 (256-bit, FIPS 180-4) — for password hashing. These functions are designed to be computationally efficient: a modern GPU can compute billions of SHA-256 hashes per second. This speed, which is a desirable property for data integrity verification and digital signatures, is catastrophic for password storage because it makes brute-force and dictionary attacks trivially fast.
The speed problem in concrete terms:
Approximate GPU Rate (RTX 4090)
Time to Exhaust 8-Character Lowercase (26⁸ ≈ 209 billion)

These rates demonstrate that unsalted fast hashes provide no meaningful protection against offline attacks. An attacker with a stolen hash database and a consumer GPU can exhaustively search the entire space of common password patterns in minutes to hours.

Without salts, identical passwords produce identical hashes — enabling precomputed lookup attacks. A rainbow table is a space-efficient data structure that stores precomputed hash chains mapping hash values back to their plaintext inputs. The technique, formalised by Philippe Oechslin in 2003, uses a reduction function to alternately hash and reduce values through chains, trading computation time for storage space.

The attack is simple: the attacker precomputes a rainbow table for the target hash function (e.g., MD5) covering the expected password space (dictionary words, common patterns, character permutations). When a hash database is obtained, each hash is looked up in the table — if found, the corresponding plaintext is immediately revealed. Because the table is computed once and reused across every breach using the same hash function, the amortised cost per cracked password approaches zero.
Why rainbow tables devastate unsalted hashes:
Deterministic output — the same password always produces the same hash, so the table lookup always succeeds for any password within the precomputed space
Cross-breach applicability — a single rainbow table for "MD5 of English dictionary + common patterns" works against every MD5-hashed password database in existence
No per-target cost — unlike brute-force attacks, the attacker pays the computation cost once during table generation, not once per target database
Two landmark breaches illustrate the consequences of first-generation password storage:

LinkedIn (2012) — In June 2012, 6.5 million password hashes were posted to a Russian hacking forum. The full scope was later revealed to be 117 million accounts. LinkedIn had stored passwords as unsalted SHA-1 hashes. Security researchers cracked over 90% of the hashes within days using dictionary attacks and rainbow tables. The breach demonstrated that even a technology company with substantial engineering resources could fail at basic password storage hygiene. LinkedIn was fined €310,000 under the UK Data Protection Act (pre-GDPR) — a penalty that would be orders of magnitude larger under current regulations.

Adobe (2013) — In October 2013, attackers exfiltrated the credential database for 153 million Adobe accounts. Adobe had not used hashing at all — instead, passwords were encrypted (not hashed) using 3DES in ECB (Electronic Codebook) mode with a single, global encryption key. ECB mode encrypts each block independently without chaining, meaning identical plaintext blocks produce identical ciphertext blocks. Combined with the use of a single key for all users, this meant that all users with the same password had the same ciphertext. Security researchers were able to cross-reference the ciphertext patterns with the (unencrypted) password hint field to deduce plaintext passwords for millions of accounts without ever recovering the encryption key. The Adobe breach remains a textbook example of why encryption is not a substitute for hashing — encryption is reversible by design (with the key), while hashing is one-way.

RockYou (2009) — In December 2009, a SQL injection attack against the social gaming site RockYou exposed 32 million user passwords stored in plaintext — no hashing, no encryption. The breach was particularly significant not for its scale but for its research value: the leaked password database became the foundational dataset for password security analysis, revealing that 123456 (0.97%), 12345 (0.87%), and 123456789 (0.82%) were the three most common passwords, with the top password used by over 310,000 users. The RockYou dataset demonstrated conclusively that users choose predictably bad passwords when unconstrained by policy.

Collection #1 (2019) — In January 2019, security researcher Troy Hunt disclosed Collection #1, a aggregated breach corpus containing 773 million unique email addresses and 21 million unique passwords. Unlike the previous entries, this was not a single breach but an aggregation of credential pairs from thousands of individual breaches. The dump demonstrated the cumulative risk of password reuse: credentials compromised in a 2012 breach remained fully viable for credential stuffing attacks seven years later. Collection #1 accelerated adoption of breach-password screening — HIBP API usage surged following its disclosure.
Plaintext is never acceptable
Wrong primitive + poor key management
Password reuse + credential aggregation
Reuse is the root vulnerability

The second generation addresses both weaknesses of the first: it defeats rainbow tables through salting and defeats brute-force through adaptive cost functions that make each hash computation deliberately slow.

A salt is a unique, cryptographically random value generated for each password and stored alongside the hash. Before hashing, the password is combined with the salt: hash(salt || password) or hash(password || salt). Because each user's salt is different, two users with the same password produce different hashes — completely defeating rainbow table attacks.
Unique per user, per password change
Two users with the same password must produce different hashes
≥ 128 bits (16 bytes) recommended by NIST
Must be long enough to make precomputation infeasible — 2¹²⁸ possible salt values means an attacker would need 2¹²⁸ rainbow tables
Cryptographically secure random (e.g., /dev/urandom, CSPRNG)
Predictable salts (e.g., username, sequential counter) reduce entropy and enable targeted precomputation
Stored in cleartext alongside the hash
The salt is not secret — it only needs to be unique. Keeping it secret would provide no additional security against offline attacks since the attacker who has the hashes also has the salts

Salting alone is necessary but not sufficient — it defeats precomputation but does not slow down brute-force attacks against individual hashes. A fast hash function with a salt still permits billions of guesses per second per hash.

bcrypt (1999, Niels Provos and David Mazières) is a password hashing function based on the Blowfish block cipher. It was the first widely adopted algorithm designed specifically for password storage, incorporating both salting and an adjustable cost factor.
Generate a 128-bit (16-byte) random salt

Derive the Blowfish key schedule from the password and salt using the Eksblowfish (Expensive Key Schedule Blowfish) setup — this is the computationally expensive step, repeated 2{{cost}}2^\{\text\{cost\}\}2{{cost}} times. Each iteration re-derives the P-array and S-boxes from the password and salt, making the key schedule itself the expensive operation
Encrypt the constant string "OrpheanBeholderScryDoubt" (192 bits) using the derived key schedule, performing 64 rounds of Blowfish encryption
Encode the result in the Modular Crypt Format

Version history: $2$ (original), $2a$ (added null terminator handling), $2b$ (current — fixed unsigned char overflow in OpenBSD implementation). The $2y$ prefix is PHP-specific and functionally equivalent to $2b$.

Critical limitation — 72-byte password truncation: bcrypt truncates passwords to 72 bytes before hashing. Characters beyond byte 72 are silently ignored and do not contribute to the hash. This is a consequence of the Blowfish P-box structure (18 × 4-byte subkeys = 72 bytes). Multi-byte UTF-8 characters mean that 72 bytes may be fewer than 72 characters. Mitigation: pre-hash the password with SHA-256 before passing it to bcrypt, or enforce a 72-byte maximum password length.
Cost factor selection (2025 guidance):
Minimum acceptable for low-value systems
Recommended baseline for most applications
Suitable for high-value systems with low login volume
Maximum practical for interactive login
The cost factor should be calibrated to achieve 250–1,000 ms per hash on the target server hardware. Verify empirically — do not rely on published benchmarks, as hardware varies significantly.

PBKDF2 (Password-Based Key Derivation Function 2, RFC 8018) applies HMAC in a loop to derive a key from a password. Despite being NIST-approved and FIPS-140 validated, it is the weakest of the four common password hashing algorithms because it has no memory-hardness property — a GPU can compute millions of PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 evaluations per second because each evaluation needs only a few hundred bytes of state.
Where c = iteration count, dklen = desired key length, hlen = HMAC output length. Each block Ti requires c HMAC evaluations.
Cryptographically random bytes

When PBKDF2 is unavoidable: FIPS 140-2/FIPS 140-3 validated environments (US federal systems, financial services with regulatory mandates) may require PBKDF2 because bcrypt and Argon2 lack FIPS certification. If mandated, use SHA-512 as the HMAC hash (faster than SHA-256 on 64-bit systems) and maximise the iteration count within the latency budget.

scrypt (2009, Colin Percival, RFC 7914) extends the adaptive cost concept to include memory hardness — it is designed to require a large amount of memory in addition to CPU time, making it resistant to GPU and ASIC attacks where compute-per-watt is high but memory-per-chip is limited.
Derive an initial key from the password and salt using PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 (1 iteration)
Use the derived key to seed the ROMix function, which sequentially fills a large memory buffer with pseudorandom data, then performs memory-dependent lookups against that buffer
Derive the final output key via a second PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 pass
Number of iterations; determines memory requirement (memory ≈ 128 × N × r bytes)
Must be a power of 2; primary cost knob
Size of sequential memory reads (in 128-byte blocks)
Affects memory bandwidth requirements
Number of independent mixing functions
Allows parallelisation but increases memory proportionally

Memory requirement: 128 × N × r bytes per thread. With the recommended parameters N=2¹⁵ (32,768), r=8, p=1, memory consumption is 128 × 32,768 × 8 = 32 MB per hash computation — forcing an attacker to allocate 32 MB of RAM per parallel crack attempt, dramatically limiting GPU throughput.

Known weakness — time-memory tradeoff: scrypt's initial PBKDF2 pass uses only 1 iteration, and its ROMix construction has been shown to admit time-memory tradeoffs more favourable to attackers than Argon2's. The tradeoff reduces memory by a factor of kkk at a cost of O(k)O(k)O(k) additional time — worse than Argon2's O(k2)O(k^2)O(k2) tradeoff penalty. This means an attacker with limited GPU memory can partially compensate by investing more computation, making scrypt's memory-hardness less robust than Argon2id's at equivalent parameter levels.
Encoded hash format (libscrypt/OpenSSL):

Argon2 (2015, Alex Biryukov, Daniel Dinu, Dmitry Khovratovich) is the winner of the Password Hashing Competition (PHC, §9.4) and the current state-of-the-art password hashing algorithm. It provides tunable memory hardness, time hardness, and parallelism, and addresses weaknesses in both bcrypt and scrypt.
❌ Vulnerable — memory access indices depend on the password, enabling cache-timing attacks
✅ Maximised — data-dependent access patterns are difficult to optimise on GPUs
Cryptocurrency proof-of-work; not for password hashing in shared environments
✅ Resistant — memory access pattern is independent of the password
⚠️ Moderate — data-independent patterns are more predictable and partially exploitable via time-memory trade-offs
Environments where side-channel resistance is paramount (shared servers, cloud)
Hybrid — first pass uses Argon2i, subsequent passes use Argon2d
✅ First pass resists side-channel; subsequent passes maximise GPU resistance
✅ Combines both resistance properties
Recommended for all password hashing (OWASP, RFC 9106)
Number of iterations over memory
Length of the derived key in bytes
OWASP alternative configuration: m=46,080 KiB (46 MiB), t=1, p=1 — equivalent security level with a higher memory / lower iteration tradeoff.
Argon2id operation: The algorithm executes in three phases:

Initial hashing — compute H0={Blake2b}({password}∥{salt}∥t∥m∥p∥{version}∥{associated_data}∥{key_length})H_0 = \text\{Blake2b\}(\text\{password\} \|\text\{ salt\} \| t \| m \| p \| \text\{version\} \| \text\{associated\_data\} \| \text\{key\_length\})H0​={Blake2b}({password}∥{salt}∥t∥m∥p∥{version}∥{associated_data}∥{key_length}) to produce the first two 1 KiB blocks of the memory matrix

Memory filling — fill a memory matrix of mmm KiB blocks across ppp parallel lanes. Each block is computed using the Blake2b compression function applied to previously computed blocks, with indices determined by a variant of the H-address function
Finalization — compute the final hash by XORing selected blocks from the memory matrix and applying Blake2b to produce the output digest
Encoded hash format (PHC string format, RFC 9106 §6):
✅ 2{{cost}}2^\{\text\{cost\}\}2{{cost}} iterations
✅ Acceptable (high iterations)

Decision hierarchy for production systems: Argon2id → bcrypt (cost ≥ 12) → scrypt → PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA512. Use Argon2id for all new systems unless FIPS compliance is required. Use bcrypt if Argon2id is not available in the platform library. Use scrypt only if neither Argon2id nor bcrypt is available. Use PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA512 only if FIPS 140-2/3 validation is mandatory. Never use MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256, DES crypt, or any unsalted fast hash.
The following sequence diagram illustrates the complete password authentication lifecycle — from registration through login and optional hash upgrade:
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: 1. User Agent captures password during registration
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: The user inputs their desired username and plaintext password into the Client Application's sign-up form.
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: 2. Client Application transmits registration credentials
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: The Client Application POSTs the raw credentials over a secure TLS connection directly to the backend /register endpoint.
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: 3. Authentication Server validates password policy rules
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: The Authentication Server verifies the password fundamentally meets required structural length boundaries (e.g., NIST SP 800-63B standard mandates) and passes basic local blocklists.
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: 4. Authentication Server computes SHA-1 prefix
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: Before finalizing the password natively, the Authentication Server computes its full SHA-1 hash and extracts exclusively the first 5 hexadecimal characters.
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: 5. Authentication Server queries HIBP k-anonymity API
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: The Authentication Server initiates a GET request to the external Have I Been Pwned API, submitting only the 5-character prefix to rigorously maintain k-anonymity privacy parameters.
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: 6. HIBP returns hash suffix list
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: HIBP responds with a plain text list encompassing all breached password suffixes that legitimately share the submitted 5-character prefix, alongside their historical occurrence counts.
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: 7. Authentication Server checks for suffix matches
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: The Authentication Server locally checks if the remaining characters of its own computed SHA-1 hash appear anywhere in the securely downloaded suffix list.
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: Rejection Scenario: If a definitive match occurs, the Authentication Server immediately rejects the registration, forcing the user to select another secret, and emits a COMPROMISED_PASSWORD_DETECTED SIEM alert.
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: 8. Authentication Server generates random cryptographic salt
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: Proceeding securely, the Authentication Server explicitly calls the OS-level Cryptographically Secure Pseudorandom Number Generator (CSPRNG) to mint a fresh, unique 128-bit contextual salt sequence.
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: 9. Authentication Server computes Argon2id hash
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: The Authentication Server passes the plaintext secret, the CSPRNG salt, and the heavily tuned memory/time parameters (m=19456, t=2) actively into the Argon2id Key Derivation Function (RFC 9106).
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: 10. Authentication Server stores PHC string in Credential Store
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: The derivative output, rigidly formatted as a unified PHC structural string (encapsulating algo, params, salt, and digest), is definitively written into the persistent Credential Store.
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: Artifact Produced: $argon2id$v=19$m=19456,t=2,p=1$c2FsdHN0cmluZw$hashdigest... (PHC Hash String)
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: 11. Authentication Server returns HTTP 201 Created
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: The backend safely concludes the provisioning transaction and routes an HTTP 201 status code back downwards to the Client Application.
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: 12. Client Application displays registration success message
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: The frontend UI formally transitions to a success state, informing the user their account is completely provisioned and ready.
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: 13. User Agent submits credentials for login
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: Some time later, the user enters their username and plaintext password natively into the application's login screen.
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: 14. Client Application transmits login credentials securely
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: The Client Application POSTs the raw payload bound directly for the /auth/login endpoint traversing TLS.
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: 15. Authentication Server queries Credential Store for username
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: The core authentication controller queries the backend Credential Store utilizing the inbound username identity.
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: 16. Credential Store returns stored PHC string
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: The Credential Store retrieves and safely returns the full PHC record string committed during the original registration flow.
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: 17. Authentication Server parses PHC components
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: The Authentication Server parses out the structural $argon2id prefix boundary, cleanly extracts the exact discrete memory/time cost parameters, and splits out the unique local salt alongside the baseline secret digest.
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: 18. Authentication Server computes candidate Argon2id hash
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: Leveraging the purely extracted salt and embedded numerical parameters, the Authentication Server computationally executes the newly submitted plaintext login password identically through the Argon2id architectural algorithm to derive a candidate structural digest.
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: 19. Authentication Server performs constant-time hash comparison
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: The Authentication Server rigorously compares the dynamically derived candidate digest against the historically stored database digest utilizing a hardened constant-time memory comparison function (e.g., CRYPTO_memcmp) to mathematically defeat timing side-channel telemetry attacks.
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: 20. Authentication Server returns HTTP 200 OK (Success Branch)
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: If the cryptographic hashes explicitly match without deviation, the Authentication Server procedurally mints a brand-new session token (or signed JWT) and seamlessly returns it traversing an HTTP 200 OK wrapper.
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: Artifact Produced: session (HTTP Cookie) and access_token (JWT)
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: 21. Client Application displays login success message (Success Branch)
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: The Client Application intrinsically consumes the established session token and correctly redirects the user's interface comprehensively into the targeted native application view.
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: 22. Authentication Server returns HTTP 401 Unauthorized (Failure Branch)
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: If the candidate cryptographic hashes fail to converge precisely (or the requested username fundamentally does not exist), the Authentication Server securely returns a generic HTTP 401 error.
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: Security Audit: The Authentication Server immediately audits a INVALID_CREDENTIALS_REJECTED SIEM event indicating authentication failure without specifying if the error stemmed from the username or the password sequence.
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: 23. Client Application displays generic error message (Failure Branch)
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: The Client Application renders a visually ambiguous "Invalid username or password" notification interface natively engineered to violently deter bulk automated account enumeration vectors.
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: 24. Authentication Server detects legacy hash algorithm (Optional)
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: Running safely inside an asynchronous background or post-authorization trigger (executed strictly on the success branch identity confirmation), the Authentication Server passively notices the ingested user record relies externally upon a deprecated, obsolete structural format (e.g., bcrypt $2b$).
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: 25. Authentication Server computes upgraded Argon2id hash (Optional)
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: Actively capitalizing on having the plaintext secret safely residing inside hot memory immediately resulting from the successfully executed login operation, the Authentication Server securely generates a new unique CSPRNG salt sequence and derives a fresh Argon2id cryptographic hash string.
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: 26. Authentication Server updates stored database hash (Optional)
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: The Authentication Server forcibly overwrites the highly vulnerable deprecated database record explicitly with the tightly bound, newly configured Argon2id PHC schema string, finalizing an utterly transparent zero-downtime structural security upgrade directly attributed to that user account.
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: Security Audit: The Authentication Server subsequently logs a ZERO_DOWNTIME_HASH_UPGRADE event to natively track the rolling progression toward organizational crypto-schema structural alignment.
passages.headingText: 9.2.6 Password Authentication Flow with Hash Verification

passages.text: The third generation represents a paradigm shift in password security thinking — from "is this password strong enough?" to "has this password already been compromised?"

The insight driving this shift is empirical: credential stuffing attacks (§21.1) succeed because users reuse passwords across services. An attacker who obtains the plaintext credentials from Breach A doesn't need to crack anything — they simply try the same username/password pairs against Service B, Service C, and onward. In this threat model, password complexity is irrelevant; the only question that matters is whether the password appears in any known breach corpus.

NIST SP 800-63B Revision 4 (second public draft August 2024, final anticipated mid-2025) codifies the paradigm shift into normative guidance. The key requirements represent a fundamental departure from legacy password policies:
15 characters (single-factor); 8 characters (with MFA)
Longer passwords provide exponentially more entropy; eliminates most common passwords by length alone
Must support passphrase-length inputs; arbitrary limits frustrate users and reduce security
"Must contain uppercase, number, symbol" rules produce predictable patterns (P@ssword1); provide false confidence
Mandatory rotation drives users toward weak, predictable modifications (Password1 → Password2 → Password3)
SHALL check (strengthened language)
Passwords MUST be checked against a list of commonly used, expected, or compromised values; rejected if found
Passwords found in breach corpora SHALL be rejected; services SHALL integrate breach detection (e.g., HIBP k-anonymity API)
Users must be able to paste into password fields; denying paste blocks password manager usage
The entire password must be used in the hash; truncation (as in bcrypt's 72-byte limit) must be mitigated
SHALL accept all printable characters including full Unicode; SHALL NOT restrict character types
Hints are recoverable social engineering vectors
Knowledge-based authenticators have been deprecated entirely
Blocklist composition — NIST specifies that the blocklist SHALL include, at minimum:
Passwords obtained from previous breach corpora (e.g., HIBP's Pwned Passwords dataset — over 900 million entries)
Repetitive or sequential characters (e.g., aaaaaa, 123456)
Context-specific words (e.g., the service name, the username, derivatives thereof)
Implementation options for breach screening:
HIBP k-anonymity API (online)
⚠️ Network round-trip (~50 ms)
HIBP offline download (7-Zip, ~22 GB)

For production systems, the recommended approach is a layered blocklist: the HIBP online API for breach detection (privacy-preserving, always current) combined with a local static list of common patterns and context-specific values (instant, no network dependency).

The fundamental tension in password policy is between composition rules (requiring character class diversity) and length (allowing longer, simpler passwords). NIST's position is clear: length matters more than complexity.
H=L×log⁡2(R)H = L \times \log_2(R)H=L×log2​(R)
Where LLL = password length and RRR = character set size. For a purely random password:
8 characters from 94 printable ASCII: H=8×log⁡2(94)≈52.6H = 8 \times \log_2(94) \approx 52.6H=8×log2​(94)≈52.6 bits
15 characters from 26 lowercase letters: H=15×log⁡2(26)≈70.4H = 15 \times \log_2(26) \approx 70.4H=15×log2​(26)≈70.4 bits
20 characters (alphanumeric): H=20×log⁡2(62)≈119.2H = 20 \times \log_2(62) \approx 119.2H=20×log2​(62)≈119.2 bits

But real users don't choose randomly. The entropy formula assumes uniform distribution — it dramatically overestimates the strength of human-chosen passwords. Users apply predictable patterns: capitalising the first letter, appending a digit or year, substituting visually similar characters (p@$$w0rd), using a single base word with predictable variations.
zxcvbn analysis (Dropbox, 2012) demonstrates the gap between naive and realistic entropy:

The lesson: a longer passphrase of common words is stronger than a shorter "complex" password because human-predictable patterns negate the entropy benefit of character diversity. Composition rules give a false sense of security while making passwords harder to remember.

The HIBP Pwned Passwords API, created by Troy Hunt, provides a practical implementation of breach-checking that preserves user privacy through a k-anonymity model. The API contains over 900 million compromised passwords sourced from real-world data breaches. The critical design challenge is: how does a service check whether a password appears in the breach corpus without sending the password (or its full hash) to a third party?
passages.headingText: 9.3.2 HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) k-Anonymity API

passages.text: 1. User Agent captures new password submission
passages.headingText: 9.3.2 HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) k-Anonymity API

passages.text: The User Agent captures the user's new password (during initial registration or periodic password change) and transmits the plaintext secret over a secure TLS channel to the backend Application Server. At this precise stage, the password resides purely in active memory on the Application Server—it has not yet been securely hashed for storage nor checked against breach corpora.
passages.headingText: 9.3.2 HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) k-Anonymity API

passages.text: 2. Application Server computes SHA-1 hash locally
passages.headingText: 9.3.2 HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) k-Anonymity API

passages.text: The Application Server independently computes the complete SHA-1 hash of the plaintext password strictly within its local memory boundary. This specific SHA-1 digest serves exclusively as a remote lookup key—not as the final database storage hash. The resulting 160-bit (40 hex character) hash enables the external k-anonymity query and is securely wiped from memory immediately after the check concludes.
passages.headingText: 9.3.2 HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) k-Anonymity API

passages.text: Note: SHA-1's known cryptographic collision vulnerabilities (e.g., SHAttered) remain structurally irrelevant here—the external API uses SHA-1 solely as a bucketed lookup index, not for cryptographic integrity.
passages.headingText: 9.3.2 HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) k-Anonymity API

passages.text: 3. Application Server extracts the 5-character hex prefix
passages.headingText: 9.3.2 HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) k-Anonymity API

passages.text: The Application Server isolates the first 5 hexadecimal characters of the SHA-1 hash, designating them as the prefix. With 16⁵ (1,048,576) possible prefixes, this operation slices the global hash space into roughly one million uniformly sized buckets. This 5-character string represents the absolute maximum entropy permitted to leave the server perimeter.
passages.headingText: 9.3.2 HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) k-Anonymity API

passages.text: 4. Application Server retains the 35-character suffix
passages.headingText: 9.3.2 HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) k-Anonymity API

passages.text: The Application Server strictly retains the remaining 35 hex characters of the full computationally derived SHA-1 hash in local memory. This suffix must never cross the external network boundary, guaranteeing the original secret cannot be reconstructed by an external observer.
passages.headingText: 9.3.2 HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) k-Anonymity API

passages.text: 5. Application Server queries HIBP API with prefix
passages.headingText: 9.3.2 HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) k-Anonymity API

passages.text: The Application Server issues an outbound HTTP GET request to the external https://api.pwnedpasswords.com/range/\{prefix\} endpoint, transmitting exclusively the 5-character prefix string.
passages.headingText: 9.3.2 HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) k-Anonymity API

passages.text: Security Audit: The Application Server records an HIBP_API_QUERY_INITIATED telemetry event to track external dependency health and latency metrics during the authentication flow.
passages.headingText: 9.3.2 HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) k-Anonymity API

passages.text: 6. HIBP API executes k-anonymity bucket query
passages.headingText: 9.3.2 HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) k-Anonymity API

passages.text: The HIBP server receives the prefix, queries its internal massive database of compromised password hashes, and actively aggregates all matching entries whose SHA-1 hash begins with the requested 5-character substring.
passages.headingText: 9.3.2 HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) k-Anonymity API

passages.text: 7. HIBP API returns suffix list
passages.headingText: 9.3.2 HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) k-Anonymity API

passages.text: The external HIBP API returns a plaintext text block containing approximately 800 discrete hash suffixes (35 hex characters each) paired tightly with the exact number of times each password appeared across all tracked global data breaches.
passages.headingText: 9.3.2 HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) k-Anonymity API

passages.text: 8. Application Server searches returned suffixes for local match
passages.headingText: 9.3.2 HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) k-Anonymity API

passages.text: The Application Server ingests the external payload and performs a strict local string comparison, actively searching the returned suffix list for the specific 35-character suffix it retained in step 4.
passages.headingText: 9.3.2 HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) k-Anonymity API

passages.text: 9. Application Server makes accept/reject decision
passages.headingText: 9.3.2 HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) k-Anonymity API

passages.text: Based decisively on the local match evaluation, the Application Server determines the password's viability. Conforming to strict modern policies (e.g., NIST SP 800-63B Rev. 4), the backend enforces a hard binary threshold: any presence whatsoever within the aggregated breach corpus mandates outright credential rejection.
passages.headingText: 9.3.2 HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) k-Anonymity API

passages.text: Security Audit: If a definitive string match is triggered, the Application Server immediately blocks the credential update and logs a BREACHED_PASSWORD_REJECTED SIEM event indicating the risk mitigation.
passages.headingText: 9.3.2 HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) k-Anonymity API

passages.text: 10. Application Server returns feedback to User Agent
passages.headingText: 9.3.2 HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) k-Anonymity API

passages.text: The Application Server transmits an HTTP 400 Bad Request (or similar semantic error state) natively back to the User Agent, carrying an explicit error message explaining the password proved insecure due to known breach exposure.
passages.headingText: 9.3.2 HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) k-Anonymity API

passages.text: Privacy properties of the k-anonymity model:
passages.headingText: 9.3.2 HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) k-Anonymity API

passages.text: The plaintext password never leaves the application server
passages.headingText: 9.3.2 HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) k-Anonymity API

passages.text: Only 5 of 40 hex characters of the SHA-1 hash are sent to HIBP
passages.headingText: 9.3.2 HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) k-Anonymity API

passages.text: Server cannot identify password
passages.headingText: 9.3.2 HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) k-Anonymity API

passages.text: Each prefix maps to ~800 possible passwords — the server cannot distinguish which one the client is checking
passages.headingText: 9.3.2 HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) k-Anonymity API

passages.text: Network eavesdropper cannot identify password
passages.headingText: 9.3.2 HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) k-Anonymity API

passages.text: Even intercepting the API request reveals only the 5-character prefix (TLS protects the response body)
passages.headingText: 9.3.2 HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) k-Anonymity API

passages.text: The API is free, requires no API key, and imposes no authentication — minimising the metadata footprint
passages.headingText: 9.3.2 HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) k-Anonymity API

passages.text: Credential stuffing is the automated injection of known username/password pairs (obtained from previous breaches) into login forms. Unlike brute-force attacks that guess passwords, credential stuffing uses real credentials — success rates of 0.1–2% are typical, and given breach corpora containing billions of pairs, this translates to millions of compromised accounts.
Mitigation operates at multiple layers:
Breach-list checking at registration/change (§9.3.2)
Prevents users from setting known-compromised passwords
Does not protect existing accounts with pre-breach passwords
IP-based and account-based throttling
Limits the number of authentication attempts per time window
Attackers distribute across botnets; too aggressive limits lock out legitimate users
Exponentially increasing delays after failed attempts (e.g., 1s → 2s → 4s → 8s)
Slows brute-force without hard lockout
Attackers may accept the delay if the reward is high enough
Temporary lockout after N failed attempts (e.g., 30 min after 5 failures)
Stops brute-force against targeted accounts
Denial-of-service risk — attackers can deliberately trigger lockouts against legitimate users
Challenge-response tests (reCAPTCHA, hCaptcha, Turnstile)
Distinguishes human users from automated bots
Friction for legitimate users; advanced bots use CAPTCHA-solving services; accessibility concerns
Browser/device characteristics (canvas fingerprint, WebGL renderer, timezone, plugins)
Detects anomalous device profiles indicating automated tools
Privacy concerns; fingerprints are spoofable; GDPR implications
Behavioural analysis (mouse movement patterns, keystroke dynamics, request timing)
Identifies non-human interaction patterns at the network/application layer
Sophisticated bots mimic human behaviour; false positives possible
Real-time breach monitoring and proactive forced password resets
Resets passwords for accounts whose credentials appear in new breaches
Requires monitoring breach intelligence feeds; user communication overhead

Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE) enables computation on encrypted data without decryption — a property that theoretically enables private set intersection (PSI) for breach checking: the client encrypts the password hash, the server computes whether it appears in the breach corpus while the data remains encrypted, and returns only the encrypted match/no-match result.

Google's Password Checkup (integrated into Chrome) uses a related cryptographic protocol — not full FHE, but an oblivious PRF-based PSI construction — to check credentials against a server-side breach database without revealing either the user's password or the unmatched entries in the database to either party. This protocol provides stronger privacy guarantees than HIBP's k-anonymity model but requires more complex cryptographic operations.
True FHE-based PSI for password breach checking remains premature for production deployment due to:

Computational overhead — FHE operations are orders of magnitude slower than plaintext operations. Current schemes require seconds to minutes per query, compared to milliseconds for HIBP's k-anonymity approach
Communication cost — FHE ciphertexts are significantly larger than plaintext (typical expansion factor: 10–1,000×), increasing bandwidth requirements
Implementation complexity — FHE libraries (Microsoft SEAL, TFHE, OpenFHE) are research-grade and require deep cryptographic expertise to use securely

Diminishing marginal privacy gain — HIBP's k-anonymity already provides strong practical privacy (the server learns only a 5-char prefix from a 40-char hash); the additional privacy guarantee from FHE may not justify the orders-of-magnitude performance cost

Assessment: FHE-based breach checking is an active research area with long-term potential, but the HIBP k-anonymity model represents the practical state of the art. For production systems, HIBP's approach provides sufficient privacy guarantees with negligible performance overhead.

Password managers are the most practical tool for enabling users to maintain unique, high-entropy passwords across all accounts. They generate, store, and autofill credentials, eliminating the human memory constraint that drives password reuse.
Single point of failure — compromise exposes all vault entries
Long passphrase (20+ characters); breach screening; hardware key unlock (Bitwarden, KeePassXC)
Database breach of cloud-synced vaults
AES-256-GCM encryption; zero-knowledge architecture (server never sees plaintext); end-to-end encryption
Credential extraction via XSS or compromised browser extension
Origin matching; autofill restricted to registered domains; no cross-domain autofill
Browser compromise could access vault
Separate process isolation; biometric/PIN re-prompt for autofill; hardware key support
Zero-knowledge sync; end-to-end encryption; server stores only encrypted blobs
Physical access to unlocked device
Automatic lock timeout; biometric re-authentication; remote wipe capability
Password manager risks that cannot be mitigated:

Master password phishing — the master password is itself a password. If a user is phished for their master password (e.g., a fake Bitwarden login page), the attacker gains access to the entire vault. Hardware key integration (WebAuthn as unlock factor) eliminates this risk
Malicious browser extensions — a compromised extension with appropriate permissions could read autofilled credentials from form fields after they are populated
Clipboard theft — copy-paste from the vault exposes the credential in the system clipboard. Some managers auto-clear after 30 seconds, but the window exists
Supply chain attacks — the password manager itself could be compromised (e.g., LastPass breach, August 2022 — attacker accessed cloud storage with proprietary binary data)
NIST guidance: NIST SP 800-63B Rev 4 explicitly requires verifiers to allow paste into password fields — blocking paste is a countermeasure against password managers and SHALL NOT be implemented.

The Password Hashing Competition (2013–2015) was an open competition to select a new password hashing standard, modelled on NIST's AES competition (1997–2000) and SHA-3 competition (2007–2012). The competition was organised in response to the growing inadequacy of bcrypt and PBKDF2 against GPU/ASIC-equipped attackers, and the lack of a formally vetted, memory-hard password hashing standard.
2012 — Call for submissions announced by an informal panel of cryptographers and security researchers (including Jean-Philippe Aumasson, Colin Percival, and others)
2013 — Submission deadline; 24 candidates submitted
2014 — First-round selection; 9 finalists announced after public review and cryptanalysis
July 2015 — Winner announced: Argon2 (Alex Biryukov, Daniel Dinu, Dmitry Khovratovich, University of Luxembourg)
Special recognitions — Four finalists received honourable mentions for innovative contributions:
Graph-based framework with provable side-channel resistance; configurable memory access patterns (Catena-BRG for Lambda-memory-hard, Catena-DBG for data-dependent graphs)
Academic use; influenced Argon2's hybrid approach
Sponge-based construction (using BlaMka or Blake2b); elegant parameterisation of memory and time costs; alternative side-channel resistance model
Used in cryptocurrency (Lyra2REv2 in Vertcoin); active academic development

Delegation feature — hash computation can be offloaded to an untrusted server without revealing the password (based on the Rabin/RSA trapdoor one-way function using modular squaring); unique among all PHC candidates
Niche use; the delegation property is valuable for constrained-device scenarios
Backward-compatible upgrade path from scrypt; rich feature set including optional ROM (read-only memory) for server-side secrets; heavily optimised for x86 platforms
Used in ALT Linux and other Linux distributions as the default password hash

Why PHC matters: Before Argon2, the password hashing landscape was fragmented — bcrypt was battle-tested but lacked memory hardness, scrypt was memory-hard but had no formal security analysis or competition vetting, and PBKDF2 was NIST-approved but pedestrian. The PHC provided the same rigorous public-review process that produced AES and SHA-3, giving the community confidence in Argon2's security properties.

A pepper is a server-side secret key applied to the password before hashing — conceptually similar to a salt but stored separately from the database (e.g., in an HSM, environment variable, or secrets vault) and not stored alongside the hash.

Defence-in-depth value: If an attacker compromises the password database but not the application server or secrets store, the pepper renders the hashes uncrackable — the attacker cannot reproduce the hash without the pepper value. This protects against database-only breaches (e.g., SQL injection), which are the most common breach vector.

Difficult — requires re-hashing all passwords with the new pepper, which is only possible during the user's next successful login. Maintain a pepper version identifier alongside each hash to support gradual rotation
≥ 256 bits (32 bytes) from a CSPRNG
HSM, KMS (e.g., AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault), or application-level secrets management. Never in the same database as the hashes
Instead of concatenating the pepper, consider HMAC-SHA256(pepper, password) as input to Argon2id — this is cryptographically cleaner and avoids length-extension issues

When migrating from a weaker hash algorithm (e.g., MD5, SHA-1, bcrypt with low cost) to a stronger one (e.g., Argon2id), the hash upgrade pattern re-hashes credentials transparently on next successful login:
User authenticates with their password
System verifies the password against the old hash (using the legacy algorithm)
If verification succeeds, the system computes a new hash using Argon2id with current parameters
The new hash replaces the old hash in the database
A flag or algorithm identifier in the stored hash indicates which algorithm was used (the encoded format's prefix — $2b$ for bcrypt, $argon2id$ for Argon2id — serves this purpose)
Migration gap: Users who do not log in during the migration window retain their old hashes. Options for handling this residual population:

Wrap hashing — re-hash the existing hash: Argon2id(bcrypt_hash). On authentication, first compute the legacy hash from the user's input, then verify against the Argon2id-wrapped hash. This upgrades the storage strength immediately for all users without requiring login

Forced password reset — after a deadline (e.g., 6 months), force password resets for all accounts still using legacy hashes. This is disruptive but ensures all credentials are processed through the new algorithm

zxcvbn (Dropbox, 2012) is a pattern-matching password strength estimator that provides realistic strength assessment by modelling how real attackers work — it identifies dictionary words, keyboard patterns (e.g., qwerty, zxcvbn), dates, repeated sequences, l33t-speak substitutions, and spatial adjacency patterns, then calculates the minimum number of guesses required to crack the password.
correct horse battery staple
L × log₂(R) where R = charset size
~59 bits (high — 4 charsets × 10 chars)
~131 bits (very high — 30 chars × lowercase)
Pattern matching + minimum-guess estimation
~3 bits (dictionary word + common substitutions + predictable digit)
~44 bits (4 uncommon dictionary words, no predictable patterns)

Naive entropy dramatically overestimates P@ssword1 because it treats each character as independently random from a 94-character set, ignoring that the password is a trivially predictable transformation of a dictionary word. zxcvbn recognises the underlying structure and estimates the actual guessing difficulty.
Use zxcvbn as a client-side feedback tool, not a server-side gate — it provides real-time feedback as the user types, guiding them toward stronger choices
Server-side enforcement should rely on length requirements (≥ 15 characters per NIST Rev. 4) and blocklist checking (§9.3.2), not on strength estimation scores
zxcvbn adds ~800 KB to the client-side bundle (including dictionaries) — lazy-load it on password creation/change forms only
Rate limiting strategies exist on a spectrum from strict lockout to transparent friction:
passages.headingText: 9.5.4 Account Lockout vs. Progressive Delays vs. CAPTCHA

passages.text: Disable account after N failures (e.g., 5) for a fixed duration (e.g., 30 min)
passages.headingText: 9.5.4 Account Lockout vs. Progressive Delays vs. CAPTCHA

passages.text: Simple to implement; effectively stops targeted brute-force
passages.headingText: 9.5.4 Account Lockout vs. Progressive Delays vs. CAPTCHA

passages.text: Denial-of-service — attackers deliberately lock out legitimate users; customer support burden
passages.headingText: 9.5.4 Account Lockout vs. Progressive Delays vs. CAPTCHA

passages.text: Increasing wait time between attempts (1s → 2s → 4s → 8s → ...); exponential backoff
passages.headingText: 9.5.4 Account Lockout vs. Progressive Delays vs. CAPTCHA

passages.text: No false lockouts; self-correcting (delay resets after success); no DoS risk
passages.headingText: 9.5.4 Account Lockout vs. Progressive Delays vs. CAPTCHA

passages.text: Attackers with patience can still make many attempts over hours; requires server-side state tracking
passages.headingText: 9.5.4 Account Lockout vs. Progressive Delays vs. CAPTCHA

passages.text: Present CAPTCHA after N failures or on suspicious patterns
passages.headingText: 9.5.4 Account Lockout vs. Progressive Delays vs. CAPTCHA

passages.text: Distinguishes humans from bots; does not block legitimate users
passages.headingText: 9.5.4 Account Lockout vs. Progressive Delays vs. CAPTCHA

passages.text: Accessibility barriers; CAPTCHA-solving services available for ~$1–2 per 1,000; user friction
passages.headingText: 9.5.4 Account Lockout vs. Progressive Delays vs. CAPTCHA

passages.text: Limit attempts per source IP address
passages.headingText: 9.5.4 Account Lockout vs. Progressive Delays vs. CAPTCHA

passages.text: Effective against single-source attacks
passages.headingText: 9.5.4 Account Lockout vs. Progressive Delays vs. CAPTCHA

passages.text: Easily bypassed with residential proxy botnets; shared NAT/VPN IPs cause false positives
passages.headingText: 9.5.4 Account Lockout vs. Progressive Delays vs. CAPTCHA

passages.text: Progressive delays + CAPTCHA after threshold + temporary lockout as last resort
passages.headingText: 9.5.4 Account Lockout vs. Progressive Delays vs. CAPTCHA

passages.text: Layered defence; each mechanism covers the others' weaknesses
passages.headingText: 9.5.4 Account Lockout vs. Progressive Delays vs. CAPTCHA

passages.text: Implementation complexity; requires careful threshold tuning
passages.headingText: 9.5.4 Account Lockout vs. Progressive Delays vs. CAPTCHA

passages.text: Recommendation: Implement a hybrid approach — progressive delays as the baseline, CAPTCHA after a configurable threshold (e.g., 5 failures), and temporary lockout only as a last resort after extreme failure counts (e.g., 20 failures in 1 hour). Complement with credential-stuffing detection at the WAF/CDN layer.

passages.text: 10 failed attempts per IP per 5 minutes
passages.headingText: 9.5.4 Account Lockout vs. Progressive Delays vs. CAPTCHA

passages.headingText: 9.5.4 Account Lockout vs. Progressive Delays vs. CAPTCHA

passages.text: Critical rule: always return the same error message for "username not found" and "wrong password" to prevent username enumeration: "The username or password you entered is incorrect."
