passages.text: Production password authentication systems should validate their implementation using established tools and test vectors to ensure correctness and conformance with algorithm specifications.
Argon2 reference implementation tests
Official test vectors from RFC 9106
Argon2i, Argon2d, Argon2id — various parameter combinations
Comprehensive hashing test vectors
Argon2id, scrypt, bcrypt (via crypto_pwhash)
Python library with extensive test vectors
bcrypt, PBKDF2, scrypt, Argon2, crypt formats
OWASP Password Hashing Tester
Online tool for parameter benchmarking
bcrypt, PBKDF2, Argon2id timing analysis
GPU-accelerated cracking performance
Measures real-world cracking speed for any hash type
Pre-deployment validation checklist:
Test vector verification — verify hash output against official test vectors from RFC 7914 (scrypt) and RFC 9106 (Argon2) to ensure the implementation is correct
Timing consistency — verify that hash computation time is consistent (±20%) across multiple runs to ensure the cost parameter is correctly applied
Salt uniqueness — verify that 10,000 consecutive password hashes all produce unique salts and unique hash values
Password truncation test — verify that passwords of 72 bytes and 73 bytes produce different hashes (if using Argon2id/scrypt) or document the truncation behaviour (if using bcrypt)
Constant-time comparison — verify that hash comparison uses constant-time operations (not byte-by-byte == with short-circuit evaluation)
PHC string round-trip — verify that encoding and parsing of PHC strings preserves all parameters correctly across serialisation boundaries
Breach screening integration — verify that HIBP k-anonymity API calls correctly identify known-compromised passwords and allow clean passwords
Rate limiting verification — verify that login rate limiting triggers correctly for both IP-based and account-based thresholds
Lockout behaviour — verify that account lockout/progressive delays function correctly and that the error message does not differentiate between "user not found" and "wrong password"
Hash upgrade path — verify that the transparent hash upgrade pattern correctly migrates legacy hashes to Argon2id after successful authentication

The previous chapter traced the evolution of password storage — from plaintext catastrophes through modern breach-intelligence-driven verification. This chapter shifts focus from protecting passwords to eliminating them. Passwordless authentication replaces the memorised secret with a different factor — something delivered to the user (a link, a code, a push notification), something the user possesses (a certificate, a hardware token), or something provisioned by an administrator (a bootstrap credential). Each method has distinct security properties, deployment constraints, and assurance-level ceilings.

This chapter surveys the major passwordless authentication methods as a taxonomy, classifying them by mechanism, factor type, phishing resistance, and NIST AAL ceiling. Each method is described as a generic architectural pattern; specific vendor products are mentioned only as illustrative examples. Methods that rely on cryptographic key pairs — FIDO2/WebAuthn, passkeys, TOTP/HOTP — are covered in dedicated chapters (§11–§14) and are not repeated here.

Organisations transitioning to passwordless authentication progress through three stages. The model is not prescriptive — different user populations within the same organisation may be at different stages simultaneously.

Stage 1 — Password as fallback: The primary authentication method is passwordless (e.g., push notification, passkey), but a password remains registered and available as a fallback. Users can choose to enter their password if their passwordless authenticator is unavailable. This stage maximises user choice and minimises lockout risk but retains the full attack surface of password-based authentication. An attacker who obtains a user's password can still authenticate regardless of the user's passwordless enrollment.

Stage 2 — Password as exception: Password is disabled for the majority of users. A small subset of users (e.g., users with incompatible devices, users who have not completed enrollment, users with accessibility requirements) retains password access through an explicit exception process. The exception requires administrative approval and is subject to additional monitoring. Most users authenticate exclusively through passwordless methods. The password attack surface is dramatically reduced but not eliminated.

Stage 3 — Password eliminated: Password is removed as an authentication method for all users. No user can authenticate with a password, regardless of circumstance. Account recovery is handled through alternative mechanisms (recovery codes, administrator-issued temporary access passes, in-person verification, backup FIDO2 keys). This stage maximises security but requires robust recovery processes and complete device coverage.
Improved — most auth is passwordless, but password attack surface remains
Strong — password attack surface narrowed to exception users
Maximum — no password attack surface
Recovery codes, backup keys, admin TAP
Terminology: Several terms related to passwordless authentication are frequently conflated but have precise, distinct meanings:
No shared secret of any kind is used during authentication
FIDO2 passkey, magic link, push notification
No password is entered, but a local PIN or pattern may be used to unlock a device-bound credential
Windows Hello (device-bound key unlocked by PIN), Android biometric unlock
Two or more authentication factors from different categories are required; may still include a password as one factor
Password + SMS OTP, Password + FIDO2 key
A subset of MFA requiring exactly two factors; commonly implemented as password + OTP
Authentication at one identity provider grants access to multiple relying parties; the authentication method itself may or may not be passwordless
SAML SSO with X.509 certificate, OIDC SSO with password

The distinction between "passwordless" and "password-free" is subtle but security-relevant. A Windows Hello PIN is not a shared secret — it is never transmitted to the server and exists only on the local device's TPM. The server never sees the PIN and cannot verify it. The authentication credential is the device-bound FIDO2 key pair; the PIN merely unlocks the local private key. This is passwordless authentication despite the user entering a PIN. Conversely, a traditional PIN used for phone banking (transmitted to the server and verified server-side) is a shared secret — it functions identically to a short password. A passwordless system that asks users to "enter a PIN" must ensure the PIN is verified locally (device-bound) rather than server-side.

Editorial Note: Passwordless ⊥ MFA. Passwordless and multi-factor authentication are orthogonal dimensions, not competing approaches. A FIDO2 security key is both passwordless (no shared secret) and multi-factor (something you have + something you are, if biometric verification is required). A magic link is passwordless but single-factor. Password + push notification is MFA but not passwordless. The strongest authentication combines both dimensions: passwordless, phishing-resistant, multi-factor.

The transition to passwordless is driven by four converging forces. Phishing resistance — passwords are inherently phishable, and phishing was the #1 initial access vector in IBM X-Force Threat Intelligence Index reports from 2022–2025; methods that bind authentication to a cryptographic key pair (FIDO2/WebAuthn) or a TLS-origin-bound mechanism (X.509 client certificates) are immune to credential phishing by design. User experience — passwords impose cognitive load and friction; the average enterprise user manages 25+ work accounts and spends 10–20 hours per year on password-related tasks, while biometrics authenticate in < 1 second and passkeys eliminate password entry entirely. Compliance mandates — NIST SP 800-63-4 requires phishing-resistant authentication for US federal systems, OMB Memorandum M-22-09 mandates phishing-resistant MFA across all federal accounts by the end of fiscal year 2025, and the EU's eIDAS 2.0 regulation creates regulatory pressure for strong, phishing-resistant authentication in financial services and cross-border digital identity. MFA fatigue — as organisations deployed MFA widely, attackers shifted from phishing passwords to phishing MFA approval; the Uber (2022) and Cisco (2022) breaches demonstrated that simple approve/deny push notifications are vulnerable to fatigue-based attacks.

FIDO2/WebAuthn as password replacement: FIDO2 (Fast Identity Online 2) is an open authentication standard developed by the FIDO Alliance and ratified as W3C Web Authentication (WebAuthn). It replaces shared-secret authentication (passwords) with public-key cryptography: the user's device generates a unique key pair per relying party (website), stores the private key in a hardware-protected enclave, and proves possession of the private key during authentication using a cryptographic signature. Detailed ceremony flows and attestation formats are covered in §14 (WebAuthn and CTAP2 Architecture); the following comparison highlights how WebAuthn differs from passwords at the protocol level:
Shared secret (user and server both know it)
Asymmetric key pair (only user holds private key)
Password hash (verifiable offline)
Public key only (cannot impersonate user)
❌ Vulnerable — users enter passwords on fake sites
✅ Immune — signature bound to relying party origin
Hash database compromise enables offline cracking
Public key database compromise has no authentication impact
❌ Stolen credentials work on any site with same password
✅ Keys are scoped to a single relying party
Cryptographic signature verification
Biometric scan (fingerprint/face) or device PIN

Registration ceremony (abridged — see §14 for full detail): the user navigates to the registration page and clicks "Create passkey"; the server generates a challenge (random byte string) and sends it with relying party information (RP ID, user ID, supported algorithms); the browser prompts the user's authenticator (platform authenticator or security key); the authenticator generates a new key pair (ECDSA P-256 or Ed25519), stores the private key in secure hardware (TPM, Secure Enclave), and creates an attestation object; the authenticator signs the challenge and returns the public key + attestation to the server; the server stores the public key credential ID and public key and discards the challenge.

Authentication ceremony (abridged): the user navigates to the login page and clicks "Sign in with passkey"; the server generates a new challenge and sends the list of allowed credential IDs for this user; the browser prompts the authenticator — the user verifies via biometric or PIN; the authenticator looks up the private key for the matching credential ID, signs the challenge, and returns the signature + authenticator data; the server verifies the signature against the stored public key, checks the challenge, and authenticates the user.

Passkey ecosystem — current status (2025–2026): Passkeys are syncable WebAuthn credentials — they provide the same phishing-resistant security as hardware security keys but are synchronised across devices via cloud services, eliminating the need for users to carry a physical token.
iCloud Keychain (end-to-end encrypted)
Platform (Touch ID, Face ID)
Google (Android 14+, Chrome)
Google Password Manager (end-to-end encrypted)
Platform (fingerprint, face unlock)
Microsoft (Windows 11 22H2+)
Platform (Windows Hello, PIN, biometric)
1Password vault sync (end-to-end encrypted)
Bitwarden vault sync (end-to-end encrypted)
Dashlane vault sync (end-to-end encrypted)
External security keys (YubiKey)
Requires security key or browser-based

Apple reported over 1 billion passkeys created across its ecosystem by mid-2025; Google reported passkey sign-ins surpassing traditional password sign-ins on several Google services; the Passkeys.org adoption tracker shows 15% of top-100 websites support passkeys as of Q1 2026; the FIDO Alliance projects 50%+ of consumer-facing services will support passkeys by end of 2027.

Password + FIDO2 security key as MFA: When a FIDO2 security key (YubiKey, Feitian, etc.) is used as a second factor alongside a password, the combination provides full phishing resistance and satisfies AAL3 (with a FIPS 140-2/3 certified key). The security key is bound to the relying party origin — phishing sites cannot use it, and the key will not sign a challenge for a different origin. Each challenge is unique (replay protection), and the attacker must physically possess the key. This combination is the strongest password-based MFA configuration available and is the NIST-recommended second factor for high-assurance applications.

A magic link is a single-use, time-limited URL sent to a user's registered email address. Clicking the link completes authentication without any password entry. The mechanism transforms email account access into the authentication factor — the user proves identity by demonstrating control of the email inbox.
User navigates to the application's login page and enters their email address
Application server generates a cryptographically random token (≥ 256 bits of entropy from a CSPRNG), associates it with the user's account, and records the creation timestamp
Server constructs a URL embedding the token: https://app.example.com/auth/verify?token=<base64url-encoded-token>
Server sends the URL to the user's registered email address via SMTP
User opens their email, clicks the magic link
Browser sends the token to the application server via HTTPS GET or POST
Server validates the token — checks existence, expiry, single-use status, and optionally IP/device binding
If valid, server creates an authenticated session for the user and invalidates the token
≥ 256 bits, CSPRNG-generated
Must be computationally infeasible to guess; 2²⁵⁶ possible values eliminates brute-force
5–15 minutes from generation
Short window limits exposure if the email is intercepted or the link leaks
Invalidated immediately after first successful validation
Prevents replay attacks; a captured link cannot be reused
Token is bound to a specific user account
Prevents token substitution — using User A's token to authenticate as User B
Optional: session/IP binding
Token can be bound to the browser session or IP address that initiated the login request
Mitigates cross-device link theft; the link only works from the device that requested it
Token construction (server-side implementation detail):

The server stores only the SHA-256 hash of the token (not the plaintext token) in its database. The plaintext token is embedded in the URL sent to the user's email. When the user clicks the link, the server hashes the received token and looks up the hash in its database. This prevents token exposure through database compromise — even if the database is breached, the attacker cannot reconstruct valid magic link URLs from the stored hashes.
Security model — email as the authentication factor:

Magic links shift the authentication trust anchor from a memorised secret (password) to the email delivery channel. The security of the authentication is therefore bounded by the security of the user's email account. This creates a transitive trust dependency:
If the email account uses strong MFA (e.g., FIDO2 key on the email provider) → the magic link provides reasonable authentication strength
If the email account uses only a password → the magic link's security reduces to the security of that password, defeating the purpose of passwordless authentication
If the email account is compromised → every service using magic links for that email address is compromised simultaneously
Weaknesses and attack vectors:

Email account compromise — An attacker with access to the user's inbox (via credential stuffing on the email provider, session hijacking, or account takeover) can intercept magic links and authenticate as the user. This is the fundamental limitation: magic links cannot be stronger than the email account that receives them

Email transit security — SMTP between mail servers uses opportunistic STARTTLS, meaning encryption is attempted but not enforced. If STARTTLS negotiation fails (downgrade attack) or is not supported by an intermediate relay, the email travels in plaintext. An attacker positioned on the network path between mail servers could intercept the magic link in transit. Modern email providers largely mitigate this through MTA-STS (RFC 8461) enforcement, but coverage is not universal

Link leakage vectors — Magic link URLs can leak through email forwarding (user forwards the email to a colleague), email preview panes in corporate email clients, link preview/prefetch by email security scanners (some scanners follow URLs to check for malware, inadvertently consuming the single-use token), and browser history/referrer headers if the landing page redirects to a third-party resource

Cross-device friction — The user initiates login on Device A (e.g., laptop browser) but may click the magic link on Device B (e.g., phone email client). Without session binding, this creates a legitimate but confusing UX where the phone browser opens an authenticated session that the user did not intend. With session binding (token bound to Device A's session), the link clicked on Device B fails — better security but worse UX

Email security scanner token prefetch — Enterprise email security gateways (MIMECAST, Proofpoint, Microsoft Defender for Office 365) scan URLs in emails by following them to check for malicious content. This scanning can consume the single-use magic link token before the user clicks it, invalidating the link. Workarounds include: (a) exempt the authentication domain from URL scanning, (b) design tokens to survive a single prefetch and only invalidate on the second request, or (c) use a POST-based confirmation flow after the initial GET

Referrer header leakage — If the magic link landing page includes links to external resources (analytics, images, scripts), the browser's Referer header may leak the magic link URL to those third-party services. The landing page should use <meta name="referrer" content="no-referrer"> and avoid any external resource loading

Assurance level: Magic links satisfy AAL1 only. NIST SP 800-63B classifies email-delivered secrets as single-factor authentication — the email inbox is the single factor (something you have — access to the email account). Magic links are not phishing-resistant (the link can be forwarded to an attacker-controlled context) and are not bound to a cryptographic key pair.

One-Time Passwords (OTPs) delivered through out-of-band channels — email, SMS, or voice call — represent the most widely deployed form of second-factor authentication. Unlike magic links, which embed the token in a clickable URL, OTP methods require the user to manually transcribe a short numeric code (typically 6 digits) from one channel (email, phone) to the application's verification prompt. This manual entry step is both a security property (the code is not a clickable URL that can be followed by automated scanners) and a UX friction point.
passages.headingText: 10.2 OTP Delivery Channels: Email, SMS, and Voice

passages.text: Mechanism: The application generates a random 6–8 digit code, stores it server-side with an expiry timestamp, and sends it to the user's email address. The user reads the code from their email and types it into the application's verification field. The server compares the entered code against the stored value.

Email OTP shares the same fundamental security model as magic links (§10.1) — email inbox access is the authentication factor — but differs in the interaction pattern. The manual transcription step provides marginal protection against link-following scanners and reduces accidental cross-device authentication. However, the same email transit risks (STARTTLS downgrade, account compromise) apply.
Typical parameters: 6-digit numeric code, 5–10 minute expiry, single-use, rate-limited to 3–5 requests per hour per account.

Mechanism: The application sends a 6-digit numeric code to the user's registered phone number via SMS (Short Message Service). The user reads the code from the SMS notification and enters it into the application. The code is validated server-side.

SMS OTP is the most widely deployed second-factor method globally — it requires no app installation, works on any mobile phone (including feature phones), and is understood by virtually all users. However, the SMS delivery channel has fundamental security weaknesses that make SMS OTP the least secure OTP delivery method:
SMS channel vulnerabilities:

Attacker contacts the mobile carrier (via social engineering or bribed insiders), convinces them to port the victim's phone number to a new SIM card controlled by the attacker. All SMS messages — including OTPs — are then delivered to the attacker's device
Complete bypass of SMS-based 2FA; the attacker receives the OTP directly

Signaling System 7 (SS7) is the signalling protocol suite underpinning the global telephone network, designed in the 1970s with no authentication or encryption. An attacker with access to the SS7 network (purchasable from some telecoms for a few hundred dollars) can intercept SMS messages by sending fraudulent routing commands that redirect messages to an attacker-controlled node
Silent interception of SMS OTPs without the victim's knowledge; no SIM swap required
Diameter protocol exploitation

The Diameter protocol replaced SS7 in 4G/LTE networks but inherits similar trust-model weaknesses — hop-by-hop routing without end-to-end encryption or robust authentication. Misconfigured Diameter interconnects allow subscriber traffic interception and location tracking
SMS interception on 4G networks; extends SS7-era vulnerabilities to modern infrastructure
Mobile malware with SMS read permissions (Android: READ_SMS, RECEIVE_SMS) can silently intercept OTP codes before the user sees them
Automated OTP theft on compromised devices
If the phone is off or unreachable, SMS may be converted to voicemail in some carriers. Default voicemail PINs (often 0000 or the last four digits of the phone number) can be exploited remotely
OTP retrieval via voicemail access

Mechanism: The application initiates an automated phone call to the user's registered number. A text-to-speech engine reads the OTP code aloud. The user listens and enters the code. Voice OTP serves primarily as an accessibility fallback — it enables authentication for users who cannot receive or read SMS (e.g., landline phones, users with visual impairments, regions with unreliable SMS delivery).
Voice OTP shares the SIM-swap and SS7/Diameter vulnerabilities of SMS OTP (the phone number is the trust anchor in both cases). Additional attack vectors include:

Voicemail interception — If the call goes to voicemail (user doesn't answer, phone is off), the OTP is recorded in the voicemail system. Attackers can access voicemail remotely using default or weak PINs

Call forwarding abuse — If the attacker can configure unconditional call forwarding on the victim's account (via social engineering the carrier or exploiting SS7), all voice OTP calls are redirected to the attacker

NIST SP 800-63B designates SMS and voice OTP as restricted authenticators — a special classification indicating known vulnerability. The final revision (published July 2025) formalises the following constraints:
The verifier SHALL offer at least one unrestricted authenticator option (e.g., TOTP app, FIDO2 key) that satisfies the target AAL
The verifier SHALL inform users of the security risks associated with SMS/voice OTP and the availability of alternative methods
The organisation SHALL incorporate SMS/voice OTP risks into its formal risk assessment
The organisation SHALL maintain a documented migration plan for transitioning away from SMS/voice OTP
The organisation SHALL implement controls against known attacks — SIM swap detection, number porting alerts, carrier account PINs

Assurance ceiling: SMS and voice OTP are permitted at AAL1, conditionally permitted at AAL2 (only when the restricted authenticator conditions above are satisfied and no unrestricted alternative is feasible), and prohibited at AAL3.
The industry trajectory away from SMS OTP is driven by three converging forces:

Regulatory pressure — NIST's restricted classification creates compliance friction for any system that relies solely on SMS OTP. Organisations subject to NIST requirements (US federal agencies, contractors, and systems aligned to NIST frameworks) must offer alternatives and document risk acceptance

Escalating SIM-swap fraud — The FBI's Internet Crime Complaint Center (IC3) reported SIM-swap complaints increased from 320 in 2018 to over 2,000 in 2022, with losses exceeding $72 million in 2022 alone. Telecom carriers have implemented number-porting verification improvements (e.g., T-Mobile Account Takeover Protection, Verizon Number Lock), but social engineering remains effective

Platform push toward passkeys — Apple, Google, and Microsoft are actively deploying passkey infrastructure (§14.6) that provides a superior UX (no code transcription, no waiting for delivery) with phishing-resistant security. As passkey adoption grows, the value proposition of SMS OTP diminishes

Despite the deprecation trajectory, SMS OTP remains deeply embedded in consumer and enterprise systems. Complete elimination is a multi-year effort constrained by user familiarity, feature-phone compatibility requirements, and regulatory frameworks in some jurisdictions that explicitly mandate SMS-based verification (e.g., PSD2 Strong Customer Authentication in the EU, which permits SMS OTP as possession evidence).
Both magic links and email OTP use email inbox access as the authentication factor. The distinction is in the interaction pattern and the resulting security properties:
passages.headingText: 10.2.6 Magic Link vs Email OTP: Comparison

passages.text: B2C secondary verification, environments with email security scanners
passages.headingText: 10.2.6 Magic Link vs Email OTP: Comparison

passages.text: Push notification authentication uses a pre-registered mobile application to deliver authentication challenges directly to the user's device. Instead of transcribing a code from one channel to another, the user responds to the challenge within the authenticator app — dramatically reducing UX friction compared to OTP methods while maintaining out-of-band verification.

Architecture overview: The push authentication system consists of four components: (1) the identity provider — the server-side authentication authority that initiates push challenges and validates responses; (2) the push service — the platform-level notification delivery service (Apple Push Notification service for iOS, Firebase Cloud Messaging for Android), providing reliable, encrypted delivery of notification payloads; (3) the authenticator app — the mobile application that receives push notifications, presents authentication challenges, and stores a device-specific cryptographic key provisioned during enrollment; and (4) the user's device — the smartphone or tablet whose secure element or keychain stores the signing key. During enrollment, the authenticator app generates a public/private key pair, registers the public key with the IdP, and obtains a device-specific push token from the platform push service. The IdP stores the public key and push token, associating them with the user's account. During authentication, the IdP sends a push notification via the push service, and the authenticator app responds with a cryptographically signed approval.

Mechanism: When the user initiates authentication (e.g., enters their username on a web login page), the application server sends a push notification to the user's registered authenticator app. The app displays a prompt asking the user to approve or deny the sign-in attempt. The user taps "Approve" → the authenticator app sends a signed approval response to the server → the server completes authentication.

Security model: The registered device is the authentication factor (something you have). The push notification channel (APNs for iOS, FCM for Android) provides a device-bound delivery mechanism that is significantly harder to intercept than SMS. The private key stored on the device can sign the approval response, providing cryptographic proof of possession.

Critical vulnerability — MFA fatigue / push bombing: An attacker who possesses the user's password (from a breach or phishing) can trigger repeated push notifications to the user's phone. The user, fatigued by the constant interruptions — especially at inconvenient times (late at night, during meetings) — may eventually tap "Approve" to stop the notifications without verifying the request's legitimacy. Notable real-world exploitation:

Uber (September 2022) — The Lapsus$ group obtained an Uber contractor's credentials and repeatedly triggered MFA push notifications. After over an hour of push bombardment, the contractor approved a request, granting the attacker full access to Uber's internal systems
Cisco (August 2022) — An attacker used voice phishing combined with MFA push spam to gain initial access to a Cisco employee's VPN
Number matching is the industry-standard countermeasure against MFA fatigue attacks. The mechanism adds a challenge-response step to the push flow.
Push notification authentication flow with number matching:
1. User Agent submits first factor (username + password) to Identity Provider

The User Agent captures the user's primary credentials (e.g., username and password) on the Identity Provider's formal login interface. This represents the first authentication factor—a memorized secret. The browser securely transmits the credentials to the Identity Provider over TLS. At this stage, the user has satisfied the initial factor of a multi-factor authentication policy but must successfully clear the second factor.
2. Identity Provider validates first factor and determines MFA requirement

The Identity Provider mathematically verifies the password against the stored credential digest (e.g., Argon2id). Upon success, the Identity Provider strictly evaluates the applicable active dynamic authentication policy (e.g., Conditional Access in Entra ID, Authentication Policy in PingAM). The engine determines that a second factor is cryptographically required—specifically leveraging an out-of-band push notification targeting a registered Authenticator App.
3. Identity Provider generates random number-matching challenge

The Identity Provider taps its CSPRNG to generate a random 2-digit number (10–99) that securely serves as the physical number-matching challenge. The Identity Provider pins this number server-side within the pending authentication session's state context, attaching a strictly enforced short expiry window (typically 60 seconds) before natural timeout.
4. Identity Provider displays challenge number on login screen

The Identity Provider renders the 2-digit number centrally on the waiting browser login page. The user must visually perceive this number and temporarily remember it to conclude the physical challenge via their phone. This mandatory step constructs a non-bypassable cognitive binding bridging the login screen and the remote Authenticator App.
5. Identity Provider transmits push payload via Platform Push Service

The Identity Provider compiles a secure push notification payload and dispatches it toward the user's registered device bridging across the Platform Push Service (Apple APNs for iOS, Google FCM for Android). The payload seamlessly includes an opaque challenge identifier (explicitly omitting the actual 2-digit answer), rich sign-in metadata (application resource name, geographic geolocation mapping, source IP), and a system-level interruption prompting the user to wake the Authenticator App.
Security Audit: The Identity Provider emits an OUTBOUND_PUSH_CHALLENGE_ISSUED SIEM event recording the challenge initiation and the targeted push service endpoints.
6. Platform Push Service delivers notification to Authenticator App

The Platform Push Service routes the notification payload cleanly to the unique hardware device mathematically registered to the user's account identity. The OS-level notification layer displays the alert on the phone's lock screen. Importantly, the sign-in metadata (application name, location, IP) surfaces directly inside the notification preview wrapper, drastically empowering the user to assess the request's legitimacy before explicitly unlocking the device.
7. Authenticator App displays challenge interface and metadata context

The Authenticator App forces the user to confront three separate 2-digit numerical options securely rendered in a multiple-choice layout grid. Only one explicitly matches the master number silently locked on the pending login screen. The Authenticator App concurrently renders the sign-in metadata context (which explicit application is demanding access, pinpointed origination location, and IP) compelling the user to actively verify the transaction context.
8. User visually processes cross-device challenge

The user looks distinctly at the waiting login screen on their primary computing interface (laptop, workstation) to manually read the uniquely generated 2-digit number. This active cross-device visual verification constitutes the structural backbone of number-matching defensive design—proving continuous physical presence bridging both the relying machine and the authenticator bounds.
9. User inputs matching response into Authenticator App

The user physically taps the correct matching number natively displayed on their mobile phone. If they correctly select the corresponding number, the Authenticator App instantly proceeds to the cryptographic approval routine. If the user arbitrarily selects an incorrect digit pairing, or taps "Deny" recognizing a fraudulent request, the process terminates.
10. Authenticator App transmits signed approval to Identity Provider

The Authenticator App securely formats an approval payload bound for the Identity Provider. The payload encompasses the selected digit value and, critically, injects a secure device-bound cryptographic signature (generated leveraging the device's securely enrolled private key, typically an ECDSA P-256 or Ed25519 signature per FIPS 186-5) proving definitive physical device possession. The payload is pushed directly to the Identity Provider traversing HTTPS, bypassing the intermediary push service network.
11. Identity Provider cryptographically validates the response

The Identity Provider sequentially executes three essential validations on the inbound payload: (1) strictly compares the natively selected digit selection against the server-stored master number ("42" = "42"); (2) validates the digital signature against the user's previously enrolled public authenticator key; and (3) verifies the entire transactional sequence completed safely within the short-lived time window constraint.

Rejection Scenario: If the device signature evaluates as structurally invalid, the number mismatched, or the time constraint expired, the Identity Provider actively rejects the authentication attempt and logs a PUSH_CHALLENGE_VERIFICATION_FAILED SIEM event.
12. Identity Provider completes authentication and establishes session

With both independent factors comprehensively authenticated (primary password + successfully verified push-challenge approval), the Identity Provider establishes an actively authenticated master session. The Identity Provider issues the finalized session token (cookie, JWT, or opaque reference wrapper) downward into the User Agent and directs the user to the underlying target application.
Artifact Produced: session (Authenticated HTTP Cookie)

The flow above illustrates the number-matching variant. The key differences from simple approve/deny are steps 3, 4, 7, 8, and 9 — these steps introduce the cognitive binding that defeats MFA fatigue attacks:
User initiates authentication at the login screen
The login screen displays a randomly generated 2-digit number (e.g., "42")
A push notification is sent to the user's authenticator app
The authenticator app displays three 2-digit numbers and asks the user to select the one matching the number shown on the login screen
If the user selects the correct number → authentication succeeds
If the user selects an incorrect number or denies → authentication fails

Why number matching defeats push bombing: An attacker triggering unsolicited push notifications cannot know the 2-digit number displayed on the login screen (the attacker is not sitting in front of the victim's screen). The user who receives an unexpected push notification has no number to match — the disconnect between "I see a push, but I don't see a number on any login screen" signals a fraudulent request.

Microsoft made number matching mandatory for all Microsoft Authenticator push notifications on May 8, 2023, removing the administrative option to disable it. The enforcement was rolled out between February and May 2023, accompanied by two additional anti-fatigue measures:

Additional context — The push notification displays metadata about the sign-in request: the application name requesting access, the geographic location (city, country) derived from the source IP, and the source IP address itself. This context helps the user identify fraudulent requests (e.g., "Sign-in to Outlook from Lagos, Nigeria" when the user is in Amsterdam)

Anomalous request suppression — Since September 2023, Microsoft Authenticator silently suppresses push notifications from requests deemed anomalous by Microsoft's risk engine. Instead of interrupting the user's phone, the notification is logged but not displayed. The user must open the Authenticator app manually to see suppressed requests. This eliminates the phone-vibration fatigue vector entirely
Context mechanisms for push authentication: Beyond number matching, additional context mechanisms enhance the user's ability to distinguish legitimate from fraudulent requests:
2-digit number displayed on login screen, must be selected from 3 options on authenticator
Defeats push bombing — attacker cannot see the login screen number
City/country derived from the source IP of the authentication request
Helps user identify unusual locations (e.g., "Lagos, Nigeria" when user is in London)
The name of the application requesting authentication
Helps user identify if the request is for an expected service
The browser, OS, or device type of the authentication request
Helps identify requests from unusual devices
Anomalous request suppression
Push notifications from requests deemed anomalous by risk engine are not displayed; user must open app manually
Eliminates the phone-vibration fatigue vector entirely
Maximum number of push notifications per user per time window (e.g., 10 per hour)
Limits the intensity of push bombing campaigns
❌ Vulnerable — user can tap "Approve" without cognitive engagement
passages.headingText: 10.3.3 Challenge-Response vs. Simple Approve: Security Comparison

passages.text: ✅ Resistant — requires active knowledge of the displayed number
passages.headingText: 10.3.3 Challenge-Response vs. Simple Approve: Security Comparison

passages.text: High risk — single inattentive tap completes authentication
passages.headingText: 10.3.3 Challenge-Response vs. Simple Approve: Security Comparison

passages.text: Low risk — must select correct number from multiple options
passages.headingText: 10.3.3 Challenge-Response vs. Simple Approve: Security Comparison

passages.text: Low — one additional selection step (~3 seconds)
passages.headingText: 10.3.3 Challenge-Response vs. Simple Approve: Security Comparison

passages.text: ❌ Not phishing-resistant — an AiTM proxy can relay the push
passages.headingText: 10.3.3 Challenge-Response vs. Simple Approve: Security Comparison

passages.text: ❌ Not phishing-resistant — number matching does not bind to origin
passages.headingText: 10.3.3 Challenge-Response vs. Simple Approve: Security Comparison

passages.text: Critical caveat: Neither simple push nor number matching provides phishing resistance in the FIDO2/WebAuthn sense. An Adversary-in-the-Middle (AiTM) proxy (§21.5) sitting between the user and the legitimate service can relay the number-matching challenge in real time — displaying the correct number on the phishing page. The user, believing they are on the legitimate site, enters the matching number on their phone, completing the authentication for the attacker. Push notification authentication is resistant to push bombing but not to sophisticated real-time phishing.
passages.headingText: 10.3.3 Challenge-Response vs. Simple Approve: Security Comparison

passages.text: AiTM relay attack — step-by-step walkthrough:
passages.headingText: 10.3.3 Challenge-Response vs. Simple Approve: Security Comparison

passages.text: Attacker operates a phishing site that proxies all requests to the legitimate IdP
passages.headingText: 10.3.3 Challenge-Response vs. Simple Approve: Security Comparison

passages.text: User enters credentials (password) on the phishing site — attacker relays to IdP
passages.headingText: 10.3.3 Challenge-Response vs. Simple Approve: Security Comparison

passages.text: IdP triggers push notification to user's authenticator app
passages.headingText: 10.3.3 Challenge-Response vs. Simple Approve: Security Comparison

passages.text: The phishing site displays the number-matching challenge (relayed from IdP)
passages.headingText: 10.3.3 Challenge-Response vs. Simple Approve: Security Comparison

passages.text: User sees the correct number on what appears to be the legitimate login page
passages.headingText: 10.3.3 Challenge-Response vs. Simple Approve: Security Comparison

passages.text: User selects the matching number on their authenticator app
passages.headingText: 10.3.3 Challenge-Response vs. Simple Approve: Security Comparison

passages.text: Authenticator app sends signed approval to IdP (not to the phishing site — the approval goes directly to the IdP's verified endpoint)
passages.headingText: 10.3.3 Challenge-Response vs. Simple Approve: Security Comparison

passages.text: IdP validates the approval and creates a session
passages.headingText: 10.3.3 Challenge-Response vs. Simple Approve: Security Comparison

passages.text: Attacker's proxy captures the session cookie and gains access
passages.headingText: 10.3.3 Challenge-Response vs. Simple Approve: Security Comparison

passages.text: The vulnerability exists because the push approval is not bound to the TLS session origin — the authenticator app approves the authentication request based on the displayed context (number, location, app name), not based on the cryptographic origin of the request. The user is the only binding element, and the AiTM proxy ensures the user sees correct-looking context. For phishing resistance, organisations should deploy FIDO2/WebAuthn alongside or instead of push-based methods.
passages.headingText: 10.3.3 Challenge-Response vs. Simple Approve: Security Comparison

passages.text: Password + push notification as MFA: When push is used as a second factor alongside a password, the combination achieves AAL2 but inherits the same AiTM vulnerability described above. The push notification does not confirm the target URL — a user may approve a fraudulent login initiated by an attacker who relayed the password to the legitimate IdP. An additional risk is re-registration attacks — an attacker initiates a password reset, then approves the push notification to register their own device as the authentication device, locking out the legitimate user. The Uber breach (September 2022) exploited MFA fatigue in exactly this configuration: the attacker spammed the contractor with MFA push notifications until one was approved, then used the session to escalate privileges.
passages.headingText: 10.3.3 Challenge-Response vs. Simple Approve: Security Comparison

passages.text: Push notification suppression attacks: In March 2024, researchers demonstrated that push notifications from Microsoft Authenticator could be intercepted and suppressed by malware with accessibility service permissions on Android. The malware detects incoming push notifications from the Authenticator app and dismisses them before the user can see them, while simultaneously completing the authentication flow through a malicious overlay. This attack requires device compromise (malware installation) and is not a protocol-level vulnerability, but it highlights the reliance on the mobile device's security posture.
passages.headingText: 10.3.3 Challenge-Response vs. Simple Approve: Security Comparison

passages.text: When to choose push authentication — decision guidance: Push authentication is appropriate when (a) a managed authenticator app is already deployed to the user population, (b) UX is a priority (single-tap approval vs. code transcription), (c) the threat model includes credential stuffing but not sophisticated AiTM phishing, and (d) number matching is enabled for moderate phishing-fatigue resistance. Push is inappropriate when (a) the organisation is a known phishing target (financial services, technology, government), in which case FIDO2/WebAuthn should be the primary method; or (b) AiTM-resistant authentication is required for regulatory compliance.
passages.headingText: 10.3.3 Challenge-Response vs. Simple Approve: Security Comparison

passages.text: Password + OTP (TOTP/HOTP) as MFA: When passwords remain the first factor, TOTP (RFC 6238) or HOTP (RFC 4226) generated by an authenticator app (Google Authenticator, Authy, Microsoft Authenticator) is the most widely deployed second factor. The combination achieves AAL2 but provides no phishing resistance — an attacker who phishes the user's password on a fake site can also phish the TOTP code in real time using an AiTM proxy (§21.5). TOTP codes expire after 30–60 seconds (partial replay protection), but the user can share both password and current OTP via social engineering. NIST SP 800-63-4 classifies TOTP as an acceptable AAL2 factor but recommends phishing-resistant alternatives (FIDO2) for higher-assurance deployments.
passages.headingText: 10.3.3 Challenge-Response vs. Simple Approve: Security Comparison

passages.text: Certificate-Based Authentication (CBA) uses X.509 digital certificates — the same PKI infrastructure that secures TLS connections — as the authentication credential. Instead of presenting a password, the user presents a client certificate during the TLS handshake, and the server validates the certificate against a trusted Certificate Authority (CA) chain.
CBA operates at the TLS layer, using the TLS client certificate authentication mechanism defined in RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3) §6.4.2:
Client initiates TLS connection with the server
Server sends a CertificateRequest message during the TLS handshake, specifying which CAs it trusts and which certificate types it accepts
Client responds with its X.509 certificate (from a smart card, TPM, or software certificate store)
Client signs a hash of the handshake transcript with the private key corresponding to the certificate's public key — the CertificateVerify message

Server validates: (a) the certificate chain up to a trusted root CA, (b) the certificate has not expired, (c) the certificate has not been revoked (via CRL or OCSP), (d) the CertificateVerify signature is valid, (e) the certificate's subject or SAN maps to a known user identity
If validation succeeds, the user is authenticated

Key property: The private key never leaves the client. In hardware-backed CBA (smart card, TPM, Secure Enclave), the private key is generated and stored within the hardware security module and cannot be exported — the signing operation occurs inside the hardware. This provides the strongest form of credential protection: even full compromise of the operating system cannot exfiltrate the private key.
The US federal government operates the largest CBA deployment in the world through two smart card programmes:
passages.headingText: 10.4.2 Smart Card Integration: PIV and CAC

passages.text: PIV (Personal Identity Verification) — Defined by FIPS 201-3 and NIST SP 800-73-5, PIV is the identity credential system for all US federal employees and contractors. Each PIV card contains:
passages.headingText: 10.4.2 Smart Card Integration: PIV and CAC

passages.text: PIV Authentication Certificate — Used for logical access (system login). Contains the cardholder's UUID and Federal Agency Smart Credential Number (FASC-N). Maps to the user's identity in the agency's directory (Active Directory or equivalent)
passages.headingText: 10.4.2 Smart Card Integration: PIV and CAC

passages.text: Digital Signature Certificate — Used for signing emails (S/MIME) and documents
passages.headingText: 10.4.2 Smart Card Integration: PIV and CAC

passages.text: Key Management Certificate — Used for encryption/decryption (key recovery supported)
passages.headingText: 10.4.2 Smart Card Integration: PIV and CAC

passages.text: Card Authentication Certificate — Used for physical access (door entry) without requiring cardholder PIN entry
passages.headingText: 10.4.2 Smart Card Integration: PIV and CAC

passages.text: PIV cards use contactless (ISO 14443) and contact (ISO 7816) interfaces. Logical access requires PIN entry to unlock the PIV Authentication Certificate's private key — combining something you have (the card) with something you know (the PIN) for two-factor authentication.
passages.headingText: 10.4.2 Smart Card Integration: PIV and CAC

passages.text: CAC (Common Access Card) — The Department of Defense's implementation of PIV, issued to all active-duty military, reservists, and DoD civilian employees. Functionally similar to PIV but with DoD-specific certificate policies and issuance processes.
passages.headingText: 10.4.2 Smart Card Integration: PIV and CAC

passages.text: Modern cloud identity providers support CBA without requiring on-premises federation infrastructure:

Cloud-native CBA pattern: The cloud IdP maintains a trust store of CA certificates. During authentication, the IdP presents a CertificateRequest to the user's browser (or app), the browser prompts the user to select a certificate (from the OS certificate store, smart card, or TPM), and the IdP validates the certificate chain against its trust store.

As an example: Entra ID CBA eliminates the historical requirement to deploy ADFS for certificate authentication. Administrators upload trusted CA certificates (root and intermediate) to Entra ID, configure authentication binding policies (mapping certificate properties to single-factor or multi-factor strength), and enable CBA as an authentication method. The binding policies determine the assurance level:
Certificate issued by a specific CA with a specific certificate policy OID
Multi-factor (the certificate policy OID indicates hardware-backed issuance)
Certificate issued by a trusted CA (any policy)
Single-factor (software certificate from a trusted CA)
Certificate with a specific subject DN from a specific CA
Multi-factor (enables precise binding for high-assurance certificates)
CBA security depends critically on the certificate lifecycle management:
CA validates identity → generates key pair → issues certificate (or, for hardware: key pair generated on-card, CSR sent to CA)
Hardware key generation (on smart card or TPM) ensures the private key never exists outside the hardware security boundary
Before expiry, a new certificate is issued for the same identity. Can reuse the existing key pair (re-key) or generate a new pair (re-certificate)
Automated renewal via auto-enrollment (Active Directory Certificate Services) prevents service interruption from expired certificates
CA publishes revocation status via CRL (Certificate Revocation List) or OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol)

CRLs are published periodically (latency: hours to days). OCSP provides real-time revocation checking but requires the OCSP responder to be available. OCSP stapling (RFC 6961) allows the server to cache and present the OCSP response, reducing client-side latency
Private key stored in smart card, TPM 2.0, or Secure Enclave
Non-exportable keys — the most critical security property for high-assurance CBA. The private key cannot be copied, backed up, or extracted from the hardware

Assurance level: CBA with hardware-bound, non-exportable keys (smart card, TPM) satisfies AAL3 (§1.1) — the highest authenticator assurance level. CBA with software-stored certificates satisfies AAL1 or AAL2 depending on the protection of the private key (e.g., OS-level key store with biometric unlock → AAL2; unprotected file-based certificate → AAL1).

QR code sign-in is an authentication pattern designed for environments where multiple users share a single device — retail point-of-sale terminals, hospital workstations, factory floor tablets, warehouse scanners, and other frontline worker scenarios. The pattern eliminates the need for the user to type credentials on the shared device, reducing both the time-per-sign-in and the risk of credential capture by keyloggers or shoulder surfing.
passages.headingText: 10.5 QR Code Sign-In for Shared and Frontline Devices

passages.text: Two distinct QR code sign-in architectures exist:
Pattern A — Dynamic QR displayed on shared device, scanned by personal device:
Shared device (e.g., kiosk, POS terminal) requests a new authentication session from the IdP
IdP generates a session-specific nonce and encodes it into a QR code
Shared device displays the QR code on its screen
User scans the QR code with their personal device (phone camera or authenticator app)
User authenticates on their personal device (biometric, PIN, or existing session)
Personal device sends the authentication assertion plus the session nonce to the IdP
IdP links the user's identity to the nonce → establishes an authenticated session on the shared device
Shared device receives session confirmation and grants access
Pattern B — Static QR assigned to user, scanned by shared device:
Each user is assigned a unique QR code (printed on a badge, lanyard, or ID card)
User presents their badge to the shared device's camera or scanner
The QR code contents identify the user (e.g., a user-specific token or identifier)
The shared device prompts for a PIN to confirm identity (second factor)
Server validates the QR code identifier + PIN combination
Authenticated session is established
Pattern A provides stronger security (the QR code is ephemeral and session-bound), while Pattern B provides faster sign-in (no need to unlock a personal phone — just present a badge and enter a PIN).

Session binding in Pattern A: The QR code contains a session-specific, cryptographically random nonce that binds the personal device's authentication to the shared device's session. The nonce is valid for a short duration (typically 2–5 minutes). An attacker who photographs the QR code must complete authentication within this window — and must possess valid credentials on the personal device.

Session isolation on shared devices: QR code sign-in is typically deployed with a shared device mode (e.g., Entra ID Shared Device Mode) that enforces global sign-out when the user's session ends. When one user signs out, all application data, tokens, caches, and local state from their session are purged from the device before the next user signs in.

Limitations of Pattern B: The static QR code on a badge is the user's identifier, not a secret — it can be photographed. Security relies entirely on the PIN as the knowledge factor. If the PIN is weak (4 digits = 10,000 combinations) or observed via shoulder surfing, the badge + PIN combination can be replayed. Pattern B is appropriate for low-assurance scenarios (e.g., clocking in to a shift) but not for accessing sensitive data.

Assurance level: QR code sign-in (Pattern B with badge + PIN) is a single-factor authenticator at AAL1 — the QR badge serves as an identifier, and the PIN is the single authentication factor. Pattern A inherits the assurance level of the personal device's authentication method (e.g., if the user authenticates with a passkey on their phone → up to AAL2).

Cross-reference: §31 provides a detailed taxonomy of cross-device authentication patterns, including QR code-mediated flows. §14.9 covers the FIDO Cross-Device Authentication (CDA) protocol, which standardises QR-code-initiated cross-device FIDO2 authentication.
Shared device challenges and mitigations:
Device-bound credentials on shared devices

If a user registers a device-bound passkey or platform authenticator on a shared device, the credential persists after the user's session ends. The next user may inherit access to the previous user's account.
Use shared device mode (Entra ID, Android Shared Device Mode) that enforces global sign-out between users. Purge all credentials, tokens, and local state at session end.
Biometric ambiguity on shared devices

Multiple users may have biometric templates enrolled on the same device. The device must support multiple biometric profiles and correctly associate the authenticated profile with the appropriate user account.
Windows 10+ supports multiple Windows Hello profiles per device. Android 12+ supports biometric per-user isolation. Kiosk devices should disable biometric and use badge + PIN instead.
No personal device for push authentication
Frontline workers may not carry personal smartphones during shifts (e.g., factory floor, clean rooms, secure facilities). Push notifications to a personal device are not viable.
Deploy FIDO2 security keys (plug-in, no mobile required), QR code sign-in (scan from badge), or on-premises badge readers (smart card).
Shift worker credential rotation
Workers on rotating shifts share physical devices. Each shift change requires sign-out of the previous worker and sign-in of the next worker — with no credential leakage between shifts.
Enforce session timeouts aligned with shift length (8–12 hours). Use QR code sign-in (Pattern A — dynamic) for fast, credential-free shift transitions.
Devices in public locations (airports, hotels, retail) require passwordless authentication that does not leave any credential residue and cannot be used to track users across sessions.
Use per-session ephemeral credentials (no persistent registration). QR code sign-in (Pattern A) with the user's personal device. Automatic full-session wipe between users.

Bootstrap credentials solve the cold-start problem in passwordless deployments: how does a user authenticate for the first time when they have not yet enrolled any authenticator? And how does a user recover access when all registered authenticators are lost, broken, or unavailable?

Traditional systems solve this with passwords — the user sets a password during onboarding and can always fall back to it. In a passwordless environment, this fallback does not exist. Bootstrap and recovery credentials fill the gap with time-limited, administrator-issued, single-purpose credentials that provide just enough access to enroll a permanent authenticator.

Administrator action — An IT administrator or automated provisioning system generates a one-time credential for the target user. The credential is typically a random alphanumeric string, 8–12 characters, with a configured lifetime

Secure delivery — The credential is delivered to the user through an out-of-band channel: in person (printed on paper), via a separate authenticated channel (corporate email to a manager who delivers it verbally), or through a trusted device (push to a verified secondary device)
First sign-in — The user enters the bootstrap credential at the IdP's login screen. The IdP validates the credential and grants a time-limited session
Authenticator enrollment — During the bootstrap session, the user is prompted (or required) to register a permanent authenticator — FIDO2 security key, passkey, authenticator app, or biometric
Credential invalidation — The bootstrap credential is invalidated after successful use (single-use) or after its time window expires, whichever comes first

As a concrete example of the bootstrap credential pattern: Entra ID's Temporary Access Pass (TAP) is an admin-issued, time-bound passcode that satisfies MFA requirements, enabling users to onboard passwordless credentials:
Configurable: 10 minutes to 30 days
Single-use (invalidated after first sign-in) or multi-use (valid for unlimited sign-ins within lifetime)
8–48 characters, alphanumeric
TAP satisfies MFA requirements in Conditional Access policies
Administrator generates via Entra admin center, Microsoft Graph API, or PowerShell

New employee onboarding — Employee's first day: IT issues a TAP. Employee uses the TAP to sign in to their Entra ID account and immediately registers a FIDO2 security key or Windows Hello for Business. After enrollment, the TAP is no longer needed

Authenticator recovery — Employee loses their phone (primary authenticator). IT issues a single-use TAP, employee uses it to sign in, registers a new phone or security key, and regains access. The TAP expires after single use and cannot be reused

Shared device bootstrapping — For environments using Windows Autopilot deployment, a TAP can be used during the Out-of-Box Experience (OOBE) to authenticate the first user without requiring a password to be set on the account
Users cannot self-generate TAPs; administrative privilege is required
Maximum lifetime is capped at 30 days; recommended: minutes to hours, not days
TAP creation, usage, and expiration events are logged in the IdP's audit trail
Should be single-use for high-security scenarios
Single-use TAPs eliminate the window between first use and expiry during which a stolen TAP could enable unauthorized access
Should not replace permanent authentication
TAP is a bridge credential — the organisation should enforce enrollment of a permanent authenticator during the TAP session

Assurance level: Bootstrap credentials are inherently AAL1 — they are shared secrets delivered out-of-band. Their security value lies not in their own assurance level but in their role as a bridge to higher-assurance permanent authenticators. A well-designed bootstrap flow ensures that the AAL1 window exists for minutes, not days.
Passwordless failure modes and fallback strategies:
User loses phone, security key, or laptop containing registered authenticator
Complete lockout — user cannot authenticate
Backup FIDO2 key (stored securely), recovery codes, administrator-issued TAP, cloud-synced passkey recovery
Hardware malfunction (broken fingerprint sensor, dead TPM)
Lockout until device is repaired or replaced
Backup authenticator (different device or hardware key), TAP
Finger injury, face change (surgery, glasses), sensor malfunction
User cannot complete user verification step
Fall back to device PIN (if configured), re-enroll biometric, use backup authenticator
No internet connectivity (offline environment, network outage)
Push notifications, OTP delivery, and passkey cloud sync fail
FIDO2 security key (works offline for signature generation, though server verification requires network); offline TOTP
Authenticator app crash or reinstallation
App data cleared, phone factory reset
Registered device key is lost
Re-enroll authenticator; if device-bound, requires backup authenticator or TAP
Push notification delivery failure
APNs/FCM outage, notification blocked by Doze mode, VPN interference
Push challenge never reaches user
Fallback to TOTP code in the same authenticator app, or SMS OTP
User refuses to adopt passwordless
Preference for familiar password workflow, privacy concerns with biometrics
User remains on password-only authentication (if still enabled)
Mandatory enrollment policy (with training and support); exception-based access with documented risk acceptance
Account recovery after extended inactivity
User has not authenticated in months; all authenticators expired or lost
Lockout with no recent authentication history
In-person identity verification, out-of-band contact via pre-registered phone/email, legal identity proofing

Account recovery is the most critical — and most frequently underestimated — challenge in passwordless deployments. When a user loses their authentication device and has no password to fall back on, the recovery mechanism must be robust enough to prevent account takeover but accessible enough to avoid permanent lockout.
High — hardware-bound, phishing-resistant
Medium — user must possess backup key
Issue two keys per user during enrollment; store one securely
Medium — single-use, must be stored securely
Medium — user must have printed/stored codes
Generate 5–10 single-use recovery codes during enrollment
Medium — time-limited, admin-issued
Low — requires helpdesk interaction
Helpdesk workflow for TAP generation (§10.6.2)
Medium-High — protected by cloud account MFA
High — automatic on new device
Cloud keychain must be configured with strong MFA
In-person identity verification
Very High — manual identity proofing
Very Low — requires physical presence
Secure facility, trained staff, identity verification protocol
Low — inherits email security
Not recommended as sole recovery method for high-assurance accounts

Best practice — defence in depth for recovery: Organisations at Stage 2 or Stage 3 of the passwordless maturity model (§10.0) should implement at least two independent recovery mechanisms. A recommended configuration: (1) syncable passkeys for automatic device recovery, plus (2) a hardware backup FIDO2 key stored securely (e.g., in a physical safe or bank deposit box), plus (3) administrator-issued TAP as a last resort. This three-layer approach ensures that no single point of failure can permanently lock out the user.

Enterprise IdPs increasingly provide extension frameworks that allow third-party authentication providers to register custom authentication methods directly within the IdP's authentication pipeline. This pattern enables organisations to use specialised authenticators (hardware tokens from niche vendors, national eID schemes, biometric solutions, legacy authentication systems) without abandoning their primary IdP.
Registration — The external authentication provider registers with the IdP by providing an OIDC-compatible endpoint (discovery URL, client ID, supported grant types)
Policy configuration — The IdP administrator configures Conditional Access or authentication policies to include the external method as an available (or required) authentication option

Authentication flow — When the IdP's policy engine determines that the external method should be invoked: The IdP redirects the user's browser to the external provider's authorization endpoint The external provider authenticates the user using its own mechanism (biometric scan, hardware token challenge, national eID wallet, etc.) Upon successful authentication, the external provider issues an ID token (OIDC) or assertion and redirects the user back to the IdP's callback URL The IdP validates the external provider's token/assertion against the pre-registered trust configuration The IdP incorporates the external authentication result into its overall authentication context (acr/amr claims) and completes the sign-in flow
The IdP redirects the user's browser to the external provider's authorization endpoint
The external provider authenticates the user using its own mechanism (biometric scan, hardware token challenge, national eID wallet, etc.)
Upon successful authentication, the external provider issues an ID token (OIDC) or assertion and redirects the user back to the IdP's callback URL
The IdP validates the external provider's token/assertion against the pre-registered trust configuration
The IdP incorporates the external authentication result into its overall authentication context (acr/amr claims) and completes the sign-in flow
Claims enrichment — The IdP can map claims from the external provider's token into the final access/ID token issued to the relying party

As an example of this pattern: Entra ID's External Authentication Methods (EAM), released in public preview in May 2024 and renamed to "External MFA" in early 2026, replaces the earlier "Custom Controls" feature. EAM operates on an open OIDC-based model:
External MFA provider registers an application in Entra ID (App Registration)
Administrator configures the external method with the provider's OIDC discovery URL and client ID
The external method appears as an available MFA option in Conditional Access policies, Privileged Identity Management (PIM) role activation, and Identity Protection risk-based policies

When triggered, Entra ID performs an OIDC authorization code flow with the external provider; the external provider authenticates the user and returns an ID token; Entra ID validates the token and grants MFA satisfaction
National identity card authenticator (e.g., Dutch DigiD, German eID/AusweisApp)
Country-specific eID protocols not natively supported by global IdPs
Proprietary token from a hardware vendor not supported by FIDO2 (e.g., legacy RSA SecurID hardware tokens)
Legacy OATH/proprietary protocols not directly compatible with the IdP's built-in methods
Third-party biometric authentication (palm vein, voice recognition, behavioral biometrics)
Specialised biometric modalities beyond standard platform biometrics
Legacy authentication bridges
On-premises RADIUS or custom authentication server wrapped in an OIDC facade
Organisation has an existing authentication investment that cannot be migrated to the cloud IdP immediately

Security considerations: The IdP delegates authentication to the external provider — the IdP's assurance guarantee is only as strong as the external provider's implementation. The IdP cannot verify how the external provider authenticated the user (was it a hardware token or a weak SMS OTP?). This requires strong contractual and technical assurance that the external provider meets the organisation's security requirements. The external provider's OIDC endpoint becomes a critical dependency — if it is unavailable, users configured for external MFA cannot authenticate.
The following table compares 15 passwordless methods across 12 security, usability, and deployment dimensions:
High-security enterprise, developer accounts
W3C WebAuthn, FIDO Alliance sync
Consumer applications, broad workforce
US federal government, defence
Enterprise managed Windows fleets
Vendor-specific (Duo, Microsoft)
Consumer apps (not recommended for enterprise)
Legacy MFA, developer accounts
Consumer onboarding, low-risk B2C
Legacy fallback (being deprecated)
Shared device + personal device
Shared devices, frontline workers
Badge + shared device scanner
Shift clock-in, low-risk access
First-time enrollment, recovery
Key observations from the comparison matrix:

Only 4 methods are phishing-resistant: FIDO2 security keys, syncable passkeys, device-bound passkeys, and TPM-backed certificates. Smart cards are TLS-bound (not origin-bound) but still prevent credential phishing since the private key cannot be extracted
AAL3 is achievable by only 3 methods: FIDO2 security keys, smart cards (PIV/CAC), and TPM-backed certificates — all hardware-bound with non-exportable keys

Cost follows a U-curve: The cheapest methods (magic links, SMS OTP) are also the least secure; the most secure methods (smart cards, FIDO2 keys) require hardware investment; passkeys occupy the sweet spot of zero marginal cost with phishing-resistant security
Standardisation correlates with security: The phishing-resistant methods are all based on open standards (W3C WebAuthn, FIDO CTAP2, FIPS 201); the weakest methods have no formal standardisation
Passwords vs passkeys — 12-dimension comparison: The following table directly contrasts passwords and passkeys (FIDO2/WebAuthn) across the most operationally relevant dimensions:
❌ Vulnerable — users enter credentials on fake sites
✅ Immune — credential bound to RP origin
❌ High — 64–73% reuse rate across services
✅ None — unique key pair per service
❌ Hash database enables offline cracking
✅ Public keys useless for impersonation
⚠️ Hash database is high-value target
✅ Public keys have no authentication value
Biometric scan or PIN (1 action)
Recovery code or re-enrollment
⚠️ Requires sync or security key
⚠️ Server requires connectivity
✅ Possible (device-local, limited use cases)
✅ Username + password on any device
❌ Device-bound; needs roaming authenticator
✅ Works with screen readers, switch devices
⚠️ Requires biometric or PIN capability
Medium (WebAuthn API + backend changes)
~7 years (WebAuthn W3C CR 2019, ISO 18073)
Second factor comparison (password + factor): When passwords remain the first factor, the choice of second factor determines the overall security properties of the MFA combination:
✅ Low (existing infrastructure)
⚠️ Acceptable with safeguards

Key takeaway from NIST Rev 4: SMS OTP is explicitly deprecated for use as a second factor due to SIM swap vulnerabilities and real-time phishing. FIDO2 security keys are the recommended second factor for high-assurance applications. TOTP remains acceptable but provides no phishing resistance.
NIST SP 800-63-4 defines three Authenticator Assurance Levels (AAL) with specific requirements for each level. The following table maps AAL requirements to passwordless method capabilities:
