passages.text: The practical implication: organisations targeting AAL2 or AAL3 must deploy FIDO2/WebAuthn or equivalent hardware-bound cryptographic authentication. OTP-based methods, push notifications, and magic links cannot achieve AAL2 in single-factor mode and are prohibited at AAL3.
passages.headingText: 10.8.1 NIST AAL Requirements for Passwordless Methods

passages.text: Transitioning from password-based to passwordless authentication requires careful planning to avoid user lockout, maintain business continuity, and achieve security objectives. Three migration strategies are commonly employed:
Strategy 1 — Parallel operation (phased rollout):
The organisation runs password and passwordless authentication in parallel for a defined transition period. Users can authenticate with either method. The rollout proceeds in phases:
Pilot phase (1–3 months) — Deploy passwordless to a small group of tech-savvy early adopters (IT department, security team). Monitor adoption rates, helpdesk ticket volume, and failure modes.
Department rollout (3–6 months) — Expand to selected departments with high password-related support costs (customer support, sales, field operations). Provide training and onboarding materials.
Organisation-wide rollout (6–12 months) — Deploy to all users. Begin monitoring password usage — users who never switch to passwordless are flagged for targeted outreach.
Password disablement (12–18 months) — After > 95% of users have adopted passwordless, disable passwords for the remaining population. Provide a grace period with administrator-assisted enrollment.
Password elimination (18+ months) — Remove password as an authentication option entirely (Stage 3 of the maturity model in §10.0).
Advantage: Lowest risk; user adoption drives the timeline; rollback is trivial
Risk: Extended parallel operation period maintains the password attack surface; requires ongoing maintenance of two authentication stacks
Recommended for: Large organisations (> 1,000 users), organisations with diverse user populations and device ecosystems

NIST SP 800-63B Rev 4 provides explicit guidance for password-to-passwordless migration in the specific case of passkeys. The recommended approach is incremental: (1) add passkey as an option alongside passwords — users can register passkeys while continuing to use passwords; (2) encourage passkey adoption — prompt users to create passkeys during login, highlight security benefits, provide onboarding tutorials; (3) default to passkey when available — for users who have registered a passkey, default to passkey login with password as fallback; (4) deprecate passwords for returning users — users with passkeys are no longer offered the password option; (5) full passwordless — eliminate password infrastructure entirely and require passkeys for all accounts. AAL implications: a single passkey satisfies AAL2 in the NIST model (memorised secret + single-factor hardware authenticator); a passkey on a hardware security key satisfies AAL3 when combined with a second factor; password-only authentication satisfies only AAL1 regardless of the password's length or complexity.
Strategy 2 — Big-bang migration:

All users are migrated to passwordless simultaneously on a defined cutover date. Every user must have a registered passwordless authenticator before the cutover. Password is disabled for all users at once.
Advantage: Faster transition (weeks instead of months); no parallel operation security risk
Risk: Higher helpdesk load during cutover; users who miss the enrollment window are locked out
Recommended for: Smaller organisations (< 1,000 users), organisations with strong IT support infrastructure, situations requiring rapid security improvement (e.g., post-breach remediation)
Strategy 3 — Opportunistic adoption:
Passwordless is offered as an option but not mandated. Users can adopt passwordless at their own pace. Password remains the default and fallback indefinitely.
Advantage: Lowest disruption; no lockout risk
Risk: Slow adoption (often < 30% after 12 months); password attack surface never fully eliminated
Recommended for: Consumer-facing applications (where mandating hardware is impractical), organisations in the early exploration phase
Passwordless adoption barriers: Despite the security advantages, significant barriers remain to widespread passwordless adoption:

Technical barriers: (1) Legacy system integration — many enterprise applications authenticate via username/password (LDAP, Active Directory, legacy API keys). Replacing these requires middleware (e.g., Azure AD → legacy app bridge) or application rewrite. (2) Account recovery without passwords — if a user loses all devices with passkeys, recovery requires an alternative mechanism (recovery codes, SMS OTP, in-person identity verification), each introducing its own security trade-offs (§10.6.3). (3) Shared devices and kiosks — passkeys are bound to a specific device/account; shared workstations, library computers, and point-of-sale terminals cannot use device-bound credentials without a roaming authenticator (security key or password manager). (4) Cross-platform portability — platform-native passkeys (iCloud Keychain, Google Password Manager) do not synchronise across ecosystems; a user with an iPhone and a Windows PC cannot use an Apple passkey on Windows without a cross-platform password manager.

Organisational barriers: (1) Helpdesk burden — password resets are a well-understood helpdesk workflow; passkey recovery is unfamiliar to support staff and users, increasing support costs during transition. (2) Compliance lag — regulatory frameworks (PCI DSS, HIPAA, SOX) were written with password-based authentication in mind; compliance teams require explicit guidance on passwordless acceptance — NIST SP 800-63B Rev 4 provides this, but auditor training lags. (3) MFA overlap — many organisations have recently invested in MFA deployment (hardware keys, authenticator apps); passwordless adoption may conflict with existing MFA policies (e.g., "require security key as second factor" is redundant when the password itself is replaced by a security key).

User experience barriers: (1) Conceptual model — users understand "type your password"; the abstract model of "your device proves you are who you say you are without sharing a secret" is unfamiliar and can create confusion during onboarding. (2) Device dependency anxiety — users worry about losing access if their phone is lost, broken, or stolen; education about recovery mechanisms is essential. (3) Biometric uncertainty — some users are uncomfortable with biometric data collection, even when the biometric template never leaves the device's secure enclave.
Passwordless deployment must align with relevant regulatory frameworks. The following table maps seven major regulations and standards to their passwordless requirements:
Phishing-resistant authentication required for federal systems; AAL2/AAL3 dictates specific method requirements
FIDO2/WebAuthn, hardware-backed CBA, smart card + PIN
SMS/voice OTP classified as "restricted authenticators" — require risk disclosure and migration plan
Federal agencies must implement phishing-resistant MFA for all accounts
Deadline: end of FY2025; enforcement via FISMA audits
European Digital Identity Wallet must support passwordless authentication; LoA High requires multi-factor with hardware binding
eIDAS-conformant eID wallets, QWACs, qualified electronic attestations
Applicable from 2026; national eID schemes (DigiD, SPID, AusweisApp2) serve as passwordless authenticators
Strong Customer Authentication (SCA) requires multi-factor; possession factor can be smartphone with enrolled certificate
Device certificates, push + number matching, biometric on device
PSD3 (proposed) may explicitly recognise passkeys as satisfying SCA requirements
"Addressable" implementation of access controls; no specific method mandated but must be "reasonable and appropriate"
FIDO2 keys recommended for clinician workstations; CBA for EHR access
OCR guidance favours phishing-resistant methods
Multi-factor authentication required for all access to cardholder data environments
FIDO2/WebAuthn, hardware tokens, push + number matching
Requirement 8.4.2 — MFA must use two or more factors from different categories
Annex A.9 (Access control) requires authentication mechanisms appropriate to the risk
Risk-dependent; FIDO2 recommended for high-risk assets
No specific technology mandate; risk assessment drives method selection
Real-world government deployments demonstrate the feasibility of passwordless authentication at scale:
FIDO2 security keys, TOTP, PIV smart card, SMS OTP (being deprecated)
300M+ eligible users; 100M+ accounts
Open-source platform; supports AAL2/AAL3; passwordless-first since 2022; all federal agencies migrating to Login.gov as shared authentication platform
FIDO2 passkeys, magic links, identity verification via certified providers
Phases: username/password → passwordless with passkeys; government-backed identity verification for LoA2/LoA3; integrates with NHS, HMRC, DWP
eIDAS-compliant national schemes
National eID cards, mobile ID apps (DigiD, SPID, AusweisApp2, MitID, FranceConnect)
Each member state implements one or more eID schemes conformant to eIDAS LoA Substantial/High; eIDAS 2.0 introduces EU Digital Identity Wallet; cross-border recognition via eIDAS node
FIDO2, face verification, OTP, digital token app
4.5M+ users (≈80% of population)
National digital identity platform; supports government and private sector authentication; highly mature — passwordless since 2018
Aadhaar-based authentication
Biometric (fingerprint, iris) + OTP
Largest biometric ID system globally; UIDAI authentication API supports passwordless biometric verification; privacy concerns regarding centralised biometric database

Choosing the right passwordless method depends on the organisation's risk profile, user population, device ecosystem, regulatory requirements, and budget. The following framework provides a structured approach across five tools.
Decision tree — AAL target → user population → phishing exposure:

Determine the target AAL level: If AAL1 is sufficient (low-risk internal applications, consumer B2C with no sensitive data): any method in §10.8 qualifies. Prioritise user experience (passkeys, magic links) over security. If AAL2 is required (enterprise workforce, applications processing non-public data, regulatory compliance): phishing-resistant methods are recommended but not strictly required for multi-factor configurations. FIDO2 passkeys or security keys are the recommended default; push + number matching is acceptable as a transitional measure. If AAL3 is required (government systems handling CUI, financial trading platforms, critical infrastructure control systems): only hardware-bound, non-exportable credentials qualify — FIDO2 security keys, smart cards (PIV/CAC), TPM-backed certificates. No software-only or OTP-based method is permissible.
Determine the target AAL level:
If AAL1 is sufficient (low-risk internal applications, consumer B2C with no sensitive data): any method in §10.8 qualifies. Prioritise user experience (passkeys, magic links) over security.

If AAL2 is required (enterprise workforce, applications processing non-public data, regulatory compliance): phishing-resistant methods are recommended but not strictly required for multi-factor configurations. FIDO2 passkeys or security keys are the recommended default; push + number matching is acceptable as a transitional measure.

If AAL3 is required (government systems handling CUI, financial trading platforms, critical infrastructure control systems): only hardware-bound, non-exportable credentials qualify — FIDO2 security keys, smart cards (PIV/CAC), TPM-backed certificates. No software-only or OTP-based method is permissible.

Assess the user population and device ecosystem: Enterprise-managed devices (Windows, macOS): Deploy device-bound passkeys (Windows Hello for Business, Mac Touch ID) as primary method. Issue FIDO2 security keys as backup for users with incompatible devices. BYOD (mixed iOS, Android, Windows, macOS): Deploy syncable passkeys as primary method (automatic cross-device recovery via cloud keychain). Require FIDO2 security key as backup for privileged accounts. Frontline / shared devices: Deploy QR code sign-in (Pattern A) or FIDO2 security keys (plug-in, no personal device required). Avoid device-bound credentials on shared devices. Kiosk / public access: Deploy QR code sign-in with ephemeral per-session credentials. No persistent registration on the kiosk device. Government / defence (high assurance): Deploy smart cards (PIV/CAC) as primary, FIDO2 security keys as secondary. Require hardware-backed UV.
Assess the user population and device ecosystem:

Enterprise-managed devices (Windows, macOS): Deploy device-bound passkeys (Windows Hello for Business, Mac Touch ID) as primary method. Issue FIDO2 security keys as backup for users with incompatible devices.

BYOD (mixed iOS, Android, Windows, macOS): Deploy syncable passkeys as primary method (automatic cross-device recovery via cloud keychain). Require FIDO2 security key as backup for privileged accounts.
Frontline / shared devices: Deploy QR code sign-in (Pattern A) or FIDO2 security keys (plug-in, no personal device required). Avoid device-bound credentials on shared devices.
Kiosk / public access: Deploy QR code sign-in with ephemeral per-session credentials. No persistent registration on the kiosk device.
Government / defence (high assurance): Deploy smart cards (PIV/CAC) as primary, FIDO2 security keys as secondary. Require hardware-backed UV.

Evaluate phishing threat exposure: If the organisation is a known target for phishing (financial services, technology companies, government agencies): mandate phishing-resistant methods (FIDO2/WebAuthn or CBA) for all users. Do not rely on push-based MFA. If phishing risk is moderate (enterprise with security awareness training, limited external attack surface): phishing-resistant methods recommended; push + number matching acceptable as a secondary option during transition. If phishing risk is low (internal-only applications, air-gapped networks, small organisations): any method at the target AAL is acceptable, though phishing-resistant methods provide the best long-term security investment.
Evaluate phishing threat exposure:

If the organisation is a known target for phishing (financial services, technology companies, government agencies): mandate phishing-resistant methods (FIDO2/WebAuthn or CBA) for all users. Do not rely on push-based MFA.

If phishing risk is moderate (enterprise with security awareness training, limited external attack surface): phishing-resistant methods recommended; push + number matching acceptable as a secondary option during transition.

If phishing risk is low (internal-only applications, air-gapped networks, small organisations): any method at the target AAL is acceptable, though phishing-resistant methods provide the best long-term security investment.
Enterprise workforce (managed devices)

passages.text: Organisations evaluating passwordless deployment should consider the following cost categories and benefit sources:
Hardware procurement (FIDO2 keys, smart card readers, kiosk hardware): $25–55 per user × total user count
PKI infrastructure setup (if deploying CBA): $50,000–200,000 for enterprise CA + issuance automation
Identity provider configuration and testing: 2–6 months of engineering effort
User training and change management: $10–50 per user (documentation, videos, in-person training)
Migration project management: 1–2 FTEs for 6–12 months
Hardware replacement (lost, broken, expired): 5–15% of hardware inventory per year
Helpdesk support for passwordless issues: initially higher (spike), then 20–40% lower than password-related support
Certificate lifecycle management (if deploying CBA): $5–15 per user per year
Cloud identity provider licensing (FIDO2 features may require premium tier): varies by vendor
Reduced password reset helpdesk costs: 20–40% reduction (Gartner estimates $25–100 per reset incident)

Reduced breach risk from credential phishing: phishing-resistant methods eliminate the #1 initial access vector; average breach cost savings of $1–4M per prevented incident (IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report 2024)
Improved user productivity: 3.5× faster sign-in with passkeys vs password + MFA (Microsoft data); 10–20 hours/year per user saved on password management tasks
Reduced compliance audit findings: passwordless deployment demonstrates proactive security investment; reduces findings related to credential management

Net benefit estimate: For a 10,000-user enterprise, a passwordless deployment (passkeys primary + FIDO2 security key backup) typically achieves positive ROI within 12–18 months, driven primarily by reduced helpdesk costs and improved productivity.
Before initiating a passwordless deployment, organisations should evaluate their readiness across five dimensions:
Identity infrastructure maturity
Is there a standards-based IdP capable of supporting FIDO2 APIs?
OIDC/SAML IdP with WebAuthn registration and authentication endpoints; SCIM provisioning support; Conditional Access or equivalent policy engine
What percentage of user devices have TPM, Secure Enclave, or biometric capabilities?
> 90% coverage = ready; 70–90% = ready with security key supplementation; < 70% = requires hardware procurement plan
For CBA deployments, is there an operational PKI with automated lifecycle management?
Active CA with automated enrollment (AD CS, Venafi, HashiCorp Vault); CRL/OCSP infrastructure; certificate policy OIDs for hardware-backed issuance
Is the helpdesk trained on passwordless troubleshooting?
Documented procedures for: lost security key replacement, failed biometric enrollment, passkey sync failure, recovery code issuance, TAP generation
Has the organisation conducted user training on passwordless methods?
User awareness campaign completed; pilot programme feedback collected; executive sponsorship secured; change champions identified in each department

A readiness score below 3/5 in any dimension indicates a gap that should be addressed before full-scale deployment. A phased rollout (Strategy 1 in §10.9) is recommended for organisations with gaps in one or two dimensions. Organisations scoring below 3/5 in three or more dimensions should consider a pilot programme before committing to a full migration.
For practitioners who need a rapid decision without working through the full decision tree:

passages.text: Understanding what not to do is as important as choosing the right method. The following anti-patterns are frequently observed in passwordless deployments:
Passwordless + password fallback (indefinite)
Deploying passwordless but keeping password as a permanent, unconditional fallback for all users

The password attack surface remains fully open. Attackers who obtain passwords (via breach, phishing, credential stuffing) can authenticate regardless of the user's passwordless enrollment. The security benefit is marginal — the organisation has the cost of two authentication systems with the security of one
Set a deadline for password disablement. Use the maturity model (§10.0) to transition through stages within 12–18 months
Relying on only one recovery method (e.g., only email OTP for account recovery)
The recovery mechanism becomes the weakest link. If the email account is compromised, the passwordless account is fully compromised — the passwordless credential provides no protection
Implement multi-layer recovery: syncable passkey (automatic), backup FIDO2 key (hardware), admin TAP (last resort)
Device-bound-only deployment without backup
Deploying only device-bound passkeys (or Windows Hello for Business) without providing backup authenticators
If the user loses their device, they are permanently locked out. There is no recovery path. This creates a helpdesk crisis and user frustration that can derail the entire passwordless programme
Issue a backup FIDO2 security key during enrollment; store it securely for recovery use
Equating push authentication with phishing resistance
Deploying push notification authentication and claiming phishing-resistant MFA compliance

Push authentication (even with number matching) is vulnerable to AiTM relay attacks. An attacker with a phishing proxy can relay the entire authentication flow in real time. This does not satisfy NIST SP 800-63-4 phishing-resistant requirements
Deploy FIDO2/WebAuthn alongside push authentication; use FIDO2 as the primary method and push as a convenience option
Ignoring the shared device problem
Deploying device-bound passwordless credentials (passkeys, platform authenticators) on shared devices without shared device mode

The next user who accesses the shared device may inherit the previous user's credentials and session. This is an authentication boundary violation — one user's passwordless credential grants access to another user's account
Use shared device mode (Entra ID Shared Device Mode, Android Shared Device Mode) that enforces global sign-out and credential purge between users
Mandating passwordless without providing hardware
Requiring all users to use FIDO2 security keys without providing the hardware
Users who cannot afford security keys, or whose devices lack TPM/Secure Enclave, are excluded from accessing the system. This creates an accessibility and equity problem
Budget hardware procurement as part of the deployment plan; offer device-bound passkeys as a zero-cost alternative where hardware is available
Deploying passwordless without user training
Rolling out passwordless authentication without user awareness, training, and support resources

Users who do not understand passwordless methods may resist adoption, incorrectly use recovery mechanisms, or fall victim to social engineering attacks that exploit confusion about the new authentication flow
Invest in comprehensive user training: documentation, video tutorials, pilot programme with feedback loops, helpdesk escalation procedures
Relying solely on syncable passkeys for high-privilege accounts
Using only syncable passkeys (no hardware backup) for administrator, root, or privileged access accounts

If the user's cloud account (Apple ID, Google Account) is compromised, all synced passkeys — including those used for privileged access — are exposed. The cloud account becomes a single point of failure for all accounts
Require hardware-bound FIDO2 security keys for privileged accounts, regardless of the primary method used for standard accounts
Measuring the success of a passwordless deployment requires tracking quantitative and qualitative metrics across security, usability, and operational dimensions:
Passwordless authentication rate (% of all sign-ins)
> 80% within 6 months of full deployment
IdP authentication logs — filter by auth method
User enrollment rate (% of users with at least one passwordless credential)
> 95% within 3 months of rollout
Phishing-resistant authentication rate (% of sign-ins using FIDO2/CBA)
> 60% within 12 months (AAL2/AAL3 users)
IdP authentication logs — filter by phishing-resistant methods
Account compromise incidents related to credential theft
50% reduction within 12 months
Security incident tracking system
Password-related security incidents (credential stuffing, password spraying, password phishing)
90% reduction within 12 months
SIEM alerts, incident reports
Average sign-in time (seconds)
< 5 seconds for passwordless; < 3 seconds for passkey
IdP authentication log timestamps
Password reset helpdesk tickets
60% reduction within 6 months
User satisfaction score (post-rollout survey)
Passwordless-related helpdesk tickets (enrollment issues, device problems)
< 5% of total helpdesk volume (after initial 3-month spike)
Mean time to recovery (MTTR) for locked-out users
< 15 minutes (with backup FIDO2 key)
Helpdesk ticketing system — time from ticket creation to resolution

Success criteria: A passwordless deployment is considered successful when all of the following are met: (1) > 80% of all authentications are passwordless, (2) > 60% of authentications for AAL2/AAL3 users use phishing-resistant methods, (3) password-related helpdesk tickets have decreased by > 60%, and (4) user satisfaction score is > 4.0/5.0. Organisations that fall short on any criterion should investigate the root cause — common issues include device coverage gaps, inadequate user training, or policy misconfiguration that defaults to password authentication.

These metrics should be reviewed monthly during the first 6 months of deployment and quarterly thereafter. Deviations from targets should trigger a root cause analysis and, if necessary, an adjustment to the deployment strategy (e.g., expanding device procurement, increasing training frequency, or simplifying the enrollment workflow).

This group transitions from human memory to mathematical proof-of-possession. It covers the evolution of one-time password generation (HOTP, TOTP, OCRA) and provides an exhaustive analysis of the FIDO2/WebAuthn standard, detailing the ceremonies, attestation formats, and transport mechanisms that enable modern passkey deployments.

HOTP — HMAC-Based One-Time Password — is the foundational one-time password algorithm upon which TOTP (§12) and OCRA (§13) are built. Defined in RFC 4226 (December 2005) by the OATH (Initiative for Open Authentication) consortium, HOTP generates short numeric codes from a shared secret and a monotonically increasing counter. Each counter value produces exactly one valid code; once consumed, the counter advances, and the previous code becomes permanently invalid.

HOTP satisfies AAL1 when used as a single factor. Combined with a memorised secret (password), HOTP tokens achieve AAL2 (§1.1) — NIST SP 800-63B classifies a look-up secret or single-factor OTP device as an AAL1 authenticator, and a multi-factor OTP device (requiring PIN activation) or a single-factor OTP combined with a memorised secret as AAL2. HOTP alone can never reach AAL3, which requires cryptographic authentication with hardware-bound keys and verifier impersonation resistance — properties that OTP schemes fundamentally lack.
The HOTP algorithm is a three-stage pipeline: HMAC computation → dynamic truncation → modular reduction. The core function is:
K — shared secret key (minimum 128 bits; recommended 160 bits to match the HMAC-SHA-1 key length)
C — 8-byte counter value, encoded as a big-endian 64-bit unsigned integer, monotonically increasing
d — number of output digits (typically 6; RFC 4226 §9.3 requires d ≥ 6 and recommends d = 6 for usability)

The algorithm computes HMAC-SHA-1(K, C) as defined in RFC 2104. The counter value C is serialised as an 8-byte big-endian integer — counter value 0 is encoded as 0x0000000000000000, counter value 1 as 0x0000000000000001, and so on.
HMAC-SHA-1 produces a 20-byte (160-bit) output. Internally, the HMAC construction computes:
Where ipad is 64 bytes of 0x36 and opad is 64 bytes of 0x5C. If K is shorter than 64 bytes, it is zero-padded; if longer, it is first hashed with SHA-1 to reduce it to 20 bytes.

SHA-1 compression function internals. SHA-1 processes input in 512-bit (64-byte) blocks through a compression function fff. The algorithm maintains five 32-bit working variables initialised to fixed constants:
For each 512-bit message block, the 16 × 32-bit words are expanded to 80 words using a recurrence relation, then processed through 80 compression rounds with round-dependent functions and constants:
Ch(x,y,z)=(x∧y)∨(¬x∧z)\text\\{Ch\\}(x,y,z) = (x \wedge y) \vee (\lnot x \wedge z)Ch(x,y,z)=(x∧y)∨(¬x∧z)
Parity(x,y,z)=x⊕y⊕z\text\\{Parity\\}(x,y,z) = x \oplus y \oplus zParity(x,y,z)=x⊕y⊕z
Maj(x,y,z)=(x∧y)∨(x∧z)∨(y∧z)\text\\{Maj\\}(x,y,z) = (x \wedge y) \vee (x \wedge z) \vee (y \wedge z)Maj(x,y,z)=(x∧y)∨(x∧z)∨(y∧z)
Parity(x,y,z)=x⊕y⊕z\text\\{Parity\\}(x,y,z) = x \oplus y \oplus zParity(x,y,z)=x⊕y⊕z

Each round updates a working variable: a←ROTL5(a)+ft(b,c,d)+e+Kt+Wta \leftarrow \text\\{ROTL\\}^5(a) + f_t(b,c,d) + e + K_t + W_ta←ROTL5(a)+ft​(b,c,d)+e+Kt​+Wt​. The security of HMAC-SHA-1 for HOTP relies on the pseudorandom function (PRF) property of this keyed compression function, not on SHA-1's collision resistance (§11.4.4).
The 20-byte HMAC output is too long to serve as a human-readable code. RFC 4226 §9.3 defines a dynamic truncation (DT) function that extracts a 4-byte (31-bit) segment from the HMAC value:
Let HS = the 20-byte HMAC-SHA-1 result (bytes indexed 0–19)
Take the low-order 4 bits of the last byte: offset = HS[19] & 0x0F — this yields an offset value between 0 and 15
Extract 4 bytes starting at offset: P = HS[offset..offset+3]
Mask the most significant bit to produce a 31-bit unsigned integer: binary_code = (P[0] & 0x7F) << 24 | P[1] << 16 | P[2] << 8 | P[3]
The MSB is masked to avoid sign-bit ambiguity across programming languages that represent integers differently (signed vs. unsigned). The result is always a positive 31-bit integer.
Visual representation of dynamic truncation:
The 31-bit integer (range 0–2,147,483,647) is reduced to a d-digit code:
For the standard 6-digit configuration: 1284755224 mod 1,000,000 = 755224.
The final HOTP code is left-padded with zeros to ensure exactly d digits — a result of 42 becomes 000042 for d = 6.
Digit-length security comparison:
Default — broad consumer use
Moderate security — enterprise
High security — banking, government

The modular reduction introduces a slight bias — 31-bit values (max 2^\\{31\\} - 1 = 2\\{,\\}147\\{,\\}483\\{,\\}647) are not evenly divisible by 10d10^d10d. For d=6d = 6d=6, codes 000–147,483147\\{,\\}483147,483 appear with probability \lceil 2^\\{31\\}/10^6 \rceil / 2^\\{31\\} while codes 147,484147\\{,\\}484147,484–999,999999\\{,\\}999999,999 appear with probability \lfloor 2^\\{31\\}/10^6 \rfloor / 2^\\{31\\}. This bias is negligible (≈0.007%\approx 0.007\%≈0.007%) and has no practical security impact.
RFC 4226 Appendix D provides test vectors using the ASCII secret key "12345678901234567890" (hex: 0x3132333435363738393031323334353637383930, 20 bytes = 160 bits):
cc93cf18508d94934c64b65d8ba7667fb7cde4b0
75a48a19d4cbe100644e8ac1397eea747a2d33ab
a4fb960c0bc06e1eabb804e5b397cdc4b45596fa
These test vectors serve as the canonical implementation verification suite — any HOTP implementation that produces different values for these inputs is incorrect.
1. Authentication Server generates cryptographic secret key

The Authentication Server independently generates a mathematically unpredictable shared secret KKK of 160 bits (20 bytes) leveraging a robust CSPRNG (e.g., /dev/urandom, CryptGenRandom). This foundational key material must remain absolutely identical synchronously across both the client hardware and the backend infrastructure.
Security Audit: The Authentication Server logs an HOTP_SECRET_GENERATED telemetry event noting the creation time and cryptographic suite, without exposing the key material itself.
2. Authentication Server delivers secret key to Client Token

The Authentication Server encodes KKK as a strict Base32 string and procedurally constructs an otpauth://hotp/ URI containing the raw secret, issuer label, and counter parameters. It renders this precise URI as a scannable QR code and displays it to the user. The Client Token (hardware authenticator or mobile app) optically scans the matrix and ingests the secret string into secure storage.
3. Authentication Server securely stores secret and initial counter

The Authentication Server permanently commits its copy of KKK into an encrypted database layer—ideally shielded by HSM-managed master keys—alongside a rigid initial integer counter value permanently seeded at C=0C = 0C=0.
Artifact Produced: HOTP_Shared_Secret (Encrypted database record)
4. User triggers OTP generation on Client Token
When prompted to authenticate, the user physically taps the hardware token button or opens their software Authenticator App, actively triggering the discrete HOTP code generation lifecycle.
5. Client Token increments internal counter

The Client Token strictly increments its internal integer counter by exactly one: C←C+1C \leftarrow C + 1C←C+1. Because HOTP depends exclusively on event synchronization rather than physical time, every discrete generation event rolls the counter permanently and irreversibly forward.
6. Client Token calculates foundational HMAC-SHA-1

The Client Token computes a mathematically secure hash by pushing the secret KKK and the newly minted counter CCC directly through the HMAC-SHA-1 algorithmic pipeline, outputting a 20-byte pseudo-random string.
7. Client Token applies dynamic truncation

In order to extract a short code from a 20-byte hash, the Client Token executes "dynamic truncation" (RFC 4226). It inspects the last 4 bits of the hash to dynamically select an offset, then strictly isolates a 4-byte chunk starting from that exact position. This definitively shrinks the broad digest into a deterministic 31-bit integer.
8. Client Token applies modular reduction
The Client Token slices the 31-bit integer down to a fundamentally human-readable size by applying a simple modulo operation (10610^6106), severing off all but the final 6 decimal digits.
9. Client Token displays final code to User
The Client Token's LCD screen or mobile interface illuminates, projecting the cleanly derived 6-digit passcode (e.g., 837265) out to the human user for consumption.
10. User Agent submits username and code
The user reads the active digits off their Client Token and manually blind-types them into the User Agent, which instantly transmits the authentication challenge payload onward to the backend server.
11. Authentication Server queries stored counter
The Authentication Server pulls the user's securely encrypted profile, safely unpacks KKK into active memory, and fetches the last known synchronized counter value, C_\\{\text\\{stored\\}\\}.
12. Authentication Server iteratively generates expected codes

Because the user realistically might have accidentally pushed their hardware token button without logging in, the Authentication Server iteratively generates a sequence of potential sequential hashes representing C_\\{\text\\{stored\\}\\} + 1, C_\\{\text\\{stored\\}\\} + 2, etc., up to an intentionally hard-capped "look-ahead" window limit (usually s=10s = 10s=10).
13. Authentication Server accepts valid code and updates internal state

If the user's submitted token completely matches any of the Authentication Server's localized look-ahead iterations, the server mathematically flags an ACCEPT state. Crucially, the Authentication Server systematically overwrites the obsolete C_\\{\text\\{stored\\}\\} with the newly matched iteration index, rigorously ensuring that exact code can never be successfully replayed.
14. Authentication Server returns authentication success to User Agent
The backend engine issues an HTTP 200 payload alongside a securely typed session cookie, granting the User Agent properly authenticated access to the target application framework.
Artifact Produced: session_cookie (Authoritative application session)
15. Authentication Server rejects invalid code and increments failure state

If the backend engine aggressively iterates through the entire look-ahead window and definitively fails to flag a mathematical match, it mandates a REJECT state and structurally increments a local brute-force failure tripwire.

Rejection Scenario: Upon exhaustion of the window without a match, the Authentication Server immediately records an HOTP_VERIFICATION_FAILED event in the SIEM and actively triggers account lockout if the failure threshold is breached.
16. Authentication Server returns authentication failure to User Agent
The authentication engine throws an HTTP 401 error payload back to the interface, denying access and rigorously dropping the session context.

The shared secret K must be generated using a cryptographically secure pseudorandom number generator (CSPRNG) — /dev/urandom on Linux, CryptGenRandom on Windows, or crypto.getRandomValues() in browser JavaScript.
Key length requirements (RFC 4226 §6):
160 bits matches the HMAC-SHA-1 internal key length; keys shorter than the hash output are zero-padded but provide less entropy than the algorithm can utilise

Keys longer than 160 bits provide no additional security for HMAC-SHA-1 — the HMAC construction hashes keys exceeding the block size (64 bytes for SHA-1) before use. However, some implementations use 256-bit or 320-bit secrets for forward compatibility with HMAC-SHA-256 or HMAC-SHA-512 when the same provisioned key may later be used with TOTP (§12) or OCRA (§13) using stronger hash functions.
The storage location of the shared secret is the most critical security decision in any HOTP deployment. Each storage option presents a distinct threat model and requires different mitigations.
All deployments (server-side requirement)
OS Keystore (Keychain/Keystore)
Secure Element (e.g., YubiKey)
Enterprise, government, high-security
Enterprise laptops, mobile devices
Banking, military, legacy enterprise
Data centres, payment processors
Government ID, banking cards

Server-side storage best practices: Encrypt secrets at rest using AES-256-GCM with an HSM-managed key; apply database-level encryption (TDE) as a defence-in-depth layer; restrict secret access to the authentication service account; audit all secret access events; rotate secrets on a defined schedule or upon suspected compromise.

The otpauth:// URI scheme is a de facto standard for OTP token provisioning, originally defined by Google for Google Authenticator and subsequently adopted by virtually every software authenticator application. The scheme is not defined in any RFC — it is documented in the Google Authenticator open-source project's Key URI Format specification.
hotp for counter-based, totp for time-based (§12)
Format: Issuer:AccountName (e.g., Acme:user@example.com). The issuer prefix is recommended but optional; the account name is required
Base32-encoded shared secret (RFC 4648, no padding). Example: a 160-bit key 0x48656c6c6f21deadbeef encodes as JBSWY3DPEHPK3PXP
Service provider name; should match the issuer prefix in the label. Displayed prominently in the authenticator app
Initial counter value (integer). Typically 0 for new provisioning. Required and specific to HOTP — TOTP URIs use period instead
Number of OTP digits (6 or 8). Most implementations only support 6
Hash algorithm: SHA1, SHA256, or SHA512. Note: many authenticator apps only support SHA1 for HOTP; specifying SHA256/512 may cause silent failures
Example provisioning URI for a production HOTP token:

The provisioning URI is typically delivered to the user as a QR code displayed during the MFA enrollment flow. The user scans the QR code with their authenticator app, which parses the otpauth:// URI and configures the token locally.
Error correction level: Level M (15% recovery) is the standard choice — provides resilience against screen glare and camera focus issues without significantly increasing QR code density
Encoding: The URI is encoded as UTF-8 text in the QR code data payload
Minimum module size: Each QR "pixel" (module) should be at least 4×4 screen pixels for reliable scanning at typical smartphone distances

Display context: The QR code should be displayed alongside a manual-entry fallback — the Base32-encoded secret in plaintext — for users who cannot scan QR codes (accessibility, screen-reader users, authenticator apps on the same device)
Security considerations during provisioning:
The QR code contains the shared secret in plaintext (Base32-encoded). Any actor who photographs or screenshots the QR code during display obtains the complete credential
The provisioning page should enforce authenticated sessions, short display timeouts, and explicit user acknowledgment before revealing the QR code

After the user confirms successful enrollment (by entering a verification OTP), the server should never display the secret again — the provisioning page should include language like "Save your recovery codes; you will not be able to view this secret again"

The fundamental operational challenge of HOTP is counter synchronisation between the client (authenticator device) and the server (verifier). Both parties must maintain the same counter value — or, more precisely, the server must be able to determine which counter value the client used to generate a given OTP.

The counter CCC is encoded as an 8-byte big-endian unsigned integer, providing a maximum of 2^\\{64\\} - 1 \approx 1.8 \times 10^\\{19\\} distinct OTP values — far exceeding any practical deployment lifetime. Even at 100 OTP generations per day, the counter would not overflow for approximately 4.9 \times 10^\\{11\\} years.
Counter desynchronisation occurs when the client's counter advances without a corresponding server-side validation:
User generates but doesn't submit
User presses the token button (incrementing the client counter) but navigates away, closes the app, or decides not to log in
Common — especially with hardware tokens where button presses are easy to trigger accidentally
User presses the token button multiple times in succession — each press generates a new OTP and increments the counter
Common with hardware tokens; less common with software tokens that display codes passively
Network failure during submission
User submits the OTP, but the request fails before reaching the server (network timeout, server error). Client counter has advanced; server counter has not
Occasional — depends on network reliability
Server-side validation failure
Server receives the OTP but rejects it due to a transient error (database lock, validation timeout). User generates a new OTP for retry
Rare but possible in high-availability architectures with eventual consistency

Each desynchronisation event increases the gap between the client and server counters by one. Multiple events accumulate — after several unused OTPs, the client counter may be significantly ahead of the server's stored value.

The server compensates for moderate desynchronisation using a look-ahead window — when verifying an OTP, the server checks not only the expected counter value C but also C+1, C+2, ..., C+s, where s is the configurable look-ahead window size.
Window size guidance (RFC 4226 §11.2):
RFC 4226 recommends a "small" look-ahead window without prescribing a specific value. In practice:
No tolerance — client and server must be exactly synchronised
Maximum security; impractical for real-world use
Reasonable default for software authenticators
Suitable for hardware tokens with dedicated buttons
Maximum practical; larger windows increase brute-force surface

Security impact of large windows: Each additional counter position in the look-ahead window gives an attacker one more chance per guess. With a 6-digit OTP output and a window of size s, the probability of a random guess succeeding on a single attempt is (s + 1) / 10⁶. For s = 20, this is 21 / 1,000,000 ≈ 2.1 × 10⁻⁵ — a modest increase from the baseline 10⁻⁶ but still within acceptable bounds when combined with rate limiting.

For severe desynchronisation (counter drift beyond the look-ahead window), RFC 4226 §11.4 defines a resynchronisation protocol that requires the user to provide two or more consecutive OTP values. The server searches a larger window (e.g., C to C + 1000) for a counter position where two sequential OTPs match the submitted pair.

The multi-OTP requirement reduces the false-positive risk of resynchronisation — the probability that two consecutive random 6-digit values match two consecutive HOTP outputs at the same counter position is (1/10⁶)² = 10⁻¹², making accidental or malicious false resync negligible.
Resynchronisation should be rate-limited and logged as a security event
Some implementations require explicit user action (e.g., a "Resync Token" button in the self-service portal) rather than performing automatic resync during normal authentication
The resync window (the larger search range) is typically 100–1,000 counter positions — large enough to recover from extended non-use but small enough to complete in reasonable time
With d output digits, each OTP has 10^d possible values. For the standard 6-digit configuration:
Probability of random guess per attempt: 10⁻⁶ (0.0001%)
Expected attempts for 50% success probability: ~693,000 (from the birthday bound: 0.693 × 10⁶)
RFC 4226 §11.3 mandates that the server MUST implement throttling after failed verification attempts. The recommended approach:
After T consecutive failed attempts (RFC 4226 suggests T ≤ a "small value"; practical implementations use T = 3 to T = 5), lock the HOTP function for that user
The lockout may be time-based (e.g., 30 minutes) or require administrative intervention
The throttling counter resets on successful authentication

Effective security with throttling: If the server locks after 3 failures, an attacker has at most 3 attempts before lockout. The probability of success across 3 random guesses is 1 - (1 - 10⁻⁶)³ ≈ 3 × 10⁻⁶ — effectively negligible.
Brute-force configuration analysis:
Per-Guess Success Probability

With standard rate limiting (3–5 failed attempts before lockout), even the most permissive window configuration (d=6d=6d=6, s=20s=20s=20) yields a per-session success probability of 6.3 \times 10^\\{-5\\} — negligible for practical attacks.

HOTP's replay protection is inherent in the counter mechanism — each counter value is valid for exactly one authentication. Once the server accepts an OTP at counter position C + n, it stores C + n + 1 as the next expected counter. Any attempt to resubmit the same OTP will fail because the server has advanced past that position.
passages.headingText: 11.4.2 No Replay Protection Beyond Counter Advancement

passages.text: An OTP is valid until used. Unlike TOTP (§12), where codes expire after a time window, an HOTP code remains valid indefinitely until either accepted by the server or superseded by the client generating a new code (advancing past it in the look-ahead window)
passages.headingText: 11.4.2 No Replay Protection Beyond Counter Advancement

passages.text: Stolen unused OTPs are dangerous. If an attacker intercepts an OTP before the user submits it (e.g., via shoulder surfing or a compromised display), the attacker can use it at any future time — there is no time-based expiry
passages.headingText: 11.4.2 No Replay Protection Beyond Counter Advancement

passages.text: Attack scenario — replay within the resynchronisation window:
passages.headingText: 11.4.2 No Replay Protection Beyond Counter Advancement

passages.text: Attacker shoulder-surfs the user's hardware token display, observing code 755224
passages.headingText: 11.4.2 No Replay Protection Beyond Counter Advancement

passages.text: User navigates away without submitting the code (counter incremented but server counter unchanged)
passages.headingText: 11.4.2 No Replay Protection Beyond Counter Advancement

passages.text: Attacker uses 755224 at any future time — it remains within the look-ahead window and is accepted
passages.headingText: 11.4.2 No Replay Protection Beyond Counter Advancement

passages.text: Limit OTP display duration (30–60 second auto-blank)
passages.headingText: 11.4.2 No Replay Protection Beyond Counter Advancement

passages.text: Moderate — reduces observation window
passages.headingText: 11.4.2 No Replay Protection Beyond Counter Advancement

passages.text: None — hardware tokens already do this
passages.headingText: 11.4.2 No Replay Protection Beyond Counter Advancement

passages.text: Require PIN before code display
passages.headingText: 11.4.2 No Replay Protection Beyond Counter Advancement

passages.text: High — attacker needs both PIN and visual access
passages.headingText: 11.4.2 No Replay Protection Beyond Counter Advancement

passages.text: Moderate — adds a step to authentication
passages.headingText: 11.4.2 No Replay Protection Beyond Counter Advancement

passages.text: Combine with password (AAL2)
passages.headingText: 11.4.2 No Replay Protection Beyond Counter Advancement

passages.text: High — attacker needs password + OTP
passages.headingText: 11.4.2 No Replay Protection Beyond Counter Advancement

passages.text: Very high — codes expire within seconds
passages.headingText: 11.4.2 No Replay Protection Beyond Counter Advancement

passages.text: None — TOTP is a usability improvement
passages.headingText: 11.4.2 No Replay Protection Beyond Counter Advancement

passages.text: Server-side transaction binding
passages.headingText: 11.4.2 No Replay Protection Beyond Counter Advancement

passages.text: The shared secret K is the single point of compromise for the entire HOTP scheme. If an attacker obtains K:
passages.headingText: 11.4.3 Key Confidentiality: The Single Point of Failure

passages.text: They can compute HOTP values for any counter position
passages.headingText: 11.4.3 Key Confidentiality: The Single Point of Failure

passages.text: They can predict all future OTPs (given the current counter value, which can be estimated from the user's login frequency)
passages.headingText: 11.4.3 Key Confidentiality: The Single Point of Failure

passages.text: There is no recovery mechanism short of re-provisioning a new secret
passages.headingText: 11.4.3 Key Confidentiality: The Single Point of Failure

passages.text: This makes secret storage the critical security control. Key extraction attacks target every stage of the key lifecycle:

passages.text: SHA-1's collision resistance was definitively broken by the SHAttered attack (2017, Google/CWI Amsterdam), which produced the first known collision — two distinct PDF documents with the same SHA-1 hash. This has led to SHA-1 deprecation for digital signatures, certificates, and data integrity verification.
passages.headingText: 11.4.4 Why HMAC-SHA-1 Remains Secure for HOTP

passages.text: However, HMAC-SHA-1 is not affected by SHA-1 collision attacks. The distinction is fundamental:
passages.headingText: 11.4.4 Why HMAC-SHA-1 Remains Secure for HOTP

passages.text: Collision resistance requires finding any two inputs x ≠ y such that H(x) = H(y). This is a property of the unkeyed hash function and is broken for SHA-1
passages.headingText: 11.4.4 Why HMAC-SHA-1 Remains Secure for HOTP

passages.text: HMAC security depends on the pseudorandom function (PRF) property — the requirement that HMAC-SHA-1(K, ·) is computationally indistinguishable from a random function when the key K is unknown to the attacker. This property holds even when the underlying hash function has collision weaknesses
passages.headingText: 11.4.4 Why HMAC-SHA-1 Remains Secure for HOTP

passages.text: The HMAC construction (RFC 2104) derives its security from the PRF property of the hash function's internal compression function, not from the hash function's collision resistance. The double hashing structure — SHA-1((K ⊕ opad) || SHA-1((K ⊕ ipad) || message)) — ensures that an attacker who cannot observe the intermediate hash state (because they don't know K) cannot exploit collision-finding techniques.
passages.headingText: 11.4.4 Why HMAC-SHA-1 Remains Secure for HOTP

passages.text: Formal result: Bellare (2006) proved that HMAC remains a secure PRF even if the underlying hash function is not collision-resistant, provided that the compression function is a PRF. No known attack compromises this property for SHA-1's compression function.
passages.headingText: 11.4.4 Why HMAC-SHA-1 Remains Secure for HOTP

passages.text: ✅ Confident — HOTP requires computing a preimage of HMAC output
passages.headingText: 11.4.4 Why HMAC-SHA-1 Remains Secure for HOTP

passages.text: ❌ No impact — Bellare's proof shows HMAC-PRF security does not require collision resistance
passages.headingText: 11.4.4 Why HMAC-SHA-1 Remains Secure for HOTP

passages.text: ✅ Confident — this is the property HOTP relies upon
passages.headingText: 11.4.4 Why HMAC-SHA-1 Remains Secure for HOTP

passages.text: RFC 4226 mandates HMAC-SHA-1 exclusively — unlike TOTP (RFC 6238, §12), which extends the algorithm to support HMAC-SHA-256 and HMAC-SHA-512. For HOTP implementations, HMAC-SHA-1 remains the correct and secure choice. Deployments requiring stronger hash functions should use TOTP instead.
passages.headingText: 11.4.4 Why HMAC-SHA-1 Remains Secure for HOTP

passages.text: Post-quantum considerations. HOTP's security in a post-quantum world depends on the symmetric-key nature of the shared secret. Grover's algorithm provides quadratic speedup for search problems, reducing effective key length from 160 bits to 80 bits — but 80-bit post-quantum security remains far beyond practical attack capability. The real quantum risk lies in asymmetric cryptography (RSA, ECC), not in HMAC-based OTP schemes. Organisations concerned about long-term quantum threats should use 256-bit secrets for forward compatibility, even though HMAC-SHA-1 will truncate them.
passages.headingText: 11.4.4 Why HMAC-SHA-1 Remains Secure for HOTP

TOTP (§12) extends HOTP by replacing the event counter with a time-derived counter: C = floor(T / X) where T is the current Unix timestamp and X is the time step (typically 30 seconds). This single change eliminates the counter synchronisation problem but introduces time synchronisation requirements.
Event-based — incremented on each button press or code generation
Time-based — derived from current time divided by step interval
Valid until used or superseded by look-ahead window
Valid for one time step (typically 30 seconds) plus optional drift tolerance
Counter drift between client and server
Clock drift between client and server; mitigated by NTP and time-step tolerance
Indefinite — an unused code remains valid
~30–90 seconds (one time step plus optional ±1 step tolerance)
High — intercepted unused codes remain valid indefinitely
Low — intercepted codes expire within seconds

Passive — code refreshes automatically every 30 seconds
Stateless — counter derived from current time (though last-used counter may be stored for replay prevention)
HMAC-SHA-1, HMAC-SHA-256, or HMAC-SHA-512

TOTP has largely superseded HOTP for interactive authentication because it eliminates the counter synchronisation burden and provides automatic code expiry. Choose HOTP when:
Deploying hardware tokens without real-time clocks (e.g., legacy event-based fobs, YubiKey OATH-HOTP mode)
Operating in air-gapped or offline environments where neither token nor server can synchronise time
Supporting embedded/IoT devices where maintaining accurate timekeeping is impractical
Compliance mandates event-based audit trails for each OTP generation (each code maps to a specific counter value)
The deployment already has a large installed base of HOTP hardware tokens
Deploying software authenticator apps (Google Authenticator, Microsoft Authenticator, Authy)
Supporting multi-device usage (user scans QR code on phone, tablet, and desktop — all generate the same code)
Time-based code expiry is desired for reduced stolen-code risk
Stateless server operation is preferred (counter derived from time, not stored)
The deployment targets consumers who expect automatic code refresh (no button press)

Multi-device synchronisation. One of the most significant practical differences is multi-device behaviour. If a user provisions the same HOTP secret on two devices and generates codes on device A, device B's counter does not advance. The next time device B generates a code, it will be at a different counter position than the server expects — leading to desynchronisation that can only be resolved by using one device exclusively or invoking the resynchronisation protocol (§11.3.3). With TOTP, all devices with the same secret and roughly synchronised clocks generate identical codes at any given moment, making multi-device use seamless. This multi-device asymmetry is the primary reason TOTP has displaced HOTP for consumer-facing MFA.
Small drift (1–5 units/steps)
Look-ahead window (s=5s = 5s=5–101010)
Look-ahead window (s=10s = 10s=10–202020)
±2 time steps (configurable)
Resynchronisation protocol (2 consecutive OTPs)
NTP correction; manual clock adjustment
Unused code generation, network failures, accidental button presses
Clock battery failure, timezone changes, NTP misconfiguration
Server-side counter advancement
Automatic (next valid time step) or manual NTP sync

Physical HOTP tokens — small devices with a button and LCD display — were the dominant second-factor form factor before smartphones made software authenticators ubiquitous. They remain deployed in regulated environments (banking, government, military) where software-based solutions face policy restrictions.
Multi-protocol (FIDO2, U2F, OATH, PIV, OpenPGP); tamper-resistant; no battery
CR2032 battery; PIN-protected; OATH-compliant
PKI + OTP combo; enterprise management via Thales LPC
SafeNet eToken 5110 (Thales)
PKI + OTP; FIPS 140-2 Level 2; enterprise provisioning
Hybrid: token + smart card; PIN required; AES-encrypted seed storage
Token2 TOTP/HOTP programmable
User-programmable via NFC; replaces lost tokens without server re-provisioning
Multiple form factors; OATH-compliant; event + time modes
A hardware HOTP token is a self-contained device with:
A button that triggers OTP generation (incrementing the internal counter)
An LCD display showing the current OTP (typically visible for 30–60 seconds before blanking)
A tamper-resistant microcontroller containing the secret key in non-extractable storage
A battery (typically CR2032 lithium coin cell) with a 5–7 year lifespan depending on usage frequency
Operation is simple: the user presses the button, reads the displayed code, and enters it into the authentication form. Each button press advances the counter by one.

Hardware token secrets are provisioned during manufacturing — the vendor generates unique secrets for each token, programs them into the microcontroller's non-volatile memory, and provides the corresponding secret records to the customer in a seed file. Seed files are typically delivered as encrypted XML or CSV files containing the mapping of token serial numbers to their secrets.
Supply chain security implications:
The token vendor has access to all secrets during manufacturing — if the vendor's systems are compromised, every token from that production batch is compromised

RSA SecurID breach (2011): Attackers compromised RSA's internal systems and extracted seed records for an undisclosed number of SecurID tokens. This information was subsequently used to attack Lockheed Martin and other RSA customers. The breach demonstrated that centralised seed management creates systemic risk — a single vendor compromise can invalidate tokens across hundreds of customer organisations
Seed files must be transported securely from vendor to customer, imported into the authentication server, and then permanently deleted from all intermediate storage

Some vendors offer customer-generated seed workflows where the customer generates the secrets and programs them into blank tokens using a provisioning station — eliminating the vendor from the secret chain of trust

Token battery replacement is typically not user-serviceable — the tamper-resistant enclosure is designed to destroy the secret if physically opened. When the battery expires, the token is disposed of and a replacement token (with a new secret) is provisioned to the user.
Battery life is determined by:
Quiescent current — the microcontroller's sleep-mode power draw (typically < 1 μA)
Active current — power consumption during computation and LCD display (typically 1–5 mA for 30–60 seconds per activation)
Usage frequency — a token used 5 times per day depletes faster than one used once per week

For a typical CR2032 cell (225 mAh), a token used 5 times per day with 30-second display windows has approximately 5–7 years of battery life — well within the typical token refresh cycle mandated by security policies.
Software authenticators have largely displaced hardware tokens for consumer and enterprise MFA due to lower cost, multi-device support, and superior user experience.

The OTP token market has undergone significant shifts across four distinct eras:
Hardware HOTP tokens (RSA SecurID, Vasco)
Enterprise regulation; PCI DSS mandate for 2FA
Transition to TOTP software authenticators
Smartphone ubiquity; Google Authenticator launch (2010)
TOTP dominant; FIDO2/WebAuthn emerging
FIDO Alliance push; passwordless initiative
FIDO2/WebAuthn primary; TOTP fallback; HOTP niche
Passkeys adoption; phishing-resistant MFA
HOTP's remaining market niches (2026):
Military and government — policies mandating hardware tokens without network connectivity
Air-gapped industrial systems — SCADA, ICS environments where timekeeping is unreliable
Legacy enterprise — large installed bases of RSA SecurID or similar tokens not yet migrated
Compliance-driven deployments — regulations specifying "hardware token" requirements

Hardware OTP token authentication market size was estimated at approximately $600–800 million in 2025 (Mordor Intelligence), with growth driven primarily by government and financial sector demand. The market is expected to gradually decline as FIDO2/WebAuthn passkeys displace OTP-based authentication for most use cases.

< 1 μs on modern CPU; ~10 μs on embedded ARM Cortex-M0

Fits comfortably on 8-bit microcontrollers
