passages.text: Recommended approach for IoT HOTP deployment: Provision device-specific secrets during manufacturing (analogous to hardware token provisioning); use large look-ahead windows (s=20s = 20s=20–505050) to tolerate extended offline periods; implement watchdog-based resynchronisation; store the counter in EEPROM or flash with wear-levelling; combine HOTP with TLS mutual authentication for defence-in-depth.
passages.headingText: 11.6.9 HOTP for IoT and Embedded Systems

passages.text: In "passkey-first" authentication architectures (e.g., Google's passwordless-by-default approach), HOTP serves as a critical backup factor for account recovery:
passages.headingText: 11.6.10 HOTP as Backup Factor for Passkey-First Architectures

passages.text: Primary authentication: FIDO2/WebAuthn passkey (phishing-resistant, biometric-activated)
passages.headingText: 11.6.10 HOTP as Backup Factor for Passkey-First Architectures

passages.text: Fallback authentication: TOTP or HOTP (software or hardware token)
passages.headingText: 11.6.10 HOTP as Backup Factor for Passkey-First Architectures

passages.text: Account recovery: Recovery codes + HOTP token for re-provisioning passkeys
passages.headingText: 11.6.10 HOTP as Backup Factor for Passkey-First Architectures

passages.text: HOTP hardware tokens are particularly well-suited for this backup role because they are independent of the user's smartphone (which may be lost alongside passkeys), provide a durable, long-lived credential that survives device replacement, and satisfy regulatory requirements for a "possession factor" independent of the primary device.
passages.headingText: 11.6.10 HOTP as Backup Factor for Passkey-First Architectures

passages.text: For detailed coverage of hardware token form factors, security key architectures (YubiKey, Titan, SoloKeys), and smart card integration, see §19 (Token Form Factor Taxonomy).
passages.headingText: 11.6.10 HOTP as Backup Factor for Passkey-First Architectures

passages.text: TOTP — Time-Based One-Time Password — is the dominant one-time password algorithm used by software authenticator apps worldwide. Defined in RFC 6238 (May 2011) as an extension of HOTP (§11), TOTP replaces the monotonic event counter with a time-derived counter, producing codes that expire automatically after a fixed interval. This single architectural change eliminates HOTP's counter synchronisation problem (§11.3) and makes stolen codes short-lived — transforming OTP from a stateful protocol into a nearly stateless one.

TOTP satisfies AAL1 when used as a single factor. Combined with a memorised secret (password), TOTP achieves AAL2 (§1.1). Like HOTP, TOTP alone can never reach AAL3 — OTP schemes lack the hardware-bound keys and verifier impersonation resistance that AAL3 requires.
The core relationship is trivially expressed:

Where T is a time-derived counter value rather than an event-driven counter. Everything below this substitution — the HMAC computation, dynamic truncation, and modular reduction — is identical to the HOTP pipeline described in §11.1.
Single-factor authentication
TOTP alone qualifies as a single-factor authenticator
User enters only TOTP code (no password) — uncommon in practice
Two-factor authentication using two independent authenticator types
TOTP + password (or TOTP + another factor)
TOTP serves as the "something you have" factor, password serves as "something you know"
Hardware-bound, phishing-resistant multi-factor authentication
TOTP lacks the hardware-bound key requirement and origin binding that AAL3 mandates

NIST SP 800-63B §9.2.5 (2024 revision) requires verifiers to offer at least one phishing-resistant authentication option at AAL2. This means TOTP, while satisfying the technical definition of AAL2 when combined with a password, does not satisfy the regulatory requirement for AAL2 in federal contexts — federal agencies must offer FIDO2 or device-bound passkeys alongside TOTP (NIST SP 800-63B, §9.2.5; OMB M-22-09).
The TOTP algorithm converts the current time into a counter value suitable for the HOTP function:
Current_Unix_Time — seconds elapsed since the Unix epoch (1970-01-01 00:00 UTC)
T0 — the Unix time from which to start counting time steps (default: 0, the Unix epoch itself)
X — the time step duration in seconds (default: 30)
T — the resulting integer counter value, used as the HOTP counter C
The counter T is encoded as an 8-byte big-endian unsigned integer — identical to the HOTP counter encoding (§11.1.1).
Example at a specific point in time:

Every device computing TOTP with the same shared secret at the same moment in time — regardless of geographic location — produces the same code. This is the fundamental property that makes TOTP work without any communication between client and server during authentication.
The full TOTP computation for a given instant:
Obtain the current Unix time in seconds (UTC). The time source must be accurate — see §12.3 for synchronisation requirements
Compute the time step counter: T = floor((unix_time - T0) / X)
Encode T as an 8-byte big-endian integer — this becomes the HOTP counter value

Compute HOTP(K, T) using the algorithm described in §11.1: a. HS = HMAC-Hash(K, T) — where Hash is SHA-1, SHA-256, or SHA-512 b. Dynamic truncation: extract a 4-byte segment from HS using the offset-based method (§11.1.2) c. Modular reduction: TOTP = truncated_value mod 10^d
a. HS = HMAC-Hash(K, T) — where Hash is SHA-1, SHA-256, or SHA-512
b. Dynamic truncation: extract a 4-byte segment from HS using the offset-based method (§11.1.2)
c. Modular reduction: TOTP = truncated_value mod 10^d
Left-pad with zeros to ensure exactly d digits
Mathematical foundation: HMAC construction and dynamic truncation
HMAC construction (RFC 2104):
HMAC(K,m)=H ⁣((K′⊕opad) ∥ H ⁣((K′⊕ipad) ∥ m))\text{HMAC}(K, m) = H\!\left((K' \oplus \text{opad}) \;\|\; H\!\left((K' \oplus \text{ipad}) \;\|\; m\right)\right)HMAC(K,m)=H((K′⊕opad)∥H((K′⊕ipad)∥m))

Where HHH is the underlying hash function, K′K'K′ is the key padded to the hash block size BBB (zero-padded if shorter, hashed first if longer), ipad=0x36\text\\{ipad\\} = \text\\{0x36\\}ipad=0x36 repeated BBB times (inner padding), and opad=0x5C\text\\{opad\\} = \text\\{0x5C\\}opad=0x5C repeated BBB times (outer padding).
Keys < 64 bytes: zero-padded; keys > 64 bytes: hashed first
Same block handling as SHA-1
Keys < 128 bytes: zero-padded; keys > 128 bytes: hashed first
Dynamic truncation algorithm (DT):
Compute HS=HMAC(K,C)HS = \text\\{HMAC\\}(K, C)HS=HMAC(K,C) — the raw HMAC byte string (20, 32, or 64 bytes)
Extract the offset: offset=HS[len(HS)−1] & 0x0F\text\\{offset\\} = HS[\text\\{len\\}(HS) - 1] \;\&\; 0x0Foffset=HS[len(HS)−1]&0x0F — low 4 bits of the last byte, giving a value in [0,15][0, 15][0,15]
Extract 4 bytes at the offset: S=HS[offset..offset+3]S = HS[\text\\{offset\\}..\text\\{offset\\}+3]S=HS[offset..offset+3] (big-endian)
Mask the most significant bit: S′=S & 0x7FFFFFFFS' = S \;\&\; 0x7FFFFFFFS′=S&0x7FFFFFFF — ensures the result is always a positive 31-bit unsigned integer
Compute the final code: OTP=S′ mod 10d\text\\{OTP\\} = S' \bmod 10^dOTP=S′mod10d — where ddd is the desired number of digits
Left-pad with zeros to ensure exactly ddd digits

The mask in step 4 is critical — it clears the most significant bit of the 4-byte integer, ensuring the result is always non-negative regardless of the HMAC output. This limits the code space to 10d10^d10d values (for d=6d = 6d=6: 106=1,000,00010^6 = 1\\{,\\}000\\{,\\}000106=1,000,000 possible codes).
RFC 6238 extends the HOTP algorithm beyond HMAC-SHA-1 to support two additional hash functions:
Mandatory — same as HOTP (RFC 4226). Remains the default for backward compatibility
Optional — provides a larger HMAC output, but the dynamic truncation still extracts only 4 bytes; security benefit is marginal for 6-digit codes
Optional — largest HMAC output; key must be correspondingly longer to fully utilise the hash capacity

The recommended key length matches the hash function's output size (RFC 6238 §9.1). Using a shorter key is permitted but wastes the hash function's capacity — the HMAC construction zero-pads keys shorter than the hash block size. Using a longer key provides no additional security — HMAC hashes keys exceeding the block size before use.

Practical reality: Despite RFC 6238 defining SHA-256 and SHA-512 support, the overwhelming majority of deployed TOTP tokens use HMAC-SHA-1. Google Authenticator, the application that defined the de facto provisioning standard (§12.5), historically supported only SHA-1. While modern authenticator apps generally support all three hash functions, many server-side implementations default to SHA-1 for maximum interoperability. The dynamic truncation step extracts the same 31-bit value regardless of the HMAC output size, so the brute-force resistance of the final 6-digit code is identical across all three hash functions — 10⁻⁶ per guess.
RFC 6238 Appendix B provides test vectors across all three hash functions. The test vectors use 8-digit TOTP codes (unlike the typical 6-digit production deployment) and a time step of 30 seconds.
Shared secret keys (per erratum 2866):

Note on erratum 2866: The original RFC 6238 text implied all three hash functions use the same 20-byte secret. Erratum 2866 (verified) clarifies that the SHA-256 and SHA-512 test vectors use longer secrets matching their respective recommended key lengths. Implementations that fail to account for this discrepancy will produce incorrect test vector results for SHA-256 and SHA-512.
Test vector results (8-digit TOTP, X = 30, T0 = 0):

These test vectors serve as the canonical implementation verification suite for TOTP — any implementation that produces different values for these inputs is incorrect. The final row (Unix time 20000000000, year 2603) tests that implementations correctly handle counter values requiring more than 32 bits, exercising the full 8-byte big-endian encoding.

Cross-validation with HOTP: The first row (Unix time = 59, time step T = 1) should produce the same result as HOTP(K, 1) with the SHA-1 secret key. Comparing with the HOTP test vectors in §11.1.4: HOTP at counter 1 produces 287082 (6 digits). The TOTP test vector at T = 1 produces 94287082 (8 digits). The last 6 digits match — 287082 — confirming that TOTP is HOTP with a time-derived counter.
Notable observations from the test vectors:

Rows 2 and 3 differ by only 2 seconds of Unix time but produce entirely different codes. This is because 1111111109/30=37037037.01111111109 / 30 = 37037037.01111111109/30=37037037.0 (step ends exactly there) and 1111111111/30=37037037.033…1111111111 / 30 = 37037037.033\ldots1111111111/30=37037037.033… (new step), confirming the floor function boundary behaviour

Row 6 (Unix time 20,000,000,000, year 2603) exercises the full 64-bit counter encoding. The counter value C=0x27BC86AAC = \text\\{0x27BC86AA\\}C=0x27BC86AA requires more than 32 bits, testing implementations that might truncate the counter to 4 bytes
Row 1 (C=1C = 1C=1) cross-validates with HOTP: the last 6 digits of the 8-digit TOTP (287082) match HOTP(K, 1) from RFC 4226
The time step X determines how long each TOTP code remains valid. RFC 6238 §9.2 recommends a default of 30 seconds but permits implementations to choose other values.
The 30-second time step represents a carefully chosen balance between security and usability:
Shorter validity → less time for an attacker to relay a captured code
Longer validity → user has more time to read, switch context, and enter the code
Average user takes 5–10 seconds to read a 6-digit code from a phone and type it into a form on another device
Network and processing latency
Server must receive and validate the code before the time step expires; network latency, server-side processing, and potential retransmission consume additional seconds
Shorter steps amplify the effect of clock drift — a 2-second clock skew consumes 20% of a 10-second window but only 7% of a 30-second window
Shorter validity → codes are replayable for less time

At 30 seconds, a user has approximately 15–25 seconds of usable time (accounting for the random position within the current step when the code is displayed, plus a few seconds of transcription). This is sufficient for the vast majority of users in the vast majority of network conditions.
Google Authenticator, Microsoft Authenticator, Authy, most implementations
De facto standard; maximum interoperability
Some enterprise deployments; historically RSA SecurID used 60-second intervals
Provides a more comfortable entry window; halves the server's per-user code generation rate for look-ahead validation
Rare; some high-security research prototypes
Minimises the replay window but creates acute usability problems — users frequently fail to enter the code before it rotates
Rare; some SMS-linked fallback systems
Accommodates SMS delivery delays (which can exceed 30 seconds in congested networks)

Very short time steps (< 15 seconds) are impractical for human-transcribed TOTP. The user must: (1) look at their phone, (2) read 6 digits, (3) switch to the login form, (4) type the digits, (5) submit the form, (6) wait for server validation. This pipeline reliably takes 8–15 seconds even for fast typists. A 10-second step means the code may rotate mid-entry, requiring the user to start over — producing frustration and increased support burden from "my code doesn't work" complaints.

TOTP's critical dependency is mutual agreement on the current time between the client (authenticator app or hardware token) and the server (verifier). Unlike HOTP, where desynchronisation results from user behaviour (§11.3.1), TOTP desynchronisation results from clock drift — the gradual divergence of a device's internal clock from the true time.
NITZ (Network Identity and Time Zone) from cellular carrier; NTP (Network Time Protocol) from OS-level time sync
Smartphones maintain excellent time accuracy because they synchronise continuously via cellular networks and NTP. Drift is negligible for TOTP purposes
NTP via OS time service (systemd-timesyncd, Windows Time Service, chronyd)
< 1 second (when NTP is active)
Standard NTP achieves sub-second accuracy; drift is only a concern if NTP is disabled or the NTP server is unreachable
±0.5 ppm → ~15.8 seconds/year

Hardware tokens lack network connectivity and rely solely on their internal oscillator. A typical ±0.5 ppm crystal accumulates ~16 seconds of drift per year. After 3 years of deployment without correction, drift may reach ~48 seconds — exceeding the ±30-second tolerance window
Manual time setting or GPS receiver
Variable — depends on how frequently time is calibrated
Systems without NTP access require periodic manual calibration or a GPS time source
Clock drift mathematical model:
Tdevice(t)=Ttrue(t)+Δ0+α⋅tT_{\text{device}}(t) = T_{\text{true}}(t) + \Delta_0 + \alpha \cdot tTdevice​(t)=Ttrue​(t)+Δ0​+α⋅t

Where Δ0\Delta_0Δ0​ is the initial time offset at deployment (calibration error), α\alphaα is the drift rate in seconds per second (typically expressed in ppm), and ttt is elapsed time since deployment. The time to desynchronisation — the time t_\\{\text\\{fail\\}\\} until drift exceeds a tolerance window of nnn steps — is:
tfail=n⋅X−∣Δ0∣∣α∣t_{\text{fail}} = \frac{n \cdot X - \lvert \Delta_0 \rvert}{\lvert \alpha \rvert}tfail​=∣α∣n⋅X−∣Δ0​∣​

For a hardware token with \alpha = 1.0 \times 10^\\{-6\\} (1 ppm), X=30sX = 30\text\\{s\\}X=30s, n=1n = 1n=1 (±30 s tolerance), and Δ0=0\Delta_0 = 0Δ0​=0: t_\\{\text\\{fail\\}\\} = 30\\{,\\}000\\{,\\}000\text\\{s\\} \approx 347\text\\{ days\\}. A typical 1 ppm hardware token fails within approximately one year without drift compensation. At 2 ppm, the failure time drops to ~174 days.
Drift sources — systematic vs. discrete:
Crystal oscillator frequency error
Manufacturing tolerance of the quartz crystal
Crystal frequency varies parabolically with ambient temperature (AT-cut crystals)
±2–10 ppm over −20°C to +70°C range
Logarithmic aging curve; highest drift in first year, decreasing over time
Battery depletion (hardware tokens)
Low battery voltage affects crystal oscillation frequency
OS updates timezone; authenticator uses UTC internally (correct implementations)
User manually sets device clock
NTP daemon applies a step correction (> 128 ms threshold)

Editorial Note: ⚠️ Critical implementation rule: TOTP implementations MUST use UTC (Unix time) internally, never local time. Timezone changes and DST transitions must not affect the time step counter computation. This is a common source of implementation bugs — particularly on embedded systems where the RTC may store local time.

Non-linear drift effects: Real crystal oscillators deviate from the linear model. Temperature-dependent drift follows a parabolic curve (seasonal swings of ±20°C can add ±2–5 ppm). Crystal aging follows a logarithmic curve (highest in year one, decreasing). Mechanical shocks can cause instantaneous frequency jumps of 0.5–5 ppm. Conservative deployment planning should use α=2ppm\alpha = 2\text\\{ ppm\\}α=2ppm even for specifications claiming 0.5 ppm.
To accommodate clock drift, the server validates the submitted code not only against the current time step T but also against adjacent time steps:
Current step only (0–30 seconds)
Maximum security; fails on any clock drift
±1 step (previous + current + next = 0–90 seconds)
Standard default — tolerates ±30 seconds of drift; probability of random guess: 3/10⁶
Accommodates hardware tokens with moderate drift; 5/10⁶ per guess
Very permissive; appropriate only for tokens with known high drift; 11/10⁶ per guess

The standard configuration of n = 1 (accept the current step, the previous step, and the next step) provides a 90-second acceptance window. This accommodates the worst-case scenario of a user generating a code in the final second of a time step and submitting it several seconds later (when the server has already advanced to the next step), as well as minor clock drift in either direction.
Security impact of tolerance window expansion:
Each additional tolerance step linearly increases the attacker's advantage in an online brute-force attack. The probability of a random 6-digit code being accepted is:
Pguess=2n+110dP_{\text{guess}} = \frac{2n + 1}{10^d}Pguess​=10d2n+1​
Security Reduction vs. n=0n=0n=0

The security impact is modest in absolute terms — even at n=5n = 5n=5, the per-guess probability is still only 1.1 \times 10^\\{-5\\}. However, the linear relationship means that tolerance should be kept to the minimum required for reliable operation. The effective replay window (the period during which a captured code can be reused) is (2n+1)×X(2n + 1) \times X(2n+1)×X seconds — for n=1n = 1n=1, X=30X = 30X=30: T_\\{\text\\{valid\\}\\} = 90 seconds. This makes server-side replay prevention (§12.4) essential.
A sophisticated implementation can track and compensate for systematic clock drift:
During each successful authentication, the server records which time step offset i (from the tolerance window) produced the match
If a user consistently authenticates with i = -1 (always matching the previous step), the server infers that the client's clock is running approximately 30 seconds slow
The server stores this drift offset and applies it when computing the expected time steps for that user — shifting the tolerance window to centre on the observed drift rather than on T
This allows the server to maintain tight security (small tolerance window) while accommodating progressive drift in hardware tokens
1. Hardware Token computes initial TOTP

The hardware token's internal crystal oscillator has drifted by +45 seconds over several years of use. When the user presses the button, the token computes the TOTP code based on this fast clock, effectively generating the code for sequence T+1 ahead of the server's true time.
2. Authentication Server scans the tolerance window

The server receives the code and primarily checks it against its own current time T_server. This fails. The server then checks the backward tolerance T_server - 1 (fails) and the forward tolerance T_server + 1 (which succeeds).
3. Authentication Server records the drift shift
Upon finding a match at the +1 window position, the server does not merely accept the token. It updates the user's permanent credential record, setting their drift_offset property to +1.
Artifact Produced: user_profile.drift_offset (Persistent numerical state)
4. Hardware Token computes subsequent TOTP
At a later date, the user logs in again. The token is still ~45 seconds fast. It computes the code based on its internal drifted clock.
5. Authentication Server applies drift compensation

Instead of starting the check at the true T_server time, the server retrieves the user's drift_offset (+1) and makes T_server + 1 the baseline for the check. The first attempt matches instantly. This prevents the token from failing entirely once its drift eventually exceeds the standard ±1 tolerance window (e.g. drifting to +90 seconds will just shift the drift offset to +3 over time).

This compensation technique is particularly important for hardware TOTP tokens deployed for multiple years. A token accumulating 15 seconds of drift per year reaches 45 seconds of drift after 3 years — outside the ±30-second default tolerance. Without drift compensation, the token would stop working. With drift compensation, the server tracks the gradual shift and adjusts automatically.

Comprehensive drift compensation algorithm — the following pseudocode extends the simplified verify_totp() (§12.3.2) with a ring-buffer drift history, consistency check, and emergency recalibration fallback:

Drift history consistency check: The algorithm only updates the persistent drift offset when the recent authentication history shows consistent drift. If the last 5 authentications show offsets of [+1, +1, +1, +1, +1], the median is +1 and the history is consistent (standard deviation = 0). If the offsets are [+1, 0, −1, +2, −1], the standard deviation exceeds the threshold and the drift offset is not updated — this indicates inconsistent drift (possibly the user is travelling between time zones or the device clock was manually adjusted).

Emergency recalibration: If the standard window fails but a code matches in the wider ±5 window, the server performs emergency recalibration — setting the drift offset to the observed value. This handles the case where a device's clock has drifted past the standard tolerance window suddenly (e.g., due to battery degradation in a hardware token or a manual clock adjustment on a phone).

Enterprise environments often require users to register multiple TOTP devices (e.g., a phone and a hardware backup token). Each device has its own clock, its own drift characteristics, and its own last-used time step. This creates several synchronisation challenges:

Per-device state management: The server must maintain separate drift offsets and last-used time steps for each registered device. A global per-user last-used step would prevent different devices from authenticating — if Device A uses step TTT, Device B cannot use step TTT even though it generated the code independently

Cross-device replay prevention: The replay cache must be scoped per-device, not per-user, to allow multiple devices to generate and submit codes for the same time step. This weakens replay protection — an attacker who captures a code from Device A can replay it against the same device's step, but not against Device B

Drift divergence over time: Different devices drift at different rates. After 6 months, Device A (phone, NTP-synced) may have zero drift while Device B (hardware token, 1 ppm) has accumulated ~16 seconds of drift. The server's drift compensation must track each device independently

Secret distribution: Adding a new device requires re-provisioning the same shared secret. If the provisioning channel is compromised (e.g., a phishing page tricks the user into scanning a QR code for the attacker's secret), the attacker gains a valid TOTP device. Mitigations include requiring the existing TOTP device to authorise the new registration, using the provisioning ceremony as a re-enrolment (generating a new secret for all devices), or out-of-band verification (sending a confirmation link to the registered email)

TOTP's time-based expiry provides better replay protection than HOTP's indefinite code validity (§11.4.2), but a TOTP code is still valid for the entire duration of its time step plus the server's tolerance window. Without explicit replay prevention, an attacker who captures a valid code can reuse it within this window.

With a 30-second time step and n = 1 tolerance, a given TOTP code is accepted by the server for up to 90 seconds (30 seconds for the previous step, 30 for the current, 30 for the next). An attacker who intercepts the code (via shoulder surfing, keylogging, or network interception) can replay it within this window.
The mitigation is straightforward: the server stores the last successfully used time step for each user and rejects any code from a time step less than or equal to the stored value:
This ensures each time step can only be used once per user, regardless of how many times the code is submitted within the validity window.
Implementation considerations:
The replay cache must be per-user — each user has their own last-used time step
In federated environments (where a user authenticates to multiple relying parties via a single IdP), the replay cache must be maintained at the IdP level to prevent cross-RP replay
The last-used step must be persisted reliably — if the server crashes and loses the in-memory state, previously used codes become temporarily reusable until the time step advances past them naturally

TOTP's security against online brute-force attacks depends on three parameters: the code space size (10d10^d10d), the tolerance window size (2n+12n + 12n+1 valid steps), and the rate limiting policy. While replay prevention (§12.4.2) addresses code reuse, brute-force attacks attempt to guess valid codes within a single time step — a distinct attack vector that requires its own analysis.
Per-attempt success probability:
Psingle=2n+110dP_{\text{single}} = \frac{2n + 1}{10^d}Psingle​=10d2n+1​
Multi-attempt success probability with AAA allowed attempts before lockout:
Ptotal=1−(1−2n+110d)AP_{\text{total}} = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{2n + 1}{10^d}\right)^APtotal​=1−(1−10d2n+1​)A
Numerical examples for d=6d = 6d=6, n=1n = 1n=1 (standard configuration):
Negligible — one random guess
Still negligible — 5 guesses
NIST-recommended maximum (NIST SP 800-63B, §9.2.2); P≈1/33,000P \approx 1/33\\{,\\}000P≈1/33,000
Unacceptable — P≈1/3,300P \approx 1/3\\{,\\}300P≈1/3,300; 100 attempts should never be allowed
Catastrophic — P≈1/333P \approx 1/333P≈1/333

The time-bounded advantage: Unlike static passwords, TOTP codes change every 30 seconds. An attacker who exhausts all allowed guesses within one time step gains no advantage for the next step — the code has changed. The attacker must restart guessing from scratch each step. With aggressive rate limiting (10 attempts per step), the expected time to brute-force a single 6-digit code with n=1n = 1n=1 is:

With account lockout policies (e.g., 10 failed attempts → 15-minute lockout), brute-force attacks against TOTP are completely infeasible — making rate limiting the single most important defence (see §12.4.4 below).

Rate limiting is the single most important defence against TOTP brute-force attacks. Without rate limiting, an attacker with a botnet can attempt all 10610^6106 possible 6-digit codes within a single 30-second time step (NIST SP 800-63B, §9.2.2).
Minimum 1-second delay between attempts
Reduces automated guessing throughput
10 failed TOTP attempts per time step
Reject further attempts until next time step
Prevents enumeration within a single code validity window
10 consecutive failed TOTP verifications
Lock account for 15–30 minutes; require password re-entry or admin unlock
Thwarts sustained brute-force campaigns
100 failed attempts per IP per hour
Mitigates distributed attacks from botnets
After 3 failures: 5 s; after 5: 15 s; after 8: 30 s
Exponentially increasing delay between attempts
Balances usability with security for legitimate users who make typos
Implementation considerations:
Rate limiting MUST be applied at the TOTP verification endpoint, not at the login page level — the password step and TOTP step should have independent rate limiters
The per-step limit (10 attempts) should reset with each new time step, allowing the user to try again with the next code
Account lockout should be temporary, not permanent — a permanent lockout creates a denial-of-service vulnerability
Rate limit counters must be stored server-side, never in client-side cookies or tokens (trivially bypassable)
The following pseudocode provides a complete, production-ready TOTP verification function incorporating rate limiting, replay prevention, and drift compensation:
Critical implementation details:

Constant-time comparison — The code comparison MUST use constant-time comparison (e.g., hmac.compare_digest() in Python, crypto.timingSafeEqual() in Node.js). Standard string comparison (==) uses short-circuit evaluation and leaks timing information about how many digits matched
Base32 decoding — The shared secret is stored as Base32 but must be decoded to raw bytes before the HMAC computation. Accept lowercase input and strip whitespace for robustness
Time source — The server's time source MUST be NTP-synchronised. Monitor synchronisation status; if NTP is lost, consider rejecting TOTP verifications until restored
Secret storage — The shared secret MUST be encrypted at rest using AES-256-GCM with a key managed by an HSM (§12.6.2)
TOTP token provisioning uses the same otpauth:// URI scheme described in §11.2.2, with the type set to totp and time-specific parameters replacing the counter.
Key differences from HOTP URIs (§11.2.2):
counter=0 (required; initial counter value)
Not present — counter is derived from time
Not present — counter is event-based
period=30 (optional; defaults to 30 seconds)
Format: Issuer:AccountName (e.g., GitHub:user@example.com)
Base32-encoded shared secret (RFC 4648, no padding)
Service provider name; displayed in the authenticator app
Time step duration in seconds. Most authenticator apps support only 30 or 60; non-standard values may be silently ignored or cause errors
Number of OTP digits (6 or 8). Production deployments almost universally use 6
Hash algorithm: SHA1, SHA256, or SHA512

The issuer parameter MUST appear both in the URI path and as a query parameter (per the Key URI Format spec). Authenticator apps use the query parameter issuer for display purposes and the path for token grouping. If the two values differ, app behaviour is undefined — some apps silently prefer the query parameter, others concatenate them

The secret MUST be Base32-encoded with no padding (no = characters). Use the RFC 4648 Base32 alphabet: A–Z and 2–7. When decoding, implementations should accept lowercase input and strip whitespace for robustness
All label components must be URL-encoded: @ → %40, : → %3A, spaces → %20 or +. Failure to encode special characters is a common provisioning bug that produces malformed tokens

The period parameter is supported by most authenticator apps, but non-standard values (anything other than 30 or 60) may be silently ignored. Always test provisioning with the target authenticator app before deploying non-standard periods
A production TOTP token for a GitHub account:

This URI, when encoded as a QR code and scanned by an authenticator app, creates a TOTP token that generates a new 6-digit code every 30 seconds using HMAC-SHA-1 with the shared secret JBSWY3DPEHPK3PXP (Base32-encoded).
The QR code provisioning process, security considerations, and manual-entry fallback requirements are identical to those described for HOTP in §11.2.3.

Backup codes (also called recovery codes or scratch codes) are single-use codes that allow users to regain account access when their TOTP device is lost, damaged, or unavailable. They serve as an emergency bypass for the TOTP factor.
Backup code design best practices:
Enough attempts for recovery without excessive attack surface
8–10 alphanumeric characters (or 8 digits)
Sufficient entropy (≈53\approx 53≈53 bits for 10 alphanumeric) to resist brute-force
Printable, human-readable (e.g., A1B2-C3D4-E5F6)
Hyphens or spaces improve readability; avoid ambiguous characters (0/O, 1/l/I)
Each code consumed upon use; cannot be reused
Use secrets module (Python) or crypto.randomBytes() (Node.js)
Print and store in a physical safe; never store electronically alongside the password
Backup codes must be accessible without electronic devices — they are used precisely when electronic access is lost
Notify user when a backup code is used
Alert the user that their TOTP device may be compromised
Allow regeneration after TOTP verification
After recovery, user generates a new set; old set is invalidated
Same rate limiting as TOTP verification
Backup codes are equally valuable to an attacker
Backup code generation example (Python):

Security consideration: Backup codes are stored in plaintext by the user (printed on paper). This makes them vulnerable to physical theft, but is an intentional design choice — backup codes must be accessible without any electronic device. The single-use property limits the damage from theft to a single authentication.

TOTP's design — a shared secret, a clock, and a standardised algorithm — requires no proprietary infrastructure. Any application that implements RFC 6238 and parses otpauth:// URIs can function as a TOTP authenticator. This openness has produced a broad ecosystem of interchangeable authenticator applications.

The cloud backup paradox (Google Authenticator, 2023): Google Authenticator's initial cloud backup implementation was not end-to-end encrypted — Google held the encryption keys, meaning TOTP secrets were accessible to Google and potentially to any attacker who compromised the user's Google Account. This created a paradox: enabling cloud backup for resilience against device loss simultaneously introduced a new attack vector — Google Account compromise → complete TOTP secret extraction. Google subsequently added E2EE in 2024, but the episode illustrates the fundamental tension between availability and confidentiality in cloud-backed authenticator apps (§12.6.2).
Cloud backup of TOTP secrets represents an inherent tension between availability and confidentiality:
❌ All tokens lost with the device; recovery requires re-enrollment with every service provider
✅ Tokens restored from cloud to new device
✅ Tokens restored from cloud to new device
✅ Vendor can access secrets (holds encryption keys)
❌ Vendor cannot access secrets (user holds keys)
Limited to device compromise
Cloud account compromise → all TOTP secrets extracted
Cloud account compromise + backup password → secrets extracted
Minimal — secrets exist only on-device
Vendor may be compelled to disclose secrets via legal process
Vendor cannot comply with disclosure requests — lacks access

The architectural recommendation: organisations with high-security requirements should mandate E2EE-capable authenticator apps or hardware tokens, and should explicitly prohibit storing TOTP secrets in password manager vaults where they co-reside with the passwords they are meant to protect.

Attacker gains access to the credential database containing TOTP secrets
Encrypt secrets at rest with HSM-backed key; use per-user encryption keys

Authenticator app backup extraction
Attacker accesses cloud backup of authenticator app secrets
Use E2EE backup (Authy); avoid non-E2EE cloud backup; hardware tokens have no cloud backup
Malware on the user's phone extracts TOTP secrets from app storage
Use hardware security keys (FIDO2) for high-value accounts; mobile device management on corporate phones
Attacker observes TOTP code displayed on user's screen
Privacy screens; push-based MFA instead of code entry in public environments
Malware captures TOTP code as user types it or copies it
Use password managers with built-in TOTP that auto-fill (reduces clipboard use)
Attacker tricks user into scanning a QR code for the attacker's secret
Educate users to never re-provision TOTP from an unsolicited QR code
Man-in-the-middle during provisioning
Attacker intercepts the otpauth:// URI in transit
Provision over TLS; verify the QR code contains the correct issuer and account name
Attacker obtains the TOTP backup/recovery codes stored by the user
Limit backup code validity; use single-use backup codes; warn users to store securely
Insider threat at authenticator vendor
Employee at vendor accesses TOTP secrets from cloud backup
Use E2EE backup where vendor cannot access secrets; prefer hardware tokens
Server-side secret storage best practices:
Encrypt at rest — TOTP secrets stored in the database MUST be encrypted using AES-256-GCM with a key managed by an HSM (Hardware Security Module)
Per-user salted encryption — Each user's TOTP secret should be encrypted with a key derived from the master key using a per-user salt, preventing detection of duplicate secrets across users
Separate secret storage — TOTP secrets should be stored in a separate database table from user passwords and profile data, limiting the blast radius of a SQL injection or data access vulnerability
Access logging — All access to the TOTP secret store should be logged and monitored; unexpected access patterns (e.g., bulk reads) should trigger alerts
Key rotation — The master encryption key should be rotated periodically (at least annually), re-encrypting all stored secrets as a background process without disrupting authentication
✅ (Duo Trusted Endpoints, WebAuthn)
✅ Native (Microsoft Authenticator)
SAML, OIDC, RADIUS (via NPS), WS-Fed
✅ (FIDO2 keys, passwordless phone sign-in)
✅ Native (Google Authenticator, Google Prompt)
✅ (Google Titan keys, on-device passkeys)
✅ Native (FreeOTP integration)
✅ (WebAuthn passkeys, security keys)

TOTP authenticators integrate with enterprise authentication infrastructure through standardised protocols. The following table summarises the primary integration methods, server requirements, and deployment context for each protocol:
RADIUS server validates OTP via shared secret + time window
FreeRADIUS with OATH module; commercial RADIUS servers (Cisco ISE, Aruba ClearPass)
Most common for VPN, Wi-Fi, and network access. TOTP shared secret stored in RADIUS user profile
SAML IdP delegates OTP validation to RADIUS or OATH server
Shibboleth, Keycloak, Azure AD with MFA adapter
OTP presented as second factor after SSO. Uses urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:ac:classes:TimeSyncToken AuthnContextClassRef
OIDC provider prompts for OTP after primary authentication
Keycloak, Auth0, Okta with TOTP MFA module
Standard OIDC + acr_values for step-up authentication (§3.5)
Not applicable — WebAuthn uses public-key cryptography, not shared secrets
TOTP and WebAuthn are fundamentally different approaches (see §14, §12.7.5)
PAM module validates OTP against OATH server
OATH-PAM, privacyIDEA, LinOTP
Linux/Unix system-level authentication; PAM module delegates to RADIUS or local OATH validation
RADIUS integration example (FreeRADIUS + OATH):
1. User provides credentials to VPN Client
The User provides their standard username, password, and the generated OTP code to the VPN Client.
2. VPN Client encapsulates credentials and forwards Access-Request to RADIUS Server
The VPN Client encapsulates the credentials into an Access-Request packet and transmits it to the RADIUS Server.
3. RADIUS Server looks up User's associated TOTP secret key
The RADIUS Server (via rlm_oauth2 or similar module) looks up the User's associated TOTP secret key (K) in the database.
4. RADIUS Server computes expected TOTP window with look-ahead
The RADIUS Server generates the expected TOTP values for the current timestep (T_\\{current\\}) as well as the adjacent look-ahead window (T±stepsT \pm stepsT±steps) to account for clock drift.
5. RADIUS Server securely compares submitted OTP against computed value array
The RADIUS Server securely compares the User's submitted OTP against the array of computed expected values.
6. RADIUS Server returns Access-Accept or Access-Reject decision
Upon successful validation, the RADIUS Server returns an Access-Accept message (recording the timestamp to prevent replay attacks); otherwise, it returns Access-Reject.
7. VPN Client resolves connection session based on RADIUS decision
The VPN Client receives the RADIUS decision and either grants the User access to the network tunnel or denies the connection.

RADIUS remains the most prevalent integration protocol for TOTP in enterprise network access scenarios (VPN, 802.1X Wi-Fi). SAML and OIDC integration is standard for web application authentication, with the OTP validation typically delegated to a RADIUS backend or native MFA module within the identity provider.

TOTP is a significant improvement over passwords alone, but it provides no phishing resistance. Understanding why requires examining TOTP's security model in the context of modern phishing techniques.
passages.headingText: 12.7 Security Limitations: Why TOTP Is Not Phishing-Resistant

passages.text: TOTP codes are derived exclusively from two inputs: the shared secret K and the current time. The code has no cryptographic relationship to the server that is requesting it — the same code is valid regardless of which website it is entered on.

Contrast with WebAuthn/FIDO2 (§14): during a WebAuthn authentication ceremony, the authenticator signs a challenge that includes the relying party's origin (e.g., https://bank.example.com). The signature is cryptographically bound to the origin — it is invalid on any other origin. If the user is on a phishing site (https://bank-login.evil.com), the authenticator refuses to sign because the origin does not match the credential's relying party ID.
TOTP has no such mechanism. The code 482931 is 482931 regardless of whether the user enters it on the legitimate site or a phishing clone.

TOTP is not a challenge-response protocol. The server does not send a challenge; the client does not sign a challenge. The code is computed entirely from local state (key + time). This means there is no dynamic server-provided input that could bind the authentication attempt to a specific session or origin.

The practical consequence of missing origin binding and challenge-response is vulnerability to real-time phishing proxies — Adversary-in-the-Middle (AiTM) attacks that relay credentials and TOTP codes to the legitimate server in real time:
1. User Agent navigates to phishing domain

The user is actively lured (e.g., via targeted spear-phishing email or SMS) to click a malicious link. The underlying User Agent navigates securely over TLS to an attacker-controlled hostname (e.g., accounts-g00gle.com) designed visually to perfectly mimic the target enterprise's legitimate authentication portal.
2. User Agent submits credentials to AiTM Proxy

The user interactively navigates the pixel-perfect cloned interface and natively blind-types their primary username and password into the rogue form. The User Agent blindly HTTP POSTs these secrets directly to the AiTM Proxy.
3. AiTM Proxy relays credentials to Legitimate Server

The phishing site systematically operates as a real-time Adversary-in-the-Middle (AiTM) proxy engine (e.g., Evilginx). It instantly opens a new TLS connection and forwards the captured plaintext payload directly to the true authentication backend.
4. Legitimate Server issues TOTP challenge to AiTM Proxy

The Legitimate Server actively validates the primary credentials, flags the account for multi-factor authentication, and issues an HTTP payload demanding a time-based token explicitly back to the AiTM Proxy engine.
5. AiTM Proxy relays TOTP challenge to User Agent
The rogue AiTM Proxy cleanly strips origin-bound security headers and seamlessly passes the native MFA challenge interface straight through to the victim's viewing User Agent.
6. User Agent submits freshly generated TOTP to AiTM Proxy

The victim, believing they are interacting safely with the true provider, opens their mobile authenticator app, manually generates a brand new 6-digit TOTP pin, and submits it to the fake interface via the User Agent.
7. AiTM Proxy relays functional TOTP to Legitimate Server

Because TOTP codes remain valid for a broad 30-second window, the AiTM Proxy possesses ample time to dynamically inject the freshly intercepted token directly into the Legitimate Server's waiting MFA validation form.

Rejection Scenario: If the MFA channel mandates phishing-resistant WebAuthn instead of TOTP, cryptographic origin mismatch occurs. The Legitimate Server permanently blocks the authentication attempt and actively triggers a HIGH_RISK_SIGN_IN_BLOCKED SIEM event.
8. Legitimate Server issues high-assurance session cookie

Assuming traditional, non-phishing-resistant factors, the Legitimate Server correctly parses the cryptographically valid TOTP pin, registers a fully authenticated session, and ships a massive, high-privilege session= cookie string securely back to the request origin (the AiTM Proxy).
Artifact Produced: ESTSAUTH (Stolen Authenticated Session Cookie)
9. AiTM Proxy intercepts and steals session cookie

Before routing the payload back to the user, the AiTM Proxy maliciously scrapes and records the raw, unencrypted session cookie into the attacker's persistent database. Threat actors systematically exploit this stolen session artifact to bypass MFA requirements permanently.

Security Audit: If the Legitimate Server employs advanced token binding (e.g., DPoP) or impossible-travel telemetry, it might detect the proxy's IP anomaly and emit an AITM_PROXY_DETECTED alert, immediately revoking the compromised session. Without these secondary defenses, the theft goes completely undetected.
10. AiTM Proxy redirects User Agent to final success state

With the session successfully hijacked, the AiTM Proxy terminates its active inspection layer by programmatically throwing the victim's User Agent through an HTTP 302 bounce, securely dropping them into the legitimate service portal natively so they remain completely unaware of the compromise.
AiTM attack step sequence (textual):
Attacker sets up a phishing site that visually replicates the legitimate login page (e.g., bank-login.evil.com mimicking bank.example.com)
Attacker operates a reverse proxy between the phishing site and the real server
User enters username and password on the phishing site → proxy relays to the real server
Real server requests TOTP code → proxy relays this request to the phishing site
User enters TOTP code on the phishing site → proxy relays to the real server within the 30-second validity window
Real server validates the password + TOTP combination → issues a session token (cookie or access token)
Proxy intercepts the session token → attacker now has an authenticated session
Attacker can access the user's account using the stolen session token — no further MFA prompts are required

The entire attack completes within seconds. The user sees a normal login flow and receives no indication of compromise. Sophisticated AiTM phishing kits (EvilProxy, Evilginx, Tycoon 2FA, Modlishka) automate this attack and are available as commercial Phishing-as-a-Service (PhaaS) offerings — see §21.5 for the full AiTM attack taxonomy.
Mitigations against AiTM attacks:
Origin-bound authentication — authenticator verifies the RP ID
✅ Fully effective — structurally immune to AiTM relay
Private key never leaves the authenticator hardware
✅ Fully effective — key material is non-exportable
TLS client certificates (mTLS)
Certificate-based mutual authentication with origin binding
✅ Effective — but poor usability; enterprise-only
Push notification with number matching
Displays a challenge number on both device and login page
⚠️ Partially effective — sophisticated AiTM kits can relay the number
Still phishable via AiTM — same fundamental weakness
Hardware-bound, origin-bound, challenge-response
✅ Fully effective — the gold standard for phishing resistance

Critical takeaway: TOTP defeats credential stuffing (the attacker lacks the shared secret), brute force (throttling limits guesses), and offline password theft (the OTP changes every 30 seconds). But it provides zero protection against real-time phishing proxies — the dominant credential theft technique against MFA-protected accounts as of 2025. TOTP is a phishable second factor.
For phishing-resistant alternatives, see §14 (WebAuthn/FIDO2), which provides origin-bound cryptographic authentication that is structurally immune to AiTM relay attacks.
The following table provides a comprehensive overview of attack vectors against TOTP-based authentication, with probability and impact assessments calibrated for a typical enterprise deployment:
Rate limiting (max 10 attempts/step) (§12.4.4)
Phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2)
Per-device last-used step cache (§12.4.2)
HSM-backed encryption at rest (§12.6.2)
Client-side secret extraction
Hardware tokens; MDM; E2EE backup
NTP synchronisation; drift compensation (§12.3)
Social engineering to re-provision
User education; provisioning verification
Single-use codes; limited validity (§12.5.4)
Password managers with built-in TOTP auto-fill
Verification endpoint timing
Constant-time comparison; rate limiting masks timing
General cryptographic practice

The dominant threat to TOTP-protected accounts in 2025–2026 is AiTM phishing (attack #2), not brute-force or code interception. AiTM kits are widely available as Phishing-as-a-Service offerings and are used in targeted attacks against enterprises, financial institutions, and government agencies. Organisations relying on TOTP as their sole MFA method should plan a migration path to phishing-resistant authentication (FIDO2/WebAuthn, device-bound passkeys) — particularly for privileged accounts and high-value targets.
The following table maps common TOTP combinations to NIST AAL levels, considering the practical security properties of each combination:
Standard enterprise MFA. Does NOT satisfy SP 800-63B §9.2.5 phishing-resistant requirement
Hardware token provides physical possession but no origin binding
User can choose TOTP (phishable) or FIDO2 (phishing-resistant) per session
Hardware-bound key with verifier impersonation resistance
TOTP (same password manager)
Both factors in the same vault — compromise yields both factors
NIST deprecated SMS OTP as an AAL2 factor (SP 800-63B-3, 2017)
Push notification (approve/deny)
Without number matching: vulnerable to push fatigue attacks
Device-bound passkey (FIDO2)
Single-factor AAL3 — hardware binding satisfies both possession and cryptographic verification
Single-factor — stolen secret yields full access
Memorised secret + TOTP + device trust
Device certificate adds possession factor but does not bind TOTP to the origin

Key regulatory implications: US federal (OMB M-22-09) requires phishing-resistant MFA for high-value assets by FY 2024. EU (NIS2) mandates MFA for essential entities, with regulators increasingly recommending phishing-resistant options. PCI DSS 4.0 (2024) requires MFA for all access to the cardholder data environment (Requirement 8.4.2) — TOTP + password satisfies this.

TOTP is the correct choice for the vast majority of modern authentication deployments — it combines strong security with low cost and broad interoperability. HOTP remains relevant only where accurate timekeeping is unavailable (hardware tokens without RTCs, air-gapped systems). SMS OTP, while convenient for consumers, is NIST-deprecated as an AAL2 factor (NIST SP 800-63B-3, 2017) due to SS7/SIM-swap vulnerabilities. Push notification provides the best user experience but requires a managed authenticator app and introduces push fatigue attack risk (§10.3).

Choosing between OTP methods: TOTP is the default recommendation for standards-based MFA — it is phishing-vulnerable but cost-free and interoperable. For phishing resistance, FIDO2/WebAuthn (§14) is structurally superior to all OTP methods. Push notification with number matching offers a middle ground: better UX than TOTP and moderate phishing resistance, but it depends on a proprietary ecosystem. SMS OTP should be avoided in new deployments unless the user base cannot install apps (see §12.5.4 for backup code alternatives).
Decision framework — when to choose each method:

Choose TOTP when deploying software authenticator apps on NTP-synced smartphones, when cost is a concern (no per-authentication fees), and when the threat model does not include sophisticated, targeted AiTM phishing — or when such threats are addressed by other controls (e.g., FIDO2 for privileged accounts)

Choose HOTP when deploying hardware tokens without real-time clocks (air-gapped environments), embedded systems with unreliable timekeeping, or situations where the token must work indefinitely without battery (e.g., YubiKey's event-based mode)

Choose SMS OTP only when the user base includes users without smartphones or with very low technical literacy, when the primary concern is reducing friction (SMS requires no app installation), and the organisation accepts the known security limitations with compensating controls

Choose Push Notification in enterprise deployments where a managed authenticator app is already deployed, user experience is the primary concern, and the threat model includes credential stuffing but not sophisticated AiTM phishing — number matching should be enabled for moderate phishing resistance

Choose FIDO2/WebAuthn instead of any OTP when the threat model includes targeted AiTM phishing (this should be the default assumption for any organisation handling valuable data), when NIST AAL3 compliance is required, or when hardware security keys can be distributed to users
For organisations planning to migrate from HOTP to TOTP, the transition must be managed carefully to avoid locking out users. The following six-step migration plan minimises user disruption:
Add TOTP support to authentication server (alongside existing HOTP)
None — HOTP continues to work
Update MFA enrollment flow to offer TOTP as default; HOTP as fallback
Communicate migration plan to existing HOTP users
Set a migration deadline (e.g., 90 days); prompt HOTP users to re-enrol with TOTP
Medium — requires user action
After deadline, disable HOTP acceptance; force re-enrolment for remaining HOTP users
High — users must re-enrol or lose access
Decommission HOTP-specific code paths from server
Special considerations for hardware token migration:
Users with hardware HOTP tokens must receive replacement TOTP hardware tokens or transition to software authenticators
Token2 and similar vendors offer programmable TOTP tokens that can be provisioned via NFC, simplifying the transition
Some hardware tokens support both HOTP and TOTP simultaneously (e.g., YubiKey 5 in OATH mode), allowing a gradual migration where the same token serves both modes during the transition period

OCRA — OATH Challenge-Response Algorithm — is the third and most sophisticated member of the OATH OTP family, extending the HOTP/TOTP framework into a full challenge-response and transaction-signing protocol. Defined in RFC 6287 (June 2011) — published the same month as RFC 6238 (TOTP) — OCRA generalises the HOTP cryptographic core by replacing the single counter input with a structured, multi-parameter DataInput that can include server challenges, client challenges, PIN hashes, session context, timestamps, and transaction data. Where HOTP (§11) and TOTP (§12) are one-way protocols — the client generates a code and the server verifies it — OCRA is bidirectional: it supports mutual authentication (both parties prove knowledge of the shared secret) and electronic signatures (the authentication code is cryptographically bound to specific transaction data).

OCRA's transaction-signing capability is its defining architectural contribution. An OCRA code computed over payment details (amount, payee, reference) cannot be reused for a different transaction — the user's approval is non-transferable. This property directly satisfies the PSD2 Strong Customer Authentication (SCA) requirement for dynamic linking (RTS Article 5), making OCRA the protocol-level mechanism behind banking hardware readers (MasterCard CAP, Visa DPA) and embedded transaction-signing implementations across European banking.

Despite its technical sophistication, OCRA remains a niche protocol — deployed almost exclusively in banking and enterprise environments. No consumer authenticator app (Google Authenticator, Microsoft Authenticator, Authy) supports OCRA. The combination of implementation complexity, UX friction from dual manual transcription, and the emergence of WebAuthn/FIDO2 (§14) as a phishing-resistant alternative has confined OCRA to its regulatory stronghold in financial services.
OCRA completes the OATH trilogy of OTP algorithms:
Bidirectional (mutual authentication, transaction signing)
Structured DataInput: challenge(s), counter, PIN hash, session info, timestamp, transaction data
HMAC-SHA-1/256/512 (identical to TOTP)
✅ Transaction data included in HMAC input
✅ Server proves knowledge of shared secret to client

The architectural relationship is a strict generalisation chain: HOTP defines the cryptographic pipeline (HMAC → dynamic truncation → modular reduction). TOTP replaces the HOTP counter with a time-derived counter. OCRA replaces the HOTP counter with a comprehensive DataInput structure that can encode any combination of challenges, counters, timestamps, PINs, session data, and transaction details — while reusing the identical HMAC-and-truncation pipeline for the final code computation.

OCRA introduces a suite string — a compact, human-readable descriptor that fully specifies the algorithm configuration for a given deployment. The suite string encodes the hash function, output length, and the exact set of DataInput parameters in use. Both parties (client and server) must agree on the suite string before any computation.
The OCRASuite string has three colon-separated components:
Protocol identifier and version
OCRA-1 (version 1; the only version defined)
The HMAC hash function and truncation length
HOTP-SHA1-d, HOTP-SHA256-d, HOTP-SHA512-d where d = number of output digits (0 = no truncation, or 4–10)
Hyphen-separated list of input parameters
Combination of: C (counter), QFxx (challenge format), PHn (PIN hash), Snnn (session length), TnG (timestamp granularity)
Formal BNF grammar (RFC 6287 §10):
CryptoFunction variants and security strength:
The output digit count d controls the final code length:
Code=Truncate(HMAC-Hash(K,DataInput)) mod 10d\text{Code} = \text{Truncate}(\text{HMAC-Hash}(K, \text{DataInput})) \bmod 10^dCode=Truncate(HMAC-Hash(K,DataInput))mod10d
Full HMAC output (raw bytes)
Machine-to-machine; no human transcription
Embedded displays with space constraints
Standard OTP (matching HOTP/TOTP conventions)
Financial transaction signing

Note on SHA-1 deprecation: HMAC-SHA-1 remains secure for OCRA's use case (keyed message authentication), despite SHA-1 being deprecated for digital signatures due to collision attacks. The HMAC construction is not vulnerable to collision-based attacks on the underlying hash function when the key is secret and randomly generated (per the security proof in RFC 2104). Nevertheless, new deployments should use HMAC-SHA-256 at minimum — the marginal computational cost is negligible on modern hardware, and it avoids audit findings in regulated environments.
Each parameter in the DataInput component is identified by a single letter (except Q, which includes format and length qualifiers):
8-byte big-endian unsigned integer
Optional. Monotonic counter synchronised between parties; loops from 0x0 to 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF
Mandatory. F specifies the format: N (numeric), A (alphanumeric), H (hexadecimal). xx specifies the maximum length in characters (04–64). Default: QN08 (numeric, up to 8 digits)
Hash output length (20/32/64 bytes)
Optional. Hn specifies the hash function: PSHA1 (20 bytes), PSHA256 (32 bytes), PSHA512 (64 bytes). Default: PSHA1
Optional. nnn specifies the length in bytes: 064, 128, 256, or 512. Default: 064
8-byte big-endian unsigned integer
Optional. n specifies the step count (1–59 for seconds/minutes, 0–48 for hours); G specifies granularity: S (seconds), M (minutes), H (hours). Default: T1M (1-minute steps)
⚠️ Moderate — case-sensitive
❌ Difficult — hex is error-prone
Requires full alphanumeric LCD
High-entropy challenges, software tokens
Transaction-derived challenges, embedded systems
Collision probability (128-bit equiv.)
✅ Dominant (CAP/DPA readers)
✅ Common (transaction signing hashes)

Editorial Note: ⚠️ Production deployments should use QN08 minimum. QN04 is vulnerable to online brute-force (10,000 guessable values). QN06 is acceptable only with strict rate limiting (≤3 attempts) and account lockout. QH20+ should be used for financial transaction signing. See §13.4 (Challenge Generation and Entropy) for the full entropy analysis.
Minimal configuration — one-way challenge-response:

HMAC-SHA-1, 6-digit output, numeric challenge up to 8 digits. This is the simplest possible OCRA configuration — functionally equivalent to HOTP where the counter is replaced by a server-provided challenge.
Challenge-response with counter and PIN:

HMAC-SHA-256, 8-digit output, with a synchronised counter, 8-digit numeric challenge, and SHA-1 hash of the user's PIN included in the DataInput. The counter provides replay protection; the PIN hash binds the computation to something the user knows.
Alphanumeric challenge with timestamp:

HMAC-SHA-256, 6-digit output, alphanumeric challenge up to 10 characters, with 1-minute timestamp granularity. The timestamp adds a time-bounding dimension — responses computed with stale timestamps are rejected.
Full configuration — counter, challenge, PIN, session, and timestamp:

HMAC-SHA-512, 8-digit output, with all five optional parameters active: synchronised counter, 8-digit numeric challenge, SHA-256 PIN hash, 128-byte session information, and 1-minute timestamp. This represents the maximum-security configuration — every available input dimension is bound into the HMAC computation.
Signature mode — hexadecimal challenge with session context:

HMAC-SHA-1, 4-digit output (shortened for embedded displays), hexadecimal challenge up to 8 nibbles (4 bytes), with 512-byte session information. The 4-digit output is explicitly noted in RFC 6287 §9.2 as suitable only when applications require very short authentication codes.
Short-interval time-bounded challenge-response:

HMAC-SHA-256, 6-digit output, numeric challenge up to 6 digits, with 5-second timestamp granularity. This configuration provides tight time-bounding — the response window is only ±5 seconds — useful for environments where challenge freshness is critical and the client's clock is reasonably synchronised.
The OCRA computation is a direct generalisation of HOTP:
K — shared secret key (recommended length matches the hash function output: 20 bytes for SHA-1, 32 bytes for SHA-256, 64 bytes for SHA-512)
DataInput — the concatenated byte array of all active parameters
HMAC-Hash — HMAC using the hash function specified in the CryptoFunction component (SHA-1, SHA-256, or SHA-512)
Truncate — dynamic truncation as defined in RFC 4226 §9.3 (identical to the HOTP pipeline described in §11.1.2)
d — number of output digits from the CryptoFunction component (0 for no truncation, 4–10 for standard use)
