\text{Current: } \text{AttestationKey}{\text{ECDSA-P256}} ;\to; \text{Hybrid: } \text{AttestationKey}{\text{ECDSA-P256}} ,|, \text{AttestationKey}{\text{ML-DSA-65}} ;\to; \text{Future: } \text{AttestationKey}{\text{ML-DSA-65}}
\text{UDS} \to \text{CDI}_0 \to \text{CDI}_1 \to \cdots \to \text{CDI}_n \to \text{Application Identity}
d = 2r \arcsin\left(\sqrt{\sin^2\left(\frac{\phi_2 - \phi_1}{2}\right) + \cos(\phi_1)\cos(\phi_2)\sin^2\left(\frac{\lambda_2 - \lambda_1}{2}\right)}\right)
P(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \alpha_k \cdot \mathcal{N}(t; \mu_k, \sigma_k^2)
\text{Cost}{total} = C{FP} \cdot P(FP) + C_{FN} \cdot P(FN) + C_{MFA} \cdot P(\text{challenge})
\text{Auth}_{displayed} = H(\text{amount} ,|, \text{payee_IBAN} ,|, \text{nonce})
\boldsymbol{\theta}{global}^{(t+1)} = \sum{k=1}^{K} \frac{n_k}{N} \boldsymbol{\theta}_k^{(t+1)}
R_{max}(\text{risk}) = R_{base} \cdot f_{decay}(\text{risk})
\text{PSI} = \sum_{i=1}^{B} (P_{expected,i} - P_{actual,i}) \ln\left(\frac{P_{expected,i}}{P_{actual,i}}\right)
\text{poll}(t) = \begin{cases} \text{POST /token with auth_req_id} & \text{if } t - t_{last_poll} \geq interval \ \text{wait} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Implementations commonly use exponential backoff on transient errors while maintaining the minimum interval on authorization_pending responses. The polling deadline should match the expires_in value — if the user has not authenticated within the expiry window, the client stops polling and treats the request as expired.
Advantages of poll mode: no inbound firewall rules needed; simple retry logic; stateless — any client instance can poll; works behind NAT.

Disadvantages of poll mode: token delivery latency bounded by polling interval; unnecessary load on the OP during idle waits; poor user experience for time-sensitive transactions (payment at a checkout counter).

In ping mode, the OP sends an HTTP POST notification to the client's pre-registered client_notification_endpoint when the user completes authentication. The notification contains the auth_req_id but does not contain tokens — the client must subsequently exchange the auth_req_id at the token endpoint to retrieve the tokens.

The Authorization header contains the client_notification_token that the client provided in the original CIBA request — this allows the client to verify that the notification originated from the legitimate OP (provided the token was generated with sufficient entropy and transmitted only over TLS).
Security advantages over poll mode:
No wasted polling requests — the client receives a proactive notification
Reduced latency — the client can immediately exchange the auth_req_id for tokens upon notification
Reduced OP load — no periodic poll requests to handle
Security advantages over push mode:

Tokens are retrieved via the standard token endpoint — the client authenticates itself during the token request, and the interaction benefits from all the security properties of the token endpoint (mTLS, PKCE, DPoP sender-constraining)
The notification payload contains only the auth_req_id — if the notification endpoint is compromised, the attacker obtains only a transaction identifier, not usable tokens

Infrastructure requirement: The client must expose an HTTPS callback endpoint reachable by the OP. This endpoint must validate the Authorization bearer token, reject notifications with unknown or expired auth_req_id values, and respond with HTTP 200 (or 204) to acknowledge receipt. The client registers this endpoint via the backchannel_client_notification_endpoint metadata field during client registration (§34.8).

Ping mode implementation guidance. The client's notification callback handler must: (1) validate the Authorization: Bearer token against the stored client_notification_token, returning HTTP 401 if validation fails; (2) extract the auth_req_id from the JSON body and look up the corresponding pending CIBA request; (3) immediately POST to the token endpoint with the auth_req_id to retrieve the tokens; (4) store the tokens and mark the request as complete; (5) return HTTP 204 to acknowledge receipt. The handler must be idempotent — the OP may retry the callback if it does not receive a 2xx response.
Advantages of ping mode: near real-time token delivery; minimal OP load (no polling); the client still initiates the token exchange, keeping tokens off the callback channel.

Disadvantages of ping mode: requires a publicly accessible notification endpoint; the client must handle authentication on incoming requests; if the notification is lost, the client has no fallback unless it also implements polling (§34.2.5).
In push mode, the OP sends the actual tokens directly to the client's client_notification_endpoint — no subsequent token endpoint exchange is needed:
Security implications of push mode:

Tokens arrive at the callback endpoint without the client performing a token endpoint exchange — the client cannot apply additional authentication (mTLS, private_key_jwt) at the moment of token retrieval

If the callback endpoint is compromised, the attacker receives fully functional tokens — unlike ping mode where the attacker only receives an auth_req_id that cannot be exchanged without proper client credentials

Token sender-constraining (DPoP — §39.3, mTLS certificate-bound tokens — §39.4) is still possible — the OP can bind the tokens to the key material presented at the Backchannel Authentication Endpoint when the CIBA flow was initiated
The id_token in push mode is digitally signed by the OP — the client must validate the signature to confirm the token's authenticity

FAPI-CIBA prohibition: The FAPI-CIBA profile (§34.4) explicitly prohibits push mode for financial-grade deployments. The rationale is that push mode's token delivery mechanism bypasses the security properties of the PKCE-protected token exchange — in poll and ping modes, the client authenticates to the token endpoint using private_key_jwt or tls_client_auth, providing an additional layer of client authentication that push mode cannot replicate.

Push mode implementation guidance. The client's push callback handler must perform a five-step validation process: (1) authenticate the callback by validating the Authorization: Bearer token against the stored client_notification_token; (2) extract the tokens (access_token, id_token, and optional refresh_token) from the JSON body; (3) validate the ID Token signature, iss, aud, and exp claims to confirm the token's authenticity; (4) correlate the auth_req_id with the pending CIBA request — if the auth_req_id is not recognised, return HTTP 404; (5) store the tokens and mark the request as complete, returning HTTP 204. The handler must be idempotent to handle OP retries. The notification endpoint is a high-value target — it must be protected with network-level access controls, rate limiting, and monitoring.
Advantages of push mode: lowest latency (single network round-trip after user approval); no polling overhead; simplest client-side state management (one callback handler).

Disadvantages of push mode: tokens travel in the callback body, requiring strong TLS and careful endpoint security; the client must validate tokens received on an inbound channel; lost callbacks require a separate recovery mechanism.
None — client polls the token endpoint
HTTPS callback endpoint required
HTTPS callback endpoint required
Client → token endpoint (active)
OP → callback (notification only); client → token endpoint (active)
OP → callback (full tokens included)
Client authentication at token retrieval
✅ Client authenticates at token endpoint
✅ Client authenticates at token endpoint
❌ No token endpoint exchange — tokens delivered via push
Callback payload sensitivity
Depends on poll interval (up to interval seconds delay)
Near-instant (notification-driven)
Near-instant (notification-driven)
Lowest — no callback infrastructure
Medium — requires callback endpoint + token endpoint exchange
Medium — requires callback endpoint; no token endpoint exchange
Simple integrations; server-side agents with polling capability
Production deployments requiring efficiency + security
Non-financial use cases where operational simplicity outweighs the push mode security tradeoffs
Deployment scenario decision guide:
POS terminal behind NAT with no public IP
No inbound connectivity; latency acceptable for retail
Call centre with load-balanced agents
Real-time delivery; tokens fetched by the correct agent instance via token endpoint
High-frequency trading with sub-second requirements
Lowest latency; tokens delivered without additional round-trip
IoT device with constrained compute
Simplest implementation; no endpoint to defend
Regulatory requirement for timely authentication; TLS infrastructure available
Mobile app acting as CIBA client
Mobile may not have stable inbound connectivity for push

A robust implementation may combine ping and poll modes to achieve the latency benefits of ping with the reliability guarantees of poll. The client registers a notification endpoint for ping mode but also implements background polling as a fallback:

If the ping notification is lost (network issue, server restart, firewall change), the client's background poll eventually retrieves the completed authentication result. This hybrid approach ensures that no authentication result is lost due to notification delivery failure. The trade-off is slight additional OP load from the fallback polling, which is typically negligible compared to the reliability gain.
Error handling across response modes:
Error callback sent with error field
expired_token after expires_in
Client notification token invalid

The binding_message parameter is the critical user-facing security mechanism in CIBA. It serves the same function as number matching in push MFA (§22.4) — it binds the user's approval to a specific, identifiable action rather than a generic "approve login" prompt. Without a binding message, CIBA authentication requests are vulnerable to a social engineering attack pattern analogous to MFA prompt bombing (§22.4): an attacker who compromises client credentials can send unsolicited CIBA requests to the user's device, and the user — seeing only a generic approval prompt with no context — may approve.
The binding message creates a cognitive link between the user's physical context and the digital approval request:
At a POS terminal: the user sees "Pay €42.50 to Acme Corp at Store #457" on their phone — they can verify this matches the terminal display
In a call centre: the user sees "Verify identity for support case #CS-2024-8847" — they can confirm they are currently on a call
For an AI agent: the user sees "Agent 'CodeBot' requests: Deploy commit abc123 to production at 2026-03-25T14" — they can evaluate the action before approving
The CIBA Core specification defines the following requirements for binding message handling:
The OP MUST display the binding message to the user — it cannot be silently processed or discarded

The OP MUST NOT modify the binding message — the user must see exactly the text the client sent. Any modification (truncation, paraphrasing, translation) breaks the binding between the client's intent and the user's consent

The binding message SHOULD be short — the specification does not define a maximum length, but practically 1–2 sentences is appropriate for mobile push notification display constraints. OPs may impose a maximum length (e.g., 200 characters) and reject requests exceeding it

Character set — the binding message is plain text (alphanumeric plus standard punctuation). OPs should support Unicode characters for internationalisation, but the client should avoid complex formatting (HTML, markdown) as the display context is typically a mobile notification
The user_code provides an additional layer of protection against unsolicited authentication requests. When a client is registered to support user codes:

The client includes a user_code in the CIBA request — typically a short alphanumeric code that the user has previously shared with the client (e.g., spoken to a call centre agent, displayed on a POS terminal)
The OP displays the CIBA request to the user and prompts the user to enter their user code
The user enters the code on their Authentication Device — if the code matches, the OP proceeds with the authentication; if it does not match, the request is rejected

The user_code prevents a scenario where an attacker with stolen client credentials sends CIBA requests to arbitrary users. Without the user code, the OP would display the request and the user might approve it without recognising the illegitimacy. With the user code, the OP requires the user to enter a code — if the user did not initiate the interaction (e.g., they are not at a POS terminal and did not speak to a call centre agent), they do not possess the code and will not approve the request.

Under PSD2's Strong Customer Authentication (SCA) requirements, the authentication code must be dynamically linked to the specific transaction amount and payee (Delegated Regulation Article 5). CIBA's binding_message parameter provides the display-layer implementation of dynamic linking:
passages.headingText: 33.3.4 PSD2 Dynamic Linking via Binding Message

passages.text: The client (e.g., a POS terminal or merchant backend) constructs a binding message containing the transaction details: "Pay €42.50 to Acme Corp at Store #457"
passages.headingText: 33.3.4 PSD2 Dynamic Linking via Binding Message

passages.text: The user sees this message on their Authentication Device and confirms it matches the intended transaction
passages.headingText: 33.3.4 PSD2 Dynamic Linking via Binding Message

passages.text: The user authenticates with biometric or PIN on the Authentication Device — satisfying the SCA two-factor requirement (possession of the device + inherence/knowledge)
passages.headingText: 33.3.4 PSD2 Dynamic Linking via Binding Message

passages.text: The resulting authentication code (access token) is cryptographically bound to this specific transaction via the auth_req_id claim in the ID token
passages.headingText: 33.3.4 PSD2 Dynamic Linking via Binding Message

passages.text: The binding message provides the human-verifiable dynamic link — the user reads the amount and payee and confirms correctness. The auth_req_id provides the cryptographic dynamic link — the tokens are tied to the specific CIBA request that contained the transaction details.
passages.headingText: 33.3.4 PSD2 Dynamic Linking via Binding Message

passages.text: The binding message operates under a trust assumption: the client is trusted to provide an honest binding message that accurately describes the transaction. The following table summarises the binding message's properties:
passages.headingText: 33.3.5 Binding Message Properties and Security Model

passages.text: Implementation-defined (typically 100–200 characters)
passages.headingText: 33.3.5 Binding Message Properties and Security Model

passages.text: Must fit on mobile notification and app screen; OP enforces limit
passages.headingText: 33.3.5 Binding Message Properties and Security Model

passages.text: ASCII printable, plus basic Latin-1 supplement
passages.headingText: 33.3.5 Binding Message Properties and Security Model

passages.text: Must render correctly on all devices; emoji support varies by OP and device
passages.headingText: 33.3.5 Binding Message Properties and Security Model

passages.text: No standardised language negotiation
passages.headingText: 33.3.5 Binding Message Properties and Security Model

passages.text: Client sends in the expected user language
passages.headingText: 33.3.5 Binding Message Properties and Security Model

passages.text: Relies on TLS and OP trust in the client
passages.headingText: 33.3.5 Binding Message Properties and Security Model

passages.text: MUST be displayed to user before approval
passages.headingText: 33.3.5 Binding Message Properties and Security Model

passages.text: OP must not allow blind approval; FAPI-CIBA enforces this as mandatory
passages.headingText: 33.3.5 Binding Message Properties and Security Model

passages.text: The security value of the binding message depends on the client's trust level:
passages.headingText: 33.3.5 Binding Message Properties and Security Model

passages.text: Man-in-the-middle on consumption device
passages.headingText: 33.3.5 Binding Message Properties and Security Model

passages.text: User sees unexpected message and can reject
passages.headingText: 33.3.5 Binding Message Properties and Security Model

passages.text: User trained to match binding message to physical context
passages.headingText: 33.3.5 Binding Message Properties and Security Model

passages.text: Client can lie about transaction details
passages.headingText: 33.3.5 Binding Message Properties and Security Model

passages.text: Client vetting (FAPI-CIBA); restricted scopes; amount limits
passages.headingText: 33.3.5 Binding Message Properties and Security Model

passages.text: User cannot distinguish requests
passages.headingText: 33.3.5 Binding Message Properties and Security Model

passages.text: Different binding messages for each request
passages.headingText: 33.3.5 Binding Message Properties and Security Model

passages.text: auth_req_id correlation per request
passages.headingText: 33.3.5 Binding Message Properties and Security Model

passages.text: Social engineering via phone
passages.headingText: 33.3.5 Binding Message Properties and Security Model

passages.text: Attacker calls user to approve
passages.headingText: 33.3.5 Binding Message Properties and Security Model

passages.text: User sees wrong binding message in secure app context
passages.headingText: 33.3.5 Binding Message Properties and Security Model

passages.text: User trained to only approve within the OP's authenticator app
passages.headingText: 33.3.5 Binding Message Properties and Security Model

passages.text: User sees unexpected message in app
passages.headingText: 33.3.5 Binding Message Properties and Security Model

passages.text: OP authenticates push; message displayed in secure app context
passages.headingText: 33.3.5 Binding Message Properties and Security Model

passages.text: Vague messages. A binding message like "Transaction approval" or "Authenticate" provides no useful context — the user cannot distinguish this from any other request. The message must identify the specific action
passages.headingText: 33.3.6 Binding Message Anti-Patterns and Best Practices

passages.text: Information overload. Including full account numbers, detailed line items, or descriptions exceeding 200 characters overwhelms the user's ability to verify the message on a mobile screen in the few seconds available for a decision
passages.headingText: 33.3.6 Binding Message Anti-Patterns and Best Practices

passages.text: Security-sensitive data in the message. Including passwords, CVV codes, or full PANs (Primary Account Numbers) in the binding message is an anti-pattern. The message should contain enough context for verification but not enough to enable fraud if the notification is intercepted
passages.headingText: 33.3.6 Binding Message Anti-Patterns and Best Practices

passages.text: Transaction-specific — include amount and counterparty: "Pay €250 to ACME Corp"
passages.headingText: 33.3.6 Binding Message Anti-Patterns and Best Practices

passages.text: Concise — keep under 50 characters for mobile notification readability
passages.headingText: 33.3.6 Binding Message Anti-Patterns and Best Practices

passages.text: Unique — include a short reference (transaction ID, order number) to prevent replay confusion
passages.headingText: 33.3.6 Binding Message Anti-Patterns and Best Practices

passages.text: Action-oriented — use a verb matching the user's mental model: "Transfer", "Pay", "Authorise", "Approve"
passages.headingText: 33.3.6 Binding Message Anti-Patterns and Best Practices

passages.text: No security secrets — never include passwords, CVVs, full card numbers, or authentication codes in the binding message
passages.headingText: 33.3.6 Binding Message Anti-Patterns and Best Practices

passages.text: The Financial-grade API (FAPI) suite, published by the OpenID Foundation, defines security profiles for OAuth 2.0 and OIDC implementations serving financial services and other high-security domains. FAPI 2.0 — finalised in February 2025 — extends the base CIBA specification with additional constraints for financial-grade security.
The FAPI-CIBA profile tightens the base CIBA specification in the following areas:
Any registered method (private_key_jwt, tls_client_auth, client_secret_basic, client_secret_post, client_secret_jwt)
private_key_jwt or tls_client_auth only — shared-secret methods prohibited
Plain form-encoded request or optional signed Request Object
Signed Authentication Request (Request Object) required — the CIBA request parameters must be transmitted as a signed JWT, preventing request tampering in transit
Poll or ping only — push mode prohibited (see §34.2.3 for rationale)
Required for payment transactions — must contain the transaction amount and payee
Required — access tokens must be sender-constrained via DPoP (§39.3) or mTLS certificate-bound tokens (§39.4)
Must contain urn:openid:params:jwt:claim:auth_req_id — binding the ID token to the specific CIBA authentication request
TLS for all endpoints; client certificates for mTLS client authentication; signing keys for Request Objects with regular rotation
Mandatory — all request parameters transmitted as a signed JWT (RFC 9101) preventing parameter tampering
Mandatory — request parameters pushed to OP server-side storage preventing injection via consumption device
Any (including symmetric HS256)
PS256 or ES256 (asymmetric only) — symmetric algorithms prohibited
RSA-OAEP or ECDH-ES (for JARM responses) — confidentiality of response payload
Mandatory — one-time-use refresh tokens limit credential exposure
Client pre-registered scopes only — limits privilege escalation at the protocol level
FAPI-CIBA client authentication methods. Three methods are permitted, each providing proof of key possession:
JWT signed with client's private key, verified with registered public key
Mutual TLS — OP validates client certificate against registered certificate or CA
Mutual TLS with self-signed certificate pre-registered with OP
The client_secret_basic and client_secret_post methods are prohibited in FAPI-CIBA because bearer tokens (shared secrets) do not provide proof of key possession.

In FAPI-CIBA, the client does not send authentication parameters directly to /bc-authorize. Instead, it first pushes them to the OP's Pushed Authorization Request endpoint (RFC 9126). This two-step process prevents parameter injection via the consumption device:
Step 1 — Client pushes signed request to /par:
The request parameter contains the full CIBA authorization request as a signed JWT (RFC 9101):
The OP validates the JWT (signature, expiry, issuer, audience, JTI uniqueness) and returns a request_uri:
Step 2 — Client references the pushed request in /bc-authorize:

The OP retrieves and validates the pushed request from server-side storage — the consumption device (POS terminal, call centre workstation) cannot modify request parameters between when the client constructs them and when the OP processes them. This is the critical security property that PAR provides for CIBA.
1. Client constructs Request Object JWT
The Financial App backend creates a signed JSON Web Token containing all authorization parameters, including requested scopes and the binding_message to be displayed to the user.
2. Client pushes Request Object to PAR Endpoint
The Client sends the JWT to the Authorization Server's PAR endpoint (/par), authenticating itself using a strong assertion like private_key_jwt.
3. PAR Endpoint returns Request URI
The server stores the pushed payload securely and returns a short-lived, unguessable Reference URI (request_uri) back to the Client.
4. Client initiates CIBA with Request URI
Instead of passing the full payload to the CIBA endpoint, the Client sends only the opaque request_uri parameter to /bc-authorize.
5. CIBA Endpoint resolves Request URI
The CIBA endpoint uses the reference to look up the previously validated and stored Request Object JWT from its internal state.
6. OpenID Provider validates payload
The core OP validates the context of the unwrapped payload, ensuring the requested parameters are authorized and the signature is intact.
7. CIBA Endpoint returns Authentication Request ID
Upon successful validation, the CIBA endpoint accepts the request and returns an auth_req_id to the Client, which begins polling or waiting for the ping notification.
The combination of CIBA's protocol architecture and FAPI's security constraints satisfies all PSD2 SCA requirements:
Two independent factors (Article 4.30)
Possession (the Authentication Device — phone with biometric-bound signing key) + Inherence (biometric on the phone) or Knowledge (PIN)
Dynamic linking (Article 5) — authentication code bound to amount and payee
binding_message contains the transaction amount and payee; the resulting tokens are bound to the auth_req_id that is linked to the specific binding message
Independence of elements (Article 9.1) — breach of one factor does not compromise another
The Authentication Device's biometric/PIN is independent of the client's credentials; the user's biometric data never leaves the device's secure hardware
Confidentiality and integrity (Article 22) — authentication code protected in transit
All FAPI-CIBA communication is over TLS; tokens are sender-constrained (DPoP or mTLS); Request Objects are signed
CIBA and FAPI-CIBA patterns are referenced or mandated in several open banking frameworks:

UK Open Banking — the Open Banking Implementation Entity (OBIE) standards reference CIBA as a mechanism for customer authentication during payment initiation, particularly for scenarios where the PSU (Payment Service User) authenticates on a separate device from the merchant's system

Berlin Group NextGenPSD2 — the NextGenPSD2 framework supports decoupled SCA approaches that align with the CIBA architecture, where the ASPSP (Account Servicing Payment Service Provider) contacts the customer's authentication device independently

Australian Consumer Data Right (CDR) — the CDR's Data Standards Body references FAPI security profiles; CIBA-style decoupled authentication is relevant for scenarios where data sharing consent is initiated by a third party (Accredited Data Recipient) and approved by the customer on their banking app

Brazil Open Finance — Brazil's Open Finance ecosystem, one of the world's largest, mandates FAPI 2.0 compliance and supports CIBA for payment initiation flows where the customer authenticates on their bank's mobile app
FAPI-CIBA requires that access tokens be bound to the client that requested them, preventing token replay if a token is intercepted. Two standard mechanisms exist:
mTLS certificate-bound tokens (RFC 8705). The OP embeds a cnf (confirmation) claim in the access token containing the SHA-256 thumbprint of the client's TLS certificate:

The resource server validates the cnf.x5t#S256 thumbprint against the client's TLS certificate on each request. If the certificate does not match, the request is rejected. This provides channel binding — the token is cryptographically tied to the TLS session.
DPoP (RFC 9449). The client generates a per-request proof JWT and includes it in the DPoP header on every resource request:

The resource server validates the DPoP proof's signature (using the public key from the token's cnf.jkt claim), verifies that the htm and htu claims match the actual request method and URL, and checks the jti for replay. DPoP does not require mTLS infrastructure — the client proves possession of a key pair on each request without requiring TLS certificate management.

Both mechanisms are supported by FAPI-CIBA (see §39.3 for DPoP and §39.4 for mTLS). The choice between them depends on the deployment's PKI maturity: mTLS provides stronger channel-level binding but requires certificate lifecycle management; DPoP is simpler to deploy but provides application-level binding only.

CIBA formalises a broader category of decoupled authentication — authentication where the consumption device (where the service is accessed) and the authentication device (where the user authenticates) are separate. This section positions CIBA within the broader decoupled authentication landscape.
Both CIBA and the Device Authorization Grant (§33.6) enable decoupled authentication, but they address fundamentally different use cases based on whether the client knows the user's identity:
Device Authorization Grant (RFC 8628)
The client (Consumption Device) — knows the user's identity via login_hint
The device — does NOT know the user's identity
Client identifies the user upfront
User self-identifies at the verification URI
Push — OP pushes authentication to the user's device
Pull — user navigates to the verification URL and enters the device code
✅ Supported — transaction-level consent
❌ Not supported — user approves a generic authorisation
Public or confidential clients
Financial transactions, AI agent approvals, POS terminals, call centres
Smart TV logins, CLI tools, gaming consoles, IoT device setup
✅ FAPI-CIBA for financial-grade security
❌ No financial-grade profile
User presence at consumption device
Not required — user may be physically elsewhere
Not required — but user must have a secondary device with a browser

CIBA → the client knows which user needs to authenticate or approve. The client sends a targeted request to that specific user's device. Example: a POS terminal processes a payment and needs the cardholder's approval; an AI agent needs its supervising human's approval to proceed

Device Authorization Grant → the device does not know who will authenticate. It displays a code and URL, and waits for any user to complete the flow. Example: a smart TV displays "Go to https://tv.example.com/activate and enter code ABCD-1234"
CIBA and push notification MFA (§20.1.2) both involve a notification to the user's phone, but they serve different architectural roles:
Client initiates — user is not present at the consumption device
User initiates — user started a login flow on the consumption device
Standalone authentication initiation — the entire authentication process begins with the CIBA request
Step-up within an existing flow — the user already entered a password or other primary credential
Required — the client must identify the user via login_hint
Not required — the user's identity is already established from the primary credential
✅ Protocol-native — the binding_message parameter
Vendor-specific — number matching (§22.4) provides a partial equivalent
The CIBA flow produces standard OIDC tokens (access_token, id_token)
The MFA challenge is a step within a larger authentication flow that eventually produces tokens
OIDC CIBA Core 1.0 (OpenID Foundation standard)
No open standard — vendor-proprietary (Microsoft, Okta, Duo each implement differently)

The key insight: push MFA is a second factor within a redirect-based flow; CIBA is a complete authentication flow without any redirect. An AI agent cannot use push MFA (there is no browser to redirect, and the agent has not started a conventional login flow), but it can use CIBA to request human approval as a standalone backchannel operation.

AI agents — LLM-based assistants, autonomous workflow engines, multi-agent orchestration systems — increasingly operate with delegated authority to perform actions on behalf of humans. The fundamental governance question: which actions can the agent perform autonomously, and which require human approval? The answer defines the agent's autonomy boundary — the line between self-directed action and human-in-the-loop escalation (§25.2).

When an agent encounters an action that exceeds its autonomous authority — a high-value financial transaction, a production deployment, a data access request for confidential information — the agent needs a standardised mechanism to:
Identify the specific human whose approval is required
Describe the action requiring approval in unambiguous terms
Deliver the approval request to the human's device
Receive a cryptographically verifiable approval or denial
Proceed with the action using tokens that encode the approved scope
CIBA provides all five capabilities as a single, standardised protocol.
The CIBA architecture maps directly to the AI agent human-in-the-loop pattern:
passages.headingText: 33.6.2 CIBA as the Agent-to-Human Consent Protocol

passages.text: 1. AI Agent evaluates action against its autonomy policy
passages.headingText: 33.6.2 CIBA as the Agent-to-Human Consent Protocol

passages.text: The AI Agent — operating rigidly within its defined authority scope — actively encounters an action that explicitly triggers a human-in-the-loop escalation. The AI Agent securely determines which human must approve the action derived from the organisational matrix policy (e.g., the team lead for deployments or the data owner for confidential data access).
passages.headingText: 33.6.2 CIBA as the Agent-to-Human Consent Protocol

passages.text: Artifact Produced: local_authorization_decision (requires human escalation)
passages.headingText: 33.6.2 CIBA as the Agent-to-Human Consent Protocol

passages.text: 2. AI Agent sends CIBA backchannel authentication request to OpenID Provider
passages.headingText: 33.6.2 CIBA as the Agent-to-Human Consent Protocol

passages.text: The AI Agent cleanly dispatches a standard CIBA request directly to the OP's Backchannel Authentication Endpoint (OpenID CIBA Core 1.0, §10.1). The login_hint uniquely identifies the specific human approver (e.g., jane.doe@example.com). The binding_message explicitly describes the action in unambiguous terms. The scope parameter securely maps to the approved operation (e.g., approve:deploy). The AI Agent heavily authenticates as a confidential client using private_key_jwt.
passages.headingText: 33.6.2 CIBA as the Agent-to-Human Consent Protocol

passages.text: Artifact Produced: pending_ciba_request_state
passages.headingText: 33.6.2 CIBA as the Agent-to-Human Consent Protocol

passages.text: 3. OpenID Provider returns auth_req_id to AI Agent
passages.headingText: 33.6.2 CIBA as the Agent-to-Human Consent Protocol

passages.text: The OpenID Provider definitively acknowledges the backend request and predictably returns a high-entropy auth_req_id (OpenID CIBA Core 1.0, §10.3). The AI Agent immediately begins polling the token endpoint at the mathematically specified interval seamlessly.
passages.headingText: 33.6.2 CIBA as the Agent-to-Human Consent Protocol

passages.text: Artifact Produced: auth_req_id (polling context)
passages.headingText: 33.6.2 CIBA as the Agent-to-Human Consent Protocol

passages.text: 4. OpenID Provider delivers approval request to Human Supervisor's device
passages.headingText: 33.6.2 CIBA as the Agent-to-Human Consent Protocol

passages.text: The OpenID Provider securely flushes a push notification specifically to the supervisor's registered Authentication Device (OpenID CIBA Core 1.0, §13). The notification visually contains the agent's identity (registered client name), the binding_message outlining the action, and the inherently requested API scopes.
passages.headingText: 33.6.2 CIBA as the Agent-to-Human Consent Protocol

passages.text: Artifact Produced: out_of_band_notification
passages.headingText: 33.6.2 CIBA as the Agent-to-Human Consent Protocol

passages.text: 5. Human Supervisor reviews the action and authenticates
passages.headingText: 33.6.2 CIBA as the Agent-to-Human Consent Protocol

passages.text: The Human Supervisor deeply reviews the unalterable binding message, heavily evaluates the action's appropriateness against operational constraints, and strongly authenticates using the device's biometric enclave mechanism.
passages.headingText: 33.6.2 CIBA as the Agent-to-Human Consent Protocol

passages.text: Rejection Scenario: If the Human Supervisor explicitly judges the action inappropriate and presses deny, the Authentication Device firmly transmits a denial payload. The OP categorically marks the state as rejected, returning access_denied to the agent and rigorously throwing a CIBA_AGENT_ACTION_DENIED SIEM event indicating a human interception of an agent-initiated action.
passages.headingText: 33.6.2 CIBA as the Agent-to-Human Consent Protocol

passages.text: Artifact Produced: local_biometric_assertion
passages.headingText: 33.6.2 CIBA as the Agent-to-Human Consent Protocol

passages.text: 6. Human Supervisor sends approval to OpenID Provider
passages.headingText: 33.6.2 CIBA as the Agent-to-Human Consent Protocol

passages.text: The Authentication Device actively transmits the cryptographic approval payload heavily encrypted to the OpenID Provider. The OP securely records the approval with a full immutable audit trail: the approver's identity (sub), the authentication methods used (amr), the assurance level achieved (acr), the exact binding message shown, the timestamp, and the client (agent) identity.
passages.headingText: 33.6.2 CIBA as the Agent-to-Human Consent Protocol

passages.text: Artifact Produced: ciba_consent_record
passages.headingText: 33.6.2 CIBA as the Agent-to-Human Consent Protocol

passages.text: 7. AI Agent polls the token endpoint
passages.headingText: 33.6.2 CIBA as the Agent-to-Human Consent Protocol

passages.text: The AI Agent gracefully executes its active polling loop request against the standard token endpoint utilizing its auth_req_id identifier.
passages.headingText: 33.6.2 CIBA as the Agent-to-Human Consent Protocol

passages.text: Artifact Produced: None (Polling transaction)
passages.headingText: 33.6.2 CIBA as the Agent-to-Human Consent Protocol

passages.text: 8. OpenID Provider returns approval tokens
passages.headingText: 33.6.2 CIBA as the Agent-to-Human Consent Protocol

passages.text: Since the human formally approved, the OpenID Provider promptly returns tokens rigidly scoped to the approved operation (OpenID CIBA Core 1.0, §14) — the access_token tightly encodes the approve:deploy scope, and the id_token intrinsically encapsulates the auth_req_id validation binding.
passages.headingText: 33.6.2 CIBA as the Agent-to-Human Consent Protocol

passages.text: Artifact Produced: access_token, id_token
passages.headingText: 33.6.2 CIBA as the Agent-to-Human Consent Protocol

passages.text: 9. AI Agent executes the approved action against the Resource Server
passages.headingText: 33.6.2 CIBA as the Agent-to-Human Consent Protocol

passages.text: The AI Agent cleanly advances the operational logic, explicitly transmitting the deployment execution request securely to the Resource Server. The payload includes the CIBA-issued access token securely placed within the standard Authorization header.
passages.headingText: 33.6.2 CIBA as the Agent-to-Human Consent Protocol

passages.text: Artifact Produced: None (Executing action)
passages.headingText: 33.6.2 CIBA as the Agent-to-Human Consent Protocol

passages.text: 10. Resource Server initiates deployment and returns status
passages.headingText: 33.6.2 CIBA as the Agent-to-Human Consent Protocol

passages.text: The Resource Server actively validates the token mathematically — securely verifying the cryptographic signature, the scope, the acr rating, and the binding auth_req_id. Upon explicit success, the Resource Server unconditionally initiates the requested backend action and returns a clean 200 OK status.
passages.headingText: 33.6.2 CIBA as the Agent-to-Human Consent Protocol

passages.text: Artifact Produced: deployment_receipt
passages.headingText: 33.6.2 CIBA as the Agent-to-Human Consent Protocol

passages.text: The binding message (§34.3) is the human operator's primary decision-making input in agent approval workflows. The operator must understand exactly what the agent wants to do based solely on this message. Effective binding message patterns for common AI actions:
passages.headingText: 33.6.3 Binding Message Patterns for AI Actions

passages.text: "Deploy v2.3.1 to prod-eu-west?"
passages.headingText: 33.6.3 Binding Message Patterns for AI Actions

passages.text: "Pay €15,000 to Vendor ABC?"
passages.headingText: 33.6.3 Binding Message Patterns for AI Actions

passages.text: "Delete S3 bucket: prod-logs-2023?"
passages.headingText: 33.6.3 Binding Message Patterns for AI Actions

passages.text: "Grant admin access to dev@startup.io?"
passages.headingText: 33.6.3 Binding Message Patterns for AI Actions

passages.text: "Set DB replicas: 5 on prod-cluster?"
passages.headingText: 33.6.3 Binding Message Patterns for AI Actions

passages.text: Not every agent action requires human approval. A tiered approval policy based on risk assessment reduces human fatigue while maintaining appropriate oversight. The agent's autonomy boundary (§25.2) maps directly to these tiers:
Read-only operations, non-sensitive data, within established policy
Auto-approved — no CIBA required
Write operations, non-financial, reversible changes
CIBA with individual approval; batch approval acceptable for similar actions
Financial transactions, irreversible changes, security-sensitive operations
CIBA with individual approval; detailed binding message required
Production deployment, data deletion, credential rotation, cross-tenant access
CIBA with MFA on authentication device; detailed binding message; optionally multi-party approval (§34.6.6)

The risk tier determination can be automated — the agent evaluates each action against an organisational policy engine that maps operation types, resource sensitivity, and value thresholds to risk levels. Actions at the "Low" tier proceed autonomously; actions at "Medium" and above trigger the CIBA escalation flow with a binding message appropriate to the information density requirements.

The pragmatic question: why not build a simpler approval mechanism using proprietary push notifications (e.g., Firebase Cloud Messaging → mobile app → webhook back to agent)? CIBA provides several properties that custom implementations would need to replicate from scratch:
passages.headingText: 33.6.5 Why CIBA: Not Custom Push Notifications

passages.text: Proprietary — every implementation is different
passages.headingText: 33.6.5 Why CIBA: Not Custom Push Notifications

passages.text: OIDF standard — interoperable across OP vendors
passages.headingText: 33.6.5 Why CIBA: Not Custom Push Notifications

passages.text: Must build custom token generation and validation
passages.headingText: 33.6.5 Why CIBA: Not Custom Push Notifications

passages.text: Standard OIDC tokens — any OAuth 2.0 resource server can validate them
passages.headingText: 33.6.5 Why CIBA: Not Custom Push Notifications

passages.text: OP natively logs all CIBA transactions with full context
passages.headingText: 33.6.5 Why CIBA: Not Custom Push Notifications

passages.text: Must build custom authorisation model
passages.headingText: 33.6.5 Why CIBA: Not Custom Push Notifications

passages.text: Standard OAuth 2.0 scopes — the access token constrains the agent's resulting authority
passages.headingText: 33.6.5 Why CIBA: Not Custom Push Notifications

passages.text: Must build custom user verification
passages.headingText: 33.6.5 Why CIBA: Not Custom Push Notifications

passages.text: OP-managed biometric/PIN authentication at the requested assurance level
passages.headingText: 33.6.5 Why CIBA: Not Custom Push Notifications

passages.text: Must design and build from scratch
passages.headingText: 33.6.5 Why CIBA: Not Custom Push Notifications

passages.text: FAPI-CIBA profile provides a certified security model
passages.headingText: 33.6.5 Why CIBA: Not Custom Push Notifications

passages.text: Must integrate with each identity provider separately
passages.headingText: 33.6.5 Why CIBA: Not Custom Push Notifications

passages.text: Any CIBA-compliant OP (Keycloak, Authlete, Ping Identity, Curity, Auth0)
passages.headingText: 33.6.5 Why CIBA: Not Custom Push Notifications

passages.text: OpenID Foundation FAPI-CIBA certification programme
passages.headingText: 33.6.5 Why CIBA: Not Custom Push Notifications

passages.text: In multi-agent architectures — where a primary agent delegates tasks to specialised sub-agents (e.g., Google's A2A protocol, Microsoft's AutoGen) — CIBA provides the standardised mechanism for any agent in the chain to escalate to a human:
Sub-Agent B encounters a decision requiring human approval
Sub-Agent B identifies the appropriate approver from the organisational policy (which may be inherited from the primary agent's context or resolved via a role-based lookup)

Sub-Agent B sends a CIBA request with a binding_message that includes the full action context — what the agent chain is trying to accomplish, which specific action requires approval, and any relevant parameters
The human approves or denies
Sub-Agent B receives the scoped tokens and proceeds (or halts if denied)
The approval is logged with full traceability — the audit trail shows which sub-agent requested approval, which human approved, and what action was authorised

This pattern is critical as AI agent autonomy increases — CIBA provides the break-glass mechanism that prevents fully autonomous agent action on high-risk operations. The protocol ensures that human oversight remains technically enforceable regardless of how many layers of agent delegation exist between the human and the final action.

Multi-party approval. Some high-risk actions require approval from multiple individuals before execution. CIBA supports this by issuing multiple /bc-authorize requests with the same binding_message but different login_hint values — one per required approver. The agent collects all auth_req_id values and waits for all corresponding tokens before executing the action:

If any approver denies or times out, the action is cancelled. This pattern is critical for operations like production deployment (requires both the team lead and the SRE on-call to approve) or financial transfers exceeding a threshold (requires both the treasury manager and a compliance officer).
Audit trail integration. Every CIBA authentication event in an agent workflow generates an audit record. For AI agent approval scenarios, the audit trail must capture:
Agent identity — which agent (client ID) requested approval
Human approver — which human approved (from the ID Token sub claim)
Action description — the binding message text that the approver saw and acted upon
Approval decision — approved, denied, or expired (with reason code)
Timestamp — when the approval (or denial) occurred
Authentication strength — the ACR value indicating what authentication method the human used (biometric, PIN, etc.)
Token metadata — access token lifetime, scopes granted, and the auth_req_id linking the approval to the specific CIBA transaction

This audit trail satisfies internal governance requirements (e.g., SOX compliance for production deployments) and regulatory obligations (e.g., PSD2 for financial transactions). The auth_req_id in the ID Token provides the cryptographic link between the approval event and the tokens subsequently used at the resource server.

The following tables summarise CIBA support across commercial identity providers, open banking regional frameworks, and open-source projects. AI agent workflows requiring CIBA should evaluate potential OP vendors against this matrix.
