If the token has been revoked, expired, or is otherwise invalid, the introspection response returns "active": false — the resource server rejects the request.

Introspection authentication: The resource server authenticates to the introspection endpoint — it must prove its own identity to prevent arbitrary parties from querying token validity. Authentication methods include client_secret_basic (HTTP Basic with the resource server's credentials), private_key_jwt, or tls_client_auth.
Performance considerations: In high-throughput architectures, introspection on every request creates a latency bottleneck. The effective validation latency depends on the cache hit ratio:

Teffective=Tcache_hit⋅Phit+Tintrospection⋅(1−Phit)T_{\text{effective}} = T_{\text{cache\_hit}} \cdot P_{\text{hit}} + T_{\text{introspection}} \cdot (1 - P_{\text{hit}})Teffective​=Tcache_hit​⋅Phit​+Tintrospection​⋅(1−Phit​)

Where P_\\{\text\\{hit\\}\\} is the cache hit ratio. A Redis-backed introspection cache with a TTL matching the token's remaining lifetime can reduce effective introspection latency from ~50 ms (network round-trip) to <1 ms:
Direct introspection per request
Redis cache, TTL = token TTL
Push-based invalidation (pub/sub)
Shared session store (no introspection)

For microservice architectures where the resource server and authorization server are separate deployments, use a short-lived opaque access token (5 minutes) with Redis-backed introspection caching. The cache TTL should match the token's exp claim.
1. Client initiates protected API request to Resource Server

The Client sends an HTTP GET request to a protected resource on the Resource Server, presenting the opaque access token in the Authorization header per (RFC 6750, §2.1). Because the token is opaque — a random string with no embedded claims — the RS cannot validate it locally and must introspect it with the Authorization Server (RFC 7662).
2. Resource server identifies opaque token and triggers introspection

The Resource Server extracts the Authorization: Bearer opaque_12xyz header from the incoming request. Since the token lacks JWT structure (no . delimiters or base64url-encoded segments), the RS determines that local validation is impossible and must invoke the Authorization Server's introspection endpoint (RFC 7662, §2) to obtain the token's metadata and status.
3. Resource server posts token to Authorization Server introspection endpoint

The Resource Server opens a TLS connection to the Authorization Server's /introspect endpoint (RFC 7662, §2) and authenticates as a confidential client, typically using client_secret_basic or tls_client_auth. It sends the opaque token as a form-encoded POST body for the AS to evaluate.
📋 Protocol artifact — RFC 7662 §2 introspection request (application/x-www-form-urlencoded).
4. Authorization Server queries token store and checks revocation status

The Authorization Server receives the introspection request and authenticates the calling Resource Server. It then looks up the opaque_12xyz token identifier in its token store (typically a Redis cache backed by a persistent database). The AS checks whether the token has been explicitly revoked (e.g., via backchannel logout or admin action) or has exceeded its exp claim, and returns the corresponding status (RFC 7662, §2.2).
5. Authorization Server returns RFC 7662 introspection response to Resource Server

If the token is valid and has not been revoked, the Authorization Server returns an HTTP 200 OK with a JSON introspection response (RFC 7662, §2.2). The mandatory active field is set to true; the response also includes the subject identifier, expiry timestamp, client that the token was issued to, and the granted scopes.
📋 Protocol artifact — RFC 7662 §2.2 introspection response (JSON).
6. Resource server evaluates introspection response and checks authorization

The Resource Server parses the JSON response and first confirms active is true. It then checks the scope claim against the /protected endpoint's required permissions using its local RBAC policy, and verifies the exp timestamp has not passed. Only if all checks pass does it proceed to serve the request (RFC 6750, §3).
7. Resource server returns protected resource to Client

With introspection confirmed and local authorization checks passed, the Resource Server processes the original request and returns the protected data to the Client in an HTTP 200 OK response. The entire introspection round-trip (steps 3–5) adds latency to each request, which is the primary trade-off of opaque tokens compared to self-contained JWTs (RFC 7662, §1).
📋 Protocol artifact — RFC 6750 protected resource response.

Opaque tokens support instant revocation: the authorization server deletes or marks the token as inactive in the server-side store. The next introspection call returns "active": false, and the token is immediately unusable. This is the defining advantage of opaque tokens over JWTs (§37.3).
The client (or any party with appropriate credentials) can explicitly revoke a token by calling the authorization server's revocation endpoint:
The authorization server always returns HTTP 200, regardless of whether the token existed or was already revoked — this prevents token enumeration attacks.
✅ Instant — delete from store → immediately invalid
✅ None — the token reveals nothing about the user, scopes, or session metadata
✅ Minimal — 32–64 characters (vs. 800+ characters for a typical JWT)
❌ Every validation requires a network call to the introspection endpoint — adds latency and creates a dependency on the authorization server's availability
❌ The authorization server is on the critical path for every API call — introspection endpoint becomes a bottleneck at high request volumes
Partial — the resource server can cache introspection responses for short periods (seconds), but caching introduces a revocation delay
✅ Server-side store can log every token issuance and validation — full visibility into session activity
❌ A downstream service that receives the token cannot validate it without contacting the issuer — requires network access to the introspection endpoint
Opaque tokens are preferred when:
Instant revocation is critical — financial services, healthcare, and compliance-driven environments where a compromised session must be terminated within seconds, not minutes

Token content must be hidden — the client or intermediary systems should not be able to read the user's identity, scopes, or session metadata from the token. This is relevant when tokens transit through untrusted proxies or are logged by intermediary systems

The Phantom Token pattern is in use — the API gateway receives an opaque token from the client, introspects it, and replaces it with a JWT for downstream microservices (§35.4). The client never sees the JWT, and the opaque token provides the public-facing revocability guarantee

A JSON Web Token (JWT — RFC 7519) is a self-contained, signed token consisting of three base64url-encoded segments separated by dots: Header.Payload.Signature. The token carries its own claims and can be validated without contacting the issuing authorization server.
Header — identifies the signing algorithm and key:

The typ header value at+jwt is defined by RFC 9068 (JWT Profile for OAuth 2.0 Access Tokens) and distinguishes access tokens from ID tokens and other JWTs in the ecosystem — preventing token confusion attacks where an ID token is presented as an access token or vice versa.
Payload — contains the claims describing the subject, issuer, audience, and authorisation scope:
Signature — the digital signature computed over the header and payload:

The resource server validates the signature using the authorization server's public key, obtained via the authorization server's JWKS (JSON Web Key Set) endpoint — typically published at /.well-known/jwks.json. If the signature is valid, the resource server trusts the claims without contacting the authorization server — this is the stateless validation property that defines JWTs.

RFC 9068 standardises the structure of JWT-formatted OAuth access tokens, defining required claims and header parameters that enable interoperability across authorization servers and resource servers:
passages.headingText: 36.3.2 JWT Profile for OAuth Access Tokens (RFC 9068)

passages.text: Issuer — the authorization server's identifier (URL)
passages.headingText: 36.3.2 JWT Profile for OAuth Access Tokens (RFC 9068)

passages.text: Subject — the user's identifier (or the client's identifier for client credentials grants)
passages.headingText: 36.3.2 JWT Profile for OAuth Access Tokens (RFC 9068)

passages.text: Audience — the resource server(s) that should accept this token. The authorization server must use distinct audience values for different resource servers to prevent cross-resource token confusion
passages.headingText: 36.3.2 JWT Profile for OAuth Access Tokens (RFC 9068)

passages.text: Expiration — the Unix timestamp after which the token must be rejected
passages.headingText: 36.3.2 JWT Profile for OAuth Access Tokens (RFC 9068)

passages.text: Issued at — the Unix timestamp when the token was created
passages.headingText: 36.3.2 JWT Profile for OAuth Access Tokens (RFC 9068)

passages.text: JWT ID — a unique identifier for the token; enables replay detection
passages.headingText: 36.3.2 JWT Profile for OAuth Access Tokens (RFC 9068)

passages.text: The OAuth client to which the token was issued
passages.headingText: 36.3.2 JWT Profile for OAuth Access Tokens (RFC 9068)

passages.text: The scopes granted — required if the authorization was scope-based
passages.headingText: 36.3.2 JWT Profile for OAuth Access Tokens (RFC 9068)

passages.text: The time the user last authenticated — useful for step-up authentication decisions (§28.5)
passages.headingText: 36.3.2 JWT Profile for OAuth Access Tokens (RFC 9068)

passages.text: Authentication Context Class Reference — the assurance level achieved during authentication (§28.7)
passages.headingText: 36.3.2 JWT Profile for OAuth Access Tokens (RFC 9068)

passages.text: Confirmation claim — binds the token to a specific key for sender constraining (§39.5)
passages.headingText: 36.3.2 JWT Profile for OAuth Access Tokens (RFC 9068)

passages.text: The typ: "at+jwt" header parameter is critical: it prevents an ID token (which has the same JWT structure but different semantics) from being accepted as an access token. Resource servers must reject JWTs without the correct typ value.
passages.headingText: 36.3.2 JWT Profile for OAuth Access Tokens (RFC 9068)

passages.text: Claim design for CIAM access tokens. In CIAM architectures, access tokens are designed for API consumption, not as session identifiers. Two claims commonly found in OIDC ID tokens are deliberately omitted: at_hash (which binds an access token to an ID token — irrelevant when the token is consumed by a resource server) and c_hash (which binds the token to a code flow — unnecessary for API-only usage). The jti claim, while optional per RFC 9068, is included to support replay detection at the API gateway layer. A representative CIAM access token payload:
passages.headingText: 36.3.2 JWT Profile for OAuth Access Tokens (RFC 9068)

passages.text: Every resource server receiving a JWT must perform all of the following validations:
passages.headingText: 36.3.2 JWT Profile for OAuth Access Tokens (RFC 9068)

passages.text: Verify the signature using the issuer's public key (from JWKS endpoint)
passages.headingText: 36.3.2 JWT Profile for OAuth Access Tokens (RFC 9068)

passages.text: Verify the issuer matches the expected authorization server
passages.headingText: 36.3.2 JWT Profile for OAuth Access Tokens (RFC 9068)

passages.text: Verify the audience includes the current resource server's identifier
passages.headingText: 36.3.2 JWT Profile for OAuth Access Tokens (RFC 9068)

passages.text: Verify the token has not expired; reject if exp is absent
passages.headingText: 36.3.2 JWT Profile for OAuth Access Tokens (RFC 9068)

passages.text: Verify the token is not used before its "not before" time
passages.headingText: 36.3.2 JWT Profile for OAuth Access Tokens (RFC 9068)

passages.text: Verify the token was issued within an acceptable time window (clock skew tolerance)
passages.headingText: 36.3.2 JWT Profile for OAuth Access Tokens (RFC 9068)

passages.text: If replay protection is required, verify the jti has not been seen before
passages.headingText: 36.3.2 JWT Profile for OAuth Access Tokens (RFC 9068)

passages.text: If present, verify the sender-constrained proof matches the presenting client
passages.headingText: 36.3.2 JWT Profile for OAuth Access Tokens (RFC 9068)

passages.text: Verify the alg in the header matches the expected algorithm (prevent algorithm confusion attacks)
passages.headingText: 36.3.2 JWT Profile for OAuth Access Tokens (RFC 9068)

passages.text: Process any crit header parameters; reject unknown critical claims
passages.headingText: 36.3.2 JWT Profile for OAuth Access Tokens (RFC 9068)

passages.text: Use a well-maintained JWT library that validates all standard claims by default. Never write custom JWT parsing or validation code from scratch.
passages.headingText: 36.3.2 JWT Profile for OAuth Access Tokens (RFC 9068)

passages.text: Stateless validation — the resource server validates the JWT by verifying the signature against the authorization server's public key (cached locally from the JWKS endpoint), checking the exp, iat, iss, aud, and nbf claims, and confirming the scope or other authorisation claims match the requested operation. No network call to the authorization server is needed. This eliminates the introspection bottleneck that limits opaque tokens (§37.2.4) and enables horizontal scaling — any resource server instance with the public key can validate any token independently.

Self-contained claims — the JWT payload contains all information needed for authorization decisions: the user's identity (sub), their roles (groups), their permissions (scope), and the authentication context (acr, amr). The resource server does not need to query a user directory or session store to make authorization decisions — everything is in the token.

Cross-service portability — the same JWT can be validated by multiple resource servers (all sharing trust in the issuing authorization server's JWKS). In a microservices architecture, the frontend BFF obtains a JWT from the authorization server, and the JWT is accepted by the orders service, the payments service, and the notifications service — each validating the same signature against the same public key.

The stateless property that makes JWTs scalable also makes them fundamentally difficult to revoke. A JWT is valid from iat to exp — there is no server-side state to delete. If a JWT with a 1-hour lifetime is compromised at minute 1, the attacker has 59 minutes of valid access. This is the critical tradeoff of JWT-based session management.
Mitigation strategies (each with significant limitations):

Short lifetimes (5–15 minutes) — reduces the exposure window but increases the frequency of refresh token exchanges. A 5-minute JWT means the client must refresh every 5 minutes — acceptable for web applications with a BFF (§35.2) where the refresh happens server-side, but problematic for SPAs where each refresh involves a round-trip to the authorization server

Short lifetimes (5–15 minutes) — reduces the exposure window but increases the frequency of refresh token exchanges. A 5-minute JWT means the client must refresh every 5 minutes — acceptable for web applications with a BFF (§35.2) where the refresh happens server-side, but problematic for SPAs where each refresh involves a round-trip to the authorization server

Token deny-list / blocklist — the resource server maintains a list of revoked jti values; every incoming request checks the deny-list before accepting the token. This works but reintroduces server-side state — negating the stateless advantage that motivated using JWTs. The deny-list must be shared across all resource server instances (Redis, distributed cache), and every request incurs a deny-list lookup. The deny-list only needs to store jti values until their exp — expired tokens are invalid regardless of blocklist status

Token deny-list / blocklist — the resource server maintains a list of revoked jti values; every incoming request checks the deny-list before accepting the token. This works but reintroduces server-side state — negating the stateless advantage that motivated using JWTs. The deny-list must be shared across all resource server instances (Redis, distributed cache), and every request incurs a deny-list lookup. The deny-list only needs to store jti values until their exp — expired tokens are invalid regardless of blocklist status

Refresh token revocation — revoke the refresh token so no new access tokens can be obtained; accept that the current access token remains valid until exp. This is the standard OAuth pattern and represents a deliberate design choice: accept a bounded exposure window (the access token's remaining lifetime) in exchange for stateless API-layer validation. The maximum exposure is the access token's Max-Age — with 5-minute tokens, the maximum exposure is 5 minutes

Refresh token revocation — revoke the refresh token so no new access tokens can be obtained; accept that the current access token remains valid until exp. This is the standard OAuth pattern and represents a deliberate design choice: accept a bounded exposure window (the access token's remaining lifetime) in exchange for stateless API-layer validation. The maximum exposure is the access token's Max-Age — with 5-minute tokens, the maximum exposure is 5 minutes

Event-based revocation (OIDC Back-Channel Logout, CAEP/SSE — §41) — the authorization server pushes revocation events to resource servers via the Shared Signals Framework (SSF). Resource servers that receive a session-revoked or token-claims-change event add the affected tokens to a local deny-list. This provides near-real-time revocation without polling, but requires resource servers to implement SSF subscriber endpoints and maintain a local deny-list — again reintroducing server-side state

Event-based revocation (OIDC Back-Channel Logout, CAEP/SSE — §41) — the authorization server pushes revocation events to resource servers via the Shared Signals Framework (SSF). Resource servers that receive a session-revoked or token-claims-change event add the affected tokens to a local deny-list. This provides near-real-time revocation without polling, but requires resource servers to implement SSF subscriber endpoints and maintain a local deny-list — again reintroducing server-side state

Token version claim — include a ver (version) claim in JWTs. The authorization server increments the version counter per user upon revocation (e.g., password change, security incident). Resource servers maintain a local cache of the latest version per user and reject tokens with stale versions. This requires only a minimal cache lookup (~1 ms per request) but revokes all sessions for a user, not individual sessions. Best suited for user-wide revocation events (password reset, account lockout):

Token version claim — include a ver (version) claim in JWTs. The authorization server increments the version counter per user upon revocation (e.g., password change, security incident). Resource servers maintain a local cache of the latest version per user and reject tokens with stale versions. This requires only a minimal cache lookup (~1 ms per request) but revokes all sessions for a user, not individual sessions. Best suited for user-wide revocation events (password reset, account lockout):

Phantom Token pattern (§35.4) — issue an opaque token to the client. An API gateway introspects the opaque token, receives a JWT from the authorization server, and forwards the JWT to the downstream resource server. The resource server validates the JWT statelessly. Revocation is achieved by revoking the opaque token at the gateway level — combining opaque token revocability with JWT stateless validation for internal service-to-service communication

Certificate and token revocation strategies apply broadly beyond JWTs — they are relevant whenever a credential must be invalidated before its natural expiry. The three principal mechanisms are CRL (Certificate Revocation List), OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol), and implicit revocation via short-lived credentials:

CRL — the CA publishes a list of revoked certificate serial numbers. The relying party periodically downloads the CRL and checks each incoming certificate against it. CRLs are simple to implement but scale poorly as the revocation list grows; delta CRLs (containing only recently revoked serials) mitigate this

OCSP — the relying party queries the CA in real time to check whether a specific credential is still valid. OCSP provides immediate revocation status but introduces network latency on every request and creates a dependency on the OCSP responder's availability. OCSP stapling shifts this burden to the credential holder, who includes a recent OCSP response in the handshake

Implicit revocation — for credentials with very short lifetimes (1 hour or less), explicit revocation is largely unnecessary. If a workload is compromised, its credential issuance is simply terminated, and the workload loses its identity within the credential's remaining lifetime. This eliminates the operational complexity of CRL distribution and OCSP responder management
The revocation strategy should be matched to the credential lifetime:
Long-lived credentials require explicit, fast revocation
Periodic rotation provides base protection; CRL for urgent revocation
Daily rotation limits exposure; OCSP for rapid response
Short lifetime makes explicit revocation unnecessary
Negligible exposure window eliminates need for revocation

Token introspection vs event-driven revocation. The revocation latency problem has driven an evolutionary progression from short-lived tokens through introspection to event-driven revocation (§41). Token introspection (RFC 7662) — where the resource server calls the authorization server on every request — provides real-time revocation status but adds ~100–500 ms of latency per request and creates an N+1 query dependency on the authorization server. Continuous Access Evaluation (CAEP) decouples the revocation signal from the token lifecycle entirely: the relying party maintains a small deny-list of revoked token identifiers, and when a CAEP event arrives, the identifier is added to the deny-list. Per-request validation then requires only an in-memory lookup (microseconds) instead of a network call. This paradigm shift means revocation no longer requires token expiry — a CAEP-enabled resource server can safely accept longer-lived tokens because compromised tokens are revoked in near-real-time via pushed events, not by waiting for exp.

Extended token lifetimes with CAEP. The CAEP guarantee that compromised tokens will be revoked in near-real-time enables a counterintuitive design: access tokens with significantly extended lifetimes. Microsoft Entra ID Continuous Access Evaluation (CAE) issues access tokens with up to 28-hour lifetimes (vs the standard 1 hour), reducing token refresh frequency and improving performance. When CAE is enabled, Entra ID adds the capol (Continuous Access Policy) claim to access tokens, signalling to resource servers that the token may be revoked at any time via CAEP events. This trades increased token exposure for operational simplicity — the trade-off is catastrophic if CAE fails, because a compromised 28-hour token without CAEP revocation grants the attacker an extended window of access. Microsoft has also introduced CAE Strict Location Enforcement (2025), which extends CAEP to enforce Conditional Access location policies in real time — closing the gap where a compromised session could continue from an unapproved location for up to 1 hour.

Using JWTs as long-lived session tokens — JWTs with hours or days of validity maximise the revocation problem. A JWT with Max-Age=86400 (24 hours) that is compromised grants the attacker a full day of access with no mechanism for the server to terminate the session. This anti-pattern typically arises when developers want to avoid maintaining a session store — "stateless sessions" — but the tradeoff is unacceptable for any application handling sensitive data.

Storing JWTs in localStorage — localStorage is accessible to any JavaScript running on the page. A single XSS vulnerability — a reflected XSS in a search parameter, a stored XSS in a user-generated content field, a compromised third-party script — allows the attacker to read the JWT from localStorage and exfiltrate it to an external server. Unlike HttpOnly cookies, localStorage has no equivalent protection against script access. Never store session tokens in localStorage. Acceptable alternatives: HttpOnly cookies (preferred), in-memory JavaScript variables (lost on page refresh — unsuitable for persistent sessions), or the BFF pattern (§35.2) where the browser never sees the JWT.
Yes (browser sends automatically)
Not applicable (manual attachment)
Not applicable (manual attachment)
Not applicable (manual attachment)

Embedding sensitive PII in JWT claims — JWTs are signed but not encrypted by default. The payload is base64url-encoded — trivially decoded by anyone who possesses the token. If the JWT transits through browser JavaScript (stored in localStorage, passed in Authorization headers that can be read by browser extensions), through application logs (API gateways often log request headers), or through intermediary proxies — the PII is exposed. Claims like email, phone_number, address, date_of_birth should not be included in access tokens unless the token is encrypted (JWE — RFC 7516) or the token never leaves a server-side context.

"Stateless sessions" — using JWTs to avoid maintaining a session store entirely. The server issues a JWT containing the full session state (user ID, preferences, cart, CSRF token) and relies on the JWT's signature for integrity. This pattern trades revocability (cannot terminate sessions), size limits (JWT grows with each added claim), and security (sensitive state is exposed in the token) for operational simplicity. The trade is rarely worth making for user-facing sessions. Use opaque session cookies (backed by a server-side store — §36.2) for browser-facing sessions, and short-lived JWTs for downstream API authentication.
Not possible (within token TTL)
Concurrent session detection
Incomplete claim validation — a resource server that validates only the signature and exp claim is vulnerable to:
Token from low-security system accepted by high-security system
Token issued for Service A accepted by Service B
Tokens valid indefinitely (equivalent to long-lived JWTs)
Algorithm confusion (CVE-2016-10555)
Attacker switches to none or HS256 to forge signatures
DPoP/mTLS binding bypassed; token used by wrong client
Future-dated tokens accepted before intended activation
The complete validation checklist is provided in the JWT Profile section (§37.3.2).

Use JWTs as short-lived access tokens (5–15 minutes) validated by resource servers at the API layer. Use opaque session cookies — backed by a server-side session store — for browser-facing session management. Combine both through the BFF pattern (§35.2):

The BFF maintains a server-side session with the browser — identified by an opaque __Host-sid cookie (§37.1.5), stored in Redis or a database (§36.2), instantly revocable by deleting the server-side session record

The BFF obtains JWT access tokens from the authorization server — using the stored refresh token (§37.4) — for every downstream API call. JWTs have 5–15 minute lifetimes and are validated statelessly by resource servers
The browser never sees the JWT — it holds only the opaque session cookie. XSS cannot exfiltrate the JWT because it exists only in the BFF's server-side memory
✅ Instant revocability — delete the server-side session → the BFF stops issuing API calls → the current JWT expires within minutes
✅ Stateless API validation — resource servers validate JWTs without session store lookups
✅ XSS resilience — session cookies are HttpOnly; JWTs never reach the browser
✅ Horizontal scalability — the session store is the only stateful component; JWTs scale horizontally

A refresh token is a long-lived credential that enables obtaining new access tokens without re-authenticating the user. When the current access token expires (after 5–15 minutes per §37.3.6), the client presents the refresh token to the authorization server's token endpoint and receives a fresh access token — without redirecting the user to a login page or requiring any user interaction.

Refresh tokens are the mechanism that decouples session duration from access token lifetime: the session can last hours or days (determined by the refresh token's lifetime), while access tokens remain short-lived (5–15 minutes) for security. Without refresh tokens, short access token lifetimes would require frequent re-authentication — every 5 minutes the user would see a login prompt.
The exposure reduction from this split can be quantified. For a session of duration T_\\{\text\\{session\\}\\} with access tokens of lifetime T_\\{\text\\{AT\\}\\}:
Naccess_token_uses=TsessionTaccess_token_TTLN_{\text{access\_token\_uses}} = \frac{T_{\text{session}}}{T_{\text{access\_token\_TTL}}}Naccess_token_uses​=Taccess_token_TTL​Tsession​​

For a 24-hour session with 5-minute access tokens: N=86400300=288N = \frac\\{86400\\}\\{300\\} = 288N=86400​300=288 uses per access token. The refresh token is exposed on only 1 request (the token refresh call), while each access token is exposed on up to 288 API calls — making the short AT / long RT split a fundamental security optimisation.

Storage: Refresh tokens are high-value credentials — they are effectively equivalent to the user's session. Compromising a refresh token grants the attacker the ability to obtain new access tokens indefinitely (until the refresh token expires or is revoked). Appropriate storage locations:
BFF server-side session store (Redis, database)
✅ Best — the browser never sees the refresh token
✅ Preferred — the BFF pattern (§35.2) keeps refresh tokens server-side
Good — not accessible to JavaScript
Acceptable if server-side storage is not feasible (SPA without BFF)
In-memory (JavaScript variable)
Good — not accessible to XSS (no DOM storage)
Impractical for persistent sessions — user loses session on every tab close
❌ Never recommended — a single XSS vulnerability exposes the refresh token
Medium — survives page navigation within SPA
Possible for SPAs with BFF fallback
Very High — phishing-resistant, requires user gesture
High-security applications with biometric re-auth
Refresh token rotation — mandated by RFC 9700 (OAuth 2.0 Security Best Current Practice, January 2025) for public clients — is an automatic token theft detection mechanism. The protocol:
The client presents refresh token RT₁ to the token endpoint
The authorization server validates RT₁, issues a new access token, and issues a new refresh token RT₂
The authorization server invalidates RT₁ — it can never be used again
The client stores RT₂ and discards RT₁
On the next refresh, the client presents RT₂, receives RT₃ + new access token, RT₂ is invalidated
The chain continues: RT₁ → RT₂ → RT₃ → RT₄ → ...

Theft detection: If an attacker steals RT₂ and uses it before the legitimate client does, the authorization server issues RT₃ to the attacker and invalidates RT₂. When the legitimate client subsequently tries to use RT₂, the request fails — but more importantly, the authorization server detects that an already-rotated refresh token has been presented. This is a definitive signal that the token was stolen (it was used by two different parties). The authorization server responds by revoking the entire token family — all refresh tokens derived from the original authorisation.
All refresh tokens derived from a single authorization grant form a token family — a lineage chain tracked by the authorization server:
If RT₂ is presented again after RT₃ has already been issued, the authorization server knows RT₂ was compromised — it was used by two distinct parties. The server instantly revokes the entire lineage.
1. Legitimate Client performs standard rotation

The Legitimate Client application securely sends its current refresh token (RT_1) precisely to the Authorization Server (RFC 6749, §9). The Authorization Server verifies it mathematically, efficiently creates a new cryptographic token pair (AT_2, RT_2), cleanly marks RT_1 as explicitly consumed in the backend, and securely returns the newly generated tokens.
Artifact Produced: new_token_set_valid
2. Attacker steals and redeems the active token

An Attacker successfully manages to exfiltrate RT_2 natively from the client device (RFC 6819, §4.6.4). Since the backend cannot actively distinguish the attacker from the legitimate client purely via bearer tokens, the Authorization Server inevitably accepts RT_2, formally generates RT_3 logically for the attacker, and decisively marks RT_2 as definitively consumed. The Attacker now actively holds a structurally valid session payload.
Artifact Produced: compromised_token_set
3. Legitimate Client accidentally reveals the theft

Later in the lifecycle, the Legitimate Client (completely unaware of the malicious theft) explicitly attempts to aggressively refresh its session mathematically using the only token payload it logically possesses: RT_2.
Artifact Produced: None (Replay detection trigger)
4. Authorization Server triggers Token Family Invalidation

The Authorization Server actively detects RT_2 structurally arriving again. Since RT_2 was officially marked consumed seamlessly in Step 2, the server mathematically proves that the exact token was duplicated (RFC 6819, §6.2.2.3). The Authorization Server immediately triggers Token Family Invalidation: decisively revoking the entire cryptographic lineage securely. When the Attacker eventually tries to use RT_3, they will be permanently blocked.

Rejection Scenario: When the backend decisively detects refresh token replay, the Authorization Server instantly revokes the entire exact token family mapping, forcibly terminates the active session, and rigidly logs a TOKEN_FAMILY_COMPROMISED SIEM event indicating active session hijacking. Both the client and attacker are cleanly locked out, dynamically forcing the user to securely re-authenticate.
Artifact Produced: token_family_revocation_record

This mechanism provides automatic compromise detection that bearer tokens inherently lack. The tradeoff: if the legitimate client's refresh request fails due to network issues (the server rotated the token but the client didn't receive the response), the client loses its refresh token. Implementations must handle this gracefully — typically by redirecting the user to re-authenticate.

RFC 9700 offers an alternative to rotation for refresh token security: sender constraining. Instead of rotating the token on each use, the authorization server cryptographically binds the refresh token to the client instance that requested it:

DPoP binding (RFC 9449 — §39.3) — the refresh token is bound to a DPoP key pair. The client must prove possession of the private key on every refresh request. A stolen refresh token is useless without the corresponding private key
mTLS binding (RFC 8705 — §39.4) — the refresh token is bound to the client's TLS certificate thumbprint. The refresh request must be made over a TLS connection presenting the same certificate

Sender-constrained refresh tokens provide stronger security than rotation — a stolen token cannot be used at all, even once — but require the client to manage cryptographic key material (DPoP key pair or TLS certificate). For confidential clients (BFF — §35.2), sender constraining via mTLS or DPoP is the recommended approach. For public clients (native mobile apps, SPAs), rotation remains the pragmatic choice because public clients cannot securely maintain long-lived key material.

Refresh token rotation enables indefinite session extension — each rotation produces a new refresh token with a fresh exp, creating an infinite chain of renewable tokens. Without an absolute expiry constraint, a user who logged in once could maintain their session forever through continuous token rotation, never re-authenticating.
Absolute expiry is a server-side policy that terminates the token family after a maximum lifetime, regardless of rotation activity:
High-security applications (banking, healthcare, government)
Regulatory compliance (PSD2, HIPAA); minimise exposure from session hijacking
Standard enterprise applications
Balance security with productivity; align with the work day
User convenience; "remember me" functionality; re-authentication for sensitive operations via step-up (§28.5)
Mobile users expect persistent sessions; biometric re-authentication for sensitive operations provides a second factor
Autonomous agents and workloads
Align with task duration; aggressive idle timeout (1–3 h); mandatory halt-on-failure on refresh failure

Autonomous agents and workloads. Absolute expiry for agents should align with the expected task duration rather than human session patterns. A long-running batch processing agent may justify 24–72 hours; a real-time API agent serving user requests should use 1–8 hours. Idle timeout is particularly critical for agents: 1–3 hours — unlike humans who may return to an idle session, a stalled agent typically indicates a malfunction, deadlock, or crashed process. Mandatory halt-on-failure: unlike interactive applications that can re-prompt the user for authentication, agents must cease processing and signal an error when token refresh fails, rather than retrying indefinitely (§37.4).

When the absolute expiry is reached, the authorization server rejects the next refresh request with an invalid_grant error, and the client must redirect the user to re-authenticate. The absolute expiry is enforced server-side — it is not encoded in the refresh token itself (which would be a JWT anti-pattern — the token's lifetime should be determined by policy, not by self-contained claims that cannot be revoked).
The effective expiry is the minimum of the rolling and absolute limits:
Teffective=min⁡(Trolling_expiry,Tabsolute_max)T_{\text{effective}} = \min(T_{\text{rolling\_expiry}}, T_{\text{absolute\_max}})Teffective​=min(Trolling_expiry​,Tabsolute_max​)
Where T_\\{\text\\{rolling\_expiry\\}\\} is the last rotation time plus the rolling window, and T_\\{\text\\{absolute\_max\\}\\} is the initial grant time plus the absolute maximum lifetime:

Platform-specific refresh token lifecycles. Enterprise identity platforms implement refresh token patterns that extend beyond the standard OAuth rotation model. Microsoft Entra ID's Primary Refresh Token (PRT) exemplifies a platform-level refresh token with integrated Conditional Access (CA) evaluation:
Device registration — during initial Entra ID join, the device generates a Device Key pair inside the TPM and registers the public key with Entra ID
User authentication — during interactive login, the user authenticates to Entra ID, which issues a PRT encrypted with the device's public key
Token acquisition — when an application requests access to an Entra ID-protected resource, the WAM broker uses the PRT to silently obtain an access token without user interaction

Continuous renewal — the PRT is valid for 14 days but is continuously renewed as long as the device remains connected and the user is active. Effectively, the PRT persists indefinitely on a trusted device

Conditional Access integration — each token acquisition via PRT is evaluated against CA policies. If a policy change revokes access (e.g., user account disabled, device compliance lost), the next PRT-based token request fails — even if the PRT itself is still valid

This per-request CA evaluation pattern means that the PRT functions as both a refresh token (providing session continuity) and a real-time policy enforcement point — the IdP re-evaluates access conditions on every token acquisition event, not just at authentication time. The PRT lifecycle is described in greater detail in §38.

The preceding sections examined each token type in isolation. This section provides a comparative analysis across all four token families — session cookies, opaque tokens, JWTs, and refresh tokens — and offers decision frameworks for selecting the appropriate token type based on architectural context, security requirements, and operational constraints. §37.5.3 extends the comparison to decentralized capability tokens (Macaroons, Biscuits, UCANs) that operate outside the traditional centralized authorization server model.

✅ Small if opaque; ⚠️ Large if JWT

The following decision table maps common requirements to the recommended token type:
API authorisation (single AS, single RS)
Simplest architecture; immediate revocation

Token types are rarely used in isolation. The following table documents common interaction patterns:
JWT access token + opaque refresh token
Client uses JWT for API calls; refreshes via opaque RT
HttpOnly cookie (carries opaque AT) + HttpOnly cookie (carries RT)
BFF manages tokens; browser only sees cookies

Browser holds opaque session cookie → BFF stores refresh tokens in server-side session → BFF obtains short-lived JWTs for downstream API calls. The browser never sees JWTs or refresh tokens

The token types covered in §37.1–§37.3 (cookies, opaque tokens, JWTs) and the architectural patterns in §37.5.2 all share a common constraint: the issuer is a centralized Authorization Server, and every delegation or scope change requires a round-trip to that server. OAuth 2.0 Token Exchange (RFC 8693) can broker delegation between parties, but the AS remains the mandatory intermediary for each hop.
passages.headingText: 36.5.3 Decentralized Capability Tokens: Biscuits, Macaroons, and UCANs

passages.text: Decentralized capability tokens offer an alternative paradigm: offline attenuation, where the token holder derives a more restricted token locally without contacting any server. The verifying resource server validates the derived token against the issuer's public key (or shared secret), confirming that every intermediate attenuation step is cryptographically sound and that the resulting token cannot grant more authority than the original. This makes them the fourth major token family — after opaque tokens, JWTs, and cookies — and the only one natively designed for decentralized delegation chains.
passages.headingText: 36.5.3 Decentralized Capability Tokens: Biscuits, Macaroons, and UCANs

passages.text: The three systems examined here — Macaroons, Biscuits, and UCANs — each implement offline attenuation differently, with distinct cryptographic models, policy languages, and revocation strategies.
passages.headingText: 36.5.3 Decentralized Capability Tokens: Biscuits, Macaroons, and UCANs

passages.text: Macaroons (Birgisson et al., NDSS 2014) pioneered the offline attenuation model by combining a bearer credential with a chain of cryptographically bound caveats. The token holder can append new restrictions — called first-party caveats — by computing a new HMAC over the previous signature and the caveat text. The resulting token is self-contained: the verifier recomputes the HMAC chain from the shared root key and evaluates every caveat in sequence.

Wire format. Macaroons use a binary encoding composed of length-prefixed key-value frames separated by 0x04 (EOT) markers. Each frame contains a caveat identifier and an HMAC tag (32 bytes for SHA-256). The entire structure is typically base64-encoded for transport:
The key structural elements are:
Identifier — a plaintext label chosen by the issuer (e.g., session_user_alice_123)
Location — the target service URL (e.g., https://api.mcp.local), serving as a context hint
Caveats — sequential HMAC-chained restriction predicates appended by any holder
Signature — the final HMAC over the entire caveat chain, derived from the shared root key

Caveat chaining. Each caveat is cryptographically bound to all preceding caveats. Given a root key k0k_0k0​, initial identifier ididid, and caveats c1,c2,…,cnc_1, c_2, \ldots, c_nc1​,c2​,…,cn​, the signature chain is:
σ0=HMAC(k0, id)σi=HMAC(σi−1, ci)\sigma_0 = \text{HMAC}(k_0,\ id) \qquad \sigma_i = \text{HMAC}(\sigma_{i-1},\ c_i)σ0​=HMAC(k0​, id)σi​=HMAC(σi−1​, ci​)

The verifier recomputes this chain from the shared root key. If any caveat is removed, reordered, or modified, the final signature diverges and the token is rejected. This provides integrity without requiring asymmetric cryptography — at the cost of requiring the root key on every verifying server.

First-party vs. third-party caveats. First-party caveats are string predicates evaluated by the verifier (e.g., tool = calendar, time < 2026-03-31T23:59Z). Third-party caveats delegate authority to an external service, which must issue a discharge macaroon proving that the constraint has been satisfied. The verifier checks both the primary macaroon chain and the discharge macaroon's signature. While powerful, third-party caveats add protocol complexity and are rarely used in practice.

Production use and limitations. Macaroons have seen limited production adoption. Google's Turbinia forensic automation framework and Apache Cassandra have been cited in academic literature, but neither represents a current, widely-deployed integration. The symmetric key model is the primary drawback: every verifying server must hold the root secret, which complicates key rotation and violates the principle of minimal secret distribution. Revocation requires external mechanisms — typically short TTLs or explicit blocklists — as the token format has no native revocation support.

Biscuits (Clever Cloud) extend the Macaroon paradigm in two fundamental ways: they replace symmetric HMAC chaining with asymmetric Ed25519 signatures (eliminating shared secret distribution), and they replace string-based caveats with a Datalog policy language (enabling declarative, queryable authorization rules).
Wire format. Biscuits are serialized using Protocol Buffers v3 (spec encoding format 6). The top-level structure is a sequence of signed blocks:

Each block contains compiled Datalog facts and rules, signed with an Ed25519 keypair. The authority block (Block 0) is signed by the issuer's long-term private key. Each subsequent attenuation block is signed by an ephemeral keypair whose public key is embedded in the block — creating a chain where every attenuator cryptographically commits to all preceding blocks:
The verifier validates each block's signature against the public key from the preceding block (or the issuer's known root public key for Block 0), then executes the aggregated Datalog program.

Datalog policy language. Biscuits use a Datalog dialect with 8 base types (variable, integer, string, byte array, date, boolean, null, and compound collections). Policies are expressed as facts, rules, checks, and allow/deny policies:

The Datalog engine uses forward chaining with fixed-point iteration. Execution is sandboxed with configurable limits (maxFacts, maxIterations, maxTimeMillis) to prevent denial-of-service via complex rules. A symbol table replaces repeated strings with numeric IDs, reducing token size on the wire.

Revocation. Each block's Ed25519 signature produces a deterministic revocation ID. The verifier checks these IDs against a revocation list — a simple text file of hex-encoded IDs, typically cached with a ~60-second TTL. This is a significant improvement over Macaroons, as revocation is per-block rather than requiring full token rotation.

Production deployments. Clever Cloud uses Biscuits internally for multi-tenant PaaS access control. The open-source biscuit-pulsar plugin (v3.7.1, 26 releases as of December 2024) integrates Biscuits into Apache Pulsar for fine-grained topic and consumer authorization, with configuration-driven setup and parallel JWT support for gradual migration. Language support spans Rust (primary implementation, ~3K GitHub stars), Python, Haskell, WebAssembly, Go, and .NET.

UCANs (User-Controlled Authorization Network) invert the traditional authorization model: instead of a central issuer granting tokens to subjects, users delegate capabilities they control to other principals using self-sovereign cryptographic identities. UCANs package capabilities as standard JWTs bound to Decentralized Identifiers (DIDs), enabling trustless verification without any central registry or authorization server.
Wire format. UCANs use DAG-CBOR encoding with CIDv1 content addressing for unique identification. The payload is a JWT with DID-based claims:
iss — the delegating principal's DID (typically did:key, a self-certifying format encoding the public key directly in the DID string)
sub — the delegatee's DID (the party receiving the capability)
prf — proof chain linking to parent UCANs by CID, forming transitive delegation chains (A→B→C→D)
att — array of capability objects, each with a with resource identifier and a can action identifier

Delegation chains. UCANs support late-bound delegation: the recipient of a capability can further delegate it to another principal by creating a new UCAN that references the original in its prf claim. The verifier walks the chain from the presented UCAN back to the root, validating each signature. This enables multi-hop delegation without any central coordination — a pattern directly applicable to multi-agent architectures where each agent may need to delegate specific capabilities to sub-agents (see §24 for the agent identity propagation discussion).

Production use: Storacha MCP Server. UCANs are the sole authentication mechanism in the Storacha decentralized storage network (formerly web3.storage), and this extends to the Storacha MCP Server — a production MCP integration where AI agents store and retrieve persistent memory using UCANs as proof of authorization. The flow is:
User creates a Space (identified by a root DID) and signs a UCAN delegating storage capabilities to an agent DID
The agent calls the storacha_store MCP tool, passing the UCAN as the proof argument
The MCP server verifies the UCAN chain offline — no AS round-trip required
Data is uploaded to IPFS/Filecoin and a Content ID (CID) is returned

This is one of the few production deployments of any decentralized capability token in an MCP context, making UCANs the most practically proven option for AI agent authorization among the three systems examined here.

Revocation. UCANs support optional revocation through signed revocation proofs, which can be distributed via Merkle trees or explicit blocklists. However, because verification is fully offline, revocation semantics are weaker than centralized systems — the verifier must have access to the revocation list, which conflicts with the local-first philosophy.
The following table compares the three decentralized capability systems against the token types already covered in §37.1–§37.3 and the sender-constrained mechanisms in §3.6:
Ed25519 / P-256 / secp256k1 (asymmetric, JWT)
User-controlled (self-sovereign)
Token-based (sender-constrained)
✅ Late-bound delegation chains
❌ Requires AS round-trip (RFC 8693)
❌ Requires AS round-trip (RFC 8693)
Datalog (facts, rules, checks)
Token introspection / blocklist
Token introspection / blocklist
Root secret shared with all verifiers
DIDs (self-certifying, no registry)
JWKS / public key distribution
PKI / certificate management
✅ Clever Cloud, Apache Pulsar
✅ Storacha MCP Server (live)

The key trade-off is clear: OAuth bearer tokens and sender-constrained variants (DPoP, mTLS) offer ecosystem breadth and mature operational tooling, while decentralized capability tokens offer offline attenuation and delegation depth that OAuth cannot match without AS round-trips. For the multi-agent delegation chains described in §24 and §39.3, UCANs currently have the strongest production trajectory, while Biscuits offer the most rigorous formal verification properties.
Decentralized capability tokens introduce a distinct session management model compared to the cookie and JWT patterns in §37.1–§37.3:

Stateless session tokens. A Biscuit or Macaroon can serve as a self-contained session credential. The authority block encodes the session's identity and base permissions; subsequent attenuation blocks encode contextual restrictions (IP range, time window, feature flags). The verifier validates the token statelessly — no session store lookup is required. This is particularly attractive for architectures described in §35.2 (BFF + microservices) and §35.4 (API Gateway + phantom token), where stateless validation at the edge reduces latency and operational complexity.

No refresh mechanism. Unlike the opaque/JWT access token + refresh token pattern (§37.3), decentralized capability tokens have no native refresh flow. When a token expires, the holder must obtain a new one from the issuer — either by re-authenticating or by presenting a valid (non-expired) token for re-issuance. UCAN delegation chains partially address this through transitive delegation: a long-lived root UCAN can be used to derive shorter-lived UCANs for specific operations, providing a form of hierarchical time-bounding without a refresh endpoint.

Cascading revocation. When a Biscuit block is revoked, all tokens containing that block are rejected — this is an inherent property of per-block revocation. For session management, this means a single revocation entry can invalidate an entire family of attenuated session tokens derived from the same authority block, which is operationally simpler than tracking individual session IDs.

Compatibility with existing session patterns. Decentralized capability tokens can be transported using the same mechanisms as conventional tokens — Authorization: Bearer headers, HttpOnly cookies, or protocol-specific metadata fields (see §37.5.2's transport comparison). The token format is transparent to the transport layer, allowing incremental adoption within existing architectures.

The comparison matrix (§37.5) and architecture recommendations (§37.5.2) address token selection for specific architectures. This section provides a cross-cutting threat analysis that applies regardless of architecture.
High risk if in localStorage
Mitigated by HttpOnly cookie
Mitigated by jti check + TTL
Mitigated by jti + short TTL

The most actionable finding: JWTs are uniquely vulnerable to side-channel token leakage via application logs. Unlike opaque tokens and cookies (which are small, random strings), JWTs contain full claims in base64url-encoded form. If a JWT appears in an application log, access log, or error report, the entire session metadata is exposed. Token logging guidance (§37.6.2) addresses this directly.
Tokens must never appear in application logs. This applies to all token types:
Token prefix (first 4 chars) + "..."
Full JWT string (contains claims)
Token prefix (first 4 chars) + "..."
The following formula provides a risk estimate based on token lifetime and exposure frequency:
Risktoken=Ptheft×Tlifetime×Vtoken\text{Risk}_{\text{token}} = P_{\text{theft}} \times T_{\text{lifetime}} \times V_{\text{token}}Risktoken​=Ptheft​×Tlifetime​×Vtoken​
P_\\{\text\\{theft\\}\\} = probability of token theft per unit time (depends on storage and transport security)
T_\\{\text\\{lifetime\\}\\} = token validity window
V_\\{\text\\{token\\}\\} = value of access granted by the token (data sensitivity, financial impact)

The key insight: halving the token lifetime approximately halves the risk exposure. This is why short-lived access tokens (5 minutes) combined with longer-lived refresh tokens are the standard recommendation — the high-frequency, high-exposure access token has minimal lifetime, while the low-frequency, low-exposure refresh token provides session continuity.

The following checklists provide a comprehensive reference for implementing session token systems correctly. Each checklist covers a specific token type and should be used as a pre-deployment validation guide.
All session cookies use the __Host- prefix (or __Secure- if Domain is required)
Secure attribute is set on all cookies (no HTTP transmission)
HttpOnly attribute is set on all credential-bearing cookies
SameSite=Strict or SameSite=Lax is set (never None unless cross-origin is required)
No Domain attribute is set unless cross-subdomain sharing is explicitly needed
Path is set to the most restrictive path that covers all endpoints using the cookie
Max-Age is omitted for session-scoped cookies; set explicitly for persistent cookies
Cookie values do not exceed 4,096 bytes
Total cookies per domain stay under browser limits (~150 per domain)
Token lifetime does not exceed 5 minutes for access tokens
exp claim is always present (reject tokens without it)
iss and aud claims are validated on every request
Signature algorithm is restricted to asymmetric algorithms (RS256/PS256/ES256/EdDSA)
The none algorithm is explicitly rejected
kid header parameter is used for key rotation support
JWKS endpoint is served over HTTPS with proper caching headers
JWT is not stored in localStorage or sessionStorage
PII is not included in JWT claims (use sub as a lookup key instead)
jti claim is included for replay detection in high-security contexts
Revocation mitigation strategy is implemented (short TTL, blacklist, version claim, or phantom token)
Refresh tokens are rotated on every use (RFC 9700)
Token family tracking is implemented for theft detection
Family revocation occurs on reuse detection
Absolute maximum lifetime is enforced (regardless of rotation)
Refresh tokens are sender-constrained (DPoP or mTLS)
Refresh tokens are stored in HttpOnly cookies (web) or platform keystore (mobile)
Token endpoint returns invalid_grant for reused tokens
Security alerts are triggered on reuse detection
Refresh token storage is encrypted at rest
Tokens have minimum 128 bits of entropy (256 bits recommended)
Token introspection endpoint is implemented per RFC 7662
Token revocation endpoint is implemented per RFC 7009
Introspection caching is implemented to mitigate latency
Cache TTL matches token remaining lifetime
Revocation response is always 200 OK (prevent token enumeration)

Secret management platforms provide centralised storage, access control, rotation, and audit for sensitive credentials used in M2M authentication contexts — OAuth client secrets, database passwords, API keys, TLS private keys, and cloud provider credentials. While the individual M2M authentication mechanisms are covered in §24, the lifecycle management of the underlying secrets is a cross-cutting concern addressed here.
Key-value, dynamic, PKI certs, SSH, database, cloud
Keys, certificates, secrets, storage keys
Yes (databases, cloud, SSH, PKI)
Limited (storage account keys only)
Yes (database credentials via RDS proxy)
Auto-rotation for storage keys, certificates
Auto-rotation with Lambda functions
Policy engine (HCL policies)
Azure RBAC + access policies
IAM policies + resource policies
Audit device (file, syslog, socket)
Azure Monitor + Activity Log
Vault Agent Injector (sidecar)

Dynamic secrets are generated on-demand with a limited lifetime — each consumer gets a unique credential, and compromised credentials expire automatically when the lease ends. HashiCorp Vault's database secrets engine exemplifies this pattern: a workload requests credentials → Vault creates a new database user with scoped permissions → returns credentials to the workload → automatically revokes them when the lease expires. This eliminates shared secrets and provides automatic, per-consumer credential isolation.
For credential rotation automation and the revocation strategy comparison table (CRL vs OCSP vs implicit), see §24.11.4 (Certificate Lifecycle Automation).

Kerberos v5 (RFC 4120) is a ticket-based network authentication protocol that remains the foundational authentication mechanism in every Active Directory (AD) environment. Despite the industry's migration toward cloud-native OIDC and OAuth, Kerberos handles billions of authentications daily across enterprise Windows networks — every domain logon, every file share access, every SQL Server connection, every IIS Negotiate authentication in a Windows domain uses Kerberos. The protocol will remain critical for at least another decade due to the immense installed base of on-premises applications that depend on Kerberos-based authentication and delegation.

This chapter provides a protocol-level deep dive into Kerberos — covering the full authentication exchange in three phases, pre-authentication mechanisms (including FAST armoring), the encryption type evolution from DES through RC4 to AES, the Privilege Attribute Certificate (PAC) and its vulnerability history, the delegation models (unconstrained, constrained S4U, and resource-based), hybrid identity integration with Entra ID, and cross-realm authentication trust mechanics.
AS and TGS exchanges (TCP preferred — UDP fragmentation issues with large PACs)
Remote administration (MIT/Heimdal KDCs)
KDC proxy (RFC 6111) for firewall traversal
Kerberos v5 is a trusted third-party authentication protocol. Three principals participate in every authentication:
Client (C) — the user or service requesting authentication

Key Distribution Center (KDC) — the trusted third party that issues tickets. The KDC comprises two logical services: the Authentication Service (AS) and the Ticket Granting Service (TGS). In Active Directory, the KDC runs on every domain controller
Service (S) — the target application or resource the client wants to access

The protocol operates through three distinct phases — each producing a cryptographic ticket that the client uses in the subsequent phase. The critical design principle: the client never transmits its long-term key (password) over the network. Instead, knowledge of the key is proven by successfully decrypting messages encrypted with it.
The user or computer account requesting access to a resource
The target service, identified by a Service Principal Name (SPN)
HTTP/web01.contoso.com@CONTOSO.COM
The trusted third party that issues tickets; runs within the LSASS process on every domain controller
1. Client sends AS-REQ to the KDC's Authentication Service

The Client actively initiates authentication by dynamically sending an Authentication Service Request (AS-REQ) directly to the Key Distribution Center (KDC) (RFC 4120, §3.1.1). The request mathematically contains the client's principal name (cname), the requested service (sname — generally krbtgt/REALM), a secure nonce for replay prevention, the supported encryption types, and the required pre-authentication data. The pre-authentication explicitly contains a timestamp encrypted precisely with the client's password-derived long-term key.
Artifact Produced: None (Network transmission)
2. KDC validates pre-authentication by decrypting the timestamp

The Key Distribution Center (KDC) securely retrieves the client's structured long-term key from the Active Directory database. The KDC securely decrypts the PA-ENC-TIMESTAMP data natively. If the cryptographic decryption solidly succeeds and the timestamp sits within the allowed clock skew bounds, the KDC mathematically confirms knowledge of the password.

Rejection Scenario: If the timestamp formally fails decryption (incorrect password) or falls completely outside the skew window, the KDC definitively returns a KRB_AP_ERR_PREAUTH_FAILED error. Consecutive failures seamlessly trigger a KERBEROS_PREAUTH_BRUTE_FORCE SIEM event indicating active password guessing or desynchronized clocks.
Artifact Produced: KDC_preauth_evaluation
3. KDC returns the AS-REP with a TGT and session key

The Key Distribution Center (KDC) dynamically generates the Authentication Service Reply (AS-REP) exactly containing two items (RFC 4120, §3.1.3): securely, the opaque Ticket Granting Ticket (TGT) strongly encrypted with the krbtgt master key, and the distinct Session Key_TGS encrypted exclusively with the client's long-term key. The Client structurally possesses the TGT but mathematically cannot read its contents.
Artifact Produced: TGT_opaque_blob, SessionKey_TGS (client-encrypted)
4. Client decrypts the session key and caches the TGT

The Client legitimately decrypts the session key payload portion exclusively using its password-derived key. Successfully decrypting this natively proves the KDC is legitimate (mutual authentication), as only the authoritative KDC could properly encrypt the session key using the client's secret. The Client securely caches the opaque TGT and the session key in its local credential memory (e.g., LSASS).
Artifact Produced: SessionKey_TGS (decrypted)
5. Client sends TGS-REQ with the TGT and an authenticator

The Client explicitly sends a Ticket Granting Service Request (TGS-REQ) precisely to the KDC to request access to the target service (RFC 4120, §3.3.1). The standard request cleanly includes the cached TGT, the target Service Principal Name (SPN), and a newly generated Authenticator strongly encrypted with the Session Key_TGS. The Authenticator logically proves the client intimately possesses the session key and actively protects against replay natively via its embedded timestamp.
Artifact Produced: TGS_authenticator
6. KDC validates the TGT and Authenticator

The Key Distribution Center (KDC) rigorously decrypts the securely formatted TGT using the internal krbtgt key, safely extracting the client's identity and Session Key_TGS. It thoroughly decrypts the Authenticator specifically using Session Key_TGS and mathematically validates the timestamp and client identity exactly.

Rejection Scenario: If the KDC definitively detects a replayed Authenticator against its replay cache, or if the timestamp falls completely outside the skew, it forcefully rejects the request, generating a KERBEROS_TGS_REPLAY_DETECTED SIEM event signalling potential Pass-the-Ticket lateral movement.
Artifact Produced: KDC_ticket_evaluation
7. KDC returns the TGS-REP with a service ticket

The Key Distribution Center (KDC) successfully generates a robust Service Ticket strongly encrypted strictly with the target service's long-term key (RFC 4120, §3.3.3). This explicitly encapsulates the client's native identity, authorization data (PAC), and the distinct client-service session key (K_\\{c,s\\}). The KDC mathematically returns this ticket alongside the K_\\{c,s\\} encrypted using the Session Key_TGS.
Artifact Produced: ServiceTicket_opaque_blob, SessionKey_SVC (TGS-encrypted)
8. Client sends AP-REQ to the target service

The Client purposefully sends an Application Request (AP-REQ) natively to the Service (RFC 4120, §3.2.1). This explicitly contains the opaque service ticket and a fresh Authenticator mathematically encrypted with the new K_\\{c,s\\} session key.
Artifact Produced: AP_authenticator
9. Service validates the service ticket and authenticator

The Service strictly decrypts the service ticket securely using its own long-term key natively, flawlessly extracting the client identity, the embedded PAC, and K_\\{c,s\\}. The Service effectively decrypts the Authenticator solidly using K_\\{c,s\\} and confidently validates the timestamp. The client is now authoritatively fully authenticated, and the Service actively evaluates authorization logic explicitly mapping the PAC groups.
Artifact Produced: service_auth_decision, PrivilegeAttributeCertificate_PAC (decoded)
10. Service optionally returns AP-REP for mutual authentication

The Service formally returns an AP-REP cleanly if explicitly requested (RFC 4120, §3.2.3). This firmly contains the client's original timestamp strongly encrypted with K_\\{c,s\\}. The Client decrypts this natively to implicitly prove the service's authenticity, definitively thwarting spoofing attacks.
Artifact Produced: None (Mutual verification)
A Kerberos ticket encapsulates all information needed for the service to authenticate the client and make authorization decisions. In the ASN.1 encoding (RFC 4120 §9.3.1):
Key ticket flags and their significance:
TGT can be forwarded to another host (required for delegation)
Ticket was forwarded from another host
TGT can be used to obtain a proxy ticket
TGT can be used for postdated tickets
Ticket is invalid until validated by the KDC
Ticket can be renewed before expiry
Ticket was issued using AS-REQ (not TGS-REQ)
Client pre-authenticated during AS exchange
Client authenticated using hardware token
Ticket requester is different from ticket client (S4U)
KDC should canonicalise the principal name (used in cross-realm referral)
Active Directory defines Kerberos ticket lifetimes through Group Policy configuration:
Computer Configuration → Policies → Windows Settings → Security Settings → Account Policies → Kerberos Policy
Maximum lifetime for service ticket
Maximum lifetime for user ticket

Tickets can be renewed (if the renewable flag is set) by presenting the ticket to the TGS before it expires. The TGS issues a new ticket with an updated endtime, up to the renew-till value. This mechanism supports long-running sessions (e.g., persistent SSH connections, database connections) without requiring re-authentication.
Pre-authentication is the mechanism that prevents offline dictionary attacks against the KDC. The necessity arises from a fundamental protocol property:

Without pre-authentication: The KDC returns an AS-REP — containing data encrypted with the user's key — to anyone who sends an AS-REQ for that user's principal name. An attacker does not need any credentials — they simply request AS-REPs for target users and attempt to decrypt the responses offline using dictionary attacks. This is the AS-REP Roasting attack

With pre-authentication (PA-ENC-TIMESTAMP): The client must prove knowledge of the user's key before the KDC issues an AS-REP. The client encrypts the current timestamp with the user's key and includes it as PA-ENC-TIMESTAMP in the AS-REQ. The KDC decrypts this to verify the client knows the password. Only after successful verification does the KDC issue an AS-REP. The encryption operation is:
PA-ENC-TIMESTAMP=EKc(patimestamp∥pausec∥padata)\text{PA-ENC-TIMESTAMP} = E_{K_c}(\text{patimestamp} \| \text{pausec} \| \text{padata})PA-ENC-TIMESTAMP=EKc​​(patimestamp∥pausec∥padata)
where KcK_cKc​ is the client's long-term key derived from the password via the string-to-key function, patimestamp is the current KerberosTime, and pausec is the microsecond component.
This mechanism has two critical properties:
Online attack prevention — the KDC must possess the client's key to decrypt the timestamp, preventing unauthenticated TGT requests entirely

Offline attack surface — if an attacker captures a valid AS-REP (which contains K_\\{c,tgs\\} encrypted with KcK_cKc​), they can perform an offline dictionary attack by trying candidate passwords, computing KcK_cKc​ for each, and checking if decryption succeeds

Active Directory enables pre-authentication by default for all user accounts. However, it can be disabled per-account via the DONT_REQ_PREAUTH (DONT_REQUIRE_PREAUTH) user account control flag. This is a common and dangerous misconfiguration exploited in AS-REP Roasting campaigns — attackers enumerate accounts with pre-authentication disabled (using LDAP queries), request AS-REPs for those accounts, and brute-force the password hashes offline using tools like Hashcat or John the Ripper. Defence: audit the DONT_REQ_PREAUTH flag regularly; it should be set on zero accounts in a properly managed domain.
Audit command: Get-ADUser -Filter \{DoesNotRequirePreAuth -eq $true\} | Select-Object Name, DistinguishedName
Remove the flag from all accounts that do not explicitly require it (the DONT_REQ_PREAUTH flag exists primarily for certain UNIX-based Kerberos implementations and specific service accounts)
Enforce AES256 encryption on all accounts — this prevents legacy RC4-based offline cracking, significantly raising the bar for AS-REP Roasting attacks
Monitor Event ID 4768 on domain controllers for AS-REQ events with Failure Code 0x0 and Pre-Authentication Type 0 (no pre-auth)

PKINIT — Public Key Cryptography for Initial Authentication in Kerberos — replaces the password-based pre-authentication with X.509 certificate-based authentication. The client presents an X.509 certificate and signs the pre-authentication data with the corresponding private key. The KDC validates the certificate chain against the domain's trusted certificate authorities and extracts the user's identity from the certificate's Subject Alternative Name (SAN).
PKINIT is the protocol that enables smart card authentication with Active Directory (§10.4). When a user inserts a PIV/CAC smart card and enters their PIN:
The Windows credential provider retrieves the certificate from the smart card
The Kerberos SSP constructs an AS-REQ with PA-PK-AS-REQ (the PKINIT pre-authentication type) containing the certificate and a signed AuthPack
The KDC validates the certificate chain, verifies the signature, maps the certificate to an AD user account (via SAN matching), and issues a TGT
The password is never involved — the user's private key on the smart card provides the proof of identity

PKINIT eliminates the password entirely from the Kerberos authentication process, making it immune to password-based attacks (credential stuffing, brute force, password spray). The security relies on the private key being non-exportable from the smart card hardware.
Highest security; provides forward secrecy
Modern alternative to DH; faster key exchange
Legacy; session key encrypted with KDC certificate's public key
Client authenticates without identity (rare)

FAST — also called Kerberos Armoring in Microsoft documentation — encrypts the entire pre-authentication exchange within a TGS session key, creating an encrypted tunnel that protects the password-derived key material from interception and offline attack.

The problem FAST solves: Standard PA-ENC-TIMESTAMP pre-authentication encrypts a timestamp with the user's password-derived key. If an attacker intercepts the AS-REQ on the network (via ARP spoofing, DNS poisoning, or compromised network infrastructure), they capture the encrypted timestamp and can attempt to brute-force the password offline — the same attack model as capturing an NTLM challenge-response. The encrypted timestamp is a password verifier — it can be attacked without interacting with the KDC.

The client must first obtain an armor TGT — a TGT that provides the encryption key for the FAST tunnel. On domain-joined Windows devices, the computer account's TGT serves as the armor TGT (the machine authenticates to the KDC at boot time using its computer account password)
The client constructs the AS-REQ with PA-ENC-TIMESTAMP as usual
The client wraps the AS-REQ's pre-authentication data inside a FAST armor — encrypting it with a key derived from the armor TGT's session key

An attacker who intercepts the AS-REQ sees only the FAST-armored envelope. To access the PA-ENC-TIMESTAMP (and attempt offline brute-force), the attacker must first compromise the armor TGT's session key — which requires compromising the computer account's password or the krbtgt key
Windows Server 2012 or higher
Windows 8 / Server 2012 or higher
Domain controllers running Windows Server 2012 or higher
Computer Config → Policies → Admin Templates → System → Kerberos → KDC support for claims, compound authentication, and armor
FAST requires an existing TGT or PKINIT certificate to derive the armor key
The armor key is derived from the armor TGT's session key:
Karmor=PRF(Kc,tgs,"armorkey"∥"fast-armor-key")K_{\text{armor}} = \text{PRF}(K_{c,tgs}, \text{"armorkey"} \| \text{"fast-armor-key"})Karmor​=PRF(Kc,tgs​,"armorkey"∥"fast-armor-key")
FAST provides two critical security benefits:
Pre-auth protection — the PA-ENC-TIMESTAMP is encrypted inside the FAST tunnel, preventing offline password attacks against the pre-auth exchange
KDC spoofing resistance — the armor key is bound to a specific KDC, making it harder for an attacker to impersonate the KDC

However, FAST has seen limited adoption in enterprise environments due to its deployment complexity and the requirement that clients must already possess a TGT (a chicken-and-egg problem for initial logon, unless PKINIT is also deployed).
Kerberos's security has evolved through successive encryption algorithm generations. Each generation's adoption is controlled by the AD domain functional level and Group Policy configuration:
passages.headingText: 38.3 Encryption Type Evolution: DES to RC4 to AES

passages.text: Disabled by default since Server 2008
passages.headingText: 38.3 Encryption Type Evolution: DES to RC4 to AES

passages.text: Disabled by default since Server 2008

passages.text: Disabled by default since Server 2008

passages.headingText: 38.3 Encryption Type Evolution: DES to RC4 to AES

passages.text: Supported but discouraged; disabled by default in Server 2025
passages.headingText: 38.3 Encryption Type Evolution: DES to RC4 to AES

passages.text: ✅ Current standard (preferred)
passages.headingText: 38.3 Encryption Type Evolution: DES to RC4 to AES

passages.text: Fully supported; recommended default
passages.headingText: 38.3 Encryption Type Evolution: DES to RC4 to AES

passages.text: Windows 10 1607+ / Server 2016+
passages.headingText: 38.3 Encryption Type Evolution: DES to RC4 to AES

passages.text: Windows 10 1607+ / Server 2016+
passages.headingText: 38.3 Encryption Type Evolution: DES to RC4 to AES

passages.text: DES-based ciphers (etypes 1–3) were formally deprecated by RFC 6649 (2012) — 56-bit keys can be brute-forced in hours on commodity hardware, and DES string-to-key uses no salting. Modern AD deployments should verify that DES is not listed in any account's msDS-SupportedEncryptionTypes attribute.
passages.headingText: 38.3 Encryption Type Evolution: DES to RC4 to AES

passages.text: The AES key derivation (RFC 3961) incorporates proper salting, unlike DES and RC4:
passages.headingText: 38.3 Encryption Type Evolution: DES to RC4 to AES

passages.text: KAES=random-to-key(DK(base-key,"kerberos"∥principal∥realm))K_{\text{AES}} = \text{random-to-key}(\text{DK}(\text{base-key}, \text{"kerberos"} \| \text{principal} \| \text{realm}))KAES​=random-to-key(DK(base-key,"kerberos"∥principal∥realm))
passages.headingText: 38.3 Encryption Type Evolution: DES to RC4 to AES

passages.text: where DK is a PBKDF2-like key derivation function iterating 4096 times. This ensures the same password produces different keys for different principals, prevents rainbow table attacks, and makes offline cracking computationally expensive.
passages.headingText: 38.3 Encryption Type Evolution: DES to RC4 to AES

passages.text: RC4-HMAC (etype 23) deserves special attention because of a critical design flaw: the Kerberos encryption key for RC4-HMAC is the NTLM hash itself — the MD4 hash of the user's Unicode password. This means:

Pass-the-Hash is Pass-the-Kerberos-Key — An attacker who obtains a user's NTLM hash (from memory dumping via Mimikatz, from SAM database extraction, from NTDS.dit extraction, or from network capture of NTLMv1 authentication) can directly use that hash as the Kerberos key to request TGTs — no password cracking required

No salting — The NTLM hash is computed without any salt (just MD4(UTF-16LE(password))). Two users with the same password have the same NTLM hash and the same RC4-HMAC Kerberos key. Rainbow tables precomputed for NTLM hashes work directly against RC4-HMAC Kerberos keys
