Kerberoasting efficiency — When RC4-HMAC is the negotiated etype for a service ticket, Kerberoasting attacks (requesting service tickets for service accounts and cracking them offline) are significantly faster because RC4-HMAC encryption is computationally cheap — billions of attempts per second on modern GPUs

Microsoft began deprecating RC4-HMAC in Windows 11 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. The deprecation is gradual — RC4 is disabled for new deployments, but existing environments can re-enable it for backward compatibility with legacy applications. The ultimate goal is AES-only Kerberos across the Windows ecosystem.
RC4-HMAC security deficiencies summary:
Key derivation does not incorporate principal name or realm
Rainbow table attacks; same password = same key across accounts
NTLM hash equals Kerberos key (K_\\{\text\\{Kerberos\\}\\} = K_\\{\text\\{NTLM\\}\\} = \text\\{MD4\\}(\text\\{Unicode\\}(\text\\{password\\})))
NTLM credential theft directly yields valid Kerberos keys
RC4 TGT created directly from NTLM hash
Bypasses Kerberos pre-authentication entirely
Multiple known biases in the RC4 keystream (RFC 7465)
Practical attacks against RC4 confidentiality exist
HMAC-SHA1 integrity check, but the underlying cipher is weak
Confidentiality not guaranteed

The krbtgt account's encryption key protects every TGT in the domain — every TGT is encrypted with this key, and only the KDC possesses it. The krbtgt account resides in the CN=Users container of every AD domain, has a randomly generated 256-character password (never used for interactive logon), and has its password changed automatically by the KDC service every 30 days on Windows Server 2012+. Its Service Principal Name is krbtgt/<realm>.
passages.headingText: 37.3.2 krbtgt Key and Golden Ticket Attacks

passages.text: TGT=EKkrbtgt(EncTicketPart)\text{TGT} = E_{K_{\text{krbtgt}}}(\text{EncTicketPart})TGT=EKkrbtgt​​(EncTicketPart)
passages.headingText: 37.3.2 krbtgt Key and Golden Ticket Attacks

passages.text: If the krbtgt key is compromised, an attacker can:
passages.headingText: 37.3.2 krbtgt Key and Golden Ticket Attacks

passages.text: Forge TGTs for any user in the domain — including Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins, and non-existent users. This is the Golden Ticket attack
passages.headingText: 37.3.2 krbtgt Key and Golden Ticket Attacks

passages.text: Set arbitrary group memberships in the forged PAC — the attacker controls the authorization data, granting themselves any privilege
passages.headingText: 37.3.2 krbtgt Key and Golden Ticket Attacks

passages.text: Set arbitrary lifetimes — the forged TGT can have a validity period of years, far exceeding the domain's ticket lifetime policy
passages.headingText: 37.3.2 krbtgt Key and Golden Ticket Attacks

passages.text: Rotate the krbtgt key regularly — at minimum annually, and immediately after any suspected domain compromise. The rotation must be performed twice (the KDC retains the previous key for a transition period; two rotations ensure the old compromised key is fully retired):
passages.headingText: 37.3.2 krbtgt Key and Golden Ticket Attacks

passages.text: Rotation 1: Kkrbtgt→Kkrbtgt′(old Kkrbtgt becomes backup key)\text{Rotation 1: } K_{\text{krbtgt}} \rightarrow K_{\text{krbtgt}}' \quad (\text{old } K_{\text{krbtgt}} \text{ becomes backup key})Rotation 1: Kkrbtgt​→Kkrbtgt′​(old Kkrbtgt​ becomes backup key)
passages.headingText: 37.3.2 krbtgt Key and Golden Ticket Attacks

passages.text: Rotation 2: Kkrbtgt′→Kkrbtgt′′(old Kkrbtgt′ becomes backup key)\text{Rotation 2: } K_{\text{krbtgt}}' \rightarrow K_{\text{krbtgt}}'' \quad (\text{old } K_{\text{krbtgt}}' \text{ becomes backup key})Rotation 2: Kkrbtgt′​→Kkrbtgt′′​(old Kkrbtgt′​ becomes backup key)
passages.headingText: 37.3.2 krbtgt Key and Golden Ticket Attacks

passages.text: After the second rotation, neither the original K_\\{\text\\{krbtgt\\}\\} nor the intermediate K_\\{\text\\{krbtgt\\}\\}' is valid. The interval between rotations should be less than the TGT renewal lifetime (default: 7 days).
passages.headingText: 37.3.2 krbtgt Key and Golden Ticket Attacks

passages.text: Additional monitoring measures:
passages.headingText: 37.3.2 krbtgt Key and Golden Ticket Attacks

passages.text: Alert on Event ID 4662 with object type krbtgt
passages.headingText: 37.3.2 krbtgt Key and Golden Ticket Attacks

passages.text: Key rotation or unauthorised modification
passages.headingText: 37.3.2 krbtgt Key and Golden Ticket Attacks

passages.text: Monitor for DS-Replication-Get-Changes extended right usage
passages.headingText: 37.3.2 krbtgt Key and Golden Ticket Attacks

passages.text: Attacker extracting krbtgt key
passages.headingText: 37.3.2 krbtgt Key and Golden Ticket Attacks

passages.text: Privileged group membership auditing
passages.headingText: 37.3.2 krbtgt Key and Golden Ticket Attacks

passages.text: Track Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins changes
passages.headingText: 37.3.2 krbtgt Key and Golden Ticket Attacks

passages.text: Detect escalation from Golden Ticket PAC
passages.headingText: 37.3.2 krbtgt Key and Golden Ticket Attacks

passages.text: Forward DC security logs to immutable SIEM
passages.headingText: 37.3.2 krbtgt Key and Golden Ticket Attacks

passages.text: Ensure log integrity for forensic analysis
passages.headingText: 37.3.2 krbtgt Key and Golden Ticket Attacks

passages.text: DCs in dedicated subnets; PAW for admin access; Credential Guard on DCs
passages.headingText: 37.3.2 krbtgt Key and Golden Ticket Attacks

passages.text: Limits paths to krbtgt extraction
passages.headingText: 37.3.2 krbtgt Key and Golden Ticket Attacks

passages.text: In Active Directory, encryption types are configured at multiple levels:
Domain level — Group Policy controls which encryption types are supported for Kerberos:
Computer Config → Policies → Windows Settings → Security Settings → Local Policies → Security Options → Network security: Configure encryption types allowed for Kerberos
Recommended setting: AES256_HMAC_SHA1 only (value: 0x80000000)
Account level — each AD object has an msDS-SupportedEncryptionTypes attribute:
0x0: None (use domain default)
0x8: AES128-CTS-HMAC-SHA1-96
0x10: AES256-CTS-HMAC-SHA1-96
0x18: Both AES types (recommended default for modern deployments)
To identify accounts still relying on RC4 without AES, audit with:

The PAC is a Microsoft-proprietary authorization data structure defined in [MS-PAC] that is embedded within Kerberos tickets to carry the user's Windows-specific security information. Despite its name, the PAC is not an X.509 certificate — it is a structured data blob placed inside the authorization-data field of the ticket's EncTicketPart. The PAC contains:
User Security Identifier (SID) — the unique identifier for the user account (S-1-5-21-<domain>-<RID>)
Group SIDs — all security groups the user is a member of (including nested group memberships)
Privilege attributes — special privileges assigned to the user (e.g., SeDebugPrivilege, SeBackupPrivilege)
Logon information — account name, domain, logon time, password last set, account control flags

Server and KDC signatures — the PAC is signed by both the KDC (using the krbtgt key) and the target service (using the service's key). These signatures provide tamper detection and provenance verification

The PAC is what makes Kerberos tickets work as authorization tokens in Windows environments — without the PAC, the service ticket proves identity but provides no information about the user's group memberships or privileges.
PAC information buffers (MS-PAC §2.1):
Logon Info (KERB_VALIDATION_INFO)
User SID, group SIDs, logon time, user flags, logon server
Up to four supplemental credentials (NTLM, Kerberos, WDigest)
HMAC signature computed with the service account's key — validates PAC integrity
HMAC signature computed with the krbtgt key — provides end-to-end integrity even if the service is compromised
SID of the account that requested the ticket (S4U scenarios)
User Principal Name and DNS domain name
Claims-based authorization data (dynamic claims, device claims)
Extended attributes (compound identity, federated claims)
The PAC contains two digital signatures that provide integrity verification — a Server Signature and a KDC Signature:
ServerSig=HMAC(Ks,PAC_data)\text{ServerSig} = \text{HMAC}(K_s, \text{PAC\_data})ServerSig=HMAC(Ks​,PAC_data)
KDCSig=HMAC(Kkrbtgt,PAC_data)\text{KDCSig} = \text{HMAC}(K_{\text{krbtgt}}, \text{PAC\_data})KDCSig=HMAC(Kkrbtgt​,PAC_data)

The Server Signature is computed using the target service's key (KsK_sKs​), while the KDC Signature uses the krbtgt key. Note that the same K_\\{\text\\{krbtgt\\}\\} is used for both TGT encryption and the PAC KDC Signature — this is the critical link that makes krbtgt compromise so dangerous, as the attacker can both forge TGTs and produce valid PAC signatures.
The PAC's signature validation has been the target of critical vulnerabilities:

CVE-2014-6324 (MS14-068) — A critical PAC forgery vulnerability (CVSS 10.0). The KDC's signature validation logic accepted PACs signed with algorithms weaker than expected — an attacker with any valid domain user credential could construct a PAC claiming Domain Administrator membership, sign it with a weak algorithm (e.g., MD5 checksum), and inject it into a Kerberos ticket. The KDC accepted the forged PAC, effectively allowing any domain user to escalate to Domain Admin. The patch (MS14-068, November 2014) enforced strict signature algorithm validation for PAC signatures. All domain controllers must be patched — modern Windows Server versions enforce PAC signature validation on all tickets.
CVE-2021-42287 / CVE-2021-42278 (noPac / sAMAccountName spoofing) — A two-vulnerability chain (CVSS 8.8) that exploits the KDC's ticket-granting logic:

CVE-2021-42278: By default, any authenticated domain user can create machine accounts (up to 10, controlled by the ms-DS-MachineAccountQuota attribute). The attacker creates a machine account with a sAMAccountName matching a domain controller's name (minus the trailing $)
The attacker requests a TGT for the spoofed machine account
The attacker renames the machine account to something else (removing the DC name collision)

CVE-2021-42287: The attacker uses the TGT to request a service ticket. The KDC looks up the TGT's principal name (sAMAccountName without $), finds no matching account (it was renamed), and falls back to appending $ — finding the real domain controller account. The KDC issues a service ticket as if the client were the domain controller
The attacker now holds a service ticket impersonating a domain controller — enabling DCSync, NTDS.dit extraction, and full domain compromise
Mitigation: Patch immediately; reduce ms-DS-MachineAccountQuota to 0; monitor for machine account creation and sAMAccountName changes.

Kerberos delegation allows a service to impersonate the client and access other services on the client's behalf — a critical requirement for multi-tier applications (e.g., a web server that accesses a SQL server on behalf of the user).

The service receives the user's forwardable TGT — the client includes its TGT in the AP-REQ, and the service extracts and caches it. The service can use this TGT to request service tickets for any service in the domain, impersonating the user.
Configuration: Set on the service account's AD object via the TRUSTED_FOR_DELEGATION flag

Security risk: ❌ Extreme. If the delegated service is compromised, the attacker possesses TGTs for every user who authenticated to that service. The attacker can impersonate any of those users to any service in the domain. The Printer Bug (SpoolService) and PetitPotam (CVE-2021-36942) attacks exploit unconstrained delegation by coercing domain controllers to authenticate to a compromised server — using the EfsRpc or PrintSpooler protocols to force the DC to authenticate, then capturing the DC's TGT to achieve domain compromise

Recommendation: Unconstrained delegation should be disabled on all service accounts except domain controllers (which require it for intra-domain replication). High-privilege accounts should be members of the Protected Users security group, which prevents credential delegation entirely. Monitor with Microsoft Defender for Identity
Constrained Delegation (S4U Extensions)
Constrained delegation limits which services the delegating service can impersonate the user to. Two S4U (Service for User) extensions provide this:

S4U2Self (Service for User to Self) — The service obtains a service ticket to itself on behalf of a user, even when the user did not authenticate to the service via Kerberos (ServiceA→S4U2SelfTicketforServiceA(asUserB)\text\\{Service A\\} \xrightarrow\\{\text\\{S4U2Self\\}\\} \text\\{Ticket for Service A (as User B)\\}ServiceA​S4U2SelfTicketforServiceA(asUserB)). Use case: the user authenticated via forms-based authentication or certificate authentication (non-Kerberos), and the service needs a Kerberos ticket to access backend resources. S4U2Self produces a service ticket for the requesting service with the user's identity in the PAC. Requires the TrustedToAuthForDelegation flag in userAccountControl

S4U2Proxy (Service for User to Proxy) — The service uses the S4U2Self ticket (or a forwarded service ticket from the user) to request a service ticket for a specific target service on behalf of the user (ServiceA→S4U2ProxyTicketforServiceB(asUserB)\text\\{Service A\\} \xrightarrow\\{\text\\{S4U2Proxy\\}\\} \text\\{Ticket for Service B (as User B)\\}ServiceA​S4U2ProxyTicketforServiceB(asUserB)). The target services are configured in the msDS-AllowedToDelegateTo attribute on the delegating service's AD account
Example: A web application (HTTP/webapp.corp.example.com) configured with msDS-AllowedToDelegateTo = MSSQLSvc/sqlserver.corp.example.com:
User authenticates to the web app via certificate (non-Kerberos)
The web app uses S4U2Self to obtain a Kerberos ticket to itself as the user
The web app uses S4U2Proxy to exchange that ticket for a service ticket to MSSQLSvc/sqlserver.corp.example.com as the user
The web app connects to SQL Server with the user's Kerberos identity — SQL Server sees the original user, not the web app's service account
Resource-Based Constrained Delegation (RBCD)

RBCD — introduced in Windows Server 2012 — inverts the configuration model. Instead of specifying on the delegating service which targets it can access (traditional constrained delegation), RBCD configures on the target resource which services are allowed to delegate to it:

Configuration: The msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity attribute on the target service's AD object contains a security descriptor listing the service accounts permitted to delegate to this resource

Administrative advantage: The resource owner controls delegation permissions — they do not need Domain Admin privileges to configure delegation (unlike traditional constrained delegation, which requires modifying the delegating service's msDS-AllowedToDelegateTo)

Security risk: RBCD introduced a new attack vector — an attacker who can modify the msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity attribute on a computer object (achievable via GenericAll/GenericWrite ACE, or by compromising a privileged account) can configure delegation to themselves and impersonate any user to the target service. The RBCD abuse attack path: (1) obtain write access to msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity on the target → (2) set the attacker's account as an allowed delegate → (3) use S4U2Self + S4U2Proxy to access the target as any user → (4) common targets include computer accounts (CIFS/RDP) and service accounts (MSSQL). Key security differences from unconstrained delegation: no forwardable TGT is included in the service ticket (limiting scope), and the front-end service never receives the client's TGT — only a forwardable service ticket to the specific back-end
Resource-Based Constrained (RBCD)
userAccountControl flag on front-end
msDS-AllowedToDelegateTo on front-end + userAccountControl flag
msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity on back-end
Resource owner (self-write or delegated)
Only specified SPNs in msDS-AllowedToDelegateTo
✅ Yes — client's TGT is embedded in the ticket
No — only a forwardable service ticket is issued
No — only a forwardable service ticket is issued
Protocol transition (S4U2Self)
Supported (requires TrustedToAuthForDelegation)
Supported (with forest trust)
Supported (requires msDS-AllowedToDelegateTo across trust)
Supported (with forest trust)
Attack surface if front-end is compromised
Complete domain compromise (via Printer Bug)
Limited to specified back-end services
Limited to the target service
❌ Never use in new deployments
✅ Acceptable with strict SPN scoping
Never use unconstrained delegation in new deployments. Migrate existing unconstrained delegation to RBCD
Prefer RBCD over traditional constrained delegation — it shifts control to the resource owner and requires fewer Domain Admin privileges
Minimise the use of S4U2Self (protocol transition) — it allows a service to impersonate users who have never directly authenticated, which is inherently risky
Protect privileged accounts — Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins, and DC computer accounts should be members of the Protected Users security group, which prevents their credentials from being delegated
Monitor delegation configurations — audit accounts with delegation-related userAccountControl flags and msDS-AllowedToDelegateTo values regularly

Restrict ACLs — ensure that only necessary accounts have write access to delegation-related attributes (msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity, msDS-AllowedToDelegateTo) on service and computer accounts

Kerberos TGT protection: Pass-the-Ticket and Credential Guard. The Kerberos TGT is stored in LSASS process memory on Windows domain-joined machines. Privileged attackers can extract TGTs and TGS tickets from LSASS via credential dumping tools (e.g., Mimikatz sekurlsa::kerberos or kerberos::ptt) and replay them on other machines — this is the Pass-the-Ticket attack. Unlike credential theft (where the attacker obtains the password or hash), Pass-the-Ticket reuses a valid Kerberos ticket directly — the ticket's remaining lifetime is the attacker's window of access. Windows Credential Guard mitigates this by isolating LSASS secrets in a Hyper-V-enforced virtualization-based security (VBS) enclave. When Credential Guard is enabled, Kerberos keys and TGT material reside in a protected LSA process that even kernel-level code cannot access — preventing Pass-the-Ticket, Pass-the-Hash, and other memory-based credential extraction techniques. Credential Guard is available on Windows 10 Enterprise/Education and Windows Server 2016+ and is a core component of Microsoft's credential protection strategy (see §38.5.5 for the hybrid identity trajectory).

Modern enterprises operate in a hybrid identity landscape: on-premises Active Directory (Kerberos, NTLM) coexists with cloud Entra ID (OIDC, OAuth 2.0). Users authenticate to Microsoft 365, SaaS applications, and custom cloud apps via Entra ID, but still need Kerberos tickets for on-premises file shares, intranet web applications, SQL Server databases, and line-of-business applications that have no cloud migration path. The hybrid identity architecture bridges these two authentication worlds.
Entra Connect (formerly Azure AD Connect) synchronizes user objects from on-premises AD to Entra ID. Three authentication methods determine how user credentials are validated:

Password Hash Synchronization (PHS) — Entra Connect computes a derivative of the on-premises password hash and synchronizes it to Entra ID. Authentication is handled entirely in the cloud — Entra ID validates the password against the synchronized hash. The on-premises AD is not involved in cloud authentication. ⚠️ Security note: PHS synchronizes the NTLM hash, which (as discussed in §38.3.1) is identical to the RC4-HMAC Kerberos key — an Entra ID tenant compromise therefore exposes credentials usable for on-premises Kerberos authentication via Overpass-the-Hash

Pass-Through Authentication (PTA) — Entra ID does not store password hashes. When a user authenticates to Entra ID, the authentication request is forwarded (via a lightweight PTA agent on-premises) to the on-premises AD domain controller, which validates the password. The password never leaves the on-premises environment

Federation (AD FS / third-party IdP) — Entra ID redirects authentication to an on-premises AD FS server (or third-party SAML/WS-Fed IdP). The IdP authenticates the user against AD and returns a SAML assertion or WS-Fed token to Entra ID
Authentication mode comparison:
Low (no on-prem dependency for cloud auth)
Yes (cached cloud credentials)
Yes (PTA agent validates via Kerberos/NTLM to DC)
Medium (PTA agent forwards to on-prem DC)
Limited (depends on PTA agent availability)
Yes (AD FS uses Kerberos to validate to DC)
Yes (AD FS can be configured for cached auth)

Seamless SSO enables domain-joined Windows devices to silently authenticate to Entra ID using their existing Kerberos TGT — the user experiences a seamless transition from on-premises Kerberos to cloud OIDC without entering credentials:
Setup: Entra Connect creates a computer account named AZUREADSSOACC in on-premises AD and shares its Kerberos decryption key with Entra ID
User accesses a cloud resource: The browser navigates to an Entra ID-protected application (e.g., https://myapp.microsoft.com)

Entra ID redirects to the autologon endpoint: Entra ID detects the domain-joined device and redirects the browser to https://autologon.microsoftazuread-sso.com/REALM — a hostname that maps to the AZUREADSSOACC service principal
Browser requests a Kerberos ticket: The browser sends a TGS-REQ to the KDC for the SPN HTTP/autologon.microsoftazuread-sso.com
KDC issues a service ticket: The KDC issues a service ticket encrypted with the AZUREADSSOACC account's key
Browser sends the Kerberos ticket to Entra ID: The service ticket is included in the Authorization: Negotiate header
Entra ID validates the ticket: Entra ID decrypts the service ticket using the shared AZUREADSSOACC key, extracts the user's identity from the PAC, and issues OIDC tokens (ID token, access token)
This bridges the Kerberos and OIDC worlds: the user's on-premises Kerberos TGT becomes the authentication vehicle for cloud single sign-on.

Critical security requirement: The AZUREADSSOACC computer account's Kerberos decryption key must be rotated at minimum every 30 days (Microsoft's recommendation). If this key is compromised, an attacker can forge Kerberos service tickets that Entra ID will accept — effectively executing a Silver Ticket attack against the cloud. The attacker can impersonate any user to Entra ID without knowing their password, bypassing MFA if Seamless SSO is configured to bypass MFA for domain-joined devices. The account password can be rolled using Set-AzureADSSOAccountPassword if compromise is suspected.

Windows Hello for Business (WHfB) enables passwordless authentication using platform-bound credentials (§14.7). In hybrid environments, WHfB users need Kerberos TGTs for on-premises resource access. Three deployment models exist:
passages.headingText: 37.5.4 Kerberos Cloud Trust for Windows Hello for Business

passages.text: Certificate trust (legacy) — Requires an on-premises certificate authority (CA) to issue certificates to WHfB users. The certificate enables PKINIT (§38.2.2) for Kerberos TGT issuance
passages.headingText: 37.5.4 Kerberos Cloud Trust for Windows Hello for Business

passages.text: Key trust — The WHfB public key is synchronized to on-premises AD via Entra Connect. The KDC validates the WHfB key during AS-REQ and issues a TGT. Requires Windows Server 2016+ DCs
passages.headingText: 37.5.4 Kerberos Cloud Trust for Windows Hello for Business

passages.text: Cloud trust (recommended) — Uses the same AZUREADSSOACC mechanism via IAKerb (Initial and Pass-Through Authentication Using Kerberos). The WHfB authentication occurs against Entra ID in the cloud, and the resulting Kerberos ticket provides on-premises access:
passages.headingText: 37.5.4 Kerberos Cloud Trust for Windows Hello for Business

passages.text: User PIN/Biometric→Device Key Pair→IAKerb Assertion to KDC→KDC validates with Entra ID→TGT issued\text{User PIN/Biometric} \rightarrow \text{Device Key Pair} \rightarrow \text{IAKerb Assertion to KDC} \rightarrow \text{KDC validates with Entra ID} \rightarrow \text{TGT issued}User PIN/Biometric→Device Key Pair→IAKerb Assertion to KDC→KDC validates with Entra ID→TGT issued
passages.headingText: 37.5.4 Kerberos Cloud Trust for Windows Hello for Business

passages.text: Certificate Trust vs Cloud Trust comparison:
passages.headingText: 37.5.4 Kerberos Cloud Trust for Windows Hello for Business

passages.text: Yes (on-prem CA infrastructure)
passages.headingText: 37.5.4 Kerberos Cloud Trust for Windows Hello for Business

passages.text: Cloud trust represents Microsoft's convergent direction: cloud-native authentication (passkeys, WHfB) as the primary mechanism, with Kerberos used transparently as a backward-compatible bridge for legacy on-premises applications.
passages.headingText: 37.5.4 Kerberos Cloud Trust for Windows Hello for Business

passages.text: Kerberos will remain operationally necessary as long as on-premises Windows applications exist. Microsoft's strategy is progressive Kerberos dependency reduction:
Short-term — Hybrid identity with Seamless SSO bridges cloud and on-premises authentication

Medium-term — IAKerb (Initial and Pass-Through Authentication Using Kerberos) and Local KDC (Windows 11+) enable Kerberos authentication for devices that do not have line-of-sight to a domain controller — proxying Kerberos requests through Entra ID. Windows Credential Guard (virtualization-based security) protects Kerberos TGTs and keys in an isolated LSA process, hardening the on-premises credential store

Long-term — Cloud-native OIDC + passkeys/WHfB as the primary authentication mechanism; Kerberos used only for legacy application backward compatibility; RC4 fully eliminated (Windows Server 2025 disables RC4-HMAC by default); AES-only enforcement domain-wide. Cloud Kerberos Trust is emerging as a feature that allows Entra ID to validate Kerberos service tickets for hybrid applications, potentially eliminating the need for Entra Connect in some scenarios

Credential compromise and revocation: CAEP/RISC parallels. Modern session management protocols (CAEP for session-level events, RISC for account-level events — see §41) address a revocation problem that Kerberos has historically handled only crudely. When a user credential is compromised in an OAuth/OIDC environment, CAEP enables the IdP to trigger a session termination cascade — all relying parties are notified via SET (Shared Signals Event) and terminate their sessions simultaneously. RISC provides the account-level counterpart: when a password is changed or an account is disabled, all connected platforms receive account-credential-change-required or sessions-revoked events. Kerberos has no equivalent real-time revocation signal — a compromised TGT remains valid until its lifetime expires or the krbtgt key is rotated (which invalidates all TGTs domain-wide). The RISC identifier-recycled event (where an IdP reassigned user@example.com to a new person after account deletion) illustrates a risk that has no Kerberos analogue: in Active Directory, a recycled sAMAccountName could grant the new user access to resources previously authorized for the old user, with no notification mechanism to downstream service owners. As hybrid environments increasingly bridge Kerberos and OIDC (via Seamless SSO, IAKerb, Cloud Kerberos Trust), the revocation capabilities of CAEP/RISC become a critical complement to Kerberos's lifetime-based security model.
1. AD Connect (Sync) exports identity context from On-Prem Active Directory

AD Connect queries the on-premises Active Directory Domain Controllers on a configurable synchronization schedule (default 30 minutes per Microsoft Entra Connect Health). It extracts User objects, group memberships, and immutable identifiers — primarily the objectGUID and sourceAnchor attributes — which serve as the cryptographic binding key between the on-prem directory and the cloud tenant (Microsoft Entra ID, §Azure AD Connect Sync Architecture).
Artifact Produced: Exported User Object Set (objectGUID, sourceAnchor, UPN)
2. AD Connect (Sync) synchronizes delegated password hashes (PHS)

AD Connect extracts the NTLM password hash (MD4 digest) from each User object and applies a second SHA-256 salted hash locally before transmitting the result over a mutually-authenticated TLS channel to Entra ID. This double-hashing scheme ensures that the cloud tenant stores an irreversible derivative — Entra ID can validate incoming authentication attempts against the stored hash but cannot recover the original password (Microsoft Entra PHS, §Password Hash Sync Technical Implementation).
3. User authenticates locally via Kerberos TGT

The user logs into their domain-joined workstation using their Active Directory credentials. The workstation obtains a Kerberos Ticket-Granting Ticket (TGT) from the on-premises KDC (RFC 4120, §3.3). This TGT proves the user's identity to any service in the domain and is cached by the Local Security Authority Subsystem (LSASS) process for the duration of the ticket lifetime (typically 10 hours, renewable up to 7 days).
4. User accesses cloud resource — Seamless SSO or WIA bridges to Entra ID

When the user accesses an Entra ID-protected SaaS application, the browser is redirected to Entra ID's authentication endpoint. If Seamless SSO is configured, Entra ID redirects the browser to https://autologon.microsoftazuread-sso.com/<realm>, which maps to the AZUREADSSOACC computer account's SPN in the on-premises AD. The browser uses the cached Kerberos TGT to obtain a service ticket for this SPN (via TGS-REQ) and presents it in the Authorization: Negotiate header to Entra ID.
5. Entra ID validates Kerberos context and issues Primary Refresh Token (PRT)

Entra ID decrypts the presented Kerberos service ticket using the shared AZUREADSSOACC key (established during Entra Connect setup). From the ticket's PAC, Entra ID extracts the user's identity (User Principal Name, Object GUID) and maps it to the synchronized cloud identity. Entra ID then issues a Primary Refresh Token (PRT) — a long-lived token that enables silent token acquisition for the user across all Entra ID-joined and registered devices. The PRT is cached in the browser's token store and in Windows CloudAP.
Artifact Produced: Primary Refresh Token (PRT) — enables seamless access to all Entra ID-connected resources.
6. User accesses SaaS application with Entra ID token

For subsequent access to any Entra ID-protected SaaS application, the browser presents the PRT to Entra ID's token endpoint and receives an access token (JWT) for the target application — no additional user interaction required. The SaaS application validates the access token's signature against Entra ID's public keys (JWKS endpoint) and grants access based on the token's claims.

Kerberos enables users in one realm (domain) to access services in another realm through trust relationships between the domains' KDCs. A trust relationship is established by sharing an inter-realm key between the two KDCs — a symmetric cryptographic key that both KDCs know and use to encrypt cross-realm referral tickets.
✅ Automatic when a child domain is created
Between parent and child domains in the same AD forest (e.g., corp.example.com ↔ us.corp.example.com)
✅ Automatic when a new tree root is added to the forest
Between tree roots in the same forest (e.g., corp.example.com ↔ subsidiary.com)
Between separate AD forests — enables cross-forest resource access
Between domains in different forests without a forest trust — legacy; scoped to the two specific domains
Non-transitive or transitive
Between AD and non-Windows MIT Kerberos realms (e.g., Linux/Unix environments running MIT KDC)
Within a forest — optimizes authentication paths between domains that are separated by multiple parent-child trust hops
When a client in Realm A requests a service ticket for a service in Realm B, the KDCs use a referral chain:
Client → Realm A's KDC: TGS-REQ for service/host@REALM_B

Realm A's KDC → Client: The KDC recognizes the target realm and returns a referral TGT — a TGT encrypted with the inter-realm key shared between Realm A and Realm B, for the cross-realm service krbtgt/REALM_B@REALM_A:
TGTB=EKinter-realm(A↔B)(EncTicketPart)\text{TGT}_{B} = E_{K_{\text{inter-realm}}(A \leftrightarrow B)}(\text{EncTicketPart})TGTB​=EKinter-realm​(A↔B)​(EncTicketPart)
Client → Realm B's KDC: TGS-REQ with the referral TGT

Realm B's KDC → Client: Realm B's KDC decrypts the referral TGT using the inter-realm key, validates the client's identity, and issues a service ticket for the requested service encrypted with the target service's key

In multi-hop trust paths (e.g., Realm A → Realm B → Realm C), the referral chain traverses each intermediate realm — the client receives a referral TGT for each hop until it reaches the realm containing the target service. Shortcut trusts optimize this by providing a direct inter-realm key between non-adjacent realms, reducing the number of referral hops.

Inter-realm key compromise: Inter-realm trust keys are symmetric — both KDCs possess the key. When a trust is established between Domain A and Domain B, a trust account is created in each domain (e.g., B$@A and A$@B), and the inter-realm key is derived from this trust account's password. Trust passwords are rotated every 30 days by default on Server 2012+. Compromise of an inter-realm key allows forging cross-realm referral TGTs (an inter-realm Golden Ticket). The attacker can construct referral TGTs claiming any user identity from the trusted realm. Trust key rotation and monitoring are critical.

SID filtering: When a cross-realm ticket is received, the target domain applies SID filtering to the PAC. SID filtering strips SIDs from the PAC that do not belong to the trusted domain's SID range — preventing SID history injection attacks. Without SID filtering, an attacker who compromises a trusted domain can forge a PAC containing Enterprise Admin SIDs from the trusting domain, achieving full compromise of the trusting forest.
SID filtering is enabled by default on all external trusts and forest trusts

SID filtering is disabled by default on intra-forest trusts (parent-child, tree-root) — because trust between domains within the same forest is inherently transitive and total. A compromise of any domain within a forest is effectively a compromise of the entire forest

Selective authentication — an alternative to full trust: forest trusts can be configured with selective authentication, where users from the trusted forest must be explicitly granted the "Allowed to Authenticate" permission on resources in the trusting forest. This provides fine-grained access control at the resource level rather than domain-wide access
Filters foreign SIDs from PAC — removes Enterprise SIDs not originating from the trusted forest and built-in SIDs; preserves domain-local SIDs from the originating forest
Enabled by default on external/forest trusts
Requires explicit "Allowed to Authenticate" ACE on each resource before trusted users can access it
Enabled during trust creation
All SIDs pass through — trusting domain fully accepts the trusted domain's SID claims
Not recommended; possible on non-Windows trusts
Use forest trusts over external trusts — Forest trusts provide transitive trust relationships, are easier to manage, and include SID filtering by default.

Enable SID filtering on all trusts — Never disable SID filtering unless absolutely required for legacy application compatibility. Disabling SID filtering exposes the trusting domain to SID history attacks.
Use selective authentication for sensitive trusts — Selective authentication provides defence-in-depth by requiring explicit access grants on each resource.
Rotate trust passwords regularly — Ensure trust account passwords are rotated on the same schedule as other high-privilege account passwords.
Monitor cross-realm TGT requests — Track Event ID 4769 for cross-realm service ticket requests to detect anomalous access patterns.
Minimise trust relationships — Each trust relationship increases the attack surface. Review existing trusts periodically and remove any that are no longer needed.

Session tokens — cookies, access tokens, refresh tokens — are traditionally bearer credentials: anyone who possesses the token can use it, regardless of how they obtained it. A session cookie exfiltrated by info-stealer malware (§22.6), an access token captured by an AiTM phishing proxy (§22.5), or a refresh token leaked through compromised logs — each is immediately usable by the attacker, indistinguishable from legitimate use. The token is the identity proof; there is no second factor binding it to the entity that originally received it.

Device-bound sessions address this by cryptographically binding session tokens to a specific device's private key. The binding transforms bearer tokens into proof-of-possession (PoP) tokens: using the token requires proving possession of the corresponding private key on every request. A stolen token without the private key is rejected by the server — the token is inert outside the device that holds the key. This chapter covers the four major mechanisms for achieving device binding — from the abandoned TLS-layer Token Binding protocol through its application-layer successors (DPoP for OAuth tokens, DBSC for browser session cookies) — and the unified cnf (confirmation) claim that ties them together at the JWT level.
The binding equation is straightforward:
valid_session=valid_token(t)∧valid_proof(t,kdevice)\text{valid\_session} = \text{valid\_token}(t) \wedge \text{valid\_proof}(t, k_{\text{device}})valid_session=valid_token(t)∧valid_proof(t,kdevice​)

Where valid_token(t)\text\\{valid\_token\\}(t)valid_token(t) validates the token's signature, expiry, issuer, and audience claims, and \text\\{valid\_proof\\}(t, k_\\{\text\\{device\\}\\}) verifies a cryptographic signature over the request using the device-bound private key k_\\{\text\\{device\\}\\}.
The threat model for bearer token theft is not theoretical — it is the primary attack vector in modern credential-based attacks:
Info-stealer malware (RedLine, Raccoon, Lumma)
Very High — millions of infections annually
Complete session hijack, lateral movement
Low — mimics legitimate traffic
High — OWASP A03 consistently
Medium — requires WAF/IDS rules
Network interception (MITM on open WiFi)
Session hijack on unencrypted channels
High — traffic analysis anomalies
Token in URL leakage (Referer header, logs)
Medium — common misconfiguration
Token exposure to third parties
Low — modern frameworks prevent

Session cookies remain the dominant session management mechanism for browser-based applications (§37.1). They are also the primary target for credential theft at scale. Info-stealer malware — Raccoon, Vidar, RedLine, Lumma — specifically targets browser cookie databases, extracting session cookies that grant access to authenticated sessions without requiring any further authentication. The threat model is straightforward:
The user authenticates to an application (potentially completing MFA, passing risk-based evaluation, satisfying device compliance checks)
The server issues a session cookie — an opaque bearer credential stored in the browser's cookie jar
Malware on the user's device reads the cookie database file (Chrome's Cookies SQLite database, Firefox's cookies.sqlite, etc.)
The attacker imports the stolen cookie into their own browser, on a different device, in a different country
The application accepts the cookie — the attacker has a fully authenticated session with all of the user's privileges

Every authentication ceremony the user completed — password, FIDO2, push notification, conditional access evaluation — is bypassed entirely. The session cookie is the post-authentication bearer credential, and once stolen, the authentication investment is worthless. Google estimates that hundreds of millions of session cookies are stolen annually through info-stealer malware campaigns, making cookie theft among the highest-volume credential compromise vectors on the web.
The info-stealer extraction pipeline follows a well-defined sequence:
Reconnaissance — enumerate installed browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Brave, Opera, Vivaldi)
Database extraction — copy Login Data, Cookies, Web Data SQLite databases
Key recovery — extract AES decryption keys from Local State (Windows DPAPI-protected)
Credential decryption — decrypt stored passwords and session cookies using recovered keys
Cryptocurrency wallet scanning — check for MetaMask, Phantom, Exodus wallet files
Exfiltration — POST encrypted payload to C2 server
Session resale — upload "fresh logs" (stolen credentials) to underground marketplaces

The time from infection to credential exfiltration is typically under 60 seconds. The stolen session cookies are sold in bulk on marketplaces such as Russian Market and Genesis, where buyers can filter by service (banks, email, social media, enterprise SaaS) and geolocation. For enterprise environments, stolen session tokens to cloud services (AWS, Azure, GCP), SaaS platforms (Salesforce, ServiceNow), and internal tools (Jira, Confluence) enable lateral movement and data exfiltration that bypasses multi-factor authentication entirely — because the session was already authenticated with valid MFA before the cookie was issued.

Device Bound Session Credentials (DBSC) is a web platform API and protocol — developed by Google and now standardised through the W3C Web Application Security Working Group — that binds session cookies to a cryptographic key pair stored in the browser's platform authenticator. The binding ensures that a stolen cookie is useless without the device's private key:

During session establishment, the browser generates an ECDSA P-256 key pair using hardware-backed key storage (TPM 2.0 on Windows and Linux, Secure Enclave on macOS, or software-backed non-extractable storage as a fallback)
The server's session record is linked to the public key — the server stores the public key (or its thumbprint) alongside the session
On subsequent requests, the browser proves possession of the private key by signing a challenge or presenting a proof-of-possession token alongside the cookie
The server validates both the session cookie AND the proof-of-possession — both must be valid for the request to proceed

The critical security property: even if malware exfiltrates every cookie from the browser's database, the private key remains locked in hardware-backed storage. The cookies are inert on any device other than the one that created the session.
DBSC relies on the platform's hardware-backed key storage for private key protection. The choice of TPM or TEE has implications for both security and portability:
Isolated CPU execution environment
Very High — keys never leave TPM
High — keys accessible only within enclave
High — TPM operates independently
Medium — TEE shares CPU with normal world
Desktops, laptops, some servers
Mobile devices (iOS Secure Enclave, Android TEE)
Requires TPM 2.0 (Windows, Linux)
Platform-specific implementations
vTPM (virtual TPM) available
Platform-specific attestation

On Windows, DBSC uses the Platform Crypto Provider to generate keys in the TPM. On macOS, the Secure Enclave serves an analogous role (though Apple Silicon Macs use a TEE rather than a discrete TPM). On Linux, DBSC uses the tpm2-tss library to interface with the TPM.
Browser calls platform crypto API to generate ECDSA P-256 key pair
Platform generates the key pair inside the TPM/TEE — private key never exposed
Public key exported in JWK format
Key identifier (public key thumbprint) stored for subsequent binding
Private key tagged with application-bound usage restriction (browser-only)
DBSC operates through a registration and refresh cycle that integrates with the server's existing session management:
When the server creates a new authenticated session, it responds with a Sec-Session-Registration header that instructs the browser to bind the session to a device key:

The browser generates an ECDSA P-256 key pair with the private key stored in non-extractable hardware-backed storage (TPM where available). The browser then sends the public key to the server's registration endpoint:
2. Short-Lived Cookie Refresh

DBSC replaces long-lived session cookies with short-lived cookies (typically 10 minutes). When a short-lived cookie expires, the browser intercepts the resulting 401 response and automatically calls the server's refresh endpoint, including a proof-of-possession signed with the device-bound private key:

The server validates the proof-of-possession signature against the stored public key and issues a fresh short-lived cookie. This refresh cycle continues for the session's lifetime — each refresh proves the device still holds the private key.
The cryptographic model underlying DBSC can be expressed as:

cookie_valid(r)=cookie_signature_valid(c)∧device_proof_valid(r,skTPM)\text{cookie\_valid}(r) = \text{cookie\_signature\_valid}(c) \wedge \text{device\_proof\_valid}(r, \text{sk}_{\text{TPM}})cookie_valid(r)=cookie_signature_valid(c)∧device_proof_valid(r,skTPM​)

Where rrr is the HTTP request, ccc is the session cookie, and \text\\{sk\\}_\\{\text\\{TPM\\}\\} is the private key stored in the TPM. Both conditions must hold for the request to proceed — a valid cookie alone is insufficient without the device-bound proof.
DBSC provides theft resistance against info-stealer malware through four defence-in-depth layers:

Private key non-extractability — the private key is generated and stored inside the TPM/TEE. Even with kernel-level access (which info-stealers achieve), the key material cannot be extracted. The TPM returns "access denied" for any raw key export attempt.

Private key non-extractability — the private key is generated and stored inside the TPM/TEE. Even with kernel-level access (which info-stealers achieve), the key material cannot be extracted. The TPM returns "access denied" for any raw key export attempt.

Signing operation binding — the TPM/TEE signs only operations initiated through the platform's crypto API. An info-stealer cannot perform a signing operation without the browser initiating it, because the TPM requires a specific authorisation value (authValue) that only the browser holds in memory.

Signing operation binding — the TPM/TEE signs only operations initiated through the platform's crypto API. An info-stealer cannot perform a signing operation without the browser initiating it, because the TPM requires a specific authorisation value (authValue) that only the browser holds in memory.

Short-lived session tokens — DBSC is designed to work with short-lived cookies (e.g., 5–15 minute expiry). Even if an attacker could somehow replay a signed request, the window of exploitation is narrow.

Short-lived session tokens — DBSC is designed to work with short-lived cookies (e.g., 5–15 minute expiry). Even if an attacker could somehow replay a signed request, the window of exploitation is narrow.

Re-registration on new device — when a user logs in on a new device, a new key pair is generated and registered. The old key remains valid only on the original device, so stolen cookies from device A cannot be used on device B.

Re-registration on new device — when a user logs in on a new device, a new key pair is generated and registered. The old key remains valid only on the original device, so stolen cookies from device A cannot be used on device B.

If an attacker steals the short-lived cookie during its brief validity window, they have at most 10 minutes of access before the cookie expires. When the cookie expires, the attacker cannot refresh it — they do not possess the device-bound private key needed to generate a valid Sec-Session-Response. The session dies on the attacker's device while the legitimate user's session continues uninterrupted.
The residual risk after DBSC deployment:

P(compromisepost-DBSC)≈P(TPM/TEE vulnerability)+P(browser-level key extraction)+P(server-side session fixation)P(\text{compromise}_{\text{post-DBSC}}) \approx P(\text{TPM/TEE vulnerability}) + P(\text{browser-level key extraction}) + P(\text{server-side session fixation})P(compromisepost-DBSC​)≈P(TPM/TEE vulnerability)+P(browser-level key extraction)+P(server-side session fixation)
Each term is substantially lower than the pre-DBSC cookie theft probability.
1. Browser sends initial login request to Relying Party
The Browser sends a standard authentication request to the Relying Party (e.g., POST /login with credentials). The RP validates the credentials and determines the session parameters (W3C DBSC, §3.1).
2. Relying Party responds with session registration challenge

The RP checks for the Sec-DBSC HTTP request header to determine whether the browser supports the DBSC protocol. If present, the RP responds with a Set-Cookie header for the session and a Sec-Session-Registration header instructing the browser to bind the session to a device key (W3C DBSC, §3.2). The registration header specifies the endpoint path and required authorization scope.
3. Browser queries Hardware TPM for new key generation

The browser calls the WebAuthn-style navigator.credentials.create() method (or the DBSC-specific equivalent) to request the hardware TPM to generate a new asymmetric keypair dedicated to this session (W3C DBSC, §4.2). This call passes through the OS TPM driver stack to the physical TPM chip.
4. Hardware TPM generates ECDSA P-256 keypair bound to device

Inside the TPM's secure boundary, a new ECDSA P-256 keypair is generated (ISO 11889, Part 2, §14.3). The private key is sealed within the TPM's non-volatile storage and cannot be exported. The TPM returns only the public key with attestation data certifying the key was generated by a genuine TPM.
5. Browser submits registration to Relying Party

The browser packages the TPM-generated public key with a Proof of Possession JWT signed with the private key, demonstrating that the client controls the corresponding private key (W3C DBSC, §4.3; RFC 9449, §4.2). The RP verifies the PoP signature and stores the public key in its session database.
📎 Artifact: DBSC Registration Record
6. Relying Party issues nonce challenge for cookie refresh

When the short-lived session cookie expires, the browser attempts a protected request. The RP responds with a 401 Unauthorized containing a cryptographically random nonce challenge (W3C DBSC, §6.2). This nonce is single-use and time-bounded (30–60 seconds), preventing replay attacks.
7. Hardware TPM signs nonce with device-bound private key

The browser delegates nonce signing to the TPM via the DBSC signing API (W3C DBSC, §4.4). The TPM retrieves the sealed private key and signs the nonce using ECDSA P-256 (ISO 11889, Part 3, §18.4). The private key material never exits the chip — only the signature is returned.
8. Browser submits signed proof for cookie refresh

The browser constructs a DPoP-style proof JWT containing the nonce signature and sends it alongside the session token to the RP's refresh endpoint (W3C DBSC, §6.3). The RP validates the signature against the stored public key and issues a fresh short-lived cookie.
9. Info-stealer extracts cookie but cannot access private key

Info-stealer malware (Raccoon, Vidar, RedLine) extracts the short-lived session cookie from the browser's SQLite database. However, the private key remains sealed inside the TPM — no software process can extract it. The stolen cookie is inert without the device-bound proof.
10. Relying Party rejects stolen cookie replay

The attacker replays the stolen cookie in a request to the RP. The RP detects the cookie has expired (or is from a different device) and issues a nonce challenge. The attacker cannot produce a valid signature — they lack the TPM-bound private key. The RP returns 401 Unauthorized, confirming DBSC successfully neutralized the theft (W3C DBSC, §6.4).
📎 Artifact: HTTP 401 Unauthorized — session protection confirmed.
DBSC has progressed from a Google-internal proposal to a shipping web platform feature:
Google publishes initial DBSC explainer document
Developers can test DBSC in Chrome Canary with origin trial token
Google presents DBSC at W3C Technical Plenary, shares cookie theft data
Chrome 124 — First public Origin Trial
DBSC available for opt-in testing in stable Chrome
Server-side library published
Google releases session-bind reference implementation (Go, Python)
Chrome 129 — Extended Origin Trial
Broader testing with improved error reporting
Major protocol simplification: registration header format changed
Mozilla begins experimental implementation in Nightly
Chrome 134 — Full rollout begins
DBSC enabled by default for Google properties
Apple begins WICG participation, WebKit prototype
Chrome 140 — Third-party cookie context
DBSC adapts for Privacy Sandbox ecosystem
Chrome 145 — General Availability
DBSC ships as a stable web platform feature

As of March 2026, DBSC is available in Chrome 145+ (enabled by default for compatible origins) and in experimental builds of Firefox. Safari support remains under development in WebKit. Server-side adoption is concentrated among large-scale web properties (Google, Microsoft, Cloudflare) with open-source implementations emerging.
DBSC is designed to avoid creating new tracking vectors:
Unique key per session — each session generates a fresh key pair. Keys are not reused across sessions or sites
No cross-site correlation — the key pair is scoped to the registering origin. Different origins cannot observe or correlate each other's device keys
User-controlled deletion — clearing site data in Chrome deletes the associated DBSC keys, terminating the device binding
No fingerprinting surface — the presence or absence of DBSC support, or the type of hardware backing (TPM vs. software), is not exposed to the server in a way that enables device fingerprinting

Graceful degradation — if the user's device lacks a TPM/TEE (e.g., certain Linux configurations, older hardware), DBSC degrades silently to standard cookie-based sessions. The server detects the absence of the Sec-Session-Response header and issues unbound cookies
The privacy properties can be formalised as constraints on the protocol:

∀ o1,o2∈Origins:o1≠o2 ⟹ key(o1)≠key(o2) orall\, o_1, o_2 \in 	ext{Origins}: o_1 
eq o_2 \implies 	ext{key}(o_1) 
eq 	ext{key}(o_2)∀o1​,o2​∈Origins:o1​=o2​⟹key(o1​)=key(o2​) ∀ s∈Sessions:clear_data(s) ⟹ key(s) is destroyed orall\, s \in 	ext{Sessions}: 	ext{clear\_data}(s) \implies 	ext{key}(s) 	ext{ is destroyed}∀s∈Sessions:clear_data(s)⟹key(s) is destroyed ∀ r∈Requests:is_cross_site(r) ⟹ ¬has_dbsc_proof(r) orall\, r \in 	ext{Requests}: 	ext{is\_cross\_site}(r) \implies 
eg 	ext{has\_dbsc\_proof}(r)∀r∈Requests:is_cross_site(r)⟹¬has_dbsc_proof(r)

The first constraint enforces per-origin key isolation (preventing cross-origin tracking via key fingerprinting). The second ensures that clearing browsing data destroys the associated keys. The third guarantees that DBSC proofs are never attached to cross-site requests.
Despite its promise, DBSC has several limitations that constrain its applicability:

Hardware dependency — DBSC requires a TPM 2.0 or equivalent TEE. Desktop Linux without TPM, certain Chromebooks, and older hardware lack support. Approximately 5–10% of active Chrome installations cannot use DBSC as of 2026.

Hardware dependency — DBSC requires a TPM 2.0 or equivalent TEE. Desktop Linux without TPM, certain Chromebooks, and older hardware lack support. Approximately 5–10% of active Chrome installations cannot use DBSC as of 2026.

No mobile browser support yet — while mobile devices have TEEs (ARM TrustZone, Apple Secure Enclave), DBSC has not been implemented in mobile browsers. Mobile cookie theft is a significant but unaddressed threat.

No mobile browser support yet — while mobile devices have TEEs (ARM TrustZone, Apple Secure Enclave), DBSC has not been implemented in mobile browsers. Mobile cookie theft is a significant but unaddressed threat.

Shared device limitations — on shared or family devices, DBSC binding is per-browser-profile. If a malware infection occurs on a shared account, the binding protects against remote replay but does not address the local user who shares the same profile.

Shared device limitations — on shared or family devices, DBSC binding is per-browser-profile. If a malware infection occurs on a shared account, the binding protects against remote replay but does not address the local user who shares the same profile.

CDN and proxy compatibility — CDNs that terminate TLS and re-establish connections to the origin must pass DBSC headers through transparently. Unlike transport-layer binding, this is straightforward (application headers are protocol-agnostic), but requires explicit CDN configuration.

CDN and proxy compatibility — CDNs that terminate TLS and re-establish connections to the origin must pass DBSC headers through transparently. Unlike transport-layer binding, this is straightforward (application headers are protocol-agnostic), but requires explicit CDN configuration.

Backup and recovery — TPM-bound keys cannot be backed up. If a user's hardware fails, they must re-register and create a new session. This is acceptable for short-lived sessions but problematic for long-lived persistent logins.

Backup and recovery — TPM-bound keys cannot be backed up. If a user's hardware fails, they must re-register and create a new session. This is acceptable for short-lived sessions but problematic for long-lived persistent logins.

Token Binding (RFC 8471, 8472, 8473 — October 2018) was the first standardised attempt to cryptographically bind security tokens to client devices. The protocol operated at the TLS layer, integrating token binding negotiation directly into the TLS handshake:
During the TLS handshake, the client and server negotiate Token Binding support via a TLS extension (token_binding — RFC 8472)
The client generates a key pair bound to the server's identity (the server's TLS certificate). The key pair is persistent — reused across connections to the same server
The client includes a token_binding extension in the (Encrypted) ClientKeyExchange or equivalent TLS 1.3 handshake message, containing the public key and a signature over the TLS handshake transcript
The server verifies the signature against the TLS handshake transcript, confirming that the client controls the private key associated with the connection
After the TLS handshake completes, the client sends a Token Binding message containing its public key and a proof-of-possession (a signature over the TLS connection's Exported Keying Material — EKM)
On every subsequent HTTP request, the client includes a Sec-Token-Binding header containing a signature over the request and the TLS channel parameters
The server binds tokens (cookies, OAuth access tokens) to the client's Token Binding public key
On subsequent connections, the client presents the same Token Binding key and proves possession — the server validates that the token was issued to this specific key holder

The critical integration point is step 3: the token binding key is bound to the TLS handshake transcript. This means the key cannot be used on a different TLS connection, even if the key material is somehow extracted, because the TLS handshake transcript (which includes client and server random values, cipher suite negotiation, and certificates) is unique per connection.
Token Binding was architecturally sound — it provided strong, transparent token binding at the transport layer. However, a combination of ecosystem factors prevented adoption:

TLS 1.3 incompatibility — Token Binding was designed around TLS 1.2's key schedule and extension negotiation model. TLS 1.3's redesigned handshake — with 0-RTT (early data), Pre-Shared Key (PSK) resumption modes, and a fundamentally different key derivation structure — broke assumptions that Token Binding relied on. RFC 8472 explicitly stated that Token Binding negotiation for TLS 1.3 was "beyond the scope of this document." An IETF Internet-Draft (draft-ietf-tokbind-tls13) attempted to bridge this gap but never progressed beyond draft status. By 2019, TLS 1.3 deployment was accelerating rapidly, and Token Binding had no path to operate on the dominant TLS version.

CDN and load balancer incompatibility — Token Binding required the TLS-terminating endpoint to propagate binding information to backend application servers. In modern CDN-heavy architectures (Cloudflare, Akamai, Fastly, AWS CloudFront), TLS termination occurs at the edge — far from the application server. CDN vendors largely did not implement Token Binding propagation, creating a deployment wall for any application behind a CDN.

Minimal browser support — Only Microsoft Edge (legacy EdgeHTML engine) shipped a full Token Binding implementation. Chrome ran an experimental implementation (removed in Chrome 70). Firefox and Safari never implemented the protocol. Without cross-browser support, server-side adoption had no viable return on investment.

RFC status — RFC 8471–8473 were published as Proposed Standards but are effectively abandoned. The IETF Token Binding working group closed. No major browser, server framework, or identity provider actively supports the protocol.

Privacy concerns — because Token Binding used a single key per server (or per origin), the public key could theoretically serve as a tracking identifier. A server that observed the same token binding key across multiple sessions (even with different IP addresses and cookies) could infer that the sessions originated from the same device. This contradicted the privacy expectations of browsers and privacy advocates.

Complexity and lack of clear use case — for OAuth 2.0 tokens, application-layer binding (DPoP) was simpler to implement and did not require TLS-layer changes. For web session cookies, the cookie theft threat was not yet perceived as acute enough (this changed after 2022 with the explosion of info-stealer malware). Token Binding occupied an awkward middle ground: too complex for web cookies, not targeted enough for API tokens.

Token Binding's conceptual contribution endures: it established the architectural pattern of binding tokens to device-held cryptographic keys. Every subsequent mechanism — DPoP (§39.3), mTLS certificate-bound tokens (§39.4), and DBSC (§39.1) — implements the same fundamental idea using different (and more practical) technical approaches. The key lesson from Token Binding's failure is that transport-layer binding is too tightly coupled to TLS implementation details; application-layer binding (DPoP) and browser-native binding (DBSC) proved more deployable because they operate independently of the TLS version and the CDN/load balancer topology.
Tight TLS integration caused incompatibilities
DBSC and DPoP operate at the application layer, independent of TLS version
Incompatible with CDN/proxy architectures
Application-layer headers pass through intermediaries transparently
Single key per server enabled tracking
DBSC uses per-origin ephemeral keys; DPoP uses application-managed keys
Required both client and server changes simultaneously
DBSC degrades gracefully; DPoP is opt-in per request
General-purpose binding lacked a compelling narrative
DBSC explicitly targets cookie theft; DPoP targets token misuse

The most significant lesson is that transport-layer binding is the wrong abstraction for PoP. The internet's architecture assumes that intermediaries (CDNs, proxies, load balancers) can terminate and re-establish TLS connections without modifying application semantics. Any mechanism that violates this assumption faces an uphill deployment battle.

The HTTP Message Signatures specification (RFC 9421), published in 2024, defines a cryptographic scheme for applying digital signatures over selected components of HTTP requests and responses. It supersedes the deprecated draft-cavage-http-signatures that saw limited adoption in early API platforms. Unlike OAuth-centric mechanisms such as DPoP (§39.3), HTTP Message Signing is a general-purpose integrity and sender-authentication primitive — it binds the identity of the signer to the content of the message itself (method, authority, path, headers, body digest) without presupposing any particular authorization framework. This makes it a foundational building block: DPoP (§39.3), mTLS certificate-bound tokens (§39.4), and the cnf claim binding pattern (§39.5) all address token constraint, whereas RFC 9421 addresses message constraint — a complementary but distinct concern.
Wire Format: RFC 9421 introduces two HTTP header fields using RFC 8941 Structured Fields syntax for deterministic, unambiguous serialization:
Signature-Input — a Dictionary Structured Field whose member keys are signature labels (e.g., sig1) and whose values are Inner Lists of covered component identifiers followed by signature parameters.
Signature — a Dictionary Structured Field whose member keys match the labels in Signature-Input and whose values are the base64url-encoded signature bytes.
A concrete signed POST request illustrates both headers in context:

The signature base — the canonical string actually fed into the signing algorithm — is constructed by concatenating each covered component's identifier and value, terminated by a special @signature-params line that includes all metadata parameters. For the example above:
RFC 9421 defines derived pseudo-components prefixed with @ that are not literal header fields but are extracted from the HTTP message context:
Host header value or HTTP/2 :authority pseudo-header
URI path component (without query string)
Query string including leading ?
Full request target (scheme + authority + path + query)

Supported algorithms include rsa-pss-sha512, ecdsa-p256-sha256, ecdsa-p384-sha384, ed25519, and hmac-sha256. Ed25519 is the recommended default for new deployments due to its compact signatures and deterministic output; RSA-PSS is retained for interoperability with existing PKI infrastructure.

Security Properties: Replay protection. RFC 9421 provides three replay mitigation mechanisms. The created and expires parameters define a temporal validity window — verifiers reject signatures whose timestamps fall outside an acceptable skew (typically ±30 seconds). The nonce parameter carries a server-assigned or client-generated unique value; the verifier maintains a short-lived store of recently seen nonces and rejects duplicates. Finally, covering @query in the signature base ensures that an attacker cannot redirect a replayed request to a different endpoint or modify query parameters without invalidating the signature. Deployments should combine all three: a bounded time window, a unique nonce, and full component coverage.

Key rotation. The keyid parameter references the signing key material by an opaque identifier resolved through a pre-configured mapping, a JWKS endpoint, or an X.509 certificate chain. Rotation proceeds by staging a new key alongside the old one in the key store, switching new signatures to the new key, and retiring the old key after a grace period during which the verifier still accepts both. This pattern avoids the key-distribution disruption that plagues shared-secret rotations in HMAC-based schemes (§24).

Relationship to TLS. HTTP Message Signatures provide integrity and authentication, not confidentiality — TLS remains mandatory for encrypting data in transit (RFC 9421, §11.1.2). The critical architectural difference is that an RFC 9421 signature survives TLS termination at proxies and CDNs. A signed request passing through a TLS-terminating CDN retains its cryptographic binding across the CDN-to-origin hop, whereas mTLS (§39.4) must be re-established at each hop or lose its binding entirely. This end-to-end property makes HTTP Message Signing uniquely suited to multi-hop API architectures.

Bearer by default. An RFC 9421 signature alone authenticates the message, not the session. Without an accompanying access token, the signature does not convey authorization — it proves that the signer held a specific private key, but the server must still determine whether that key's holder is authorised to perform the requested action. In OAuth ecosystems, HTTP Message Signing is typically layered with DPoP (§39.3) or mTLS-bound tokens (§39.4) to achieve both message integrity and token-based authorization.

RFC 9421 vs. DPoP (RFC 9449): Technical Comparison: OAuth 2.0 Demonstrating Proof-of-Possession (DPoP) (RFC 9449) is an OAuth-specific profile that applies the concept of HTTP message binding in a constrained, opinionated way. Understanding the distinction is essential when choosing a sender-constraint mechanism:
HTTP Message Signing (RFC 9421)
General-purpose; any HTTP message
OAuth 2.0 token binding only
Flexible selection of any combination of derived components and header fields
Fixed: HTTP method, URI, timestamp, and access token hash
Native HTTP headers (Signature-Input, Signature)
Multiple labels per message (e.g., client + gateway)
keyid references externally managed keys
Embedded JWK in JWT payload; confirmed via cnf.jkt in access token
Not inherent; must be combined with token mechanism
Built in via ath claim (base64url SHA-256 of token)
Asymmetric + symmetric (HMAC)

The practical decision rule: use DPoP when you need OAuth token binding (the most common case in web and mobile applications), and use HTTP Message Signing directly when you need per-request integrity without OAuth infrastructure — for example, in machine-to-machine APIs (§24), financial services transaction signing, or service-mesh authentication where deploying a full OAuth authorization server is disproportionate to the threat model. The two mechanisms can coexist on a single request: DPoP binds the access token while an RFC 9421 signature covers the full request body and headers.

Adoption and Implementation: DPoP has become the primary vehicle for sender-constrained tokens in OAuth ecosystems and is implemented across major identity providers (Auth0, Keycloak, Microsoft Entra ID). Direct HTTP Message Signing adoption is still in an early phase but is accelerating in regulated industries:

Financial services. The UK Open Banking specification mandates HTTP signature authentication for PSD2-compliant APIs between third-party providers and banks. The European Payments Council is exploring HTTP signatures for cross-border transaction verification.

API gateways. Reverse proxies and API gateways can verify RFC 9421 signatures before forwarding requests to internal services, enabling zero-trust internal architectures where services validate message integrity rather than trusting the network perimeter.

No major cloud first-class support. AWS uses its own Signature Version 4 (SigV4), Azure relies on Managed Identity and mTLS, and Google Cloud has no native HTTP Message Signing API. Adoption is therefore concentrated in self-managed infrastructure and regulated industries.

Open source libraries exist in several languages (http-message-signatures in JavaScript, http-signature-java in Java, community crates in Rust) but none has achieved the maturity or adoption of mainstream OAuth libraries. Implementers should use the IETF test vectors and validate canonicalization carefully — naive string manipulation of HTTP fields is the most common source of verification failures.

Use Cases in Authentication and Session Management: API authentication without OAuth. In microservices environments where a full OAuth authorization server is overkill (§24), HTTP Message Signing provides lightweight per-request authentication. Each service holds an asymmetric key pair; incoming requests carry a signature over the method, path, and body digest; and the receiving service validates the signature against a pre-distributed public key. This eliminates shared-secret management while providing stronger non-repudiation than API keys or HMAC.

Enhancing bearer tokens with per-request integrity. A standard OAuth bearer token proves that the holder was authorised at token-issuance time but does not bind subsequent API calls to the token. Layering an RFC 9421 signature on top of a bearer token — signing the authorization header field alongside method and path — ensures that each individual request is cryptographically authenticated, not just the token. This closes the gap between bearer tokens (which trust the transport) and proof-of-possession tokens.

Service-to-service authentication through intermediaries. RFC 9421 supports multiple signatures per message, enabling chain-of-custody verification. A client signs the original request; an API gateway verifies the client's signature and appends its own; and the backend service verifies both signatures independently. This multi-hop delegation pattern is impractical with mTLS (§39.4) because TLS session binding does not survive proxy hops, but it maps naturally to HTTP Message Signing's architecture.

Transactional API request binding. In financial and regulatory APIs, individual transactions must be non-repudiable. Signing the full request — method, path, query parameters, body digest, and content-type — creates a cryptographic audit trail. Unlike DPoP, which binds only the method and URI to the token, HTTP Message Signing can cover arbitrary header fields and the request body, providing the level of transactional integrity required by PSD2 and similar regulatory frameworks.

DPoP (Demonstrating Proof-of-Possession at the Application Layer) — RFC 9449 (September 2023) — is an OAuth 2.0 extension that binds access tokens and refresh tokens to a client-generated key pair. DPoP operates entirely at the application layer (HTTP headers) — independent of TLS version, CDN configuration, load balancer behaviour, or any transport-layer mechanism. This transport independence is DPoP's defining architectural advantage over Token Binding and mTLS.

The core protocol is straightforward: the client generates an asymmetric key pair, includes a signed proof (a DPoP proof JWT) with every token request and API call, and the server validates that the proof was signed by the key bound to the token. A stolen access token without the private key cannot generate valid proofs — the token is sender-constrained.
1. Client Application generates ECDSA P-256 key pair

The Client Application actively generates an asymmetric cryptographic key pair (typically ECDSA P-256) within an isolated runtime environment (e.g., Web Crypto API for SPAs, Secure Enclave for iOS). The private key is strictly retained in local storage and never leaves the device. This key pair forms the cryptographic foundation for the DPoP sender-constraint bindings (RFC 9449, §4.1).
Artifact Produced: dpop_key_pair (private/public key binding)
2. User initiates login sequence
The User interacts natively with the Client Application to directly begin the authentication sequence, dynamically triggering the standard user-facing authorization flow.
3. Client Application sends Authorization Request to Authorization Server

The Client Application securely delegates authentication by directing the user's browser seamlessly to the Authorization Server, initiating a standard OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code flow with PKCE (response_type=code). At this stage, DPoP semantics strictly do not impact the authorization boundary.
4. User authenticates to Authorization Server

The User directly authenticates against the Authorization Server securely using their primary credentials and mandatory Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA), actively establishing their identity session.
5. Authorization Server returns Authorization Code to Client Application
The Authorization Server dynamically issues a highly ephemeral, single-use Authorization Code, transparently returning it cleanly to the Client Application explicitly via the registered redirect URI.
6. Client Application constructs DPoP Proof for Token endpoint

The Client Application programmatically generates a DPoP Proof JWT specifically targeted for the token endpoint. This JSON payload heavily incorporates the HTTP method (htm=POST), the exact target URI (htu=/token), a globally unique JWT ID (jti), and an issuance timestamp (iat). Since no access token exists yet, the ath (access token hash) claim is explicitly omitted (RFC 9449, §4.2).
7. Client Application signs token DPoP Proof

The Client Application mathematically signs the prepared DPoP Proof JWT securely using its tightly held local private key. The corresponding public key is embedded natively in the JWT's jwk header explicitly to allow server-side algorithmic validation.
8. Client Application sends Token Request with DPoP Proof to Authorization Server

The Client Application actively transmits the standard token exchange POST request exactly to the Authorization Server, structurally embedding the signed DPoP Proof JWT decisively within the newly introduced DPoP HTTP header alongside the authorization code and PKCE verifier.
9. Authorization Server validates Token DPoP Proof

The Authorization Server definitively parses the DPoP header and rigorously validates the proof JWT. This mandates mathematically verifying the cryptographic signature against the embedded public jwk, asserting the htm strictly equals POST, confirming the htu exactly matches the token endpoint URI, and checking iat freshness and jti uniqueness to fundamentally prevent credential replay attacks.

Rejection Scenario: If the cryptographic signature formulation fails or the htu/htm fields do not perfectly match the exact ingress HTTP request parameters, the Authorization Server aggressively rejects the entire transaction, issuing a standard 401 Unauthorized with an invalid_dpop_proof error and dynamically generating a DPOP_PROOF_VALIDATION_FAILED SIEM event indicating potential protocol tampering.
10. Authorization Server computes JWK thumbprint

Upon totally successful DPoP proof validation, the Authorization Server logically extracts the public key exclusively from the proof's jwk header and mathematically computes its robust SHA-256 JSON Web Key (JWK) Thumbprint (RFC 7638). This cryptographic hash flawlessly serves as the definitive sender-constraint hardware identifier.
11. Authorization Server issues Access Token with thumbprint binding

The Authorization Server dynamically constructs the Access Token cryptographic payload, explicitly embedding the securely computed JWK Thumbprint directly under the cnf.jkt (Confirmation: JWK Thumbprint) claim. This fundamentally binds the resulting access token mathematically to the client's localized private key structure.
12. Authorization Server returns bounded Token Response

The Authorization Server securely returns the officially generated token set, deliberately designating the token_type dynamically as DPoP instead of the standard Bearer representation. This explicitly signals dynamically to the client that all subsequent API utilization strictly requires valid DPoP proofs.
Artifact Produced: dpop_bound_access_token (with embedded cnf.jkt claim), refresh_token
13. Client Application constructs DPoP Proof for Resource access

Before successfully accessing protected data, the Client Application dynamically forms a new DPoP Proof JWT actively mapped to the specific targeted resource endpoint. This strictly includes htm=GET and htu=/api/data.
