Multi-Session Support: The sid claim allows the IdP to target a specific session, supporting scenarios where the user has multiple concurrent sessions (e.g., desktop + mobile). The jti claim enables idempotent processing — the RP ignores duplicate logout tokens.
Webhook Overhead: SPs are strictly mandated to engineer and expose a publicly accessible, robust HTTPS webhook to synthesize incoming tokens.

The Eventual Consistency Gap: A narrow, inevitable window of vulnerability exists strictly between the exact moment of IdP session termination and the exact moment the SP backend successfully processes the incoming webhook token.

Stateful Constraints: The SP is implicitly required to maintain state server-side to proactively look up and invalidate the targeted session by sid or sub. Modern Single Page Applications (SPAs) leveraging wholly stateless JWT sessions fundamentally cannot process back-channel logouts without engineering an awkward, centralized token deny-list (which defeats the entire premise of the stateless architecture).

No Browser Cookie Cleanup: Back-channel logout terminates the server-side session but does not clear the browser cookie. If the RP's session cookie remains valid in the browser, the RP must handle the "session not found" condition gracefully on the next request.
Verdict: Back-channel logout represents the rigorous, recommended OIDC logout standard for all contemporary identity implementations.

SAML SLO Protocol Mechanics and Bindings SAML Single Logout (codified strictly in SAML 2.0 Profiles §6.4) governs the complex mechanics of federated logout for legacy enterprise infrastructure. Within the SAML SLO paradigm:

The initiating SP crafts a heavily signed XML <LogoutRequest> designating the user's specific NameID and contextual SessionIndex. <samlp:LogoutRequest xmlns:samlp="urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:protocol" ID="_req123456" IssueInstant="2026-03-24T12:00:00Z" Version="2.0"> <saml:Issuer>https://sp.example.com/metadata</saml:Issuer> <saml:NameID Format="urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:1.1:nameid-format:emailAddress"> user@enterprise.local </saml:NameID> <samlp:SessionIndex>session-uuid-555</samlp:SessionIndex> </samlp:LogoutRequest>

The central IdP securely ingests this request, verifies the XML enveloping signature, programmatically parses its internal session index, and subsequently crafts identically targeted <LogoutRequest> XML messages.

The IdP distributes these localized requests to all peripheral downstream SPs currently holding interconnected active sessions. This distribution mechanism is entirely dictated by the target SP's configured SAML Protocol Binding configuration. It routinely occurs via an intricate HTTP-Redirect Binding (a brittle front-channel ping-pong sequence shuttling the raw payload strictly via massive, DEFLATE-compressed, base64-encoded URL parameters through the browser) or via a robust SOAP Binding (explicit back-channel server-to-server XML POST requests securely traversing the backend infrastructure).

Every single peripheral SP is strictly mandated to successfully halt and process the core request, evaluate the structural SessionIndex, explicitly execute local session termination in its server-side cache, and aggressively construct a definitive XML <LogoutResponse> asserting that the designated session footprint is fundamentally dead.

The IdP painstakingly aggregates every single incoming status vector chronologically across the network array and ultimately distills a single final, summarizing <LogoutResponse> to physically bounce back to the originally initiating SP interface.

The Fragility of SAML SLO Chains SAML SLO is structurally characterized by profound fragility. The protocol strictly requires that every single participating SP accurately processes and responsibly responds to the termination loop. If an isolated SP midway through the loop simply fails to respond, responds exceedingly slowly, or throws a stack trace, the entire aggregated logout transaction routinely stalls, locking the user's browser in a blank state. Furthermore, front-channel SAML SLO inherently relies on a dizzying, looping chain of HTTP 302 redirects forcefully bouncing the browser between a dozen discrete SP domains. Every individual redirect represents an independent point of catastrophic network failure.

The Pragmatic Enterprise Alternative Due to this cascading unreliability, the vast majority of scale enterprise environments deliberately disable SAML SLO or selectively provision it for only a minuscule subset of deeply trusted core SPs.

The pragmatic, prevailing industry standard completely sidesteps SAML SLO by relying wholly on compressed session maximums augmented by Conditional Access. If all peripheral SP downstream sessions are engineered to expire organically within a rigid 1-hour window, and the central IdP forcefully demands re-authentication coupled with Conditional Access compliance checks on every new application launch, then overtly terminating the central IdP root session logically neutralizes long-term access. The surviving SP sessions seamlessly "age out" naturally within the short, bounded timeout window, successfully achieving eventual global logout while entirely evading the disastrous protocol overhead of SLO.
Logout architectural scope strictly dictates exactly what session surface area is being targeted for termination.

Single-App Logout (Local Logout): The user explicitly logs out of one specific application. The backend destroys that specific application's session, but the root IdP session and all other federated SP sessions remain fully active. Navigating directly to a different federated application will trigger silent re-authentication because the IdP root session is still pristine. This represents the most robust, predictable, and reliable logout model in production identity because it explicitly isolates the action to a single domain, sidestepping the nightmare of federated global coordination.

Global Logout (Single Logout): The user's semantic intent is to aggressively eradicate all access everywhere simultaneously. To succeed, the application must explicitly route the browser to the central IdP's logout endpoint (e.g., /v2/logout). This forces the IdP to kill its root session cookie, blindly blast back-channel notifications to all connected SPs, and subsequently force the local app to clear its own cookies.

Global logout is technically mandatory solely for localized threat vectors: shared or kiosk devices (ensuring the next operator inherits absolutely no active session state), zero-day security compromises (where stolen credentials mandate immediate termination to halt data exfiltration), and strict regulatory compliance controls (e.g., HIPAA dictating instant session death upon a clinician explicitly clocking out).

"Logout is hard" — The Industry Consensus Because reliable global logout across an arbitrary multi-vendor federation remains a mathematically unsolved problem at scale, the emerging centralized consensus recommendation operates strictly on a tiered architectural compromise. Enterprise security architects must fundamentally categorize their integrated service providers into targeted assurance groups:

Strict Real-Time Enforcement (Kiosk & High-Risk SPs): For physical kiosk devices, shared clinical iPads within highly regulated healthcare environments, and core corporate financial ledgers, orphaned session survival presents catastrophic, fundamentally unacceptable business security routing risks. These highly-targeted applications unilaterally mandate strict OIDC Back-Channel Logout integrations coupled heavily with aggressive, deeply shortened absolute idle session timeouts (e.g., maximum 10-15 minutes of idle tolerance before localized forced aging occurs).

Eventual Consistency Acceptance (Standard SPs): For vast structural swaths of standard digital productivity infrastructure (IT ticketing service portals, intranet collaboration wikis, asynchronous HR utilities), the extreme operational overhead of enforcing and validating a deeply federated logout termination loop is mathematically unjustifiable. Security architectures fundamentally accept eventual consistency within these domains by explicitly leveraging short, rigid absolute session timeouts (allowing the session to die organically within a predictable 60-minute window). If the root IdP session is systematically terminated, the user structurally cannot renew these peripheral sessions; they will simply, seamlessly age out gracefully.

Event-Driven Signal Architecture: For instantaneous, asynchronous session revocation triggered purely by centralized threat intelligence anomalies (treating explicit, manual user logout identically to receiving a generalized Impossible Travel Alert or localized malware detection event), advanced architectures explicitly deploy the Continuous Access Evaluation Protocol (CAEP / SSE, §41). CAEP structurally flips the brittle federated logout paradigm; instead of desperately chaining fragile HTTP requests horizontally through user web browsers, the central, authoritative IdP explicitly pushes a real-time session-revoked event payload directly into a secure, multi-tenant backend streaming bus that all systematically interconnected SPs actively consume.
Tiered Logout Architecture Production deployments increasingly match the logout mechanism to the sensitivity of the target resource:
Financial systems, admin consoles
Back-channel logout + CAEP + session expiry
External SaaS, partner portals
CAEP event push + short session max-age
Session expiry (no active logout)

As extensively documented in the architecture discussions (§35.1, §35.2), the introduction of an OAuth Proxy or a strictly defined Backend-for-Frontend (BFF) fundamentally subsumes frontend session management. In these architectures, the proxy traverses beyond mere token mediation; it assumes the role of the authoritative session manager for the entire local application perimeter.
The proxy entirely centralizes the session lifecycle:
It processes session creation by securely caching downstream access and refresh tokens locally (or in a distributed cache like Redis) after a successful backend OAuth authorization code exchange.
It autonomously executes token renewal by transparently exchanging stored refresh tokens for fresh access tokens before downstream expiry.
Each client implements refresh logic
Revoke token at IdP + clear client state
Clear one cookie + revoke one RT
Each API calls introspection endpoint
Proxy validates once, results shared
Re-authenticate with new scope
Proxy re-acquires token with different scope
Per-client, easily circumvented
Per-session (harder to circumvent)
Distributed across all services

Architectural Translation Layer The OAuth proxy essentially functions as a real-time cryptographic translation layer. The frontend browser client is deliberately and entirely naive to the underlying existence of standard OIDC tokens; it simply passes a hardened, opaque, encrypted standard HTTP cookie blindly on every outbound network request. The localized proxy systematically intercepts this inbound cookie, decrypts or resolves it to explicitly query the internal Redis session datastore, extracts the raw structural access_token, and seamlessly injects it into the upstream backend request strictly as a standard Authorization: Bearer <token> HTTP header.
HttpOnly session cookie (opaque, random)
Refresh token (encrypted cookie or server-side store)
JWT access token (Bearer header)
CSRF token (Double Submit Cookie)
Token metadata (expiry, scopes, user info)

The session cookie presented by the browser is a simple opaque random value with no embedded claims. The proxy maps this cookie to the stored token state, performs refresh if needed, and injects the appropriate Authorization: Bearer <jwt> header into the upstream request. This decoupling means the browser never sees a JWT (reducing token theft surface), token rotation is entirely server-side (no client-side refresh logic), and multiple upstream APIs can receive differently-scoped tokens from the same session.
1. SPA triggers isolated login hook

Reacting to a human user event natively, the Browser SPA initiates a standard GET /login call directed exclusively at the specialized Backend-For-Frontend (BFF) operational proxy sitting locally on the same parent network domain.
2. Proxy dispatches standalone OIDC OAuth flow

Instead of returning Javascript logic to the insecure Browser environment, the BFF proxy fully assumes the complex OAuth orchestration natively. It negotiates the intricate Authorization Code Flow explicitly with the remote central Identity Provider.
3. Identity provider returns high-level token suite

The robust OP validates the central authentication logic properly and formally issues complex JSON Web Tokens (an Access Token and Refresh Token array structurally) back securely to the isolated local BFF node logically bypassing the frontend SPA.
4. Local proxy defangs token state and secures context

The BFF Proxy caches the high-value JWT artifacts securely inside its highly protected continuous memory structure (e.g., a Redis persistence engine), cleanly mapping them to an ephemeral UUID alphanumeric session tracking string natively.
5. BFF sets highly restricted transparent cookie vector

Instead of exposing tokens to XSS fundamentally, the BFF returns a redirect terminating in a rigid Set-Cookie header establishing the UUID logic. The explicit __Host- prefix cleanly mandates HTTPS logic locally while enforcing HttpOnly, Secure, and SameSite=Strict browser constraints structurally preventing token theft vectors.
6. SPA attempts cross-proxy downstream network call

The human user navigates a native application dashboard actively. The Browser blindly issues an underlying XHR GET /api/data execution explicitly attaching the impenetrable __Host-Session secure cookie payload flawlessly toward the local BFF.
7. BFF extracts and resolves UUID mapping state

The robust BFF interceptor validates the inbound cookie string structure smoothly. Leveraging the UUID mapping gracefully against the secure Redis data cluster, it safely pulls the genuine original Access Token JWT out of its protected vault locally.
8. Proxy submerges access token in internal protocol headers

Removing the opaque session cookie organically, the BFF seamlessly constructs a completely new, internal environment backend payload mapping. It forcefully injects the genuine recovered JWT structurally inside the rigid Authorization: Bearer HTTPS backend protocol specification structurally.
9. Upstream microservice Enclave algorithmically evaluates JWT

The deeply hidden backend Microservice endpoint directly receives the complex JWT. Unaware of cookies entirely natively, it simply statelessly computes the mathematical OP signature array validating the JSON payload flawlessly.
10. Microservice confirms resource ownership delivering target data

Confirming logical authorization parameters structurally, the protected API enclave intelligently returns an HTTP 200 OK securely housing the explicitly requested dataset dynamically over TLS to the waiting local BFF proxy node.
11. BFF finalizes bridge delivering unencumbered UI state

Upon validation of the backend array transmission perfectly, the central BFF logically acts as an identical reverse proxy, streaming the target data organically down seamlessly to the frontend Javascript SPA runtime completely cleanly.

Proxy-Level Termination Orchestration Consequently, during the logout lifecycle, the entire federated execution sequence is vastly simplified and perfectly centralized globally within the gateway architecture:
The user manually triggers the explicit /logout endpoint uniquely handled by the OAuth proxy.

The proxy actively destroys its own localized, HttpOnly and Secure session cookie, instantly severing the user's fundamental browser access to any downstream API protected directly behind the gateway boundary.

Prior to arbitrarily redirecting the user to the IdP for global federated termination, the proxy purposefully executes a critical, direct back-channel HTTP call to the authorization server's dedicated revocation endpoint (RFC 7009). It proactively submits the cached, long-lived refresh token to be explicitly killed, guaranteeing the token family mathematically cannot be resurrected by a malicious actor exploring a compromised cache infrastructure weeks later.
The proxy conclusively computes the standard HTTP 302 redirect string, instructing the browser to elegantly execute the IdP's explicit /v2/logout or end-session flow.
CIAM Identity Provider Integration Patterns

The OAuth proxy's centralised position in the session lifecycle makes it the natural integration point for real-time session revocation signals from external identity providers. Two dominant CIAM provider patterns illustrate this:

Okta Shared Signals Framework (SSF). Okta was one of the first identity providers to implement the OpenID Shared Signals Framework specification. Okta's SSF implementation supports both push and poll delivery modes, transmits CAEP events (session-revoked, credential-change, token-claims-change) in real-time, and integrates with Okta's threat detection engine to automatically emit events when suspicious activity is detected. An SSF Stream Management API at /api/v1/streams allows receivers to register and manage streams, with subject identifier formats supporting iss_sub, email, and opaque. Okta positions SSF as the foundation for its Zero Trust architecture, enabling real-time session revocation across SaaS applications that integrate with Okta as their identity provider. An OAuth proxy receiving Okta SSF events can immediately invalidate the corresponding session cache entry and clear the client cookie, achieving sub-second logout propagation.

Google RISC Implementation. Google was the original pioneer of the RISC protocol, implementing it as part of Google Identity Services. Google delivers RISC events via webhook (push mode) to registered relying parties and supports all eight RISC event types, with Account Purged being the most critical for GDPR compliance. Relying parties register a RISC receiver endpoint in the Google Cloud Console, and Google provides event verification libraries for Java and Python. Subject identifiers use the email format (Google account email addresses), and TLS 1.3 is enforced with signature verification via Google's JWKS endpoint. Google's RISC implementation predates the OpenID SSF standardisation effort and was a primary driver of the RISC specification; Google has committed to migrating to the OpenID SSF standard format. For consumer-facing CIAM architectures relying on Google as a social identity provider, the proxy must implement a RISC receiver endpoint that validates incoming events, maps the email subject to the local session, and triggers immediate session termination.

As modeled in the risk-based authentication framework (§28.6), an application session's assurance level is not a static binary property. Session elevation transpires when an active, mathematically valid session established at a basic assurance tier (e.g., AAL1, utilizing a localized password) is dynamically upgraded to a more resilient assurance tier (e.g., AAL2, demanding cryptographic MFA) strictly when the user requests access to sensitive, isolated resources. The underlying session structure is updated natively in place to reflect this elevated Level of Assurance (LoA).
Low (profile viewing, content browse)
Password only (already satisfied)
Medium (order placement, data export)
OTP sent to registered device
High (financial transaction, PII access)
FIDO2 security key or biometric
Critical (admin operations, bulk data export)
FIDO2 with PIN + device trust verification

Crucially, elevation lifetimes are explicitly decoupled from the base session lifetime. For example, a global AAL1 persistent session might gracefully endure for an 8-hour shift, but an aggressive AAL2 elevation might only survive for 15 minutes. Following that 15-minute window, the user retains uninterrupted access to standard intranet resources, but will be seamlessly intercepted and prompted to re-provide a FIDO2 credential before modifying financial routing settings.
Inherits base session lifetime
Lower (elevated access persists as long as base session)
Better (one session, seamless)
Separate short-lived token (5–15 min)
Higher (elevated access expires quickly)
Moderate (may require re-step-up)
Highest (each sensitive op requires step-up)
Worse (repetitive for bulk operations)
Most CIAM platforms adopt the independent elevation token approach, issuing a separate JWT with elevated acr claims and a short lifetime (typically 5–15 minutes).
OIDC Context and Claim Mechanics

The acr and acr_values interface: When a downstream Relying Party identifies an operation requiring high assurance, it triggers a new OIDC authorization request appending the acr_values parameter (Authentication Context Class Reference), explicitly establishing the required security floor (e.g., urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:ac:classes:PhishingResistant). If the IdP verifies that the current root session does not meet this vector, it forces an interactive step-up challenge. The subsequent ID token securely asserts the accomplished assurance level via the acr claim. \{ "iss": "https://idp.example.com", "sub": "user-123", "aud": "finance-api-client", "acr": "urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:ac:classes:PhishingResistant", "amr": ["pwd", "fido2"], "exp": 1742915000 \} Note: The Authentication Methods References (amr) array serves as a crucial localized diagnostic. It transparently confirms to the RP exactly how the user stepped up (identifying they sequentially utilized a standard password and a hardware FIDO2 token during the session lifecycle context).

The auth_time claim: This token claim explicitly documents the precise Unix timestamp denoting when the user last performed an interactive, human-present authentication ceremony. RPs analyze the auth_time to rigidly enforce policy "freshness". If corporate policy dictates, "a user must have authentically interacted within the last 5 minutes to submit an irreversible payment," an ID token possessing an auth_time evaluating to 30 minutes old will be overtly rejected by the RP. The RP subsequently re-routes the browser to the IdP with parameters prompt=login and max_age=300, forcibly demanding fresh human interaction without intrinsically destroying the underlying foundational session.
User must present credentials regardless of existing session
IdP authenticates to the specified assurance level
Re-authenticate if older than 5 min
IdP re-authenticates if auth_time is more than 300s in the past
User must re-authenticate to the specified ACR

When the IdP receives a request with max_age=300 and the user's last auth_time was 120 seconds ago, the IdP can satisfy the request without user interaction (session is fresh enough). If auth_time was 400 seconds ago, the IdP must prompt the user for re-authentication.

Real-Time Session Revocation: Vendor Comparison

The following table compares the four leading platforms for real-time session revocation and continuous access evaluation, providing federated identity provider selection guidance for CIAM/WIAM architects:
CAEP (proprietary extensions)
Session revoked, location change, policy change
Partial (CAEP claim; proprietary delivery)
Partial (original RISC, migrating)
Partial (CAEP events, SSF transport)
Deep (Entra ID Conditional Access)
Deep (Falcon Zero Trust Assessment)

Notable architectural differences: Okta is the only vendor with full OpenID SSF compliance including poll delivery mode, making it the strongest fit for CIAM architectures that cannot maintain a persistent webhook receiver. CrowdStrike Falcon is unique as a non-identity-provider CAEP transmitter, demonstrating that the SSF mesh architecture supports signal sources beyond traditional IdPs. Microsoft Entra ID CAE offers the deepest ecosystem integration (Conditional Access + Defender + Intune) but remains partially proprietary in its delivery mechanism.

Mobile operating systems historically forced identity architects into a precarious compromise: embed an in-application WebView to collect credentials (which violently exposes the raw password to the app's un-trusted memory space and utterly isolates the resulting session cookie), or bounce the user explicitly and cleanly to the default System Browser.

The industry-standard resolution—codified heavily in RFC 8252 (OAuth 2.0 for Native Apps) and the resulting AppAuth SDK pattern—structurally eliminates the isolated WebView anti-pattern. Modern mobile session architectures explicitly mandate the rigorous use of ASWebAuthenticationSession (iOS) or Custom Chrome Tabs (Android).
The Isolated AppAuth Lifecycle
When a user interactively authenticates, the native mobile application legally summons an OS-level system browser overlay.

The user authenticates natively against the IdP via this overlay. Crucially, the resulting persistent session cookie (ESTSAUTHPERSISTENT or equivalent) is physically written exclusively to the mobile OS's shared System Browser cookie jar, securely outside the native app's sandbox.

The IdP securely returns an authorization code via a mathematically registered deep link (e.g., com.enterprise.app://callback). The native app inherently exchanges this explicit code for highly bound access and refresh tokens.

Session Synchronization: The native mobile application orchestrates its localized session entirely via the ongoing rotation of the refresh token. However, if the user subsequently downloads an arbitrarily adjacent corporate application, that new application's explicit invocation of ASWebAuthenticationSession natively accesses the deeply shared System Browser cookie jar. The IdP universally recognizes the pristine root session and silently issues a fresh authorization code, magically achieving Single Sign-On across discrete, deeply sandboxed mobile applications without explicitly sharing token material natively between the separate binaries.
ASWebAuthenticationSession + Safari cookie jar
All apps using the same IdP domain
Chrome Custom Tabs + Chrome cookie jar
All apps using Chrome for auth
ASWebAuthenticationSession + Safari cookie jar
All apps using Safari for auth

When a user authenticates to ciam.example.com in App A, the system browser sets session cookies for that domain. When App B later opens the system browser for the same IdP, the existing session cookies are sent — the user is silently authenticated without re-entering credentials.
Keychain (kSecClassGenericPassword)
Hardware-backed encryption (Secure Enclave)
Android Keystore-backed encryption
Platform Keychain via libraries
Delegates to native Keychain/Keystore

Critical rules for mobile token storage: never store tokens in NSUserDefaults (iOS) or SharedPreferences (Android) — these are unencrypted or weakly encrypted and accessible to other apps on rooted/jailbroken devices. Ensure no logging framework captures refresh tokens or access tokens. On Android, server-side token revocation with short lifetimes is preferred over client-side cleanup on app uninstall (which is unreliable).

⚠️ Session desynchronisation caveat: If the user logs out of the IdP in App A, the system browser clears the IdP session cookies. App B may still hold valid access and refresh tokens obtained from the previous session. Without back-channel logout or token revocation, App B's tokens remain valid until expiry. This is the mobile manifestation of the same one-to-many session disconnect described in §40.3.
EDR Integration: CrowdStrike Falcon as CAEP Transmitter

An emerging pattern in the CAEP ecosystem is the use of Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) platforms as CAEP transmitters, extending continuous access evaluation beyond pure identity events to encompass full device security posture. CrowdStrike's Falcon platform integrates with the Shared Signals Framework to emit device posture events: when Falcon detects a device going out of compliance (malware detected, OS exploit, disabled security controls), it generates a CAEP Device Compliance Change event transmitted through the SSF infrastructure to all registered receivers. Receivers can immediately block access from the non-compliant device, enabling real-time device trust evaluation without requiring periodic MDM polling. For mobile CIAM architectures, this pattern is particularly relevant — an OAuth proxy or BFF receiving CrowdStrike CAEP events can instantly terminate sessions originating from compromised mobile devices, closing the gap between identity-based and device-based access control in the WIAM domain.

Modern browser privacy engines—specifically Apple's Intelligent Tracking Prevention (ITP), Mozilla's Enhanced Tracking Protection (ETP), and Google's evolving Privacy Sandbox—have fundamentally broken foundational, decade-old session architectures.

passages.text: These engines structurally neutralize cross-site tracking by inherently blocking third-party cookies by default. In a federated identity context, if the downstream SP (operating on app.com) attempts to silently verify session state against the IdP (operating on idp.com) using a hidden <iframe> (the legacy OIDC prompt=none pattern), the browser aggressively strips the IdP's session cookies from that internal background request.
passages.headingText: 40.11 The Browser Privacy Engine Session Crisis

passages.text: The Silent SSO Failure: Deprived of its cookies, the IdP mathematically cannot identify the active user and categorically rejects the silent SSO request. The frontend Single Page Application (SPA) is subsequently forced to execute rigid, full-page redirects explicitly back to the IdP, violently interrupting the localized UX state and killing unsaved work.
passages.headingText: 40.11 The Browser Privacy Engine Session Crisis

passages.text: The Front-Channel Logout Failure: As examined comprehensively in §40.4, legacy front-channel logout physically cannot execute because the localized <iframe> logout requests originating from the IdP fundamentally cannot transmit the required target SP session cookies due to these same privacy boundaries.
passages.headingText: 40.11 The Browser Privacy Engine Session Crisis

passages.text: Architectural Remediation To survive this structural privacy revolution, modern identity architectures are being systematically rebuilt to strictly avoid cross-site tracking flags:
passages.headingText: 40.11 The Browser Privacy Engine Session Crisis

passages.text: Custom Domains (CNAMEs): Deploying the IdP strictly under a delegated subdomain of the primary application (e.g., login.app.com speaking to app.com). Because both origin endpoints mathematically share the top-level domain entity (eTLD+1), the browser natively treats the token exchange and resulting session cookies strictly as first-party context, deliberately bypassing ITP restrictions comprehensively.
passages.headingText: 40.11 The Browser Privacy Engine Session Crisis

passages.text: The BFF Pivot: Eradicating browser-based silent SSO iframes entirely by securely pushing the token exchange infrastructure permanently behind a Backend-for-Frontend (BFF) proxy (§35.2), heavily leveraging localized HttpOnly, SameSite=Lax cookies that explicitly execute completely invisibly to backend ITP mechanics.
passages.headingText: 40.11 The Browser Privacy Engine Session Crisis

passages.text: Modern interactive applications fundamentally rely on persistent, long-lived bidirectional network protocols—specifically WebSockets, Server-Sent Events (SSE), and gRPC streams—to transit real-time data. These streaming protocols drastically complicate the standard HTTP session validation lifecycle, directly attacking the core architectural premise of short-lived access tokens.

In a standard transient REST architecture, the API gateway or backend resource server independently validates the access token's exp (expiration) claim on every discrete, individual HTTP request. However, WebSockets execute precisely a single initialization handshake (the HTTP 101 Switching Protocols upgrade), after which the connection fundamentally remains physically clamped open indefinitely for hours or days, exchanging raw binary or masked text frames entirely without any inherent HTTP request headers.

The Initial Handshake Vulnerability The dominant, fatal anti-pattern involves extracting the user's localized access_token and recklessly passing it via the initial HTTP upgrade request (either via the standard Authorization header, or dangerously embedded as a URI query parameter to forcefully bypass native browser WebSockets API limitations). The gateway validates the token strictly at time T0T_0T0​ and confidently opens the tunnel. If that specific JWT subsequently expires 5 minutes later, the persistent TCP connection structurally remains completely open, highly authorized, and unrestricted, rendering the entire short-lived CIAM token rotation paradigm logically useless.

Continuous Assertion Architecture To cryptographically secure long-lived streams, enterprise architects must explicitly implement application-level continuous assertion mechanisms that fundamentally mirror the discrete HTTP request security cycle:

Ticket-Based Initialization: Rather than blindly exposing raw access tokens on the wire during the vulnerable upgrade handshake, the frontend explicitly requests a single-use, deeply short-lived (e.g., 10 seconds) "WebSocket Ticket" via a standard, heavily authenticated REST POST call. The SPA frontend subsequently passes strictly this unique, disposable ticket during the 101 Protocol Upgrade, completely shielding the core session artifacts and preventing token leakage into CDN logs.

In-Band Re-Authentication: The application protocol layer operating inside the established WebSocket is structurally upgraded to explicitly enforce token lifecycle events natively. When the core backend event-loop detects that the initializing access token is mathematically approaching true expiration, it actively orchestrates a custom binary or JSON control frame demanding the client transmit a fresh token securely down the pipe.

Hard Gateway Termination Constraints: As a definitive infrastructural fail-safe, modern enterprise API gateways explicitly configure and enforce hard maximum-connection lifetimes (e.g., forcefully severing all WebSocket streams mechanically every 55 minutes without exception). This violently drops the transport connection, categorically forcing the client application to physically re-negotiate the core HTTP upgrade using a demonstrably fresh, cryptographically valid access token obtained via recent stateless refresh token rotation. This deliberate chaos-engineering approach strictly bounds the absolute maximum survival window of an orphaned, post-logout session connection.
Ticket expiry (30s) + session lifetime
Challenge interval (60–300s)
Ticket (30s) + challenge (60–300s) + event (< 5s)
1. Browser SPA requests ephemeral WebSocket ticket from Initial REST API

The Browser SPA initiates the WebSocket authentication handshake by issuing an HTTP POST request to the /ws/ticket endpoint of the Initial REST API (RFC 6750, §2.1). This pre-connect ticket exchange is necessary because the WebSocket protocol (RFC 6455, §4.1) does not support custom headers during the Upgrade handshake, so the SPA must present its OAuth 2.0 bearer token through a dedicated REST endpoint that can validate it against the authorization server.
2. Initial REST API validates bearer token and mints ephemeral ticket

Upon receiving the ticket request, the Initial REST API validates the presented bearer token by verifying its signature, expiration, and audience claims (RFC 6750, §2.1; RFC 7519, §4.1). Once the token is confirmed valid, the server generates a cryptographically random, single-use ticket — typically a UUID v4 — and stores it in a shared ephemeral data store such as Redis with a short time-to-live (TTL) of 30 seconds. This approach follows the pattern described in the OWASP WebSocket Security Cheat Sheet, binding the ticket to the authenticated session to prevent ticket substitution attacks.
3. Initial REST API returns ephemeral ticket in HTTP 200 response

The Initial REST API responds with an HTTP 200 OK containing the ephemeral ticket identifier and its expiration timestamp in a JSON body. This ticket is intentionally short-lived and single-use: the WebSocket Gateway will consume and invalidate it during the upgrade handshake (RFC 6455, §4.2.2), preventing replay attacks where a captured ticket could be reused to establish an unauthorized connection. The ticket value is passed as a query parameter during the WebSocket Upgrade request since custom headers are unavailable in that phase.
Artifact Produced: WebSocket Ticket (wss-auth-12qw)
4. Browser SPA initiates WebSocket Upgrade with ephemeral ticket

Armed with the ephemeral ticket, the Browser SPA opens a WebSocket connection to the WebSocket Gateway by issuing an HTTP GET request with the Upgrade: websocket header (RFC 6455, §4.1). The ticket is passed as a query parameter (?ticket=wss-auth-12qw) because the WebSocket handshake does not support custom headers — this is a well-known limitation that necessitates the separate ticket-acquisition step. The connection is made over wss:// (TLS-secured WebSocket) to maintain confidentiality of both the ticket value and all subsequent application data in transit.
5. WebSocket Gateway validates and consumes single-use ticket

Upon receiving the Upgrade request, the WebSocket Gateway extracts the ticket from the query string and performs an atomic lookup-and-delete operation against the shared Redis store. This consume-on-read pattern ensures the ticket can only be used once, eliminating the replay attack vector where an intercepted ticket could be reused within its 30-second TTL window (OWASP WebSocket Security Cheat Sheet). If the ticket is missing, expired, or already consumed, the Gateway rejects the upgrade with an HTTP 403 Forbidden response, preventing any unauthenticated WebSocket connection from being established.
6. WebSocket Gateway completes HTTP-to-WebSocket protocol switch

After successful ticket validation, the WebSocket Gateway sends the 101 Switching Protocols response (RFC 6455, §4.2.2), confirming the transition from HTTP to the WebSocket protocol. At this point the underlying TCP connection remains open but the framing protocol changes: both the Browser SPA and the Gateway can now exchange bidirectional messages using WebSocket frames (opcodes 0x1 for text, 0x2 for binary). This persistent connection bypasses the stateless request-response model of HTTP, which is precisely why the subsequent in-band token refresh mechanism is necessary to maintain security over the long-lived session.
Artifact Produced: WebSocket Session (persistent bidirectional connection)
7. Browser SPA sends in-band access token refresh via WebSocket control frame

Because the WebSocket connection persists independently of the HTTP session, the Browser SPA must periodically re-assert its authentication state without requiring a full re-handshake. Before the current OAuth 2.0 access token expires, the SPA sends an application-level JSON control message with type: "auth_refresh" containing a freshly obtained access token through the established WebSocket tunnel. This in-band token rotation pattern is recommended by the OWASP WebSocket Security Cheat Sheet and avoids the cost of tearing down and re-establishing the WebSocket connection merely to update credentials (RFC 6455 does not define a mechanism for mid-stream authentication).
8. WebSocket Gateway validates refreshed access token

The WebSocket Gateway receives the auth_refresh control frame, extracts the JWT, and performs standard token validation — verifying the signature against the authorization server's public key (JWKS), checking the exp claim, and confirming the token's audience and issuer (RFC 7515, §4.1; RFC 7519, §4.1). This validation is identical to what the Initial REST API performed in step 2, but now it happens within the WebSocket message processing pipeline rather than through an HTTP middleware chain. If validation fails, the Gateway immediately terminates the WebSocket connection with a close code (e.g., 4001 for custom "Authentication Failed"), ensuring that compromised or expired tokens do not remain associated with an active session.
9. WebSocket Gateway acknowledges token refresh with auth_ack response

Upon successful token validation, the WebSocket Gateway sends an auth_ack control message back to the Browser SPA, confirming that the session's authentication context has been updated. This acknowledgment serves a dual purpose: it signals to the client that it should schedule the next token refresh before the new token's exp claim elapses, and it provides a heartbeat-like confirmation that the connection remains healthy (RFC 6455, §6.5.2). If the client does not receive an auth_ack within a reasonable timeout — or receives a negative acknowledgment — it should assume the session is no longer valid and initiate a full re-authentication flow from step 1.

In modern web development, users frequently operate utilizing dozens of concurrent browser tabs. If a user natively logs out from Tab A, Tabs B through Z must systematically acknowledge that state change immediately, otherwise they will physically display stale, highly-sensitive data or desperately throw rapid 500 Internal Server Error HTTP failures as their internal heartbeat checks blindly strike the terminated IdP endpoint.
Tab Synchronization Mechanisms Frontend engineering architectures must strictly enforce synchronous state propagation actively across the localized browser environment:

The BroadcastChannel API: The explicit modern standard for cross-document messaging. When a user unequivocally executes a logout flow on Tab A, the localized Single Page Application (SPA) explicitly fires a \{"type": "LOGOUT_EVENT"\} payload into a natively instantiated BroadcastChannel('auth_channel'). Every sibling tab inherently listens to this explicit isolated channel. Upon receiving the payload, they instantly halt all pending XHR/Fetch network requests, systematically shred their localized Redux/Zustand memory caches, and seamlessly navigate the physical DOM to the unauthenticated landing route.

The localStorage Event Bus: As a highly robust fallback vector for legacy constraints, SPAs write an explicit cryptographic termination timestamp strictly to localStorage.setItem('auth_sync', Date.now()). Background sibling tabs securely register a hardened window.addEventListener('storage', ...) hook explicitly monitoring that specific storage key. When the storage event structurally fires, the sibling tab inherently understands the root session has been deliberately compromised or terminated and gracefully redirects the physical rendering tree.
Synchronization Event Types A well-designed multi-tab session architecture defines a standard set of synchronization events:
User clicks logout in any tab
Clear tokens, redirect to login
Access token silently refreshed
Update in-memory token store
Server returns 401 on API call
Redirect to login with session expired message
Step-up auth initiated in one tab
Prompt user for step-up if they attempt sensitive action
Update session assurance level
User updates profile in settings
Server notifies account lock
Immediate redirect to lock notification page
User revokes data processing consent
Disable features requiring consent
Race Conditions and Deduplication Multi-tab synchronization introduces race conditions that must be handled carefully:

Concurrent refresh: Two tabs simultaneously detect token expiry and both initiate a refresh. The IdP may reject one request due to refresh token reuse detection. Solution: use a refreshLock flag in localStorage — only one tab at a time may hold the lock.
Concurrent logout: Multiple tabs send logout requests simultaneously. Solution: the logout endpoint should be idempotent (revoking an already-revoked token returns success).

Event ordering: A token_refresh event may arrive after a logout event in a slow tab. Solution: each event carries a monotonically increasing sequence number; the receiving tab ignores events with sequence numbers lower than the highest it has processed.

DR-0003's §38 covers session revocation extensively (direct revocation, backchannel logout, frontchannel logout). This section addresses a harder problem: propagating a revocation signal across independent domains — services that have their own authentication systems and have independently issued sessions for the same user.

Consider this scenario: Alice authenticates with her corporate IdP (Azure AD) and gets sessions at Service A (Jira), Service B (Slack), and Service C (GitHub). Alice's IT admin detects suspicious activity on her account and revokes her session at the IdP. The IdP can propagate this revocation to Jira, Slack, and GitHub if they participate in the same federation (via OpenID Connect Back-Channel Logout). But what about Service D, an independent SaaS application where Alice authenticated with her email and password (not via the corporate IdP)? Service D has no knowledge of Alice's session revocation.
This is fundamentally a notification problem: how do independent domains learn about security events that affect sessions they have independently issued?
Email notification. "We detected suspicious activity. Please log out of all services." Relies on user action, not automated.
Browser-based broadcast. BroadcastChannel API works within the same origin, not cross-origin.
Webhooks between arbitrary domains. Requires pre-registered trust relationships — not realistic for independent services.

Backchannel logout (within a federation). OpenID Connect Back-Channel Logout 1.0 (§40.4) is the standard solution for federated environments. The IdP pushes logout tokens to each RP via a registered backchannel URI. This works well but requires all parties to be in the same federation.

RISC (Relying Party Incident Specification and Control). Google's RISC protocol uses Security Event Tokens (SETs, RFC 8935) to notify RPs about security events. An IdP that detects an event (credential change, account disable) sends a signed SET to each RP's registered webhook. The RP validates the SET's signature and takes action (revoke session, require re-auth).
RISC is limited to the Google ecosystem but demonstrates the pattern that a standardized event notification protocol could provide.

Short session TTL + re-authentication. For truly independent domains, the most practical approach is short session lifetimes. If a session expires every 15–30 minutes and the user must re-authenticate, the window of vulnerability after a revocation is bounded. The user may be inconvenienced, but the security impact is limited.

Token introspection at session validation time. If sessions are backed by tokens (JWT or opaque), the RP can validate the token's revocation status on every request (or every N requests) by calling the issuer's introspection endpoint. This provides near-real-time revocation but adds latency to every request.
All services in same OIDC federation
Google ecosystem (Google as IdP)
Short session TTL + periodic re-auth
Short TTL + token introspection + backchannel logout
User-initiated logout from all services
"Log out of all devices" feature + RP iteration

The key takeaway: there is no universal standard for cross-domain revocation across independent services. Organizations should implement short session TTLs as a baseline, add backchannel logout for federated services, and monitor emerging standards (SET, CAEP session-revoked events) for future improvements.

The traditional session management paradigm relies on a fundamental "trusted until expired" model. When a user successfully authenticates against an Identity Provider (IdP), they are issued a session credential (typically a JWT access token — §37) that explicitly authorizes access for a defined duration, usually between 5 and 60 minutes. If a critical security event occurs during that validity window — a user's account is suspended by an administrator, their credentials are leaked on the dark web, or their device is compromised by a malware infection — the resource provider remains entirely unaware of the catastrophic change in state. This architectural delay creates the revocation gap: the hazardous, exploitable window between a security incident occurring at the Identity Provider and the resource provider actually terminating the active session.
The probability of a security incident occurring during a token's validity window is non-trivial. For a JWT with a 1-hour lifetime, the expected window of exposure after a credential compromise is:
tˉexposure=Tvalidity2=3600 s2=1800 s\bar{t}_{\text{exposure}} = \frac{T_{\text{validity}}}{2} = \frac{3600\,\text{s}}{2} = 1800\,\text{s}tˉexposure​=2Tvalidity​​=23600s​=1800s

For an organisation with nnn concurrent sessions and an incident rate λ\lambdaλ per session per second, the expected number of active sessions operating under compromised credentials at any given time is:
Ncompromised=n⋅λ⋅TvalidityN_{\text{compromised}} = n \cdot \lambda \cdot T_{\text{validity}}Ncompromised​=n⋅λ⋅Tvalidity​

With n=50,000n = 50\\{,\\}000n=50,000 concurrent sessions, \lambda = 10^\\{-6\\} incidents per second, and T_\\{\text\\{validity\\}\\} = 3600\,\text\\{s\\}: N_\\{\text\\{compromised\\}\\} = 180 sessions — an exposure unacceptable in environments handling sensitive data.

For years, the industry attempted to mitigate the revocation gap by artificially shrinking the access token lifetime (down to 5 minutes) and forcing frequent refresh token exchanges. This approach heavily taxes the authorization server, increases network latency, and degrades the user experience during transient network outages, while still mathematically guaranteeing at least a five-minute window for an attacker to pivot inside the environment.
The industry has progressively moved toward faster revocation through several evolutionary stages:
Reduce T_\\{\text\\{validity\\}\\} to 5–15 min
RP calls IdP introspection endpoint per request
3. Pushed authorisation requests (PAR)
IdP pushes security events to RPs in real time

CAEP represents the culmination of this evolution: it combines the low latency of push-based notifications with the specificity of event-driven actions, all without imposing per-request overhead on the relying party.

Continuous Access Evaluation (CAE) fundamentally eliminates this gap. It represents a paradigm shift from static, time-bound trust to dynamic, event-driven policy enforcement. Instead of waiting patiently for a token to expire or a user to interactively re-authenticate, Identity Providers and security tooling continuously monitor the user's risk posture and immediately push revocation signals to resource providers the exact moment a material change is detected. For the related but harder problem of propagating revocation across independent, non-federated domains — where no shared IdP or federation relationship exists — see §39.14. This chapter comprehensively dissects the open standards enabling this real-time architecture — the Shared Signals Framework (SSF), the Continuous Access Evaluation Protocol (CAEP), and Risk Incident Sharing and Coordination (RISC) — alongside their concrete integration into Zero Trust architectures.

The Continuous Access Evaluation Protocol (CAEP) is an OpenID Foundation finalized specification (2025) that defines a standardized set of security event types and payloads for communicating access-relevant contextual changes in near real-time. Where traditional OAuth/OIDC implementations explicitly accept the revocation gap as an unavoidable architectural trade-off for scalable, stateless token validation (§37.3.4), CAEP provides the standard vocabulary for independent platforms to terminate trust dynamically.
passages.headingText: 41.1 CAEP Event Types and Real-Time Session Revocation

passages.text: In a legacy, non-CAEP architecture, an administrator disabling a rogue user account in the directory only prevents future token issuance. The user's existing, mathematically valid access tokens remain operational at the resource server until their exp claim is reached. CAEP solves this elegantly by standardizing the mechanism by which the IdP notifies the downstream resource provider that the session context is fatally invalidated.
passages.headingText: 41.1 CAEP Event Types and Real-Time Session Revocation

passages.text: CAEP defines several core event types (governed under the base URI https://schemas.openid.net/secevent/caep/event-type/), each carrying specific semantics dictating how the receiver must handle the active sessions:
passages.headingText: 41.1 CAEP Event Types and Real-Time Session Revocation

passages.text: .../caep/event-type/session-revoked
passages.headingText: 41.1 CAEP Event Types and Real-Time Session Revocation

passages.text: The IdP explicitly terminated the user's SSO session (e.g., proactive threat response, admin action, explicit logout).
passages.headingText: 41.1 CAEP Event Types and Real-Time Session Revocation

passages.text: Terminate all localized sessions mathematically associated with the subject; add the session ID to an in-memory deny-list; reject all active access tokens derived from the revoked session immediately.
passages.headingText: 41.1 CAEP Event Types and Real-Time Session Revocation

passages.text: .../caep/event-type/token-claims-change
passages.headingText: 41.1 CAEP Event Types and Real-Time Session Revocation

passages.text: The user's fundamental authorization attributes (RBAC roles, group memberships, geographic risk tier) were modified.
passages.headingText: 41.1 CAEP Event Types and Real-Time Session Revocation

passages.text: Re-evaluate all authorization policies using the new claims payload; seamlessly upgrade or downgrade active access without forcing full front-channel re-authentication. The reason field (e.g., admin-initiated, policy-update, certification-review) helps the receiver decide whether to force full re-authentication or silently refresh claims.
passages.headingText: 41.1 CAEP Event Types and Real-Time Session Revocation

passages.text: .../caep/event-type/credential-change
passages.headingText: 41.1 CAEP Event Types and Real-Time Session Revocation

passages.text: The user's password, FIDO2 webauthn passkey, or primary MFA authenticator was updated, added, or purged.
passages.headingText: 41.1 CAEP Event Types and Real-Time Session Revocation

passages.text: Terminate highly sensitive sessions; require the user to actively re-authenticate with the newly provisioned credentials. This event type is closely related to the RISC Account Credential Change Required event but operates at the session level rather than the account level.
passages.headingText: 41.1 CAEP Event Types and Real-Time Session Revocation

passages.text: .../caep/event-type/assurance-level-change
passages.headingText: 41.1 CAEP Event Types and Real-Time Session Revocation

passages.text: The Authentication Assurance Level of the session degraded (e.g., a hardware token was reported lost, breaking the AAL3 binding).
passages.headingText: 41.1 CAEP Event Types and Real-Time Session Revocation

passages.text: Re-evaluate conditional access rules against the current AAL (NIST SP 800-63B); proactively challenge the user for step-up authentication (§28.5) if the degraded level is below the resource's AAL_\\{\text\\{min\\}\\}.
passages.headingText: 41.1 CAEP Event Types and Real-Time Session Revocation

passages.text: .../caep/event-type/device-compliance-change
passages.headingText: 41.1 CAEP Event Types and Real-Time Session Revocation

passages.text: The user's device fell out of MDM compliance, failed an OS boot integrity check, or the local EDR agent disabled the antivirus.
passages.headingText: 41.1 CAEP Event Types and Real-Time Session Revocation

passages.text: Instantly revoke access to any sensitive applications requiring a managed device context (§18); redirect the user's application flows to dedicated remediation portals. The payload may include a device_compliance object with compliant (boolean), reason (string), and remediation_url (string) fields.
passages.headingText: 41.1 CAEP Event Types and Real-Time Session Revocation

passages.text: .../caep/event-type/ip-change
passages.headingText: 41.1 CAEP Event Types and Real-Time Session Revocation

passages.text: The user's source network egress changed dramatically mid-session (e.g., indicating impossible travel vectors, or a sudden pivot to a Tor exit node).
passages.headingText: 41.1 CAEP Event Types and Real-Time Session Revocation

passages.text: Suspend the active session context and trigger risk-based verification workflows (§29.2). The payload includes current_ip, previous_ip, and a risk_score (0.0–1.0) computed by the transmitter's risk engine, used to determine the appropriate response: allow (low risk), challenge with step-up MFA (medium risk), or revoke (high risk).
passages.headingText: 41.1 CAEP Event Types and Real-Time Session Revocation

passages.text: Not all CAEP events require the same urgency of response. The following severity matrix maps event types to recommended response times:
passages.headingText: 41.1 CAEP Event Types and Real-Time Session Revocation

passages.text: Critical events should bypass any batching or deduplication logic and be processed immediately upon receipt.
passages.headingText: 41.1 CAEP Event Types and Real-Time Session Revocation

passages.text: CAEP as the Ultimate JWT Revocation Strategy
passages.headingText: 41.1 CAEP Event Types and Real-Time Session Revocation

passages.text: The JWT revocation problem (§37.3.4) stems from a fundamental architectural tension: JWTs are self-contained and validated statelessly, yet revocation inherently requires state. Three conventional strategies each impose a distinct trade-off — short lifetimes cap exposure but degrade user experience; token blacklists (deny-lists of revoked jti values) reintroduce server-side state on every request; token version claims provide per-user granularity but cannot target individual sessions. CAEP resolves this tension by decoupling the revocation signal from the token lifecycle entirely. When a session termination event occurs, the IdP publishes a CAEP session-revoked event through the SSF, all subscribed Relying Parties receive the SET, and each RP adds the affected JWT's jti to a local deny-list or invalidates the session — achieving near-real-time revocation without polling the IdP or requiring per-request introspection (RFC 7662). This event-driven model (sometimes called Strategy 4 in JWT revocation taxonomies) provides immediate revocation while preserving the stateless validation benefit of JWTs for the vast majority of requests that do not involve a revoked token.
passages.headingText: 41.1 CAEP Event Types and Real-Time Session Revocation

passages.text: The time from revocation event to enforcement can be modelled as:
Tenforce=Tdetect+Tpropagate+Tprocess+TapplyT_{\text{enforce}} = T_{\text{detect}} + T_{\text{propagate}} + T_{\text{process}} + T_{\text{apply}}Tenforce​=Tdetect​+Tpropagate​+Tprocess​+Tapply​
T_\\{\text\\{detect\\}\\} — time for the transmitter to detect the revocation trigger (typically <100 ms)
T_\\{\text\\{propagate\\}\\} — network delivery time (typically 10–200 ms for push delivery)
T_\\{\text\\{process\\}\\} — time for the receiver to validate and parse the SET (typically <10 ms)
T_\\{\text\\{apply\\}\\} — time to update the deny-list (typically <1 ms for an in-memory set)
For a well-configured SSF deployment, the total enforcement latency is typically under 500 ms, compared to the 0–60 minute exposure window of static tokens.

CAEP events are packaged and systematically delivered as Security Event Tokens (SETs). Formally defined in RFC 8417, a SET is a JSON Web Token (JWT) specifically structured to convey discrete security states rather than static identity claims. The JWT header specifies the signing algorithm and key:

The payload inherently includes an events overarching object containing the event type URI mapped to the specific contextual data concerning the state change. A complete SET also carries standard JWT claims (sub, exp, txn) alongside the event-specific payload:

Because SETs are standard, cryptographically signed JWTs, the receiver must rigorously verify the authenticity and integrity of the revocation signal. The validation logic mandates verifying the JWT signature using the IdP's JWKS public keys, validating the aud (audience) claim matches the receiver's identity, ensuring the iat (issued at) is within an acceptable time skew, and strictly recording the jti (JWT ID) in a local replay cache to ensure attackers cannot capture and replay historic session termination events to create denial-of-service conditions.
The following table documents all SET claims defined by RFC 8417:
Issuer identifier (the SSF Transmitter)
Subject of the event (the affected user)
https://idp.example.com/user/72f8e9a1
Intended audience (the SSF Receiver)
https://app.example.com/caep-receiver
Unique event identifier (for deduplication)
Issuance time (epoch seconds)
Expiration time (epoch seconds)
Map of event type URIs to event payloads
Transaction identifier (correlates multiple events)

The events claim is the core of the SET — a JSON object where each key is an event type URI and each value is the event-type-specific payload. Multiple events can be bundled in a single SET, although best practice is one event per SET for processing clarity.
When a Receiver receives a SET, it must perform the following validation steps before processing the event:
Verify JWT signature using the Transmitter's public key (fetched from JWKS endpoint)
Reject SET; log signature failure
Verify aud claim matches the Receiver's registered client identifier
Reject SET; log audience mismatch
Verify exp claim has not passed (SET is not expired)
Reject SET; do not process expired events
Verify iss matches a trusted Transmitter identifier
Reject SET; log unknown issuer
Verify jti has not been previously processed (deduplication)
Discard SET; log duplicate event
Verify iat is within acceptable clock skew (typically 300 seconds)
Reject SET; log clock skew violation
Verify events claim contains at least one recognised event type URI
Reject SET; log unknown event type
Parse event payload and validate subject identifier format
Reject SET; log malformed payload

The deduplication check (step 5) is critical for exactly-once processing guarantees. Receivers must maintain a cache of recently processed jti values. The cache size depends on the expected event volume:
Scache=Revents⋅TdedupS_{\text{cache}} = R_{\text{events}} \cdot T_{\text{dedup}}Scache​=Revents​⋅Tdedup​

Where R_\\{\text\\{events\\}\\} is the event arrival rate and T_\\{\text\\{dedup\\}\\} is the deduplication window (typically 2× the maximum SET lifetime). For a system processing 10,000 events per second with a 2-hour deduplication window: S_\\{\text\\{cache\\}\\} = 72\\{,\\}000\\{,\\}000 entries — requiring a distributed cache (Redis, Memcached) rather than an in-memory set.
SETs must be signed using an asymmetric algorithm. The following algorithms are permitted per RFC 8417:
Minimum acceptable for legacy systems
Recommended for new deployments
For high-security environments
Recommended; efficient and secure
For high-security environments
Maximum security; slower verification
Preferred over RS256/384/512 (better security proof)

HMAC-based algorithms (HS256, HS384, HS512) are not permitted for SETs, as they require symmetric key sharing between Transmitter and Receiver, which violates the federated trust model. The Transmitter publishes its public keys via a JWKS endpoint, typically at /.well-known/jwks.json.

While the CAEP specification strictly defines what is being communicated (the vocabulary and event types), the Shared Signals Framework (SSF) defines how the messages are technically delivered across distributed networks. SSF provides the robust, underlying publisher-subscriber mechanism used to configure, route, and reliably deliver both CAEP and RISC events globally.
The SSF architecture introduces three fundamental components operating in concert:

Transmitter (Publisher): The authoritative entity generating security events. This is typically a primary Identity Provider (Entra ID, Okta), a specialized device management system (Microsoft Intune, Jamf Pro), or a cybersecurity threat detection platform (CrowdStrike Falcon, SentinelOne).

Receiver (Subscriber): The operational entity actively consuming events to autonomously enforce access policy. This is usually a Resource Provider (a SaaS application, an API gateway, Microsoft 365 services) or a centralized SIEM/SOAR ingestion engine.

Stream: The formally configured, persistent pipeline established between a Transmitter and a Receiver. The stream mandates which specific event types the receiver is authorized to consume, how subjects are identified across the boundary, and the preferred transport method for delivery.

A massive hurdle in inter-organizational security telemetry is agreeing on who a user is. SSF resolves this by standardizing subject identification formats, allowing a Transmitter to unambiguously reference a user regardless of how the Receiver internally models or stores identities.

iss_sub (Issuer and Subject): The most common format, heavily used in OIDC federations. It identifies the subject by pairing the identity provider (iss) with the robust, immutable subject ID (sub). \{ "format": "iss_sub", "iss": "https://idp.example.com", "sub": "7a3b4c9d-8888-4f1b" \}
email: Identifies the user via their assigned email address. Highly interoperable but susceptible to identifier recycling issues. \{ "format": "email", "email": "alice.smith@example.com" \}
phone_number: Identifies the user via an E.164 formatted MSISDN. \{ "format": "phone_number", "phone_number": "+12065550100" \}

opaque: A non-identifiable, privacy-preserving identifier, typically a GUID. Widely used when referencing the unique identifier of the event stream itself, ensuring that event telemetry cannot be correlated to human identities outside of the cryptographic tunnel. \{ "format": "opaque", "id": "e5d230dc-0ebe-455a-b22c-54b29f4d6f6" \}
The choice of format affects privacy, portability, and correlation accuracy:
Low (pseudonymous within issuer)
High (within same issuer federation)
Default for CAEP events within a federation
Medium (carrier portability issues)
Mobile-centric identity scenarios
High (no correlation possible)
Low (single transmitter only)
Stream-level identifiers; privacy-sensitive contexts
SSF supports two distinct delivery topologies, catering to disparate network constraints:

Push Delivery: The Transmitter sends HTTP POST requests directly to a pre-registeredwebhook endpoint on the Receiver. Push delivery provides true zero-latency, systemic revocation. The Receiver must acknowledge the payload synchronously with an HTTP 202 Accepted response; if the Receiver is offline or returns a server error, the Transmitter initiates an aggressive exponential backoff retry mechanism to guarantee delivery.

Poll Delivery: The Receiver periodically connects to the Transmitter's dedicated event endpoint to fetch a batch of new events. While inherently less instantaneous than push delivery, polling is an absolute structural necessity for Receivers deployed behind strict corporate intranets (e.g., on-premises legacy applications) that cannot securely expose a public inbound webhook to the internet.
Transmitter delivers SETs to Receiver via HTTP POST
Low (Receiver exposes webhook)
Real-time revocation; low-latency requirements
Receiver periodically fetches SETs from Transmitter endpoint
30–300 seconds (polling interval)
Low (no firewall changes needed)
Environments where push is blocked; batch processing

To establish these pipelines, the Transmitter publishes a standardized metadata document at the /.well-known/ssf-configuration endpoint. This configuration exposes supported event types, signature algorithms, and the URIs for its dynamic stream management APIs. Receivers utilize these REST APIs (executing authenticated POST and DELETE requests) to dynamically add or remove specific subjects from their observation stream, ensuring they only receive telemetry for users actively logged into their platform.

The SSF Stream Management API provides the control plane for the publisher-subscriber relationship. It allows the Receiver to programmatically dictate exactly what telemetry it wants to consume, preventing the Transmitter from needlessly spraying events for users who have no active session with the Receiver.
The standard defines endpoints for CRUD operations on the stream itself, as well as endpoints for adding and removing subjects from the stream.
A Receiver registers a new stream by sending an HTTP POST to the Transmitter's stream registration endpoint:
The Transmitter responds with 201 Created, returning the stream identifier and confirming which events are supported:
Adding a Subject to a Stream

When a user logs into a SaaS application (the Receiver), the application makes a POST request to the Transmitter's stream management endpoint to actively subscribe to events for that specific user. Receivers may also submit a batch of subjects in a single request:

The Transmitter responds with 200 OK, officially binding the subjects to the stream. From this point forward, if alice.smith experiences a credential compromise or an IP change, the Receiver will receive the corresponding CAEP or RISC SET payload.
Removing a Subject from a Stream

When the user explicitly logs out of the SaaS application (or their local session naturally expires), the Receiver must clean up the subscription to stop receiving unneeded events. The SSF specification uses the DELETE method for subject removal:
This active management ensures the event bus remains highly efficient, specifically targeting only the slice of users that represent current operational risk to the Receiver.
Receivers can query the current status of a stream to verify it is active:
CIAM-Scale Session Termination: Progressive Revocation Model

In consumer identity and access management (CIAM) deployments — where millions of users hold stateless JWT access tokens across hundreds of micro-services — session termination requires a layered strategy that progressively shortens the revocation window. The four-level model illustrates how SSF/CAEP fits into the broader revocation architecture:
1 — Short access token lifetime
Primary defence; passive "revocation by patience"
2 — Refresh token revocation
Delete opaque refresh token from server-side store
Cuts off new access token issuance
3 — CDN-edge revocation list
IdP publishes revoked jti set to CDN-backed cache (30 s TTL); API gateways check per request
Covers mid-flight JWTs at global scale
4 — CAEP event-driven revocation
IdP pushes CAEP session-revoked SET via SSF; RPs add jti to local deny-list
Near-real-time revocation without polling

Levels 1–3 operate on passive or eventual-consistency models: the access token expires (Level 1), the refresh token is rejected on next use (Level 2), or the revocation list propagates through the CDN cache hierarchy (Level 3). CAEP (Level 4) is the only mechanism that provides active, push-based revocation — the IdP initiates the signal, and the RP acts on it in near-real-time. In production CIAM deployments, all four levels operate simultaneously: Levels 1–2 handle the common case (normal session expiry and logout), Level 3 provides a fallback for RPs that have not yet adopted SSF, and Level 4 ensures critical events (account compromise, credential change) propagate to SSF-enabled RPs within sub-second latency.

Working parallel to CAEP within the bounds of the Shared Signals Framework is the Risk Incident Sharing and Coordination (RISC) specification. While CAEP focuses on real-time session modifications enabling dynamic access control within a local enterprise, RISC focuses on communicating profound, cross-platform account-level security incidents across organizational boundaries.

RISC is particularly important in consumer-facing scenarios where a user may have accounts across multiple services — for example, a Google account used to sign in to Spotify, Netflix, and GitHub. When the Google account is compromised, RISC enables Google to notify all connected services so they can take protective action.
RISC defines the following critical, long-tail incident types:
Account Credential Change Required
.../risc/event-type/account-credential-change-required
The transmitter has definitive, cryptographic evidence the user's password or authentication mechanism was compromised.
Proactively invalidate existing sessions; prompt the user for mandatory, unskippable credential rotation on next login attempt.
.../risc/event-type/account-purged
The user's account entity was permanently deleted.
Securely cleanse associated federated profile data; terminate upstream SSO bindings immediately.
.../risc/event-type/account-disabled
The account was administratively suspended due to egregious policy violations, fraud, or acute risk.
Halt operational reliance on the account for SSO; suspend associated downstream resources.
.../risc/event-type/account-enabled
A previously suspended or frozen account was formally restored by administrators.
Re-enable access flows and standard validation processes utilizing the account.
.../risc/event-type/identifier-changed
The user modified a primary routing identifier (e.g., updated their primary email address).
Update internal routing databases; proactively prevent security notifications from flowing to the obsolete identifier.
.../risc/event-type/identifier-recycled
A phone number or email address was formally reassigned to a completely different human user.
Crucial for preventing takeover via recycled MSISDNs (§22.3). Immediately mathematically detach the identifier from the current user account to prevent SMS 2FA interception.
.../risc/event-type/recovery-activated
The user legitimately initiated a high-risk account recovery flow (e.g., lost password, lost hardware MFA device).
Elevate baseline risk scoring; actively delay highly sensitive operations; challenge for secondary out-of-band verification.
.../risc/event-type/sessions-revoked
The transmitter terminated all sessions globally, indicating a massive active incident response process.
Terminate all downstream sessions immediately; engage SOC playbooks.
RISC events share the same JWT envelope as CAEP events. An identifier-recycled event to prevent 2FA hijacking manifests as follows:
An account-disabled event signals that the account was administratively suspended — for example, due to detected suspicious activity:

Though structurally identical on the network layer (they both leverage SETs over the SSF publisher-subscriber fabric), their fundamental applications are strictly segregated by intent, duration, and target audience:
Continuous Access Evaluation Protocol (CAEP)
Risk Incident Sharing and Coordination (RISC)
Session-level events within a federation
Account-level events across platforms
Real-time revocation of active sessions
Cross-platform account security coordination
Pseudonymous (iss_sub, opaque)
Directly identifying (email, phone_number)
Fine-grained (specific session, specific claim change)
Coarse-grained (entire account state change)
Organisational IdP, SSO provider
Consumer identity provider (Google, Microsoft, Apple)
Internal resource servers, microservices
Third-party applications, federated services
<1 second (real-time revocation)
Minutes to hours (batch processing acceptable)
Push (primary), Poll (fallback)
Push (primary), Poll (fallback)
Pseudonymous identifiers; subject privacy preserved
Email/phone exposed; privacy trade-off for cross-platform correlation
Zero Trust, insider threat prevention
GDPR data erasure, account lifecycle management
Small (single session ID + event type)
Small (account identifier + event type)
Single event per SET recommended
Single event per SET required

The historical OIDC Back-Channel Logout specification (§40.4) was an early standard aimed at solving federated logout, but it is effectively superseded by CAEP for modern architectures. Where Back-Channel Logout is rigidly tied to explicit, binary logout events, CAEP provides a vastly richer, granular, continuous expression of integrated security state.
The Identifier Recycled Anti-Pattern

The RISC identifier-recycled event deserves special attention. When a user deletes their account and the identity provider recycles the identifier (e.g., reassigning user@example.com to a different person), relying parties that retain data associated with the old user must be notified to prevent data leakage. Without RISC:
Alice creates account alice@gmail.com on App X, stores personal data.
Alice deletes her Google account.
Bob registers alice@gmail.com on Google.
Bob signs in to App X using alice@gmail.com.
App X serves Alice's personal data to Bob.

With RISC, step 2.5 would insert an identifier-recycled event, allowing App X to purge Alice's data before Bob ever accesses it. This flow is mandated by GDPR Article 17 (right to erasure) and is increasingly required by app store policies.

Key Takeaway: CAEP is the right choice for real-time session revocation within an organisation's Zero Trust architecture. RISC is the right choice for cross-platform account lifecycle management, particularly in consumer identity scenarios.

The Continuous Access Evaluation paradigm is rapidly migrating from standardization into ubiquitous commercial deployment. Major IAM platforms and security vendors are currently cementing SSF as the definitive standard for interoperable zero-trust security telemetry.
Microsoft Continuous Access Evaluation (CAE)

Microsoft's implementation of CAE within Entra ID serves as the most mature, heavily utilized production case study globally. Entra ID acts as the core foundational signal Transmitter, while Microsoft 365 services (Exchange Online, SharePoint Online, Teams) act as integrated, highly responsive Receivers.

Critical Event Evaluation: Microsoft's CAE bypasses the typical polling delays of OAuth refresh cycles entirely. If a specific critical event occurs in Entra ID — an IT admin deletes the user account, officially disables a user, resets their password, explicitly enables multifactor authentication for the user mid-session, or an administrator manually revokes all refresh tokens via the portal — a definitive session revocation signal is pushed directly to relying services like Exchange Online or SharePoint Online. The Microsoft 365 resource computationally drops the active network connection and strictly rejects the user's existing access token in near real-time, severing access far faster than waiting for a standard JWT expiration.

The 28-Hour Long-Lived Token: A profound architectural consequence of implementing CAE is how it fundamentally alters accepted token lifetimes. Because the Identity Provider mathematically guarantees it will proactively notify the Resource Provider of any critical security incident via the SSF mechanism, Microsoft 365 services can confidently issue access tokens with radically extended lifespans, scaling up to 28 hours (as opposed to standard industry 60-minute JWTs).
